title: A Rare Case of Lambert-Eaton Myasthenia Syndrome Associated with Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary: A 57-year-old man with a 2-month history of lepromatous leprosy treated with rifampicin, clofazimine, and dapsone presented with abdominal distension, constipation, vomiting, and a 10-kg weight loss. He was vitally stable, with peripheral lymphadenopathy and a distended abdomen with shifting dullness. Abdominal CT showed terminal ileal mural thickening, enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes, mesenteric fat stranding, and free intraperitoneal fluid, suggesting either granulomatous infection or a neoplastic process. Paracentesis revealed atypical large lymphocytes; flow cytometry showed an abnormal CD4/CD8 double-negative T-cell population (38%) with multiple aberrancies. Cervical lymph node biopsy confirmed high-grade peripheral T-cell lymphoma, and bone marrow was uninvolved. He was staged as IV and started on dexamethasone. After developing severe sepsis requiring one week in the ICU, he recovered and received six cycles of dose-adjusted EPOCH chemotherapy with intrathecal methotrexate. Aft

title: Capnocytophaga canimorsus Septicemia With Sepsis-Induced Coagulopathy and Endocarditis
case_summary:
An 81-year-old woman presented with 2 days of fever, dizziness, and weakness following a finger dog-bite three days earlier. She also noted nausea and vomiting. Her medical history included coronary artery disease on prasugrel, hypertension on amlodipine, ramipril, and metoprolol, and a peritonsillar abscess 6 months prior.  

On arrival: temperature 39.9 degreesC, blood pressure 107/57 mmHg, pulse 110/min, respiratory rate 29/min, oxygen saturation 90% on 12?L by reservoir mask. Examination revealed a livid fingertip wound on the right third digit and marked mottling of both legs to the groin. Heart and lung sounds were normal.  

Laboratory studies showed CRP 186.2?mg/L, creatinine 1.41?mg/dL, platelets 114???10^9/L, and arterial lactate 6.5?mmol/L. Chest radiograph demonstrated basal atelectasis. She was diagnosed with sepsis, received fluids and piperacillin-tazobactam, and was transferred to the ICU.  

Over the next 48 hours she became anuric and required vasopressors. Repeat lab

title: Vitamin B12 deficiency-induced pseudothrombotic microangiopathy without macrocytosis presenting with acute renal failure: a case report
case_summary:
A 46-year-old Moroccan man with a history of cocaine and alcohol abuse and a 10-pack-year smoking history, detained for 3 months, was referred after prison doctors noted severe anemia (hemoglobin 43 g/L). He reported 2 months of progressive fatigue, arthromyalgia, mild abdominal pain, upper finger paresthesia, left ear tinnitus, and recurrent headaches. He denied fever, bleeding, or bowel changes.

On examination he was afebrile (36  degreesC), blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 80 bpm, and oriented. Findings included pallor, slight epigastric tenderness, and a left tympanic membrane perforation. There was no lymphadenopathy, purpura, or hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic exam was normal except for mild finger paresthesia.

Laboratory tests showed: hemoglobin 36 g/L, MCV 87 fl, hematocrit 10.8%, RDW 27%, neutropenia (0.59?10?/L), and a normal platelet count (15?10?/L). Creatinine was 176.8 ?mol/L with azotemia of 24.9 mmol/L. Peripheral smear showed marked anisopoikilocytosis and 10% s

title:
Beware the Unexpected Infection: Disseminated Varicella
Zoster Virus Mimicking A Drug Eruption
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 79-year-old man was admitted with abdominal pain and diagnosed with ileitis and a partial small-bowel obstruction. His medical history included an intraductal papillary neoplasm of the bile duct with liver and presumed lung metastases, for which he had been receiving capecitabine for 8 weeks. He also had a widespread papulovesicular rash with hemorrhagic centers on his face, chest, and back, which first appeared after initiation of capecitabine and worsened with each subsequent treatment. Hematologic studies did not suggest either a drug eruption or a viral infection. Because of concern for an adverse cutaneous reaction to capecitabine, the drug was discontinued and systemic corticosteroids were started. Dermatology was consulted, and punch biopsy specimens were obtained for histological examination and direct immunofluorescence.

title: Disseminated nocardiosis in a patient with AIDS and B-cell non-Hodgkin's lymphoma: a case report
case_summary:
A previously healthy male smoker presented with a 3-week history of asthenia, intermittent fevers up to 38.5  degreesC with chills, and dyspnea on moderate exertion. Over the preceding year, he had noted involuntary weight loss of approximately 20 kg, nocturnal diaphoresis, and progressive asthenia; for 2 months he also had abdominal distension and nonbloody, nonmucoid diarrhea. 
On examination, oropharyngeal candidiasis was noted. A fourth-generation HIV test was positive. Laboratory studies showed white cells 2.8 ? 10^3/uL, hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, platelets 135 ? 10^3/uL, lactate dehydrogenase 1860 U/L, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 80 mm/h. CD4+ count was 44 cells/mm^3, CD8+ count 345 cells/mm^3, with a CD4/CD8 ratio of 0.128. A tuberculin skin test was negative.
Chest CT demonstrated a 5 ? 2.5 cm subpleural bulla in the left lower lobe apical segment, diffuse hypodensity of the lung parenchyma with hypodense?walled lesions predominantly in the lower lobes, pleural thickeni

title: Duodenal perforation secondary to stent migration after ERCP for hepatobiliary tuberculosis: Case report of a lethal complication in a young patient
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 26-year-old woman from Manila presented with a 5-month history of jaundice associated with intermittent epigastric pain, weight loss, pruritus, and tea-colored urine. She had no comorbidities or prior surgeries. On examination, she was normotensive (110/70 mmHg), heart rate 80 bpm, afebrile, with icteric sclerae and generalized jaundice; the abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender. Transabdominal ultrasound showed a nondilated common bile duct, intrahepatic ductal dilatation with segmental areas of dilation, and biliary sludge. A diagnosis of choledocholithiasis with possible biliary stricture secondary to hepatobiliary tuberculosis was made. 

ERCP revealed a patulous ampulla, a nondilated CBD tapering at the common hepatic duct over 13.9 mm, dilated intrahepatic ducts (right 10.1 mm, left 7.3 mm), and no filling defects. After graded dilation of the common hepatic duct stricture and partial sphincterotomy, a 17-cm Fr 8.5 plastic biliary stent was placed with good bile egress.

title: Multiple electrolyte imbalances and mixed acid-base disorder posing a diagnostic dilemma: a case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old Hispanic American woman visiting from out of state was brought to the emergency department with sudden-onset stiffness and weakness of her upper and lower extremities, involving wrists, ankles, fingers, and toes. Symptoms began acutely while waiting outdoors in direct sunlight. She reported a rapid heart rate, shortness of breath with minimal exertion, diaphoresis, anxiety, numbness, and transient loss of mobility in her right leg. She denied fever, headache, musculoskeletal pain, carbohydrate binge, diarrhea, vomiting, laxative use, tobacco, recreational drugs, or chemical exposures. Her past medical history was significant only for Hashimoto's hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She described two similar, self-limited episodes in the past (one 7 months earlier affecting only her arms and one 2 weeks earlier lasting 2 hours and resolving with rest and hydration).

On examination, she was alert and oriented. Vital signs showed a heart rate of 150/min, blood pressur

title: Cytokine release syndrome treated with tocilizumab following ipilimumab-nivolumab combination therapy in advanced renal cell carcinoma
case_summary: A 50-year-old man with metastatic renal cell carcinoma (clear?cell histology with sarcomatoid features, pT1bN0M1) presented initially with left neck and anterior chest pain. He underwent robot?assisted radical nephrectomy and palliative radiotherapy (20 Gy) to a painful sternal lesion. Baseline laboratory tests showed a CRP of 18.21 mg/dL, hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL, and corrected calcium 10.6 mg/dL. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a 64 mm left renal mass, multiple pulmonary nodules, and a lytic sternal lesion; MRI confirmed metastatic lesions in the sternum and right ilium. He was classified as poor risk and started on nivolumab (3 mg/kg) plus ipilimumab (1 mg/kg). Five days after the first dose, NSAIDs were restarted for right thigh pain. On day 10 after the second ICI dose, he developed a fever of 38.5  degreesC with marked elevations in CRP and interleukin-6. Empiric broad?spectrum antibiotics were initiated. Blood and urine cultures were negative, and chest and abdominal CT scans showe

title: Hemolytic uremic syndrome associated with Escherichia coli O157:H7 infection in older adults: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
An 86?year?old woman was transferred to the intensive care unit for evaluation of thrombotic microangiopathy and acute renal failure. One week earlier, she had acute onset abdominal pain with bright red blood per rectum and some breathing difficulty. Abdominal CT scan showed nonspecific colitis, and she was started on levofloxacin and metronidazole. Urine studies were negative for infection. She became confused with word?finding difficulty and slurred speech, without focal neurologic deficits, and was electively intubated for altered mental status and oxygen support. At the outside hospital, a diagnosis of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) with suspected thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) was made, and she was transferred for higher?level care. 

Her medical history included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and hypothyroidism. There was no known autoimmune or hematologic disorder. On examination she was intubated, with scattered purpura and ecchymoses on the skin and coarse

title: Sweet syndrome in a patient with rectal adenocarcinoma and HIV following neoadjuvant chemoradiation
case_summary:
A man in his 40s with well-controlled HIV on antiretroviral therapy presented with a 2-day history of fever, tender erythematous plaques and pustules, and joint swelling. Seven months earlier, he had been diagnosed with Stage IIIA rectal adenocarcinoma and had completed the last of eight cycles of neoadjuvant capecitabine, oxaliplatin, and radiotherapy 9 days before admission; he had never received granulocyte-colony stimulating factor.

On examination, he had edematous pink-to-red nodules with pustules symmetrically on the scalp, posterior neck, shoulders, and back. There was erythema, swelling, and decreased range of motion of the right wrist, ankle, and knee. Initial concern was for septic arthritis: blood cultures were obtained (later negative), and he was started on intravenous vancomycin. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 135.2 mg/dL (normal <3.1) and a hemoglobin level of 10.9 g/dL (normal 13.3-17.7). Rheumatoid factor, anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide ant

title: Cocaine Use and Splenic Rupture: A Rare Yet Serious Association
case_summary:
A 58?year?old man with a history of polysubstance abuse and HIV infection on antiretroviral therapy presented with a 2?day history of shortness of breath and cough. He also described a vague pain in the left chest and upper abdomen aggravated by deep inspiration. Although he initially denied recent drug use, he later admitted to smoking crack cocaine 2 days earlier.

On examination, his temperature was 37.8 degreesC, heart rate 115/min, respiratory rate 22/min, and blood pressure 115/66 mm Hg. Chest auscultation revealed equal breath sounds. Abdominal examination showed vague tenderness in the epigastrium and left upper quadrant without guarding or rigidity.

Laboratory studies revealed a leukocyte count of 16,200/mm? with 88% neutrophils, hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, creatinine 4.1 mg/dL, lactate 2.2 mmol/L, and creatine kinase 41,000 U/L. Urine toxicology was positive for cocaine metabolites. Chest radiography showed bilateral basal opacities suspicious for early consolidation. He was diagn

title: Diagnosis of acute Q fever using targeted next?generation sequencing: A case report
case_summary: A 26-year-old man presented with severe headache, persistent high fever, chills, and nausea beginning 3 days after a hiking trip to Baiguzha Mountain. On day -3, he developed sudden headache, fever to 39.5?C, chills, sweating, and nausea. A self-performed CRP was 13.7 mg/L. He self-treated with loxoprofen sodium, moxifloxacin, and oseltamivir without relief. On day 0, he continued to have fevers up to 40.4?C and intractable headache. Initial laboratory tests on day 2 post-admission showed mildly elevated AST and direct and indirect bilirubin levels, hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and elevated lactate. High-resolution chest CT and viral tests for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 were negative. Despite intravenous fluids and electrolyte correction, fevers fluctuated between 38.6?C and 40.4?C, headache persisted, body weight decreased by 3 kg, and liver enzymes rose further. On examination, he had conjunctival edema. He reported sleeping under an air conditioner before symptom onset and noted two sma

title: Rare case of pure red cell aplasia secondary to smoldering multiple myeloma successfully treated with daratumumab - case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 30-year-old woman with no prior hematologic, autoimmune, or endocrine history presented with several weeks of exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and near-syncope due to severe anemia, requiring ~15 RBC transfusions. She took no medications, had no recent viral illness, and had no family history of blood disorders. Examination revealed conjunctival pallor without hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy.

Initial laboratory studies showed:
- Hemoglobin 4.2 g/dL, MCV 106.8 fL  
- Reticulocyte count 0.8%, absolute retic count 19???10^9/L  
- WBC 4.03???10^9/L, platelets 213???10^9/L  
- Erythropoietin 1504 mIU/mL  
- Ferritin 324 ng/mL, CRP 6.97 mg/dL, ESR 69 mm/hr  
- Workup negative for hemolysis, autoimmune disease, nutritional deficiencies, and parvovirus B19

Peripheral smear: normocytic, normochromic RBCs without rouleaux or schistocytes; occasional plasmacytoid lymphocytes; normal platelets.  

Serum protein electrophoresis: M-spike 1.87 g/dL; immunofixation: IgG ?.  
IgG 2338 mg/L; ? fre

title: Acute Lassa Virus Encephalitis with Lassa Virus in the Cerebrospinal Fluid but Absent in the Blood: A Case Report with a Positive Outcome
case_summary:
A 38-year-old woman from Ekpoma, Edo State, Nigeria, presented with 4 days of high-grade fever, chills, and headache. She had no sore throat, vomiting, diarrhea, mucosal bleeding, neck pain, or neck stiffness. Six months earlier she had cared for her twin sister, who had a prolonged febrile illness complicated by acute sensorineural deafness. At presentation, temperature was 39.5  degreesC, blood pressure 120/80 mm Hg, pulse 96 bpm, and respiratory rate 24 /min. A blood smear showed scanty Plasmodium falciparum; serum RT-PCR for Lassa virus was negative. Blood cultures remained sterile. Physical examination was unremarkable except for two 1 ? 1.5 cm right submandibular lymph nodes. She was treated with artemisinin-based combination therapy, ceftriaxone, and metronidazole. Eight days later she was discharged at her request on oral quinine, doxycycline, and cefuroxime, after repeat malaria smear was negative but fever persisted.

Two days after discharge, she was readmitted with irration

title: A white man with Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease mimicking lymphoma, preceded by frequent episodes of tonsillitis: a case report
case_summary:
A 23-year-old white man was admitted with a 2-week history of suddenly enlarged left cervical lymph nodes unresponsive to multiple antibiotics. One week before admission, he developed fever up to 39  degreesC, fatigue, weight loss, and night sweats. His medical history was notable for over 20 episodes of bacterial tonsillitis in the past 2 years; his brother died young of a nasopharyngeal malignancy. He denied travel to endemic regions. Physical examination revealed a few separate, slightly tender left cervical lymph nodes with normal overlying skin and asymmetric left palatine tonsillar enlargement without exudate. His temperature reached 39.5  degreesC, responsive only to intramuscular metamizole; the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed leukopenia (2.2-3.3 ? 10^3/?L), anemia (hemoglobin 12 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (127 ? 10^3/?L), granulocytopenia, monocytosis, and 1% lymphoid cells on smear. Coagulation studies revealed aPTT 39.8 s and D-dimer 1.9 ?g/mL

title: "K Cramps," Recurrent Abdominal Pain in a Patient with Chronic Ketamine Use: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 31-year-old man with chronic ketamine use, major depressive disorder, and chronic back pain presented to the emergency department with two days of progressively severe back pain and chest tightness, similar to a self-limited episode eight weeks earlier. He reported intramuscular and intravenous ketamine use (1-3 g/day) for eight months after three years of daily insufflation (0.5-1 g/day). On examination he was afebrile, blood pressure 131/86 mm Hg, heart rate 77 bpm, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air; he was alert but in mild distress. There was mild tenderness of the thoracic paraspinal muscles and mild left upper quadrant abdominal tenderness without other abnormalities. 

Laboratory tests--including a complete blood count, complete metabolic panel, urinalysis, electrocardiogram, troponin, and D-dimer--were within normal limits. Chest radiographs were negative for pneumonia, pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, or subcutaneous air. A thoracic spine MRI showed no infection, fluid

title: Rhabdomyolysis as cause, consequence, or mimicker of myocardial infarction: A case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man was brought to the emergency department because of progressive fatigue, diffuse myalgia, and altered mental status. His medical history included chronic ischemic heart disease status post coronary-artery bypass grafting, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease.  

On arrival, he reported several days of worsening leg and shoulder pain with an inability to walk independently. Vital signs showed hypotension, and an electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation, a left bundle-branch block, ST-segment depression, and peaked, high T waves. Laboratory tests demonstrated a troponin T level of 532?ng/L (normal <?14?ng/L) and NT-proBNP of 5579?ng/L (normal <?125?ng/L). A temporary pacemaker was placed because of bradyarrhythmia.  

Urgent coronary angiography showed severe three-vessel atheromatous disease with an 80% stenosis in the left anterior descending artery and sub-occlusive lesions in the right corona

title: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presenting as reversible intrapulmonary arteriovenous shunts with hypoxia, fever and progressive jaundice: a case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 43-year-old man with a history of former smoking and diabetes presented with recurrent fever and progressive dyspnea of one year's duration. One year earlier, he had been evaluated for fever of unknown origin and hypoxemia (ABG on room air: pH 7.41; PaCO? 36.6 mmHg; PaO? 65.0 mmHg), with mildly elevated procalcitonin (0.26 ng/mL), C-reactive protein (22.5 mg/L), and ferritin (910 ng/mL). Comprehensive screening for infection, autoimmune disease, and malignancy was negative. Chest CT and pulmonary angiography were normal despite an elevated D-dimer (1902 ng/mL) and abnormal V/Q scan; empirical anticoagulation resolved his symptoms.

On current presentation he again had fever and dyspnea. ABG on room air showed pH 7.46; PaCO? 32.0 mmHg; PaO? 44.0 mmHg. Initial blood counts, liver enzymes, and bilirubin were normal. Two weeks of broad-spectrum antibiotics yielded no improvement. On referral, he had mild anemia (hemoglobin 112 g/L) and marked elevations of CRP (313 mg/L), ESR (111 mm/h),

title: Sepsis as the Grand Mimic of Secondary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis: Serratia marcescens Bacteremia with Concomitant Decompensated Cirrhotic Liver Disease
final_diagnosis: Sepsis with acute end organ dysfunction
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man with decompensated alcohol-related cirrhosis (MELD-Na 36), type 2 diabetes, and active intravenous drug use presented after being found unresponsive in a car. He had a history of bacteremia with Lacticaseibacillus species 3 months earlier.  
On arrival, he was in pulseless electrical activity arrest; return of spontaneous circulation was achieved after 20 minutes of CPR. He was hypothermic (29.3  degreesC), heart rate 75 beats/min, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, blood pressure 135/94 mmHg on norepinephrine infusion, and GCS 3 with endotracheal tube placement. Physical examination revealed diffuse bilateral crackles, ascites with positive fluid wave, flank dullness, and 3+ pitting edema of the lower extremities.  
Initial laboratory tests showed a metabolic acidemia with elevated lactate, marked liver function test abnormalities, thrombocytopenia, anemia, leukopenia, and coagulopathy. CT angiography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated extensive bilateral a

title: Giant Benign Struma Ovarii with High-Grade Fever, Elevated CA 125, and Hormonal Function in an Adolescent Patient
case_summary:
A 16-year-old girl presented with a one-week history of persistent low-grade fever and slight cough treated with ibuprofen. A general practitioner noted an increase in abdominal girth from a giant palpable mass and referred her urgently. She reported progressive abdominal enlargement for one year, associated with intermittent mild pain; her menstrual cycles were normal. On examination, she was afebrile; there was a giant, tense, relatively mobile abdominal mass with a regular surface, an umbilical hernia, and a brown-black lesion on the right thorax. 

Abdomino-pelvic ultrasound showed a thin-walled, multilocular cystic mass occupying nearly the entire abdomen and pelvis, with transonic fluid and multiple septa; the ovaries were not visualized, and there was no free fluid. MRI demonstrated a well-defined 115 ? 224 ? 350 mm cystic tumor originating from the right ovary, multiloculated, containing some T1-hyperintense loculi, with moderate mural and septal enhancement after gadolinium; t

title: Kawasaki's disease: An unusual presentation
case_summary:
A 4-year-old girl was brought to the hospital with intermittent fever for 4 days, generalized edema and oliguria for 2 days, and an erythematous macular rash on her trunk and limbs for 1 day. She also had two episodes of vomiting. There was no history of jaundice or previous similar illness.

On examination, she was febrile, tachycardic (heart rate 134/min), and hypotensive (blood pressure 88/50 mm Hg) with poor peripheral perfusion. She had anasarca, a blanching macular erythematous rash over her trunk, hands, and legs, and multiple nontender, nonmatted cervical lymph nodes (largest 1.5 ? 1.5 cm). Her lips were erythematous and cheilotic, but she had no strawberry tongue or conjunctival injection. Abdominal examination revealed tender hepatomegaly and splenomegaly.

Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, white cells 10,000/?L (72% neutrophils), platelets 430,000/?L, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 4 mm in the first hour. Blood cultures were negative and periph

title: Cocaine nephropathy: A rare cause of abnormal nephrograms
case_summary: A 30-year-old otherwise healthy man presented to the emergency department with bilateral flank pain, nausea, and vomiting after regular cocaine use over the preceding 2 to 3 weeks. He took no medications and had no significant past medical history. On examination, his blood pressure was 153/95 mmHg and other vital signs were normal; he had bilateral costovertebral angle tenderness, more pronounced on the right. Laboratory data revealed a leukocyte count of 12.9 K/uL and a serum creatinine of 1.51 mg/dL; hemoglobin, platelets, electrolytes, liver enzymes, and lactate were within normal limits. Intravenous fluids were administered for acute kidney injury, and contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis was performed 65 seconds after injection of 125 mL of iopamidol. Images demonstrated bilateral heterogeneous, mottled, and striated nephrograms with mild symmetric stranding of the perinephric fat; there were no urinary calculi, no collecting-system dilatation, and the

title: Asymptomatic multinodular splenoma (splenic hamartoma) in a child with sickle cell anemia
case_summary:
An 8-year-old boy with homozygous sickle cell anemia was evaluated during a routine ultrasonographic check. He had a history of multiple vaso-occlusive crises, including acute splenic sequestration, parvovirus B19 infection, and pulmonary toxoplasmosis. Despite a baseline hemoglobin level of 7-8 g/dL, his growth and psychomotor development were normal.

Physical examination was unremarkable, with no splenomegaly or hepatomegaly. Laboratory studies showed a red blood cell count of 2.03 ? 10^12/L, a white blood cell count of 17.70 ? 10^9/L, and a platelet count of 294 ? 10^9/L. Hemostasis tests, liver enzymes, total bilirubin, serum creatinine, and carcinoembryonic antigen levels were all within normal limits.

Abdominal ultrasonography followed by contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed splenomegaly with three hyperechoic splenic masses measuring 3.0 cm, 2.5 cm, and 2.0 cm. The masses had irregular shapes, poorly defined borders, and a rough capsule. Possible differential diagnoses included

title: An atypical presentation of multidermatomal herpes zoster: a case report
case_summary: A 30-year-old man presented with a 3-day history of low-grade fever, sore throat, and generalized malaise. His primary care physician diagnosed a viral upper respiratory tract infection and advised ibuprofen and paracetamol. The next day he noted an erythematous, pruritic, warm, raised rash on his left anterolateral chest extending to the medial axilla, with a similar lesion on his left mid-back. Twelve hours later, at an emergency department visit, he was thought to have an allergic reaction to ibuprofen; he received intravenous antihistamine and hydrocortisone, was advised to stop ibuprofen, and was discharged with topical steroid cream and oral antihistamine. Twelve hours after that, he presented again with worsening itchiness and heat from the rash, severe enough to disrupt sleep. His medical history was notable only for gout on allopurinol; he denied other medications, allergies, travel, or sick contacts. He was febrile (38.2? degreesC) and tachycardic (108?bpm). Examination revea

title: Infective endocarditis causing acute aortic occlusion in a patient with systemic lupus erythematosus: A rare case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A female Aboriginal Australian in her 30s was brought to the emergency department after acute-onset confusion and bilateral lower-limb paralysis noted 1 hour earlier. Her history was notable for systemic lupus erythematosus with class IV lupus nephritis and previous necrotising fasciitis requiring free-flap reconstruction complicated by a postoperative deep-vein thrombosis. Medications included mycophenolate 1 g twice daily and prednisolone 7.5 mg daily. She denied tobacco, alcohol, or drug use.

On examination, respiratory rate was 24 breaths per minute, heart rate 165 beats/min (sinus tachycardia), temperature 39.0  degreesC, and Glasgow Coma Scale score 14 (E4 V6 M4). Both lower limbs were cold, pulseless, insensate, and flaccid.

Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 112 g/L, white blood cell count 7.61 ? 10^9/L, platelet count 134 ? 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 8.2 mg/L, INR 1.2, aPTT 49.3 s, fibrinogen 1.7 g/L, and lactate 4.8 mmol/L. Four hours later, platelet count fell to

title: Felty syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old African American woman with a 3-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presented with fever (maximum, 101.5  degreesF), chills, and right ear pain. She had multiple recent admissions for fever, cough, and neutropenia, including sepsis from bilateral pneumonia treated with doxycycline and cefuroxime. One week before this admission she was diagnosed with otitis media (treated with amoxicillin) and noted to have multiple lip ulcers. Her mother (health proxy) reported no travel or sick contacts. 

On examination the patient was febrile (101.5  degreesF) and tachycardic (117 beats/min) with normal breath sounds, bilateral tympanic-membrane injection, pharyngeal erythema, lower-lip ulcers, abdominal tenderness, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and decreased range of motion of the right wrist without active synovitis. 

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 3.5 ? 10^3/?L with an absolute neutrophil count of 25 cells/mm^3, hemoglobin 7.6 g/dL (MCV, 71 fL), platele

title: Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis responding to withdrawal of gluten: a case report
case_summary:
A previously healthy 7-year-old white girl was evaluated for 2 weeks of persistent fevers, tonsillitis unresponsive to antibiotics, migratory arthralgias, weight loss, and a widespread maculopapular rash. There was no hepatosplenomegaly. Initial laboratory studies showed:
? Thrombocytopenia (64 ? 10^9/L)  
? Anemia (hemoglobin 80 g/L)  
? Elevated alanine aminotransferase (1038 IU/L)  
? Hypofibrinogenemia (1 g/L)  
? Markedly raised ferritin (71 378 ?g/L)  

Bone marrow biopsy demonstrated occasional hemophagocytosis. Immunologic studies revealed normal perforin expression by flow cytometry, normal cytotoxic T-lymphocyte and natural killer cell granule release on stimulation, and wild-type PRF gene sequencing. Autoimmune tests (rheumatoid factor, ANCA, anti-double-stranded DNA, anti-citrullinated protein antibody) and a viral panel (EBV, parvovirus, HHV-6, enterovirus, adenovirus, CMV, HIV, hepatitis) were negative.

She was treated per the HLH-2004 protocol with etoposide, dexamethas

title: A Rare Case of Primary Bilateral Adrenal Lymphoma
case_summary: A 52-year-old Malay woman presented with a 3-week history of intermittent fever and an unintentional 10-kg weight loss over 3 months. Her medical history included asthma, hypertension, and hyperlipidaemia. She denied cough, sputum, haemoptysis, dysuria, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, or recent travel. On examination, her temperature was 40  degreesC, heart rate 113 beats/min, and blood pressure was normal. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. The abdomen was soft and non-tender, with no masses. There was no lymphadenopathy or stigmata of chronic liver disease. Initial laboratory tests showed microcytic hypochromic anaemia (haemoglobin 9.8 g/dL; MCV 75.2 fL) and lactate dehydrogenase 2140 U/L; renal function was normal. Contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis revealed bilateral homogeneous adrenal masses (left, 3.7 ? 5.4 ? 6.4 cm; right, 5.5 ? 2.7 ? 5.9 cm), a 0.8-cm left supraclavicular lymph node, and subcentimetre mediastinal nodes. Early-morning cortisol

title: Occult child abuse presenting as pneumatosis intestinalis and portomesenteric venous gas - a case report
case_summary: A 1-year-old boy was brought to the emergency department for altered consciousness. He had no history of fever, upper respiratory symptoms, feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, or known trauma. His past history included prematurity at 29 + 3 weeks' gestation and stage IA necrotizing enterocolitis. The patient's parents were young with a history of drug abuse. On arrival his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 3 (E1V1M1). Vital signs: temperature 33.5  degreesC, heart rate 124 beats/min, respiratory rate 18/min, blood pressure 58/47 mmHg. Examination revealed a distended, guarded abdomen with absent bowel sounds; there were no skin bruises, and retinal examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 13 g/dL, white blood cell count 30 240/uL (13% neutrophils), pH 6.76, pCO2 22.5 mmHg, HCO3 3.2 mmol/L, base excess -31.6, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 1.07 mg/dL, AST 485 U/L, ALT 366 U/L, Na 133 mmol/L, K 6.1 mmol/L, Cl 100 mmol/L. Chest radiography demonstrated diffu

title: Acute Liver Injury as a Manifestation of Secondary Syphilis
case_summary: A 49-year-old Hispanic man with essential hypertension and a remote history of treated syphilis presented with 1 week of severe, dull right upper quadrant abdominal pain rated 6-7/10, persistent and nonradiating. He reported progressive jaundice, dark urine, fatigue, nausea, and anorexia. He denied fevers, rashes, other medications, alcohol use, illicit drugs, tattoos, transfusions, or toxin exposures. On examination, he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. He had icteric sclerae and generalized jaundice without rash and was alert and oriented. Laboratory tests showed AST 1280 U/L, ALT 1652 U/L, ALP 147 U/L, total bilirubin 19.9 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 15.5 mg/dL, and INR 1.6. Platelets were 100 ? 10^9/L, with otherwise normal blood count and metabolic panel. Abdominal ultrasound and MRCP demonstrated normal liver size, no steatosis, no bile duct dilatation, mild splenomegaly, and no masses. Viral hepatitis panel showed immunity to hepatitis B with undetectable HBV DNA; HSV1/2 IgM,

title: Superwarfarin Exposure: An Important Uncommon Cause of Painless Bleeding
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man presented with 5 days of painless bleeding, initially from the right nostril, followed by black stools and cola-colored urine. His history included hypertension and type II diabetes; medications were daily aspirin, metformin, and a recent short course of azithromycin. He denied bleeding disorders, anticoagulant use, smoking, alcohol, or illicit drugs. On examination, he was hemodynamically stable but had active right-sided epistaxis; lungs were clear and the abdomen was soft without bruising.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, hematocrit 32.4%, lactate dehydrogenase 662 IU/L, prothrombin time > 120 s, activated partial thromboplastin time > 240 s, and INR > 10. Urinalysis revealed 4+ blood and > 50 RBCs per high-power field. CT of the head and sinuses demonstrated mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed bilateral multilobar airspace infiltrates (most prominent in the left lower lobe) and a 2-cm right

title: Failing in reverse: a case report of reverse Takotsubo syndrome complicating peripartum
case_summary:
A 31?year?old woman in labor at 36.2 weeks' gestation (gravida 4, para 3) was admitted for delivery. Her obstetric history was notable for obstetric hemorrhage during her second delivery and an anaphylactic reaction to ketorolac after her third cesarean section. She had allergies to acetaminophen, ketorolac, and penicillin. 

An epidural anesthetic with lidocaine and adrenaline was placed uneventfully, but 5 minutes later she developed hypotension (blood pressure 60/40 mmHg), dyspnea, and desaturation (SpO? 84% on 7 L/min O?). She was intubated and treated with intramuscular adrenaline 1 mg for presumed anaphylactic shock, and underwent emergency cesarean section with minimal bleeding. Persistent hypotension was managed with two 500 mL normal?saline boluses and a noradrenaline infusion (5 ?g/min). 

On arrival to the intensive care unit she was sedated on mechanical ventilation (FiO? 35%, SpO? 99%), with mean arterial pressure 70 mmHg on noradrenaline. Examination revealed normal heart

title: Angiosarcoma associated with Kasabach-Merritt syndrome presenting with typical signs and symptoms of pulmonary tuberculosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 44-year-old man from Afghanistan presented with a 6-week history of initially dry cough that became productive, followed after 2 months by daily hemoptysis of approximately two tablespoons. He also reported low-grade fevers with sweating (no rigors), an 8% weight loss over 2 months, moderately severe epigastric pain radiating to the right upper quadrant for 1.5 months, and vague mild lower back pain for 1 month. His past medical history was notable only for enteric fever 15 years earlier.  

Initial investigations in Afghanistan showed a leukocyte count of 12,000/?L with 20% eosinophils, hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, and platelets 210,000/?L. High-resolution CT of the chest revealed bilateral nodular infiltrates and mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy. He received steroids for presumed eosinophilic pulmonary disease without response. Tuberculosis tests, including GeneXpert PCR and interferon-? release assay, were negative, but he was treated empirically with anti-tuberculous therapy for 1

title: Severe SARS?CoV?2 infection in a 32?week pregnant woman treated with Remdesivir?Dexamethasone combination therapy: A case report
case_summary:
A 37-year-old Iranian woman, gravida 1 at 32 weeks and 6 days of a singleton pregnancy, presented with a 2-day history of fever and dyspnea. Her past medical history was notable only for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine 50 ug daily. Her body mass index was 30 kg/m^2. She reported no known exposure to persons with SARS-CoV-2 infection. 

On July 13, 2021, SARS-CoV-2 was confirmed by real-time RT-PCR, and she was admitted to the tertiary hospital. Vital signs on admission included blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, pulse 83 beats/min, respiratory rate 23 breaths/min, temperature 38.0  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air (98% with an oxygen mask). Low-dose high-resolution chest CT showed multifocal ground-glass opacities suggesting extensive lung involvement. She was admitted to the ICU and started on intravenous remdesivir (200 mg loading dose, then 100 mg daily for 5 days), dexamethasone (6 mg every 12 h for four doses, then 6 mg daily), and subcutaneous enoxaparin 40 mg d

title: Toxic cutaneous responses from inhalant abuse
case_summary:
A 24-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a one-week history of a progressive, tender, blistering rash on the left upper chest, shoulder, arm, and hand. He also had erosions and pain of his lips, oral mucosa, and tongue with hemorrhagic crusting at the oral commissures and a crusted plaque on the nasal tip. He denied medication use, topical exposures, travel history, insect bites, new sexual contacts, or similar eruptions in close contacts. He had a prior, self-resolving rash on the right leg and groin one month earlier. He denied alcohol and illicit drug use and reported no chemical exposures at work.

On examination, there were blanching erythematous to violaceous patches and plaques with sharply demarcated borders on the left chest and arm, tender tense bullae on the left shoulder and arm, and yellow-brown crusted plaques on the right thumb. Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation was present on the proximal right leg and inguinal fold.

In the emergency depart

title: Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 Alpha variant (B.1.1.7), original wild-type severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, and cytomegalovirus co-infection in a young adult with acute lymphoblastic leukemia, case report, and review of the possible cytomegalovirus reactivation mechanisms
case_summary: An 18-year-old man with acute lymphoblastic leukemia in remission on maintenance chemotherapy (vincristine, prednisolone, methotrexate, 6-mercaptopurine) presented in late December 2020 with 1 week of abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, oral aphthous ulcers, skin rash, excessive drooling, anorexia, weight loss, and decreased urine output. He had a history of SARS-CoV-2 infection 1 month earlier without respiratory symptoms, and a prior history of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis and pan-sinusitis. On admission, he was febrile (38.8  degreesC); other vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory studies showed WBC 15,370/mm3, hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, platelets 80,000/mm3, ESR 27 mm/h, CRP 19 mg/L, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.75 mg/dL, sodium 122 mmol/L, potassium 2.8 mmol/L, AST 39 U/L, ALT 51 U/L, total bilirubin 2.9 mg/dL, albumin 3.5 g/dL, PT 66 s, PTT 44.5 s, INR 5.14. Abdominal ultrasound showed a normal-sized liver with diffusely increased echogenicity (grade 2 fatty chang

title: Acute Epstein - Barr virus hepatitis without mononucleosis syndrome: a case report
journal: Gastroenterology and Hepatology From Bed to Bench
case_summary:
A 23-year-old woman presented with a 3-day history of fever, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, mild right upper-quadrant abdominal pain, and jaundice. One week earlier, she had experienced coryza and malaise lasting 4 days that resolved without treatment. She denied drug or alcohol use and had no recent travel. 

On examination, her temperature was 38.5  degreesC and heart rate was 110 beats/min. She had scleral and cutaneous icterus and mild right upper-quadrant tenderness without Murphy's sign. Liver span on the midclavicular line was 110 mm, and the spleen measured 170 mm. There was no cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudate, mucosal rash, or skin rash.

Abdominal ultrasonography showed a liver of normal size with increased echogenicity (grade 2 fatty change), splenomegaly (175 mm), normal portal-vein diameter and flow, a normal suprahepatic inferior vena cava, a normal gallbladder and biliary tree without evidence of cholestasis, and mild ascites.

Laboratory results were notable for

title: Glycogen hepatopathy in type-1 diabetes mellitus: A case report
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman with a 30-year history of poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus presented with one day of fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. She described cramping left-sided pain (6/10) relieved by lying down. She denied pruritus, jaundice, fever, or bleeding. Six months earlier, she was admitted with similar symptoms and underwent an appendectomy; at that time, she had elevated liver enzymes. Her medications included insulin glargine and lispro (which she had omitted for 2 days), amlodipine, metoprolol, aspirin, gabapentin, oxycodone, and low-dose pravastatin. She denied alcohol, tobacco, and recreational drugs. Family history was notable for type 1 diabetes in an aunt and fatty liver disease in both parents and her aunt.

On examination, she was obese and afebrile (BP 147/94 mmHg; pulse 80/min; BMI 31.4 kg/m2). The abdomen was soft with mild left-lower-quadrant tenderness; the liver was palpable 4 cm below the right costal margin (span 13 cm) with a smooth edge;

title: Incidental Eosinophilic Chromophobe Renal Cell Carcinoma in Renal Allograft
case_summary: A 31-year-old man with end-stage renal disease secondary to membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis underwent a living nonrelated kidney transplant at age 16. He received cyclosporine A, prednisone, and azathioprine but was poorly compliant, suffered multiple acute rejection episodes, and developed severe chronic allograft damage. Eleven years posttransplant, he resumed hemodialysis; four weeks later he presented with a 3-day history of watery diarrhea, vomiting, fever with rigor, mild diffuse abdominal pain, and one episode of gross hematuria. His temperature was 37.9 degreesC, pulse 101/min, respiratory rate 20/min, blood pressure 138/93?mmHg, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Examination revealed mild generalized abdominal and graft tenderness. Urinalysis showed WBCs 7/HPF, RBCs 1/HPF, and protein 600?mg/dL. Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 143?g/L, WBC count 3.9?10^9/L, platelets 150?10^9/L, normal electrolytes, BUN 6.2?mmol/L, and creatinine 801?umol/L. Abdominal ultras

title:
Renal Vein Thrombosis on Point-of-care Ultrasound in the Emergency
Department: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 16-year-old female with well-controlled HIV infection, idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, menorrhagia, anemia, thyroid disease, and a complicated right tympanomastoidectomy two days earlier presented with 8 hours of severe right-sided abdominal pain and non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. She endorsed constipation but was passing flatus and denied fever, chills, cough, dyspnea, diarrhea, bleeding, urinary symptoms, or headaches. Medications included eltrombopag, abacavir-lamivudine, dolutegravir, levothyroxine, cephalexin, and ferrous sulfate. She denied sexual activity and substance use.  
Vital signs: T 97.8  degreesF; HR 87 bpm; RR 18 bpm; BP 112/78 mm Hg; SpO? 100% on room air. She was in moderate distress, with right upper and lower quadrant tenderness and guarding but no rebound, masses, or costovertebral angle tenderness. The remainder of her cardiopulmonary, neurologic, and skin exams was unremarkable. While in the ED, she had two episodes of gross hematuria.  
Laboratory stu

title: Sequential Use of Aminophylline and Theophylline for Temporizing Neck Cancer-Induced Carotid Sinus Syncope
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man presented following a 2-week history of frequent syncopal events. The episodes occurred at night when lying in bed and were provoked by rightward neck rotation. After he was found unresponsive on the bathroom floor without seizure or incontinence, he presented to our hospital. He also noted a gradually enlarging right neck mass in addition to new taste changes and right lower facial numbness. He denied orthostatic, exercise-induced, or daytime syncope and was not taking any medications. During this presentation, he had unintentional weight loss but denied fever, night sweats, exertional chest pain, or shortness of breath. His smoking history was significant for 3 packs per day for the last 40 years. On physical examination, there was a right suprahyoid, firm neck mass from the level of the right earlobe to the middle of the neck without overlying skin changes or lymphadenopathy. Heart and lung sounds were normal.

His 12-lead electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhyt

title: Drug-induced hypersensitivity syndrome induced by propylthiouracil: case report and literature review
journal: Allergy, Asthma, and Clinical Immunology : Official Journal of the Canadian Society of Allergy and Clinical Immunology
case_summary: A 24-year-old woman was treated with propylthiouracil (100 mg three times daily) for hyperthyroidism. Three weeks after starting therapy, she developed fever (39-40  degreesC) and a diffuse, pruritic maculopapular rash that resolved after drug discontinuation and antihistamines. One month later, she presented with recurrent fever (38.8  degreesC), nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and generalized edema. Laboratory evaluation revealed eosinophilia (6.4%, normal 0.2-5.0%), monocytosis (15.6%, normal 3.0-12.0%), atypical lymphocytes (13%), anemia (hemoglobin 80 g/L), thrombocytopenia (53?10^9/L), elevated alanine aminotransferase (199 U/L), and elevated creatinine (227 umol/L). Thyroid function tests showed low free triiodothyronine, normal free thyroxine, and suppressed thyroid-stimulating hormone. On transfer, immunoglobulin G was elevated (2890 mg/dL); IgM serologies were negative for coxsackievirus, Epstein-Barr virus, and cytomegalovirus, and human herpesvirus-6 DNA was undetectable by pol

title: Light chain Amyloidosis (AL) associated with multiple myeloma revealed by peripheral bilateral polyarthritis: a case report
case_summary: A 50-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain involving the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. The pain sometimes awoke him at night, was not relieved by rest, and had gradually increased in intensity. He also reports asthenia, anorexia, and weight loss. His past medical history is notable for repeated pulmonary infections. On examination, there is reducible deformity of both hands with ulnar deviation of the fingers and flexion at the MCP and PIP joints, without joint swelling or skin nodules. A painless macroglossia with lateral indentations is noted, causing moderate dysphagia. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 66 mm/h, C-reactive protein of 59 mg/L, normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 9 g/dL), and corrected serum calcium of 3.1 mmol/L. Total protein is 69 g/L and serum creatinine is 82 ?mol/L. Serum protein electrophoresis reveals hypogammaglobulinemia (6.35 g/L) without a monoclonal peak, and immunofixa

title: Diffuse leukoencephalopathy in a 29-year-old male with hypertensive emergency
case_summary:
A 29-year-old man was referred to the emergency department by his ophthalmologist after a blood pressure measurement of 250/150 mmHg and findings of retinal hemorrhages. He reported a 2-week history of blurred vision, a 2-month history of headache, and several days of unsteady gait. Family members noted recent confusion. He denied chest pain, dyspnea, arthralgias, urinary symptoms, smoking, illicit drug use, and over-the-counter intake except occasional ibuprofen. He had previously consumed alcohol heavily but had reduced intake 2 weeks earlier. He had no significant past medical history and no features of Cushing syndrome. On examination, his blood pressure was 250/150 mmHg, and an S4 gallop was auscultated. Neurologic exam was notable only for visual disturbance.

Laboratory studies showed creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L, bicarbonate 21 mEq/L, chloride 109 mEq/L, hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL dropping to 10.5 g/dL the next day, indirec

title: Diffuse Bone Marrow Metastasis as the Initial Presentation of an Occult Breast Cancer
case_summary:
A 64?year?old woman presented with headache and fever for 1 week. She described diffuse pain over her calvarium without focal neurologic deficits. Body temperature was 38.7  degreesC. Examination revealed no lymphadenopathy, no palpable breast masses, and no abdominal masses. Laboratory studies showed:
? Hemoglobin 7.0 g/dL (normocytic anemia), MCV 89.3 fL  
? White?cell count 12 000/mm3 (neutrophils 61.7%, lymphocytes 26.7%, monocytes 10.8%)  
? Platelet count 325 000/mm3  
? Alkaline phosphatase 158 IU/L (normal 32-91)  
? Lactate dehydrogenase 292 IU/L (normal 98-192)  
? D-dimer 6570 ng/mL (normal 0-500)  
Coagulation times were normal. Chest radiograph showed no pulmonary lesions. A noncontrast CT scan of the head showed multiple osteolytic lesions in the skull compared to films 6 years earlier. A CT scan of the chest and abdomen demonstrated mixed osteolytic and osteosclerotic lesions throughout the skeleton but no clear primary tumor. Serum immunofixation electrophoresis showed

title: Human Borrelia miyamotoi infection: A cause of persistent fever and severe hyperthermia in New England
case_summary:
A 75-year-old man presented with a 3-day history of progressive generalized weakness and 1 day of altered mental status. Collateral history indicated that he was last seen well the previous evening but was found unresponsive at home the next day. His medical history included coronary artery disease, hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, benign prostatic hypertrophy, and gastroesophageal reflux disease. He was independent in activities of daily living, remained active outdoors (primarily boating), and had no known tick bites. He took no psychiatric medications, drank little alcohol, and had ceased tobacco use decades earlier.

On arrival in the emergency department, his temperature was 103.0  degreesF, heart rate 81 beats/min, blood pressure 143/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 94 % on room air. Laboratory studies showed a lactate of 3.2 mmol/L, C-reactive protein 10.3 mg/dL, procalcitonin 3.72 ng/mL, D-dimer 4.95 mg/L FEU, creatinine 1.72 mg/dL (baseline norma

title: Serum sickness-like reaction following an administration of the first dose of inactivated COVID19 vaccine
case_summary:
A 43-year-old previously healthy woman presented with a 9-day history of a pruritic skin eruption, fever, myalgia, arthralgia, and malaise. Four days before onset, she received her first dose of an inactivated COVID-19 vaccine (CoronaVac). The eruption began as a single pruritic patch on the chest and then became generalized over 9 days, with randomly distributed blanchable erythematous macules, patches, and excoriated papules and plaques on the trunk and extremities, some with reticulate erythema. She also reported fever (38.1  degreesC), severe myalgia and arthralgia, and tender cervical lymphadenopathy. She denied any recent infections, new medications, other vaccinations, or blood transfusions.

Laboratory data revealed leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance and marked elevations of inflammatory markers: ESR 42 mm/hr (normal 4-20), CRP 165.7 mg/L (0-5), ferritin 3210 ng/mL (15-150), and LDH 336 U/L (125-220). Chest radiograph, urinalysis, and serum creatinine were normal. Serolo

title: Clinical misdiagnosis of influenza infection with a confusing clinical course: A case report
case_summary: A 32?year?old woman with a history of hypothyroidism and major depressive disorder presented with a 3?day history of fever, chills, anorexia, myalgia, and a dry cough that later became productive. She then developed vomiting and watery secretory diarrhea, and her family reported one episode of bloody diarrhea followed by hypotension and tachycardia. On admission, she was somnolent; temperature 37 degreesC, blood pressure 77/42?mmHg, heart rate 136?beats/min, respiratory rate 20?breaths/min. Physical examination revealed severe dehydration. Initial laboratory values included a creatinine of 0.8?mg/dL, arterial pH 7.16, WBC 3300/uL, platelet count 79 000/uL, hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, CRP 152 mg/L, ESR 83 mm/h, and normal BUN. She received aggressive fluid resuscitation without hemodynamic improvement and was started on vasopressors. Echocardiography was normal. She was transferred to the ICU for refractory shock and treated with broad?spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin, meropenem) and corticos

title: A rare case of neuroleptic malignant syndrome presenting with serious hyperthermia treated with a non-invasive cooling device: a case report
case_summary: A 22-year-old man was brought to the ICU after being found on the street with fluctuating consciousness--initially agitated, then somnolent. On arrival he was hypotensive, tachycardic, and breathing labored; his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 6, necessitating endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation under sedation with fentanyl, midazolam, and etomidate. He was hemodynamically unstable (shock index, 2), had mild muscle rigidity, and a core temperature of 42.0 degreesC. No medical history was available. Antipyretic drugs had no effect. Laboratory studies showed marked rhabdomyolysis (creatine kinase elevated), lactate dehydrogenase 2020 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 785 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 157 U/L, pH 7.27, lactate 5.8 mmol/L, hemoglobin 18.7 g/dL, glucose 6.9 mg/dL, sodium 148 mmol/L, potassium 4.5 mmol/L, and central venous oxygen saturation 59%. Toxicology screening detected only a cannabis metabolite and the benzodiazepine used for sedation. A noncontrast head CT

title: Sepsis after non?perforated acute appendicitis
case_summary:
A 1.5-year-old boy was admitted with a 4-day history of fever, diarrhea, and vomiting. On the first hospital day he had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. On hospital day 4 his level of consciousness declined and he was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit. He is the first child of a healthy family and resides in a village. His past history was notable only for pneumonia at 6 months of age; there was a family history of febrile seizures. He was initially treated with ceftriaxone, vancomycin, phenytoin, and acyclovir. 

On examination he appeared toxic. Temperature was 39.0 degreesC, heart rate 160 beats/min, respiratory rate 45 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Glasgow Coma Scale was 8. The abdomen was soft but there was mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant; rectal examination was unremarkable. 

Initial laboratory studies showed normal electrolytes, blood gases, coagulation profile, and liver and kidney function. Peripheral white-cell count was 11

title: Pulmonary Malakoplakia by Rhodococcus equi in an HIV-Infected Patient in Mexico: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man from southern Mexico presented with a 1-year history of persistent nonproductive cough and more than 10% unintentional weight loss. He denied fever or dyspnea initially. Over time he developed whitish sputum, mild hemoptysis, fevers to >102.2 degreesF, and resting dyspnea, prompting transfer to a referral center. He reported MSM exposures, no animal contacts, and no tobacco, alcohol, or illicit-drug use. He was unvaccinated as an adult.

On examination, temperature was 102.7 degreesF, heart rate 120/min, and respiratory rate 30/min. Findings included oral candidiasis; multiple purple skin and palatal lesions with elevated borders; bilateral, nontender, indurated cervical lymphadenopathy; a hyperdynamic precordium; asymmetric chest expansion; diminished breath sounds and fremitus; and a flat percussion note over the left hemithorax.

Laboratory studies showed normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, MCV 80.9 fL, RDW 17.3%), leukocytes 6500/uL with lymphocy

title: Eculizumab as a bridge to immunosuppressive therapy in severe cold agglutinin disease of anti-Pr specificity
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man with no known chronic illness was brought to the emergency department with one week of fatigue and progressive dyspnea. On arrival, he was tachypneic and hypotensive, and preliminary laboratories revealed: hemoglobin 21 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 154 fL, indeterminate platelet count, creatinine 219 ?mol/L, and venous blood gas pH 7.19. He was intubated for respiratory distress and started on renal replacement therapy for acute kidney injury.  

His only medications were amlodipine and ezetimibe. He denied fevers, diarrheal illness, melena, or sick contacts. Physical examination showed jaundice but no petechiae, and a digital rectal examination was negative for blood.  

Initial investigations revealed:  
? A significant degree of red blood cell agglutination on peripheral blood film  
? A positive direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test for C3  
? Haptoglobin < 0.07 g/L  
? Indirect bilirubin 83.1 ?mol/L  
? Lactate dehydrogenase 1776 U/L (normal < 225 U/L)  
? A cold agg

title: A Case of Previously Undiagnosed Systemic Lupus Erythematosus and Mycobacterium tuberculosis Infection Presenting as Diffuse Alveolar Hemorrhage
case_summary:
A 19-year-old woman presented with two weeks of progressive dyspnea on exertion and cough productive of blood-tinged sputum. She reported a 30-lb weight loss, drenching night sweats, and a history of menorrhagia. Six months prior, she had been incarcerated for one year. She denied smoking, multiple sexual partners, or drug use.

On transfer to our hospital, her temperature was 36.5  degreesC, blood pressure 126/84 mm Hg, pulse 115 beats/min, respiratory rate 15 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 90% on room air (improving to 96% on 2 L nasal cannula). Examination revealed an anxious young woman with coarse crackles bilaterally, regular tachycardia, and 1+ pitting edema of the lower extremities.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL, hematocrit 28.6%, MCV 91 fL, platelets 198 ? 10^3/uL, electrolytes within normal limits, creatinine 0.66 mg/dL, BUN 5 mg/dL, PT 14.2 s, PTT 24 s, INR 1.1, and a negative respiratory viral panel. Urinalysis revealed 176 RBCs/HPF without casts, 7 WBC

title: A successful antimicrobial regime for Chromobacterium violaceum induced bacteremia
case_summary:
A 4-year-old boy in Ho Chi Minh City presented with a 3-day history of fever, fatigue, vomiting, and anorexia. Three days earlier, he had sustained a puncture wound to his right ankle after playing outdoors. He was HIV-negative and had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated for 8 months with rifampicin, 4-aminosalicylic acid, and ethambutol; he had no other underlying illnesses.

On admission, his temperature was 39.5 degreesC; pulse, 180 beats per minute; blood pressure was low; respiration rate, 57 breaths per minute. Examination revealed crackles in both lung fields, pale sclera, cervical lymphadenopathy (nodes ?2 cm), hepatomegaly without jaundice, and two small abdominal blisters. The puncture site showed mild erythema but no frank pus.

Initial laboratory studies showed a normal white blood cell count with neutrophil predominance and hemoglobin of 11 g/dL. Platelets were 154 K/?L and C-reactive protein was 312 mg/L (normal, 0-5 mg/L). Serum electrolytes were notable for sodi

title: Unusual case of Hashimoto's encephalopathy and pseudo-obstruction in a patient with undiagnosed hypothyroidism: a case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old Indian man presented with a 2-day history of altered sensorium and abdominal distension. Over the preceding 3 months he had slow mentation, inattention, word?finding difficulty, agitation, persecutory delusions, visual hallucinations, and constipation. He denied any medication use.

On examination he was drowsy with a hoarse voice, dry skin, puffy face, madarosis, cold extremities, and slowed reflexes. His abdomen was distended with absent bowel sounds. Chest and cardiovascular examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed macrocytic hypochromic anemia (hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, MCV 100 fL), leukopenia (3,900/mm3), and normal glucose, liver and kidney function, arterial blood gases, and electrolytes. Septic workup was sterile. Chest radiograph and ECG were normal. Abdominal ultrasound and plain radiographs demonstrated diffuse gaseous distension with dilated loops and air-fluid levels. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT confirmed dilated small-bowel loops; whole?body

title: Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet (HELLP) syndrome in a 26-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 26-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of cystic fibrosis (CF) and CF-related diabetes presented at 29 weeks of gestation with increasing nausea and worsening respiratory symptoms. She had mild CF lung disease (pre-pregnancy FEV1 2.62 L [87% predicted]; current FEV1 2.09 L [69% predicted]), pancreatic insufficiency, chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection, and CF-related diabetes managed with insulin (hemoglobin A1c, 6.1%). She had no microalbuminuria. Her first pregnancy, eight years earlier, was uncomplicated and resulted in a healthy child at 36 weeks. 

Her second pregnancy was unplanned; she was followed in a high-risk clinic by obstetrics, respiratory medicine, and endocrinology. At 26 weeks, she had an outpatient pulmonary exacerbation (FEV1 2.13 L [71% predicted], blood pressure 118/87 mm Hg) treated with oral ciprofloxacin. At 28 weeks, she developed worsening nausea, increased respiratory symptoms, and left upper quadrant discomfort; blood pressure was 123/90 mm Hg, an

title: Rare Complications of Acute Appendicitis: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 37-year-old man presented with 12 days of cyclical fevers and vague abdominal discomfort. He described the pain as an "L" shape extending from the right upper quadrant to both lower quadrants. His fevers recurred every 12 hours, reaching 101-103.4?F, initially responsive to acetaminophen and ibuprofen but later refractory. He also noted an initial headache, myalgias, anorexia, and profound fatigue that limited his work as a personal trainer.

Three days into his illness, his primary care evaluation revealed no leukocytosis, no abdominal tenderness or guarding, and normal vital signs; testing for Lyme disease, anaplasma, babesia, ehrlichiosis, and influenza was negative. After six days of fevers, an urgent?care visit again showed no leukocytosis, diffuse arthralgias, negative blood smear for parasites, and persistent elevations in alanine aminotransferase and alkaline phosphatase. 

On arrival in the emergency department, his temperature was 99.2?F, blood pressure 149/80 mmHg, heart r

title: Relapsing Hypoglycemia Associated with Hypocarnitinemia Following Treatment with Cefcapene Pivoxil in an Elderly Man
case_summary:
An 85-year-old Japanese man was brought to the emergency department with one hour of excessive diaphoresis and drowsiness. Three days earlier, he had started oral cefcapene pivoxil hydrochloride for suspected urinary tract infection. His history included subtotal gastrectomy with Billroth II reconstruction for gastric cancer four months earlier, total laryngectomy for laryngeal chondrosarcoma, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent catheterization. He had become withdrawn, ate only carbohydrates since losing his teeth, and rarely consumed meat or dairy products.

On arrival he was cachexic and drowsy but arousable with stimulation. Vital signs were stable. A finger-stick glucose measured 47 mg/dL; intravenous 50% dextrose transiently improved his symptoms. He was admitted and started on a 10% glucose infusion plus empirical piperacillin-tazobactam.

Initial laboratory studies showed: blood glucose 58 mg/dL, normal insulin (9.5 ?U/mL) with slightly e

title: Giant cellulitis-like Sweet syndrome with myofasciitis: Multimodality imaging findings and streptococcal trigger
case_summary:
A 34-year-old woman with a history of uterine leiomyoma presented with 4 days of fever, generalized myalgia, and severe right thigh pain. On arrival she was hypotensive, altered, and noted to have a neutrophilic leukocytosis, elevated creatine kinase, increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, and lactate. Contrast-enhanced MRI of the right thigh showed small-volume perifascial fluid and extensive reticular muscle edema of the vastus lateralis and intermedius centered along their fascial plane, with sparing of the rectus femoris. A small focus of nonenhancing intramuscular T2 hyperintensity with peripheral reticular enhancement suggested early myonecrosis. There was milder diffuse T2 hyperintense edema in the biceps femoris and semitendinosus and a small asymmetric knee effusion with lateral synovial hyperenhancement.

The initial differential included infectious and noninfectious myofasciitis (eg, early pyomyositis, necrotizing fasciitis, toxic shock syndrome). Bloo

title: Hypotension Associated with Advanced Hodgkin Lymphoma
case_summary:
A 61-year-old man presented with a 3-day history of right flank pain, dysuria, urinary urgency and frequency, and occasional hematuria, associated with fever, chills, and rigors. Over the preceding 3 months, he had a 40-pound weight loss, drenching night sweats, and intermittent low-grade fevers. His history was notable for nephrolithiasis treated conservatively 3 weeks earlier and multiple past episodes of renal colic treated with lithotripsy. He was a 30 pack-year smoker and drank alcohol occasionally.

On arrival, his blood pressure was 78/49 mmHg (MAP 59 mmHg), temperature 97.3 degreesF, heart rate 80 beats/min, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 98-99% on room air. Hypotension did not improve after 3 L of normal saline. He was diagnosed with urosepsis and admitted to the ICU, where he received norepinephrine and broad-spectrum antibiotics.

Examination showed mild pallor, no icterus or edema, and right costovertebral angle tenderness. Laboratory studies revealed hemogl

title: An Interesting Case of Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis in a Postpartum Female With Recent COVID-19 Vaccination
case_summary:
A 22-year-old Hispanic woman, 5 days postpartum after a term vaginal delivery complicated by possible chorioamnionitis, initially presented with 2 days of fever to 38.9  degreesC and chills. She was hemodynamically stable; blood and urine cultures were negative, and she completed 2 days of IV antibiotics.

Six days later she was readmitted with fever to 40.3  degreesC, chills, progressive fatigue, malaise, nausea, vomiting, diffuse pain, and headache. She denied abdominal or breast pain, noted minor vaginal bleeding, and reported receiving a second dose of the Pfizer COVID?19 vaccine 1 week before delivery. Her history was notable for gestational diabetes, hyperlipidemia, three prior spontaneous miscarriages, an ectopic pregnancy, and a molar pregnancy; there was no family history of autoimmune disease or malignancy.

On examination: temperature 39.0  degreesC; pulse 110 /min; blood pressure 100/67 mmHg; respirations 14 /min; O? saturation 98% on room air. She appeared diaphoretic and

title: Systemic Capillary Leak Syndrome associated with hypovolemic shock and compartment syndrome. Use of transpulmonary thermodilution technique for volume management
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Trauma, Resuscitation and Emergency Medicine
case_summary: A 41-year-old man presented with severe bilateral leg pain and a 2-week history of flu-like illness with fever up to 39  degreesC treated as an outpatient. On initial evaluation at an outside hospital, he was tachycardic (102 bpm), hypotensive (65/50 mmHg), and appeared severely dehydrated. He received 15 L of intravenous fluids on hospital day 1. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 22.3 g/dL, hematocrit 60.4%, serum total protein 2.3 g/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 37 mg/dL, white blood cell count 15,900/uL, C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/dL, and procalcitonin <0.5 ug/L. Electrolytes and liver-function tests were within normal limits. Arterial blood gas revealed pH 7.06 and bicarbonate 11.9 mmol/L. Creatine kinase was 124 U/L on day 1, rising to >7000 U/L on day 2. Chest radiography showed a small right pleural effusion; echocardiography and abdominal ultrasound were unremarkable. Lower-extremity duplex ultrasound showed no thrombosis. Compartment pressures measured 100 mm

title: Related or not? Development of spontaneous Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease in a patient with chronic, well-controlled HIV: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man with long-standing, well-controlled HIV infection, hepatitis B, hypertension, and peripheral vascular disease presented with 3 months of progressive subacute neurocognitive decline. His symptoms included conceptual apraxia, apathy, memory impairment, and gait disturbance, initially attributed to "pseudo-dementia" and polypharmacy. His symptoms worsened despite medication adjustments and culminated in admission for altered mental status and aspiration pneumonia.

On transfer, he appeared cachectic and chronically ill. Temperature was 101.2 degreesF, blood pressure 140/82 mm Hg, heart rate 90 bpm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on 2 L/min by nasal cannula. He was minimally interactive and mute but followed simple commands. Cranial nerves were intact. Motor examination showed increased tone in all extremities with atrophic bulk, bilateral upper-extremity clonus, and hyperreflexia (3+ at biceps and brachioradialis); lower-extremity reflexes wer

title: Granulomatous interstitial nephritis in a patient with SARS-CoV-2 infection
case_summary:
A 62-year-old Caucasian man was admitted with six days of cough, fever, myalgia, and chills. He had tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 three days after symptom onset. His only medical history was hyperlipidemia managed with atorvastatin; he had no known allergies and denied tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. On admission his renal function was normal. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed diffuse bilateral ground-glass pulmonary infiltrates, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, moderate pleural effusions, and normally sized kidneys with preserved perfusion.

Two days later he developed acute respiratory distress syndrome requiring intubation, prone positioning, and empiric amoxicillin-clavulanate; hydroxychloroquine was also given after exclusion of G6PD deficiency. His course was complicated by atrial fibrillation, paralytic ileus, and oliguria. By hospital day 9, he had acute kidney injury with a serum creatinine peak of 519 ?mol/L and severe metabolic acidosis, promp

title: A rare distributive shock diagnosed only by medical history and inspection
case_summary:
A 78-year-old Japanese man was brought to the emergency department for altered consciousness. Approximately 1 hour before arrival, he had been found sitting alone on a railway platform. Paramedics noted disorientation but were told he had been well 3 hours earlier. On arrival to the ED, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was E3V3M5.

Physical examination revealed systemic skin flushing and warmth without wheals, mucosal swelling, or pruritus. Vital signs were: heart rate 80 beats/min, blood pressure 64/33 mmHg, temperature 35.6 degreesC, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air.

Venous blood gas analysis showed metabolic acidosis and elevated lactate (pH 7.289; bicarbonate 11.3 mmol/L; PvCO2 23.6 mmHg; lactate 9.3 mmol/L). Electrocardiogram and echocardiography were normal. A working diagnosis of distributive shock of unknown origin was made, and the patient received 2000 mL of lactated Ringer's solution. Empirical piperacillin/tazobactam was started after two sets of blood cultures.

A whole?

title: Rare case of metronidazole?induced encephalopathy in a multiple transplant recipient: Clinical and radiological insights
case_summary:
An 18-year-old man with a history of congenital malrotation and infantile volvulus underwent liver, small-bowel, and pancreas transplantation at age four, complicated by chronic liver failure, short-bowel syndrome, portal vein thrombosis, reflux esophagitis, immune thrombocytopenia, and azathioprine-induced hemolytic anemia. He also has recurrent C. difficile colitis and post-transplant ulcerative ileitis managed with long-term metronidazole. The initial metronidazole dose was 250 mg twice daily, increased over time to 750 mg twice daily three months before presentation. Other medications included prednisolone, mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus, warfarin, and lansoprazole, with no recent changes aside from metronidazole.

He presented with a one-day history of slurred speech and a three-week history of unsteadiness and dizziness. He denied vertigo, limb weakness, sensory changes, headache, fever, or other systemic symptoms. Vital signs were normal. He was alert and oriented with an unr

title: Fifty?eight?year?old female with abdominal migraine: A rare cause of episodic gastrointestinal disturbance in adults
case_summary:
A 58-year-old woman presented with multiple episodes of coffee-ground emesis over 24 hours, preceded by nearly 20 episodes of greenish, nonbloody emesis beginning 3 days earlier. She reported a 7-month history of similar 2- to 3-day episodes of moderate, continuous, dull, midline abdominal pain associated with nausea, anorexia, and a mild headache without visual changes or aura; these episodes required three prior hospital admissions. Between admissions she was symptomatic enough to need assistance with activities of daily living. She denied fever, chills, bowel?habit changes, weight loss, or skin findings. Her medical history included coronary artery disease treated with a drug-eluting stent 2 months earlier; she was on aspirin and ticagrelor. Prior outpatient therapies (omeprazole, ondansetron, sucralfate, meclizine, scopolamine, topiramate) had failed. 

On examination, her abdomen was soft, mildly distended, and tender in the epigastric and periumbilical areas without guarding or r

title: Delayed granulomatous eruption of the nose associated with ruxolitinib
case_summary:
A 77-year-old woman with JAK2V617F-positive post-essential thrombocythemia myelofibrosis treated with ruxolitinib for 5 years, Hashimoto thyroiditis, hypertension, depression, and osteoarthritis presented with redness and swelling of her nose. On examination, there was an erythematous papule on the nasal dorsum. 

A shave biopsy revealed a moderate-to-dense dermal infiltrate of lymphocytes, histiocytes, and a few plasma cells extending from the reticular dermis to the base of the specimen, with one focal area of caseation necrosis. Periodic acid-Schiff stain was negative for organisms. Six months later, multiple pink papules coalesced into a plaque on the nasal dorsum. A second shave biopsy showed a dermal lymphohistiocytic infiltrate surrounding a ruptured hair follicle. Gomori methenamine-silver nitrate and Fite stains were negative for organisms. Immunoperoxidase studies on paraffin sections showed predominantly CD3+ T cells (75% CD4+, 25% CD8+) with preserved CD2 and CD5 and mildly

title: Extramedullary plasmacytoma of the pancreas as an uncommon cause of obstructive jaundice: a case report
case_summary:
A 46-year-old West Indian man presented with a two-day history of intermittent rectal bleeding, coffee-ground vomitus, and symptoms of anemia. He had a history of peptic ulcer disease but denied nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory-drug use. Three weeks earlier, he noted jaundice with pruritus, dark urine, constitutional symptoms, and back pain.

On examination, he was anemic and deeply jaundiced but hemodynamically stable. There was minimal epigastric tenderness and a palpable, non-tender distended gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign). Digital rectal examination revealed altered blood, and nasogastric aspirate was coffee-ground in appearance. Clinicians considered an upper gastrointestinal bleed secondary to peptic ulcer disease, periampullary pancreatic carcinoma, and locally advanced gastric carcinoma.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, normal coagulation studies and electrolytes, and liver-function tests consistent with obstructive jaundice. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a

title: Cytomegalovirus reactivation with high viral load in a patient of coronavirus disease 2019 acute respiratory distress syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 63-year-old Indian man with no prior medical history presented with 7 days of fever and dry cough and 1 day of mild dyspnea. He was started empirically on azithromycin, cetirizine, and paracetamol and had a positive SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR test. He was admitted to the ICU when his respiratory rate rose to 34-36 breaths per minute and SpO? fell to 84% on room air; he remained hemodynamically stable. Initial laboratory studies showed normal hemogram, renal and liver function, and procalcitonin; C-reactive protein was 45 ng/ml, ferritin 611 ng/ml, D-dimer 2.8 ug/ml, and fibrinogen 575 mg/dl. Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral patchy infiltrates. He required noninvasive ventilation for moderate ARDS and received remdesivir, tocilizumab, methylprednisolone, enoxaparin, and empiric cefoperazone-sulbactam.  

In week 5 of illness, after testing negative for SARS-CoV-2, he was transferred to a non-COVID ICU and remained on NIV. Labs showed mild azotemia, sodium 132 mEq/l, potassium 3.3 mmol/

title: Primitive neuroectodermal tumor of the liver: a case report of a rare case in pediatrics
case_summary: A 4-year-old girl presented with fever, right upper-quadrant abdominal pain, weight loss, night sweats, and decreased appetite. Two weeks earlier, she had bacterial sinusitis. On examination, she had pale mucous membranes, multiple enlarged lymph nodes, and hepatomegaly.
