Laboratory studies showed elevated lactate dehydrogenase (1374 U/L), alanine aminotransferase (114 U/L), aspartate aminotransferase (68 U/L), a normal alpha-fetoprotein level (1.19 ng/mL), and a mild normocytic, normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 11 g/dL, hematocrit 34.5%). Chest radiography revealed well-defined radiopaque nodules in both hemithoraces. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated hepatomegaly due to a 14?7?9.5 cm heterogeneous intrahepatic mass with calcification, cystic and necrotic components, irregular lobulated borders extending into the hepatic hilum, compression of the right kidney and inferior vena cava, flow on Doppler, and locoregional lymphadenopathy. Non-contrast chest CT confirmed multiple subpleural no

title: Acute respiratory distress syndrome following administration of gadolinium contrast agent: a case report
case_summary:
A 15-year-old previously healthy girl underwent contrast-enhanced pelvic MRI with a gadolinium-based agent for evaluation of persistent pelvic pain. A few minutes after receiving gadolinium, she developed shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, nausea, and vomiting, and presented to the emergency department. On arrival, she had a transient loss of consciousness. Vital signs were: blood pressure 84/40 mmHg, heart rate 122/min, respiratory rate 30/min, temperature 36.4  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 78% on room air. On pulmonary auscultation, bilateral rales were present; there was no rash or lip/tongue swelling. She spoke in full sentences and was placed on 10 L/min oxygen via non-rebreather mask.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 18.6 g/dL, white blood cell count 15,360/mm3, C-reactive protein 41.8 mg/L, lactate dehydrogenase 176 U/L, and D-dimer 1.7 mg/mL. B-type natriuretic peptide and troponin levels were low. Transthoracic echocardiogram was normal. Chest computed tomogra

title: Haemolytic uremic syndrome following fire ant bites
case_summary:
A 21-year-old healthy male soldier presented two weeks after sustaining multiple bites from suspected fire ants on his left arm. Shortly after the bites, he developed a local reaction with flare, wheal, and subsequent erythema, edema, and induration, followed two days later by spiking fevers treated at a local clinic. Five days after the bite, he experienced severe nausea, vomiting, and a progressive decrease in urine output. Initial treatment with intravenous fluids, acetaminophen, and metoclopramide provided no improvement, and he was transferred to our hospital.

On examination, temperature was 37.0 degreesC, blood pressure 150/84 mmHg, pulse 75/min, and respiratory rate 20/min. He was mildly obese (BMI 28.2 kg/m2) and had pale conjunctivae; there was no edema, icterus, abdominal mass, or palpable kidney enlargement.

Laboratory values on admission were: sodium 128 mEq/L, potassium 3.9 mEq/L, BUN 153 mg/dL, creatinine 20.1 mg/dL, calcium 8.4 mg/dL, phosphorus 3.9 mg/dL, albumin 4.2

title: Eosinophilic arthritis--A case report
case_summary: A 30-year-old Japanese woman presented with a 1-week history of edema and pain in her wrists and ankles. She was initially treated with a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug by her orthopedist without relief. She denied use of any other medications or supplements and had no history of allergies or parasitic infections. Family history was notable only for her mother's hyperthyroidism. On examination, there was edema of both wrists and ankles, more pronounced on the right; there was no pitting edema of the lower limbs, goiter, cardiac murmur, or abnormal lung sounds. Her blood pressure was 110/67 mmHg. Chest radiograph and pulmonary function tests were normal. Initial laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 11 400 cells/mm3 with 15.5% eosinophils (1767 cells/mm3); hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL; platelets 253 000 cells/mm3; serum electrolytes, renal and liver function tests, and thyroid function were within normal limits. Immunologic assays, including rheumatoid factor, antistreptolysin O,

title: Acute myeloid leukaemia presenting as acute liver failure--a case report and literature review
case_summary: A 75-year-old woman presented with several days of fatigue, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, and jaundice. She denied fever, sick contacts, recent infections, travel, or new medications. Her past history included Parkinson's disease managed with aspirin, calcium-vitamin D3, and rasagiline. On examination she was afebrile and icteric. Laboratory values at initial presentation showed hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL; white blood cell count 6.35 k/uL (63% neutrophils, 21% lymphocytes, 13% monocytes); platelets 30 k/uL; alkaline phosphatase 92 U/L; AST 62 U/L; ALT 104 U/L; total bilirubin 4.5 mg/dL (direct 2.4 mg/dL). Serologic tests for Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, hepatitis viruses, and HIV were negative. Autoimmune markers including antinuclear, antimitochondrial, anti-Smith, and liver-kidney microsomal antibodies were negative. Magnetic resonance cholangiogram revealed marked hepatomegaly, moderate splenomegaly, and nodular areas of decreased T2 signal in the liver and spleen. Over the

title: Duodenal pseudomelanosis (pseudomelanosis duodeni): a rare endoscopic finding
case_summary: An 86-year-old woman presented with 3 days of watery diarrhea that became progressively dark and occasionally bloody. She denied abdominal pain, fever, or vomiting. This was her second similar episode in one month. Her history included hypertension and three ischemic strokes; she has been bedridden for 2 years. Medications were hydralazine, captopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and aspirin. On examination she was lethargic with slurred speech, dehydrated, blood pressure 96/60 mmHg, pulse 96 bpm. Neurologic exam showed complete left hemiplegia and central facial palsy. Laboratory tests revealed normal electrolytes and renal function; hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL with normal MCV and RDW; leukocytes and platelets were normal. Upper endoscopy performed for suspected GI bleeding showed an esophageal hernia, Los Angeles grade A esophagitis, enanthematous gastritis, and non-bleeding gastric angiodysplasia. The duodenal mucosa appeared lightly pale with multiple small black spots throughout the distal bulb

title: Congenital erythropoietic porphyria five years observation with standard treatment: a case report
case_summary: A 22-year-old man was referred for evaluation of chronic photosensitive skin lesions and hematologic abnormalities. He was born to non-consanguineous parents and was well until age 7, when after outdoor play in the sun he developed a vesicular rash on his face and upper extremities. He was admitted to a regional hospital and received red-blood-cell transfusions. During childhood and adolescence, he was followed in dermatology clinics for persistent photosensitivity and disfiguring scarring, and was ultimately suspended from military service because of suspected cutaneous porphyria. On examination, he had infected bullae on the face and hands, mutilation of the distal index fingers and nasal bridge, violaceous discoloration of the gums (erythrodontia), coarse hypertrichosis, and splenomegaly 3 cm below the costal margin. He reported pink-red discoloration of his urine and stools. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 11.7 g/dL (normal 13.0-18.0), a reticulocyte count of 2.5% (normal 0.2-

title: Radiographical bony lesions after discontinuation of immunosuppressant therapy: bone involvement in sarcoidosis
case_summary:
A woman of African descent in her 50s with end-stage renal disease presented with a 6-month history of insidious, gradually progressive low back pain radiating to the posterior legs and toes, with numbness in the right anterolateral thigh. The pain worsened with sitting, standing, and walking and was not associated with muscle weakness. She denied fevers, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Thirteen years earlier, she had undergone a living-donor renal transplant for focal segmental glomerulosclerosis and had been maintained on tacrolimus and mycophenolate until xenograft nephrectomy and discontinuation of immunosuppression 6 months before presentation.  
Laboratory studies revealed a normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, hematocrit 31.7%), normal platelet and leukocyte counts with a normal differential, creatinine at her baseline of ~8 mg/dL, normal electrolytes, urinalysis with proteinuria, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 25 mm/hr, and negative blood and urine cul

title: Arsenic toxicity manifesting as profuse watery diarrhea during induction therapy for acute promyelocytic leukemia
case_summary:
A 72-year-old woman with morbid obesity (BMI 42) presented with fatigue, nosebleeds, and easy bruising.  
Initial laboratory evaluation showed:  
? WBC 5.34 K/?L, hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelets 13 K/?L  
? Peripheral smear with 19% promyelocytes, 9% granulated blasts, and Auer rods  
? Coagulation studies: PT/INR 18.3 s/1.5, PTT 30.2 s, D-dimer 8115 ng/mL, fibrinogen 77 ng/mL  
Renal function was at baseline (creatinine 0.8 mg/dL). Cytogenetics revealed t(15;17) and trisomy 8, with PCR confirming a PML-RARA fusion, establishing acute promyelocytic leukemia.  

She began induction therapy with oral all-trans retinoic acid 45 mg/m2 twice daily on admission and intravenous arsenic trioxide 0.15 mg/kg daily starting on day 5. On day 8, she developed dyspnea, headache, and edema consistent with differentiation syndrome, which resolved with dexamethasone.  

Between days 19 and 30, she experienced intermittent loose stools progressing to fever (102 degreesF) on day 30. Broad-spectrum antibi

title: A haemorrhagic pericardial cyst compressing the right side of the heart: a case report
case_summary: A 73-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with a 4-month history of progressive dyspnoea (NYHA class III) and leg and facial oedema without cough, fever, or night sweats. Symptoms did not improve with diuretic therapy, prompting referral to cardiology. On examination, he had markedly engorged neck veins. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed subtotal compression of the right side of the heart by an external mass. Laboratory studies showed creatinine 3.5 mg/dL (GFR 18 mL/min), NT-proBNP 2543 pg/mL, and D-dimer 1393 ug/L. Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT demonstrated a large (89 ? 58 mm) intrapericardial thick-walled mass with fluid accumulation, homogeneous enhancement of the cyst wall, maximal pericardial thickness of 2 mm, and compression of the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple supracarinal pretracheal lymph nodes measured up to 19.7 ? 12.3 mm. The imaging diagnosis was a large pericardial cyst with signs of concomitant infection. The patient was referred for surgic

title: Multifocal Osteosarcoma: Multiple Primaries or Metastases? A Report of Rare Case and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A previously healthy 10-year-old boy presented with 10 days of progressively worsening aching pain in the right lower limb after a trivial fall; he became unable to walk. He had no fever, weight loss, or pain elsewhere. Birth and family history were unremarkable, and immunizations were up to date. On examination, there was tenderness and swelling over the right knee; the remainder of the physical and systemic examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10 g/dL, platelet count 67,000/uL, markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase (9587 U/L) and lactate dehydrogenase (3414 U/L), parathyroid hormone at the upper limit of normal for age (169 pg/mL), and normal serum calcium and other biochemical parameters. Serologies for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were negative.  
Radiographs of the right femur demonstrated a sclerotic lesion in the lower metadiaphysis with a wide zone of transition, mild spiculated periosteal reaction, cortical breach at the medial epicondyle, and a s

title: A middle-aged female with recurrent sinopulmonary infections: a case report
case_summary:
A 42-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted with one day of high-grade fever, yellow productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, and resting shortness of breath. She reported 15 years of chronic dyspnea and fatigue and 10 years of chronic loose stools. One week earlier she had been discharged from the ICU after septic shock with multiorgan failure requiring two weeks of ventilatory support and temporary hemodialysis.

Her past medical history included asthma (15 years), COPD (5 years), recurrent sinus and ear infections since adolescence (mastoidectomy 4 years ago), GERD, IBS (10 years), migraines, and bipolar disorder. She was a 20-pack-year smoker. Medications included levalbuterol, montelukast, fluticasone/salmeterol, lansoprazole, loratadine/pseudoephedrine, hydrocodone, quetiapine, and topiramate.

On examination, she appeared chronically ill. Temperature was 99.8 degreesF, pulse 137/min, respirations 26/min, BP 109/59 mmHg, and O2 saturation 88% on 2 L nasal oxygen. Bibasilar rhonchi

title: Non-Mass-Forming Myeloid Sarcoma of the Colon Presenting With Diarrhea
case_summary:
A woman in her mid-70s with a history of left-sided diverticulosis presented with 2 months of worsening nonbloody diarrhea, left lower-quadrant abdominal pain, decreased appetite, and fever. She had been treated empirically for diverticulitis without improvement. She denied recent travel, infectious contacts, or medication changes. Outpatient blood tests showed leukocytosis (WBC 23.63 ? 10^9/L) and anemia (hemoglobin 81 g/L). The automated differential flagged immature granulocytes; a manual smear revealed a left shift to the blast stage with approximately 6% circulating blasts. She was referred to the emergency department. 

Abdominal computed tomography demonstrated extensive sigmoid diverticulosis but no bowel wall thickening or mass. Blood and urine cultures showed no microbial growth. Stool culture and parasite enzyme immunoassay were negative. Clostridioides difficile serology was negative. Repeat blood counts showed persistent blasts. Bone marrow biopsy revealed 65%-80% myelobla

title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging-negative, Rituximab-resistant Neurolymphomatosis as a Paradoxical Presentation of Relapsed Primary Adrenal Lymphoma
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man was evaluated for a 3-month history of fever, malaise, and chills. His past history included primary aldosteronism with a right adrenal mass treated medically for two years. Initial laboratory tests showed anemia (hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (28?10^3/uL), leukopenia (3.19?10^3/uL), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (586 U/L), and an elevated soluble interleukin-2 receptor level; aldosterone was below the lower limit of normal. Computed tomography revealed bilaterally enlarged adrenal glands (right 4.3?1.6 cm, left 4.0?2.5 cm) and hepatosplenomegaly (spleen 12.5?3.6 cm) without lymphadenopathy or ascites. On examination, he was febrile (39.0?), tachycardic (122 beats/min), and hypotensive (91/56 mmHg off antihypertensives); there was abdominal distention and pretibial edema but no palpable lymphadenopathy.

A random skin biopsy was negative for lymphomatous infiltration. Bone marrow biopsy showed diffuse proliferation of large atypical lymphoid cells that were

title: The Sinus That Breeds Fungus: Subcutaneous Zygomycosis Caused by Basidiobolus ranarum at the Injection Site
case_summary: A 58-year-old woman from Central India presented with a two-year history of a persistent discharging sinus in the right gluteal region. Two years earlier, she had received an intramuscular injection there for fever and back pain and subsequently developed multiple abscesses that required incision and drainage twice. She completed a six-month course of antituberculous therapy with no apparent clinical improvement. On examination, there was an indurated, nontender subcutaneous swelling in the right gluteal region with a single sinus tract draining serous, nonfoul-smelling pus. Systemic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 11.6 g%, white-cell count 7000/mm3, platelets 250,000/mm3, fasting blood sugar 90 mg%, postprandial blood sugar 130 mg%, serum creatinine 1.0 mg%, chest radiograph normal, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 15 mm at one hour. A presumptive diagnosis of soft-tissue infection due to atypical mycobacteria was made. The sinus tract was excised, a

title: Talaromyces marneffei: A challenging diagnosis in a kidney transplant patient
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man was evaluated for a 6-month history of frequent, urgent, and painful urination without fever. He had end-stage renal disease secondary to IgA nephropathy and underwent a living-donor kidney transplant in January 2001. His immunosuppressive regimen included mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus (switched from cyclosporine after treatment for acute T-cell-mediated rejection in December 2021), and prednisone. Serum creatinine had been stable at 140-165 ?mol/L.  
On initial evaluation in June 2022, urine protein was 0.15 g/L, urinary white blood cells were 64.6/?L, and serum creatinine was 165 ?mol/L. Empiric levofloxacin failed to improve his symptoms. On admission, physical examination showed pitting edema of both lower limbs. Urine cultures grew Enterobacter cloacae, and meropenem was started.  
Renal ultrasound demonstrated multiple heterogeneous echo foci in the transplanted kidney (largest 2.3 ? 2.0 cm) with poor blood perfusion. Chest CT revealed masses in the upper an

title: An Uncommon Cause of Chronic Nonhealing Perianal Ulcer
case_summary:
A 52-year-old man presented with a 1-year history of a painful perianal ulcer that began as a small nodule and progressively enlarged. He reported a significant loss of appetite and weight over the past 6 months but denied diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, cough, night sweats, joint pains, or eye symptoms. He had no comorbidities, addictions, intravenous drug use, or high-risk sexual behavior and no known history of tuberculosis or household contacts with TB.

On examination, there was an irregular perianal ulcer measuring 10 ? 6 cm, extending from the 2 o'clock to the 6 o'clock position around the anal verge, with pinkish granulation tissue and areas of slough. Bilateral inguinal lymph nodes were palpable, discrete, firm, and up to 2 cm in diameter.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin of 14 g/dL, leukocyte count of 6040 cells/mm^3, platelet count of 445,000/mm^3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 73 mm/hr, total protein 8 g/dL, albumin 3.6 g/dL, creatinine 0.3 mg/dL, and fasting gluc

title: Rituximab-induced interstitial lung disease in a patient with follicular lymphoma: A rare case report
journal: Lung India : Official Organ of Indian Chest Society
case_summary:
A 33-year-old non-smoker woman with stage III?B follicular lymphoma diagnosed by lymph node biopsy and immunohistochemistry (CD10+, CD20+, Bcl-2+, Bcl-6+, Ki-67 22%) and uninvolved bone marrow received four cycles of R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone). Two weeks after the fourth cycle, she was admitted with three days of exertional dyspnea and dry cough without fever. On examination, she was tachypneic with bilateral basal crackles. Arterial blood gas showed hypoxemia. Complete blood count, renal and liver function tests, urine analysis, and D-dimer were normal. ECG showed sinus tachycardia. Chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral reticulonodular infiltrates. High-resolution CT of the lungs revealed bilateral patchy ground-glass opacities, predominantly in the upper lobes. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern. 

To exclude infection, sputum Gram stain, Ziehl-Neelsen stain for acid-fast bacilli, fungal and Pneumocystis carinii s

title:
Cytotoxic corpus callosum lesion and mild CSF pleocytosis during
hantavirus infection: a case report
case_summary:
A middle-aged man with no prior medical history presented with a 3-day history of intense throbbing headache (9/10), high fever up to 40.6  degreesC, and diffuse limb aches. He had no photophobia, phonophobia, vomiting, altered mental status, or meningeal signs. Neurologic and general physical examinations were unremarkable. He denied recent travel, animal exposures, gardening, or new medications.

Laboratory studies on admission showed leukocytes 7.7 ? 10^3/ul, hemoglobin 15.9 g/dl, platelets 102 ? 10^3/ul, creatinine 1.3 mg/dl, estimated GFR 70 ml/min/1.73 m^2, AST 60 U/l, ?-GT 60 U/l, and CRP 12.6 mg/dl. Urinalysis revealed proteinuria (urine albumin/creatinine ratio 1496 mg/g). Over the next days, platelets transiently increased above normal, creatinine peaked at 2.4 mg/dl, then improved.

Lumbar puncture on day 1 showed 11 cells/ul (9 lymphocytes), total protein 386 mg/l, normal glucose and lactate, an intact blood-brain barrier by Reiber diagram, negative PCR for CMV, EBV, HSV-1/

title: Haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis after heart transplantation: a case report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man with celiac disease underwent orthotopic heart transplantation in 2011 for giant cell myocarditis. His maintenance immunosuppression consisted of tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisolone, later intensified with everolimus after recurrent acute cellular rejection episodes. In 2013, he received a pacemaker for complete heart block. Graft function remained stable until early May 2017, when he developed fever and malaise without focal symptoms. After 7 days at home without improvement, he was admitted for evaluation.

On admission, laboratory studies demonstrated a marked inflammatory reaction, but real?time PCR assays for multiple viruses and bacterial cultures were all negative. An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia at 108 beats per minute, supraventricular extrasystoles, right bundle branch block, left anterior fascicular block, and signs of left ventricular hypertrophy. Chest radiography was unremarkable, and abdominal ultrasonography showed no hepa

title: Erosive cheilitis as an early manifestation in DRESS syndrome
final_diagnosis: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
case_summary:
A 69-year-old woman with a 17-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, 10 years of hypertension, dyslipidemia, and gout was referred for oral mucosal lesions. Six weeks before, she had started allopurinol for a gout flare. A week before presentation, she developed fever with chills, night sweats, and malaise. The next day, she noted bluish spots on her lower limbs and significant lip swelling. Symptomatic treatment in the emergency department provided no relief. A dermatologist prescribed prednisolone mouthwash, and she was admitted.

On admission, temperature was 38.7  degreesC, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, pulse 67 beats/minute. Examination showed a confluent erythematous maculopapular rash over the trunk and extremities, sparing the face, scalp, palms, and soles; Nikolsky's sign was negative. Oral examination revealed painful erosive cheilitis, crusting of both lips, and confluent ulcerations of the labial mucosa. No lymphadenopathy was noted; cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinati

title: Pseudo-thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura presenting as multi-organ dysfunction syndrome: A rare complication of pernicious anemia
case_summary:
An 86-year-old woman with a history of right hip replacement was found by her granddaughter to have altered mental status after 2 days of feeling unwell. She had no known medication use, alcohol, smoking, or recreational drug history. There was no history of neurologic symptoms or prior similar episodes. In the emergency department, she was intubated for respiratory distress and altered mental status. Vital signs: temperature not documented, pulse 110 bpm, blood pressure 148/100 mm Hg, respiratory rate 32 per min, O? saturation 80% on 6 L by mask. Examination revealed dehydration, rapid shallow respirations with wheezing, mild pedal edema, and no trauma. 

Initial laboratory studies showed: WBC 6.5 ? 10^3/uL; hemoglobin 3.2 g/dL; hematocrit 9.6%; MCV 127 fL; platelets 59 ? 10^3/uL; reticulocyte count 7.5% (corrected 1.6%); LDH 7077 IU/L; haptoglobin 14 mg/dL. Electrolytes were normal. Arterial blood gas revealed severe metabolic acidosis (anion gap 29). BUN 34 mg/dL; creatinine 1.9 mg/

title: A case of total ophthalmoplegia associated with a COVID-19 infection: case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus presented with a 4-day history of left periocular pain, a drooping eyelid, and limitation of left-eye movements. Two weeks earlier, she had a severe left-sided headache unresponsive to sedatives, accompanied by fatigue, a dry cough, and fever. She denied trauma or recreational drug use.

On examination, there was complete external ophthalmoplegia of the left eye, total left ptosis, and mild chemosis. The left pupil was semi-dilated and nonreactive to light. Hertel exophthalmometry showed 3 mm of proptosis on the left. Uncorrected visual acuity was 0.6 (0.18 LogMAR), intraocular pressure was 16 mmHg, and color vision was normal. She was alert and oriented. Tendon reflexes and muscle tone were normal, with no meningeal or cerebellar signs. Ear, nose, and throat examinations were unremarkable. The sensory examination was normal initially; left corneal hypoesthesia developed 2 days later.

Laboratory studies, in

title: Case report of a tibial fracture in a patient suffering from gout: An atypical site, the importance of differential diagnosis1, 2
case_summary: A 60-year-old man with a 9-year history of severe tophaceous gout affecting multiple joints, maintained on colchicine and febuxostat, presented after a syncopal episode and ground-level fall with direct trauma to his right forearm and knee. He was unable to bear weight on the right leg due to knee pain. On admission, hemoglobin was 9.8 g/dL, creatinine 1.29 mg/dL, and lactate dehydrogenase 247 U/L; other blood tests were unremarkable. Radiographs of the right knee showed a lateral tibial plateau fracture through a round radiolucent lesion. A noncontrast CT scan demonstrated an intra-articular fracture extending to both intercondylar spines and a 29 ? 27 ? 26 mm focal lytic lesion with non-sclerotic "map-like" margins, cortical thinning posteriorly, no periosteal reaction or trabecular edema, and a solid matrix (50-90 HU) without calcifications; severe osteoporosis, diffuse popliteal vascular calcifications, and joint effusion were also noted. Bedside ultrasound revealed diffuse periart

title: Atovaquone-Proguanil: A Promising Therapy for Persistent Relapsing Babesiosis
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia in remission on ibrutinib presented with several weeks of generalized weakness, easy fatigability, confusion, and falls. He denied fever, night sweats, chills, weight loss, rashes, focal weakness, or diarrhea. He enjoyed fishing and hunting but recalled no tick bites. He had received multiple blood transfusions in the past and reported a history of hemolysis with quinine use. 

On examination, he was comfortable at rest, without rash or lymphadenopathy; he became dyspneic only after prolonged conversation. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 5.1 g/dL, hematocrit 15.7%, total bilirubin 5.1 mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 1699 U/L, and a peripheral smear with rare spherocytes. Ibrutinib was discontinued, and he received steroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, and rituximab for presumed CLL-associated Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia or acute viral illness, with mild improvement.

Weeks later, he was readmitted with worsening fatigue

title: Evans syndrome during pembrolizumab therapy for upper urinary tract cancer
case_summary: A 56?year?old man with metastatic upper-tract urothelial carcinoma had been receiving pembrolizumab for 26 months (33 cycles) after progression on chemotherapy. He presented with a 2-day history of fever and general fatigue. On examination, he had pale eyelid conjunctiva, yellowish ocular conjunctiva, and mild subcutaneous bleeding on the lower extremities. Laboratory tests showed a hemoglobin concentration of 4.7 g/dL (baseline 10.5 g/dL), a platelet count of 81?10^3/?L (baseline 195?10^3/?L), an elevated lactate dehydrogenase level of 223 U/L (baseline 110 U/L), total bilirubin 2.7 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 1.7 mg/dL, indirect bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 69 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 113 U/L, ?-glutamyl transpeptidase 223 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 518 U/L. Coagulation studies were notable for a prothrombin time-international normalized ratio of 1.13 and activated partial thromboplastin time of 31.2 seconds. Immunologic testing revealed a cold agglutination re

title: Gadolinium-Induced Acute Graft Pancreatitis in a Simultaneous Pancreas-Kidney Transplant Recipient
case_summary:
A 32-year-old woman, two years after simultaneous pancreas-kidney transplant for type I diabetes, presented with sudden onset of constant, dull, generalized abdominal pain accompanied by fever, nausea, and vomiting. She denied trauma, alcohol use, and changes in bowel or urinary habits. She had undergone MRI of the brain with gadobutrol 48 hours earlier. Her medications included mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus, prednisolone, and other maintenance therapies. 

On arrival, she was hypotensive (90/29?mmHg), tachycardic (100?bpm), and hypothermic (34.3 degreesC), with cool extremities. Blood glucose was 6.5?mmol/L. Examination revealed tenderness over the transplanted pancreatic graft without renal graft tenderness. Arterial blood gas showed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.24; lactate 3.2?mmol/L). Laboratory tests revealed WBC 22.3???10^9/L, creatinine 159?umol/L (baseline 85?umol/L), urea 12.4?mmol/L, and serum lipase 3368?U/L (normal 0-60). Liver tests and CRP were unremarkable. Tacrolimus tr

title: Acute Hepatitis and Pancytopenia in Healthy Infant with Adenovirus
case_summary: A previously healthy 23-month-old girl presented with a 2-day history of rhinorrhea, cough, decreased energy, sleepiness, tactile fever, and on the day before admission two episodes of nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting and several episodes of nonbloody diarrhea. She also developed generalized facial, arm, and leg swelling. She had completed a 10-day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a urinary tract infection, with the last dose 5 days earlier. On examination she was fussy but consolable, with mild bilateral conjunctival injection, dry lips, scattered bilateral crackles without tachypnea or retractions, and mild tachycardia without murmurs. Generalized edema, including periorbital and pedal edema, was noted. There was no jaundice, and her mentation was intact. Laboratory studies showed an AST of 889 IU/L, ALT of 740 IU/L, albumin 2.2 g/dL, INR 1.5, PTT 55 seconds, and normal bilirubin. Initial CBC revealed leukopenia (3.5 K/uL) and hemoglobin 10 g/dL; by hospital day 2 she had de

title: Chlorambucil-induced cytomegalovirus infection: a case report
case_summary:
An 81-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 10-day history of fever and nonproductive cough, accompanied by abdominal pain, malaise, and weight loss. His medical history included hypertensive cardiomyopathy and idiopathic membranous nephropathy (stage III) diagnosed several months earlier, for which he began chlorambucil (10 mg daily) and methylprednisolone (0.5 mg/kg daily) 22 days before presentation due to persistent nephrotic syndrome and worsening renal function.

On admission, temperature was 38  degreesC, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 93% on room air. There were no oral lesions. Lung examination revealed widespread rhonchi. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, with epigastric tenderness.

Laboratory studies showed: white blood cell count 2.3 ? 10^9/L (neutrophils 82%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 96 ? 10^9/L, lactate dehydrogenase 965 U/L, serum creatinine 2.8 mg/dL, urea 164 mg/dL,

title: Disseminated Necrotizing Leukoencephalopathy Complicating Septic Shock in an Immunocompetent Patient
case_summary: A 90-year-old woman with hypertension presented with drowsiness, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, tachycardia, and hypotension (BP 88/62 mmHg). Physical examination was notable only for drowsiness; there were no focal neurologic or meningeal signs. Laboratory studies showed thrombocytopenia (85,000/uL), leukocytosis (16,860/uL; neutrophils 15,800/uL), elevated C-reactive protein (18.1 mg/dL), blood urea nitrogen (93 mg/dL), creatinine (2.8 mg/dL), hyperlactacidemia (38 mg/dL), and metabolic acidosis (HCO3- 19.8 mmol/L). Urinalysis revealed leukocyturia (>500 leukocytes/uL). Chest radiography and ECG were unremarkable; renal ultrasound showed right pyelocaliceal dilation with perirenal fluid. A diagnosis of acute pyelonephritis with septic shock and acute kidney injury was made. Blood and urine cultures grew Escherichia coli. Broad-spectrum antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, and hemodynamic support led to rapid normalization of vital signs, inflammatory markers, and mental status

title: Severe Neonatal Presentation of Progressive Familial Intrahepatic Cholestasis Type 4 in an Omani Infant
case_summary: A one-month-old boy was referred with a 1-week history of progressive jaundice, poor feeding, dark urine and 2 days of irritability. There was no fever, vomiting or acholic stools. He was born at 36 weeks (birth weight 1.9 kg) to first-cousin parents; two older siblings are healthy. On examination he was irritable, pale, and jaundiced; growth parameters were below the 3rd percentile. The liver edge was palpable 2 cm below the costal margin; there was no splenomegaly or ascites. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 3.8 g/dL, reticulocyte count 5%, lactate dehydrogenase 782 U/L, prolonged PT and APTT with INR >17.4, total bilirubin 237 ?mol/L (84% conjugated), ALT 79 U/L, AST 261 U/L, and GGT 36 U/L. Metabolic work-up (newborn screen, urine reducing substances, ammonia, CK), infectious and endocrine evaluations were all negative. Brain MRI demonstrated multiple intracranial hemorrhages. Abdominal ultrasound showed a liver of normal size and echotexture. He received red-cell and plasma tr

title: Visceral leishmaniasis complicated with hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis and resistant to amphotericin B: a case report
case_summary:
A 15-month-old Arab girl was admitted with 15 days of fever and reduced activity and weight. She had no dyspnea, cyanosis, rash, or diarrhea. Immunizations were up to date; she had typhoid fever 4 months earlier treated with oral cefixime. On exam, she was alert but pale, febrile (39.5  degreesC), blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, pulse 120 beats/min, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, weight 8.5 kg. Liver and spleen were palpably enlarged 4 cm and 6 cm below the costal margins. 

Initial laboratory studies: hemoglobin 71 g/L; platelets 104 ? 10^3/uL; white blood cells 6.8 ? 10^3/uL (16% neutrophils, 73% lymphocytes, 7% monocytes); ESR 135 mm and 150 mm at 1 and 2 hours. Peripheral smear showed hypochromic microcytic anemia, poikilocytosis, reactive lymphocytosis, and mild thrombocytopenia. Wright test was negative; Widal test was positive (S. typhi O 1/320; H 1/640; S. paratyphi A and B 1/320). Chest radiograph was normal; abdominal ultrasound confirmed hepatosplenomegaly.

Bone marrow aspirat

title: Pulmonary Toxoplasmosis Diagnosed on Transbronchial Lung Biopsy in a Mechanically Ventilated Patient
case_summary:
A 37-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented with a 1-week history of dry cough and dyspnea on exertion. She denied fever or chills but reported a 20-lb weight loss and anorexia over 2 months. She immigrated from Nigeria 2 years earlier, is a lifelong nonsmoker, and takes no medications. 

On examination she was in mild respiratory distress. Temperature was 99  degreesF, pulse 152 beats/min, blood pressure 116/83 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. She had hepatomegaly; the remainder of the examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 94 000/uL, AST 664 U/L, ALT 1703 U/L, LDH 3462 U/L, and CPK 2742 U/L. A rapid HIV test was positive; CD4 count was 8 cells/uL and HIV viral load exceeded 10 million copies/mL. Hepatitis B surface antigen and core antibody were positive, with a viral load >17 million IU/L.

Chest radiography revealed bilateral homogeneous ground-glass opacities. Chest CT showed l

title: Recurrent Inflammatory Myositis as an Extra-Intestinal Manifestation of Dormant Ulcerative Colitis in a Patient on Long-Term Mesalamine
case_summary: 28-year-old Hispanic man with no past medical history presented with three weeks of progressively worsening bloody diarrhea and diffuse myalgias localized to the bilateral lower extremities. He denied recent travel, sick contacts, or new medications; he had completed a 10-day course of amoxicillin for presumed streptococcal pharyngitis without improvement. On admission, temperature was 101.8 degreesF, WBC 17,600/mm3, hemoglobin 9.7 g/dL (decreasing to 8.9), ESR 114 mm/hr, CRP 33.6 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 183 IU/L, AST 145 IU/L, CPK 1433 IU/L, and potassium 2.7 mmol/L. Hepatitis panel, HIV, coccidioidomycosis, gonorrhea and chlamydia PCR were negative. Stool studies were negative for enteric pathogens and C difficile. CT of the abdomen and pelvis was unremarkable. Colonoscopy revealed continuous colitis from cecum to rectum; biopsies showed crypt abscesses and mixed lamina propria inflammation consistent with ulcerative colitis. He was treated with IV fluids, mesalamine, and a predn

title: Expanding spectrum of illness due to community-associated methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus: a case report
case_summary:
A 52-year-old African American woman with untreated HIV infection (CD4 396 cells/?L) and a history of discoid lupus, hepatitis C, active tobacco, alcohol, and intravenous drug use presented with three months of progressive abdominal pain, distension, and fever. She had not been hospitalized or on antiretroviral therapy, and denied sick contacts. On examination, her temperature was 39.6  degreesC; she had a distended abdomen with right costo-vertebral angle tenderness and track marks on the right lower extremity. Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 9240/?L (71% neutrophils), hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL, albumin 1.9 g/dL; chemistry, amylase, and lipase were normal. Urinalysis revealed 5-10 RBCs/hpf without WBCs; pregnancy test was negative. A urine drug screen was positive for cocaine and opiates.  
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a 17.5 cm ? 12.8 cm multicystic mass arising from the right kidney, containing multiple septations but no evid

title: Giant isolated hydatid lung cyst: two?case reports
case_summary: A 13-year-old Maasai boy from rural Arusha, northern Tanzania, presented with a 2-month history of progressive dyspnea and dry cough that turned blood-streaked over the previous 2 weeks. He reported unintentional weight loss and intermittent low-grade fevers. There was no known tuberculosis contact, and a course of unspecified antibiotics at a local dispensary yielded no improvement. He lives on a farm and keeps livestock. On examination, he was moderately pale, afebrile (36.7? degreesC), pulse 85 beats/min, blood pressure 108/60?mmHg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Chest expansion was greater on the right side, percussion was dull, and air entry was reduced over the right hemithorax. Abdominal and neurologic examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.4?g/dL, leukocyte count 7.39?10^9/L, platelet count 492?10^9/L, ESR 95?mm/hour, serum creatinine 42??mol/L, urea 3.77?mmol/L, and normal electrolytes and bilirubin; urine analysis was unremarkable. A p

title: Postpartum Ovarian Vein Thrombosis: Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 20-year-old woman presented on postpartum day five with gradually worsening right lower back and lower abdominal pain that began on day three. She was primiparous, had an uncomplicated term vaginal delivery with epidural anesthesia, and was discharged on day one post-delivery. She described the pain as sharp and positional and denied fever, malodorous vaginal discharge, dysuria, incontinence, or trauma. Her medical history included idiopathic osteochondromas and benign thyroid nodules; there was no personal or family history of hypercoagulability.  

On examination, she appeared uncomfortable but was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Vital signs were: temperature 37.1  degreesC, heart rate 119 beats/min, blood pressure 135/82 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Abdominal examination revealed right lumbar tenderness and right suprapubic tenderness without rebound or guarding.  

The initial differential diagnosis included ascending infecti

title: One clot after another in COVID-19 patient: diagnostic utility of handheld echocardiogram
case_summary:
A 63-year-old woman with hypertension presented during the COVID-19 pandemic with three weeks of progressive shortness of breath on minimal exertion and fatigue. On arrival she was hypoxic (SpO2 74%) and tachypneic (30 breaths/min), prompting endotracheal intubation. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Soon afterward she developed persistent hypotension requiring vasopressor support.

Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm at 70 bpm, a tall R wave in V2, and symmetric T-wave inversions in the precordial leads. A chest radiograph after intubation demonstrated bilateral patchy infiltrates. The initial SARS-CoV-2 PCR test was negative; a repeat test 24 hours later was positive. Laboratory studies revealed a troponin T of 0.07 ng/ml (peak 0.22 ng/ml; reference < 0.01 ng/ml), an NT-proBNP of 12 945 pg/ml, a white-cell count of 21.1 ? 10^3/ul, creatinine 1.45 mg/dl, C-reactive protein 25.9 mg/dl, ferritin 546 ng/ml, and D-dimer 4.34 mg/l (reference ? 0.63 mg/l). Serial ECGs rem

title: Ganglionar tuberculosis infection evolving to hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis after anti-programmed cell death 1 treatment for high-risk melanoma: a?case report
case_summary: A 76-year-old Caucasian man in Brazil with stage IIIC superficial spreading melanoma of the trunk (Breslow thickness 5.0 mm, ulcerated, pT4b pN1a M0) underwent wide excision and sentinel-node biopsy showing micrometastasis. He received adjuvant nivolumab (3 mg/kg every 14 days) for 12 months without significant adverse events. Two months after completing therapy, he developed weakness, anorexia, daily fevers to 38.0  degreesC, night sweats, weight loss, diffuse petechiae, and splenomegaly. He denied recent travel, new medications, and sick contacts. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL, platelets 68 ? 10^3/mL, fibrinogen 138 mg/dL, and ferritin 20 406 ug/L. ^18F-FDG PET-CT showed intense uptake in the spleen and in cervical, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and abdominal lymph nodes, with an SUVmax of 14.8 in an infracarinal node. Bone marrow aspiration was hypercellular without other abnormalities. He met five of eight criteria for HLH and was treated with dexamethasone and in

title: Dubowitz syndrome: common findings and peculiar urine odor
case_summary:
A 5-year-old girl was referred for failure to thrive. She was the fifth child of unrelated parents from the same village in southern Lebanon. Born at term via vaginal delivery, her birth weight was 2200 g; the pregnancy was uneventful, and there was no maternal medication use, smoking, or alcohol intake. Her perinatal course was notable for neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy. Family history included mental retardation in a maternal uncle and a congenital heart defect in an older brother.

In early infancy, she had feeding difficulties, failure to thrive, recurrent respiratory infections, and eczematous skin lesions. Since birth, her urine had a musty odor, although repeated urine analyses were normal. Developmentally, she was hyperactive with significant speech delay (vocabulary of 10-15 words; few two-word sentences); gross and fine motor skills were age-appropriate, and she understood simple commands but had a short attention span and impulsive behavior.

On examination, her he

title: Incidental Hepatic Granulomata as the Initial Presentation of Crohn's Disease in a Pediatric Patient
case_summary:
A 9-year-old immunized girl with a history of migraines and anxiety presented with 2-3 weeks of right flank and right upper-quadrant abdominal pain. Over the previous 3 months she had unintentional weight loss from the mid-70th percentile to the 14th percentile. She denied fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, or hematochezia. Initial laboratory evaluation showed an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 41 mm/hr; complete blood count, metabolic panel, liver panel, antitissue transglutaminase IgA, total IgA, and urinalysis were otherwise unremarkable.  
Abdominal ultrasound revealed two ill-defined, hypoechoic lesions in the right hepatic lobe. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated five T1-hypointense, T2-hyperintense lesions in the posterior right hepatic lobe, each measuring 1.0-2.6 cm in diameter. An ultrasound-guided needle biopsy of a hepatic lesion showed necrotizing granulomata with chronic periportal and lobular inflammation.  
Acid-fast bacillus and G

title: Chronic Pulmonary Melioidosis Masquerading as lung malignancy diagnosed by EBUS guided sheath technique
case_summary:
A 41-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a four-day history of intermittent non-specific chest pain, on a background of progressive exertional dyspnea over several months and recent loss of appetite. She denied fever, cough, hemoptysis, pleuritic pain, or upper respiratory symptoms. She had no significant past medical history, was not diabetic, and took no medications. She smoked 30 pack?years. She lived in Darwin, Northern Territory, and was an avid gardener who often walked barefoot and used large amounts of potting mix. She had no known tuberculosis exposures, no relevant travel or occupational history, and no alcohol or drug misuse.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Cardiac and respiratory examinations were unremarkable; there was no clubbing, edema, cyanosis, or lymphadenopathy. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 11.5?10^9/L with neutrophilia of 7.9?10^9/L and a C-reactive protein of 15 mg/L. Serial troponins, liver and re

title: Deep cerebral venous thrombosis mimicking influenza-associated acute necrotizing encephalopathy: a case report
case_summary: A 52-year-old woman presented with headache and progressive alteration of consciousness. Five days earlier, she had developed fever and cough and tested positive for influenza B by nasal swab; she received a single inhalation of laninamivir. On admission she was stuporous (Glasgow Coma Scale E2V4M6) without focal neurologic deficits or meningeal signs. Laboratory tests showed iron-deficiency anemia (hemoglobin 7.8 g/dl, MCV 63.2 fl, iron 11 mg/dl), mild thrombocytosis (434?10^3/?l), an elevated D-dimer (3.9 ?g/ml), and an IL-6 level of 43.9 pg/ml. Renal, hepatic and electrolyte panels were normal. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) had an opening pressure of 300 mmH2O, xanthochromia with 1820 red cells/?l, protein 622.2 mg/dl, and 4 white cells/?l; cultures were negative, and PCR assays for herpes simplex virus, varicella-zoster virus, Epstein-Barr virus, and cytomegalovirus were all negative. Brain MRI revealed bilateral thalamic and caudate-nucleus lesions with left internal capsule involveme

title: From Blocking Shots to Blocking GI Transit, This Professional Basketball Player Does It All: A Case Report on Small Bowel Obstruction
case_summary:
A woman in her mid-20s who is a professional basketball player presented with a 7-hour history of sharp, worsening abdominal pain rated 7/10, initially epigastric then migrating to the right lower quadrant. She had nausea and vomiting in the emergency department, for which she received IV ondansetron and a bolus of lactated Ringer solution. She denied diarrhea, constipation, fever, chills, dysuria, or vaginal discharge. Her last menstrual period was within the past month, and she reported a low likelihood of pregnancy. She had no history of abdominal surgery, inflammatory bowel disease, or other gastrointestinal disorders. Medications, alcohol, tobacco, and drug use were denied.

Vital signs: temperature 36.8  degreesC, blood pressure 131/65 mm Hg, heart rate 60 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination revealed a nondistended abdomen with normal bowel sounds in all quadrants and tenderness in the right lower quadrant without guarding or rebo

title: Diagnostic uncertainties in congenital syphilis: A case report
case_summary:
A 43-day-old male infant, born at 31 weeks of gestation (birth weight 2250 g), presented with 10 days of bilateral lower-limb swelling and 8 days of skin peeling on the lower limbs and buttocks. He was born after an uncomplicated antenatal course, exclusively breastfed, but failed to gain weight and had dropped to 1700 g. Initially evaluated for sepsis at a local hospital, he had a positive septic screen, dilated bowel loops on abdominal radiography, hemoglobin of 5.9 g/dL (transfused twice), and was treated with meropenem and vancomycin for presumed necrotizing enterocolitis with late-onset sepsis before transfer.

On arrival, he was afebrile. The lower limbs showed ichthyotic, desquamative skin changes with mild swelling. The liver edge was palpated 2 cm below the right costal margin. Laboratory testing revealed hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL, white-cell count 40,000/mm3 with neutrophil predominance, platelets 70,000/mm3, corrected reticulocyte count 8%, and a positive direct Coombs test. Perip

title: Drug-induced hepatitis after Sinopharm COVID-19 vaccination: A case study of a 62-year-old patient
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes mellitus presented with weakness, jaundice, pruritus, and weight loss that began three days after his second dose of the inactivated Sinopharm COVID-19 vaccine. He had no symptoms after the first dose and denied recent fever, cough, sore throat, or dyspnea. His medications included metformin, glibenclamide, and losartan. He had no history of alcohol use or immunodeficiency.

On examination, he was alert with yellow-tinged skin and scleral icterus. The abdomen was soft and nontender, with a normal liver span (10 cm) and no hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, or lymphadenopathy. Vital signs were: RR 30/min, HR 85/min, BP 140/90 mmHg, T 36.8  degreesC.

Abdominal ultrasonography showed mild hepatic parenchymal coarseness and wall thickening of the common bile duct; endoscopic ultrasonography and MRCP were normal. Laboratory tests revealed ALT 722 U/L, AST 435 U/L, ALP 512 U/L, total bilirubin 8.0 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL, negative ANA, ASM

title: Hemolytic anemia due to acute cytomegalovirus infection in an immunocompetent adult: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 44-year-old Caucasian man with no prior medical history was admitted for a 30-day history of fever and progressive asthenia. Fifteen days earlier he had been diagnosed in the emergency department with acute CMV infection (CMV IgM positive, CMV IgG negative, viremia 12 698 copies/mL). Initial labs showed ALT 47 U/L, AST 71 U/L, ALP 304 U/L, LDH 600 U/L, hemoglobin 11.3 g/dL, C-reactive protein 30.9 mg/L, and ESR 19 mm/h. No antiviral therapy was initiated. After discharge, fever and asthenia persisted. 

On admission, he was pale and asthenic; temperature 38  degreesC; heart rate 100 beats/min; and had moderate hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies revealed hemolytic anemia: RBC count 2.43 ? 10^6/mm^3, hemoglobin 7.9 g/dL, reticulocyte count 16.7%, LDH 778 mU/mL, total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL (indirect 2.0 mg/dL), and undetectable haptoglobin. Direct and indirect Coombs tests were negative; cold agglutinins and cryoglobulins were mildly positive. A total-body CT scan showed only moderate

title:
Disseminated focal 18F-fluoro-deoxyglucose uptake upon granulocyte
colony-stimulating factor therapy mimicking malignant bone infiltration: case report of a
patient with very severe aplastic anemia
case_summary: A 71-year-old man presented with dyspnea and fatigue that had worsened over 3 weeks. Laboratory tests showed severe pancytopenia with a leukocyte count of 2.4 G/L (neutrophils 0.3 G/L), hemoglobin 75 g/L, platelet count 7 G/L, and reticulocyte count 13 G/L. He had no history of novel drug intake or overt infections. An iliac crest bone marrow biopsy revealed a cellularity of 5% without clonal blasts. He was started on G-CSF (filgrastim 4 ug/kg/day) and received erythrocyte and platelet transfusions. Further work-up by histology, next-generation sequencing, and flow cytometry was inconspicuous for myelodysplastic syndrome, paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, or myeloproliferative disorders, supporting a diagnosis of very severe aplastic anemia. Computed tomography revealed an anterior mediastinal mass. For further evaluation, an [18F]FDG-PET/CT scan was performed, showing a moderately hypermetabolic mediastinal lesion (SUVmax 5.1) and multiple intensely FDG-avid focal skeletal lesions,

title: Primary Pancreatic Undifferentiated Pleomorphic Sarcoma
case_summary: A 48-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of nonradiating left lower quadrant abdominal pain that worsened with eating. He also reported low-grade fever, malaise, loose stools, and a 10-lb weight loss over 3 months. He denied nausea, vomiting, melena, jaundice, trauma, recent infections or travel, alcohol use, and NSAID use. His father had prostate and colon cancer. On examination, he was tachycardic but afebrile and normotensive; weight was 77 kg, BMI 22.4 kg/m?. Abdominal exam showed tenderness in the left lower quadrant without a palpable mass. Laboratory studies revealed neutrophilic leukocytosis (14.3 thou/mcL), microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, MCV 24.3 pg), an elevated HbA1C (7.1%), elevated alkaline phosphatase (245 U/L), elevated ALT (58 U/L), and a normal CA 19-9. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a 6.8 cm complex cystic mass in the tail of the pancreas with mild ductal dilatation. MRI demonstrated a 9.2 cm macrolobulated pancreatic mass without local e

title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency Associated with Hepatosplenic T-Cell Lymphoma Mimicking Juvenile Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
case_summary:
An 8-year-old girl with a history of recurrent upper respiratory infections, pneumonias with pleural effusions, and hypogammaglobulinemia was diagnosed with common variable immunodeficiency (IgG 268-497 mg/dL [NR 952-1538], IgA < 6 mg/dL [111-335], IgM 55-122 mg/dL [59-151], absent specific antibody responses). She received prophylactic antibiotics and monthly IVIG with a reduced infection rate but recurrent hospitalizations for septic shock, pneumonia, otomastoiditis, CMV infection, and UTIs.  

At age 12, she developed pancytopenia (Hb 10.2 g/dL, WBC 3790/mm?, platelets 108 000/mm?), hepatosplenomegaly, reticulocyte count 1.2%, and LDH 164 mg/dL. Two bone marrow aspirations showed erythroid hyperplasia and granulocyte hypoplasia. Autoantibodies including ANA, RF, anti-dsDNA, and antiphospholipid antibodies were negative.  

At age 17, she presented with fever, oral ulcers, alopecia, arthritis of wrists and elbows, headache, and cough. She developed pleural and large pericardial effus

title: Emergency surgery for tubo-ovarian abscess identified extended-spectrum beta-lactamase-producing Escherichia coli: the first case presentation revealing causative bacteria
case_summary: A 41-year-old woman presented with acute abdominal and back pain. Two days earlier, she had been treated at an outside hospital for a presumed urinary tract infection with systemic antibiotics, but her abdominal pain worsened and she developed high fever. She had no significant past medical history. On examination, her temperature was 40.0  degreesC; she had mild abdominal tenderness with rebound and marked pallor of the conjunctiva. Laboratory studies showed: Hb 7.3 g/dL, WBC 16,500/?L, platelets 18,000/?L, CRP 18.0 mg/dL, AST 146 IU/L, ALT 79 IU/L, LDH 899 IU/L, total protein 4.6 g/dL, albumin 2.0 g/dL, creatinine 3.1 mg/dL, prothrombin time 44.1 %, fibrin/fibrinogen degradation products 239.4 ?g/mL, D-dimer 75.1 ?g/mL, antithrombin III activity 53 %, base excess ?13.0 mmol/L, and lactate 12.9 mmol/L. Tumor markers and viral hepatitis serologies were normal. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed thick-walled multilocular cystic lesions of both ovaries without pelvic ascites. T

title: A unique case of hemolytic?uremic syndrome secondary to enteropathogenic E?Coli
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman with early-stage breast cancer presented with 2 days of weakness, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, and watery nonbloody diarrhea following a restaurant meal. In the emergency department, temperature was 37.4 degreesC, heart rate 156 beats/min, and blood pressure 95/63 mm Hg; she was alert and oriented. Laboratory studies showed WBC 21 430/?L, hemoglobin 14.8 g/dL, platelets 227 000/?L, potassium 3.5 mEq/L, lactate 8.68 mg/dL, creatinine 2.2 mg/dL, BUN 37 mg/dL, and creatine kinase 10 846 U/L. She was diagnosed with hypovolemic shock and acute kidney injury secondary to rhabdomyolysis, received aggressive IV fluids and empiric antibiotics, and was admitted to the ICU.

Over the next five days, she became anuric and transitioned from continuous renal replacement therapy to hemodialysis. She developed severe thrombocytopenia (nadir 50 ?10^3/?L) and anemia (hemoglobin nadir 5.0 g/dL). A peripheral blood smear showed numerous schistocytes and reticulocytosis, and laboratory te

title: Peripheral neuropathy in severe COVID?19 resolved with therapeutic plasma exchange
case_summary:
A 44-year-old previously healthy man presented in June 2020 with 12 days of fever (peak 38.3 degreesC), persistent cough, anosmia, diarrhea, myalgias, and progressive bilateral lower-limb weakness. He had unprotected contact with his SARS-CoV-2-positive brother one week before symptom onset.

On examination, temperature was 38.1 degreesC, other vital signs were within normal limits, and oxygen saturation was 78% on room air; high-flow nasal cannula (60 L/min, FiO2 40%) improved SpO2 to 89%. Neurologic examination revealed bilateral lower-limb muscle strength of 3/5, gait ataxia, and areflexia at the knees and ankles; sensation was intact. Lung auscultation showed bilateral crackles at the bases.

Emergency electromyography of the lower limbs demonstrated delayed distal latencies with normal conduction velocities. Upper limbs were not tested; no biopsy was performed. Laboratory studies showed lymphocytopenia (0.51 ? 10^9/L), elevated C-reactive protein (247 mg/L), lactate dehydrogenase

title: A diagnostic dilemma: distinguishing a sulfasalazine induced DRESS hypersensitivity syndrome from a CD30?+?lymphoma in a young patient
May 2020: Is It Always COVID-19 No Matter What?
case_summary:
A 23-year-old man, non-smoker with no prior medical history, presented in May 2020 with 3 days of fever (up to 38 degreesC), cough, and worsening shortness of breath. On examination he was febrile, working at a respiratory rate of 24 breaths/min, and had bilateral wheezing. He denied asthma, chronic sinusitis, nasal polyps, new drug use, vaping, occupational exposures, or second-hand smoke.  
Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (14.45?10^3/?L) with eosinophilia (1.8?10^3/?L; 12.5% of leukocytes), neutrophils 61.6%, lymphocytes 17.7%, monocytes 7.6%, basophils 0.6%. C-reactive protein was 1.53 mg/dL, procalcitonin negative, lactate dehydrogenase 384 U/L, interleukin-6 12.4 pg/mL, D-dimer 230 ng/mL. Arterial blood gas on 35% FiO2 showed pH 7.39, PaO2 59 mmHg, PaCO2 40 mmHg, HCO3 25 mmol/L (PaO2/FiO2 = 168).  
High-resolution chest CT demonstrated bilateral ground-glass opacities in the left upper lobe (lingular segment) and right lower lobe. Nasopharyngeal swabs for SARS-CoV-2 were ne

title: Pancreatic panniculitis: the "bright" side of the moon in solid cancer patients
case_summary:
A 77-year-old man presented with a one-month history of multiple, slightly painful nodules on his lower legs. His past medical history included hypertension, dyslipidemia, and a prior myocardial infarction; medications were aspirin, diltiazem, lovastatin, and valsartan. Two weeks after onset, the nodules increased in number and induration, and his general practitioner referred him to dermatology. A presumptive diagnosis of panniculitis was made, and prednisone 25 mg daily was prescribed without improvement.

On admission, he was afebrile, blood pressure was 160/70 mmHg, and the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Cutaneous examination revealed multiple, ill-defined, firm, erythematous-violaceous nodules, 2-4 cm in diameter, distributed on both lower legs; one nodule on the left leg showed superficial ulceration.

Laboratory studies showed:  
? Serum lipase 6027 U/L (normal 13-60) with normal amylase  
? Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (normocytic anemia)  
? Creatinine 1.26 mg/dL  
? ES

title: Sloughing Esophagitis: A Not So Common Entity
journal: International Journal of Biomedical Science : IJBS
case_summary: A 55-year-old woman with diabetes mellitus, seizures, and hypertension was admitted after a witnessed seizure and fall, during which she lay immobile on the floor for several hours. Her outpatient medications were valproic acid, aspirin, amlodipine, metformin, and a multivitamin. On examination she appeared frail; cardiac and neurologic findings were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed a creatine kinase level of 22 000 U/L, a serum creatinine level of 5 mg/dL, and a hemoglobin level of 9.8 g/dL. She was diagnosed with rhabdomyolysis, acute renal failure, anemia, and uncontrolled epilepsy, and was treated with intravenous hydration, with gradual improvement in renal and muscle markers. On hospital day 10, she developed dysphagia and odynophagia to solids and liquids, accompanied by upper chest pain and discomfort. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed plaque-like whitish material with linear streaking throughout the esophagus, without strictures, ulcers, or masses. Biopsy specimens sh

title: Prolonged suppressive antibiotic therapy for inferior vena cava filter infection following emphysematous pyelonephritis and cystitis: a case report
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man residing in a nursing home presented with decreased consciousness, tachypnea, hypoxia, and hypotension. His medical history included long?standing type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with dapagliflozin, bladder and bowel dysfunction after a thoracic spinal cord injury, chronic prednisolone therapy for pemphigoid, and placement of an inferior vena cava (IVC) filter 12 years earlier.

On admission, his temperature was 38.1  degreesC, pulse 100 beats/min, blood pressure 78/32 mm Hg, respiratory rate 42 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on a 10 L reservoir mask. He had glossoptosis and bilateral coarse crackles; the abdomen was soft without tenderness. Laboratory data showed a white blood cell count of 20 100/?L, C-reactive protein 22.3 mg/dL, and serum lactate 5.3 mmol/L. Urinalysis revealed pyuria and bacteriuria.

Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated intramural gas in the bladder wall and gas in the left renal pelvis and parenchyma. He was diagnosed with septic shock du

title: An atypical presentation of small bowel obstruction and perforation secondary to sporadic synchronous intra-abdominal desmoid tumours
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 54-year-old Nigerian man was brought to the emergency department in an acute confusional state. He reported a three-week history of colicky abdominal pain associated with intermittent vomiting, hiccupping, and polydipsia; he denied weight loss, hematemesis, or rectal bleeding. The day before presentation, he underwent oesophago-gastro-duodenoscopy, which showed gastritis and a gastric ulcer in the antrum and pre-pyloric region, with a large volume of retained gastric contents despite a 10-hour fast; biopsies were taken. His medical history was notable only for hypertension; he had no prior abdominal surgery or family history of fibromatoses or polyposis syndromes.

On examination, he was febrile, dehydrated, and disoriented, with no focal neurologic deficits. Chest and cardiovascular examinations were unremarkable. Abdominal examination revealed mild distention and tenderness without peritoneal signs or palpable masses; bowel sounds were scanty. Rectal examination was unremarkable.

title: Unforeseen Hodgkin Lymphoma Hidden behind Hypercalcemia
case_summary:
A 61-year-old woman with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, stroke, and hypothyroidism presented with altered mental status, nausea, multiple episodes of nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting, and dizziness. She reported a three-month history of intermittent night sweats, diffuse body aches, and 10 lb of unintentional weight loss. She denied fever, chills, respiratory symptoms, excessive dairy or antacid use, recent travel, or sick contacts. Her medications included vitamin D supplements, last taken three months earlier. 

On admission, vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination revealed dry oral mucosa and multiple painless, mobile, firm lymph nodes measuring 2-3 cm in the left anterior cervical region, right axilla, and right inguinal area. 

Initial laboratories showed: microcytic anemia, monocytosis, acute kidney injury with a prerenal pattern (BUN/creatinine ratio > 20), hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, and a serum calcium level of 16.5 mg/dL (corrected to 16.7 mg/dL). S

title: Revision Total Hip Arthroplasty due to Catastrophic Osteolysis Caused by Massive Chronic Expanding Hematoma
case_summary:
An 84-year-old woman with dysplastic osteoarthritis had undergone bilateral total hip arthroplasty 18 years earlier. Five years before presentation, she noted a gradually enlarging mass in her left medial thigh and began to experience discomfort in the left hip when walking. At evaluation she required a walker and had a large, palpable mass in the left medial thigh. 

Plain radiographs and CT showed a high-centered acetabular cup with extensive osteolysis around the acetabulum and ischium (Paprosky type 3A) and a catastrophic proximal femoral defect exposing the medial side of the stem (Paprosky type 4). MRI revealed a 25 ? 14 cm multilobulated soft-tissue mass with a thick-walled pseudocapsule, heterogeneous low-to-intermediate signal on T1-weighted images, low-to-high signal on T2-weighted images, and peripheral enhancement after gadolinium. CT angiography demonstrated no active arterial bleeding but displacement of the femoral artery and vein. Laboratory tests were notable only for

title: Early diagnosis of co-existent ?-thalassemia and alkaptonuria
case_summary:
A 1-year 5-month-old boy was brought for evaluation of reddish discoloration of his nappies and clothes, noted over the preceding weeks, and breath-holding spells. There was no foul odor to his skin or urine, no history of dysuria, poor urinary stream, bleeding, fever, rash, abdominal pain, constipation, or altered sensorium. The parents were first cousins; the mother had thalassemia trait. 

On examination, his weight was 8.5 kg, head circumference 44 cm, and length 81 cm. He was alert, with no jaundice or dehydration. Vital signs: SpO2 94% on room air, heart rate 146/min, respiratory rate 26/min, blood pressure 73/44 (mean 54) mm Hg. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams were unremarkable; abdomen soft, liver 1 cm below costal margin, no splenomegaly; normal reflexes, hypotonia, and no meningeal signs. 

Initial laboratory studies:  
- Urine routine examination and abdominal ultrasound were normal.  
- Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, microcytic hypochromic anemia: hematocrit 31.9%, MCV 68 fL, M

title: Pulmonary Kaposi Sarcoma: An Uncommon Cause of Respiratory Failure in the Era of Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy--Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 26-year-old African American man with AIDS presented with pleuritic chest pain, exertional dyspnea, and hemoptysis for 3 weeks. He reported a 10-pound weight loss over 6 months, drenching night sweats, and fever. He denied tobacco use, neurological, gastrointestinal, or dermatological symptoms, and had no recent travel. He was poorly compliant with dapsone and elvitegravir/cobicistat/emtricitabine/tenofovir. Three months earlier, he was admitted for similar complaints and diagnosed with atypical pneumonia; a CT scan then showed diffuse fine noncalcified nodular densities in both lungs and small to moderate bilateral axillary, mediastinal, and hilar adenopathy. Bronchoscopy was unrevealing, and bronchoalveolar lavage was negative for Pneumocystis jirovecii, acid-fast bacilli, and fungal infection.

On examination, he had bilateral axillary and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy and signs of right lower lung zone consolidation. There were no skin or mucosal lesions. Laboratory studies sho

title: HIV-related bilateral inflammatory myofibroblastic tumors of the adrenal gland: a case report and literature review
case_summary: A 44-year-old man with a 13-year history of untreated HIV infection presented with a 3-week history of persistent, dull bilateral back pain, fever, fatigue, and weight loss. He declined antiretroviral therapy and on admission had a CD4+ T-lymphocyte count of 6 cells/uL and an HIV RNA load of 10 001 391 copies/mL. Physical examination was notable only for costovertebral angle tenderness. Laboratory studies showed mild anemia (hemoglobin 103 g/L), elevated aspartate transaminase (79 U/L) and ?-glutamyltransferase (207 U/L), and hypoalbuminemia (30.7 g/L). There was no serologic or microbiologic evidence of cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, fungi, or tuberculosis. Renin, angiotensin, aldosterone, adrenocorticotropic hormone, cortisol, and catecholamine levels were within normal limits. Unenhanced abdominal computed tomography revealed two large (67 ? 38 mm on the left, 56 ? 58 mm on the right), heterogeneous, hypodense masses adjacent to both adrenal glands. Contrast enhancement was mo

title: Cutaneous tuberculous gummas in a patient with polymyositis*
case_summary:
A 33-year-old man with polymyositis on irregular methotrexate (15 mg weekly) and prednisone (40 mg daily) presented with a 6-month history of painful, warm subcutaneous nodules of various sizes and gummatous lesions developing over the previous 2 months. He had intermittent fevers responsive to metamizole, throbbing limb pain worse with movement and relieved by rest, and proximal muscle weakness of the pectoral and pelvic girdles. One year earlier he had completed 6 months of rifampicin, isoniazid, and pyrazinamide for pulmonary tuberculosis.

On admission he was febrile, tachycardic, and tachypneic; blood pressure was normal and no lymphadenopathy was noted. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (21 100 leukocytes/mm3), thrombocytosis (573 000 platelets/mm3), normocytic normochromic anemia, elevated lactate dehydrogenase (515 U/L), and mildly elevated creatine phosphokinase (282 IU/L). A chest radiograph was normal. During hospitalization leukocytosis and thrombocytosis persisted, LD

title: Pheochromocytoma-induced shock: a case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old woman with a history of systemic hypertension treated with atenolol presented with a 4-5 day history of progressively worsening headache, neck pain, severe anxiety, chest discomfort, nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, tachypnea, dry cough, and lightheadedness. She denied fever or urinary symptoms. Laboratory evaluation demonstrated leukocytosis (22,500 leukocytes/mm3) with neutrophilia. She was started on ceftriaxone for suspected meningitis.

On arrival, she was alert and oriented, with preserved motor function. Extremities were cold and poorly perfused. Vital signs were: BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 140 bpm, RR 42 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 85% on room air (96% with 5 L/min supplemental O2). Cardiac examination was normal. Pulmonary auscultation revealed diffuse inspiratory rales bilaterally. The abdomen was distended. There was no neck stiffness or focal neurologic deficit. Pupils were equal and reactive; plantar responses were flexor; deep tendon reflexes were normal.

title: Hepatosplenic T-cell lymphoma in a 47-year-old Crohn's disease patient on thiopurine monotherapy
case_summary:
A 47-year-old Caucasian man with a 33-year history of penetrating Crohn's disease (Montreal L2, B3+P) presented with two weeks of painless jaundice, unintentional weight loss, and malaise, without fever. His Crohn's disease had been in remission for five years on azathioprine (150-200 mg daily) following prior budesonide, mesalamine, and limited infliximab exposure; he had undergone right and left hemicolectomies and later a permanent ileostomy. 

On examination, he was afebrile, icteric, and had palpable hepatosplenomegaly without lymphadenopathy. 

Laboratory tests showed:  
? Hemoglobin 7.7 mmol/L (8.5-11.0)  
? White blood cell count 4.6?10^9/L (4.0-10.0)  
? Platelet count 42?10^9/L (150-400)  
? Bilirubin 175 ?mol/L (0-17)  
? Alkaline phosphatase 265 IU/L (0-125)  
? ?-Glutamyl transferase 154 IU/L (0-45)  
? Aspartate aminotransferase 250 IU/L (0-34)  
? Alanine aminotransferase 437 IU/L (0-44)  
? Lactate dehydrogenase 701 IU/L (0-247)  

Contrast CT and PET-CT confirmed hepat

title: Scurvy Masquerading as Septic Arthritis in a Case of Cerebral Palsy
case_summary:
A 4-year-old girl with cerebral palsy, psychomotor delay, and severe spastic paraplegia was admitted with a 3-day history of fever and bilateral knee pain. She was born preterm at 1600 g after fetal distress. Her feeding history initially consisted solely of milk products. On examination, she was pale, anxious with strangers, febrile (38.8  degreesC), pulse 124 bpm, blood pressure 100/64 mm Hg, respiratory rate 22/min. Oral hygiene was poor; skin was normal. One day after admission, she developed tense bruising and swelling around both knees with pain on palpation of the distal femoral metaphyses; passive motion was nearly complete. 

Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 9220/?L, hemoglobin 5 g/dL (microcytic hypochromic anemia), platelets 440,000/?L, ESR >140 mm/h, CRP 90 mg/L, and iron 7 ?mol/L; alkaline phosphatase, calcium, and phosphate were normal. 

Knee radiographs demonstrated osteopenia, a dense "white line" at the tibial metaphyses, metaphyseal rarefaction, periost

title: A rare case of dengue encephalopathy complicating a term pregnancy
case_summary:
A 28-year-old woman at 37 weeks' gestation presented with a 4-day history of fever. On day 5 she became irritable, somnolent, and aggressive, and subsequently unresponsive to commands. Examination revealed T 38.5  degreesC, anicteric sclerae, no pallor, and a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 9 (E2 V2 M5). Respiratory, cardiovascular, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. There was no neck stiffness; Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs were negative. Cranial nerves were intact, and there were no pyramidal or extrapyramidal signs. Fundoscopy showed no papilledema.

Laboratory studies on admission:
- White cell count 9,000 cells/mm3; hemoglobin 10 g/dL; mean corpuscular volume 85 fL; platelet count 60,000 cells/mm3, falling to 8,000 cells/mm3; hematocrit 33%.  
- ESR 20 mm/h; C-reactive protein 4 mg/dL; LDH < 200 U/L.  
- Peripheral blood film: thrombocytopenia without microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.  
- Urinalysis: no proteinuria or hematuria.  
- Aspartate aminotransferase 25 U/L risi

title: Pyoderma gangrenosum after totally implanted central venous access device insertion
case_summary: A 90-year-old man with known myelodysplastic syndrome with refractory anemia and myelofibrosis, transfusion- and thrombapheresis-dependent, underwent implantation of a right subclavian totally implanted central venous access device. On postoperative day 7, he developed dyspnea and fever to 38.6  degreesC and was admitted to the hospital. He had positive troponin levels, anterolateral ischemic changes on ECG, and severe anemia (hemoglobin 68 g/L) prompting a diagnosis of ischemic left ventricular dysfunction. Examination of the device insertion site revealed marked skin inflammation with a central necrotic ulcer and violaceous, undermined edges. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (11.8 ? 10^9/L) with neutrophilia and a C-reactive protein level of 175 mg/L. On postoperative day 8, the device was removed and cultures were obtained. The patient was started on cefepime and vancomycin, later switched to imipenem and teicoplanin because of persistent fever and progressive renal impairment

title: An Uncommon Complication of a Common Tropical Infection in a Kidney Transplant Recipient - A Case Report
case_summary: A 27-year-old man, 6 months after deceased-donor kidney transplantation for IgA nephropathy, presented with 3 days of high-grade fever, retro-orbital pain, headache, and myalgia. His brother, a household contact, had been diagnosed with dengue fever. He was on mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus (trough 7 ng/ml), and steroids; graft function had nadir creatinine 0.8 mg/dl. He denied abdominal pain, dysuria, jaundice, arthralgia, rash, or neurologic symptoms. On exam he was dehydrated, hypotensive (90/60 mmHg), tachycardic, and otherwise unremarkable. Initial labs showed pancytopenia (hemoglobin 10.4 g/dl, WBC 2000/mm3, platelets 39 000/mm3), acute graft dysfunction (creatinine 2.1 mg/dl), hyponatremia (Na 127 mEq/L), and ECG with sinus tachycardia. Abdominal ultrasound revealed mild splenomegaly. Intravenous fluids stabilized his blood pressure; tacrolimus dose was reduced, mycophenolate was held, and stress-dose steroids were given. Dengue NS1 antigen was positive; tests for leptospiros

title: Copper Deficiency Mimicking Myelodysplastic Syndrome: Zinc Supplementation in the Setting of COVID-19
case_summary:
A 48-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with progressive fatigue. Initial outpatient laboratory tests showed anemia and leukopenia, and he was directed to the emergency department. On arrival he was hemodynamically stable. Review of systems was notable for bilateral hand paresthesia previously attributed to cervical spondylosis. He reported decreased exercise tolerance, having gone from eight miles of bicycling per day to difficulty carrying groceries. Six months earlier he had recovered from COVID-19 infection; he denied other recent infections, family history of malignancy or autoimmune disease, and new medications, but endorsed taking multiple over-the-counter supplements of unknown composition.

Initial studies revealed a macrocytic anemia (hemoglobin, 6.7 g/dL; mean corpuscular volume, 107 fL), leukopenia (white blood cell count, 1.4 ? 10^3/?L; absolute neutrophil count, 490/?L), and thrombocytosis (platelets, 459 ? 10^3/?L). Iron studies showed low serum i

title: Cholestasis and Seizure Due to Lead Toxicity: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man presented with one month of constant, severe right upper-quadrant and periumbilical abdominal pain lasting 1-2 hours, postprandial nausea, vomiting, and eight days without defecation but with flatus passage. He had a 20-year history of raw opium use and had undergone cholecystectomy three weeks earlier for gallstones without relief of his pain. He reported chronic constipation for one year. He denied pruritus or changes in urine or stool color.
