On examination he was hemodynamically stable. His sclerae were mildly icteric, and there was bluish discoloration of the periodontal tissues (Burton's sign). The abdomen was distended with diminished bowel sounds and mild tenderness in the periumbilical and right upper-quadrant regions without rebound or guarding.

Laboratory studies showed normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, MCV 82 fL), normal leukocyte count, AST 100 IU/L, ALT 76 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 3100 IU/L, GGT 1057 IU/L, total bilirubin 3.5 mg/dL, direct bilir

title: Acute epiglottitis as the initial presentation of pediatric Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
case_summary:
A previously healthy 5-year-old girl presented with a one-day history of high fever (39 degreesC), sore throat, stridor, and shortness of breath. She was intubated for airway obstruction due to epiglottitis, and a lateral neck radiograph confirmed a grossly swollen epiglottis. Throat swab and blood cultures grew Haemophilus influenzae type f. 

On hospital day 2, she developed anemia (hemoglobin 63 g/L), thrombocytopenia (platelets 53,000/mm?), acute renal failure (creatinine 143 ?mol/L), hypertension (BP 123/80 mmHg), and evidence of hemolysis (positive direct antiglobulin test) with elevated APTT (41 sec) and D Dimer. Urinalysis showed microscopic hematuria, proteinuria (0.3 g/L), and casts. Supportive care included antibiotics and blood-product transfusions.

On day 7, she developed seizures associated with severe hypertension (BP 170/100 mmHg). CT of the brain showed multiple subcortical white-matter hypodensities bilaterally, and MRI demonstrated extensive T2 hyperintense changes

title: Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma: The Great Imitator
case_summary:
An 80-year-old man presented with one month of nonproductive cough, fatigue, low-grade fevers, a 2.5-kg weight loss, and a syncopal episode. His history included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, nephrolithiasis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and treated Lyme disease one year earlier. He had a remote 22 pack-year smoking history. Three months before admission he noted a tick bite treated with amoxicillin; his symptoms transiently improved but recurred, and he was prescribed doxycycline, which he did not complete.

On admission he was febrile (38.2  degreesC) with a platelet count of 66 000/mm3. During his stay at an outside hospital he developed progressive anemia and thrombocytopenia. CT scans of the head, chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no central nervous system lesions, lymphadenopathy, or hepatosplenomegaly; small bilateral pleural effusions were noted. Bone marrow biopsy was unrevealing, and flow cytometry showed no clonal cell population.

After transfer to the intensive care unit

title: Drug-Induced Lupus with Leukocytoclastic Vasculitis Associated with Apixaban
case_summary:
An 86-year-old man was admitted for generalized weakness and dyspnea on exertion after noting a new nonpruritic petechial rash on both feet. Fifteen days earlier, he had undergone placement of a WATCHMAN left atrial appendage occlusion device and was converted from rivaroxaban to apixaban and aspirin, and from flecainide to amiodarone. His medical history included diverticulosis with partial bowel resection, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation complicated by gastrointestinal bleeding, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage IIIb, major depressive disorder, and obstructive sleep apnea. Home medications also included trazodone, citalopram, sitagliptin, metoprolol, pravastatin, oxybutynin, and tamsulosin. He denied chemical exposures, insect bites, or prior allergic reactions.

On examination, he was afebrile, normotensive, with an irregularly irregular rhythm and no respiratory distress. He had a nonblanching, nonpainful petechial rash on the feet that later sprea

title: Disseminated Mycobacterium Avium Complex in an Adolescent with Perinatally-Acquired HIV Infection
case_summary:
A 13-year-old boy was admitted with three months of dry cough, intermittent low-grade fever, and severe anemia. He reported one year of fatigue and failure to gain weight. His history was notable for frequent upper respiratory infections but no serious illnesses or hospitalizations. He lived with his maternal grandparents; his mother died of hepatitis B complications, and there was no recent travel or sick contacts. 

On examination, his weight was at the 5th percentile and height at the 3rd percentile for age. He had oral thrush, bilateral submandibular and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, a protuberant abdomen with splenomegaly 10 cm below the left costal margin, and decreased muscle mass. 

Laboratory studies showed lymphopenia (500 cells/mm3), hemoglobin 3.9 g/dL with reticulocytopenia (reticulocyte count 2.8%), and positive HIV antibody by ELISA and Western blot. HIV-1 RNA was 8,510,000 copies/mL (log10 = 6.93) and CD4 count was 3 cells/uL (0.6%). An interferon-gamma release assay

title: A Rare Cause of Reversible Renal Hemosiderosis
case_summary:
A 54-year-old white man with a 10-year history of HIV infection (CD4 count 180 cells/uL), chronic hepatitis C, and a mechanical mitral valve on warfarin presented with 5 days of fatigue, nausea, and anorexia following 10 days of bloody diarrhea. He denied abdominal pain, oliguria, or gross hematuria. His antiretroviral regimen included atazanavir, emtricitabine, tenofovir, and ritonavir. He admitted occasionally taking extra warfarin. On examination, he was afebrile; supine blood pressure was 109/65 mmHg, falling to 95/55 mmHg on standing. Jugular venous pressure was low, and there was no edema, rash, or murmur. 

Laboratory studies revealed a rise in serum creatinine from 1.4 mg/dL (3 months earlier) to 19.9 mg/dL on admission. Urinalysis showed 5-30 RBCs per high-power field and hyaline casts but no granular or RBC casts; the urine albumin : creatinine ratio was 217 g/mol. Renal ultrasound showed normal-sized kidneys with mildly increased right renal parenchymal echogenicity. He rece

title: Deadly combination of Vaping-lnduced lung injury and Influenza: case report
case_summary: A 27?year?old African American man with a 2?year history of cigarette use and tetrahydrocannabinol vaping presented with two weeks of nonproductive cough, subjective fever, rhinorrhea, chills, myalgia, diarrhea, and vomiting. He had been seen two days earlier for presumed viral upper respiratory infection and discharged. On return, he was tachypneic and tachycardic with leukocytosis and lymphopenia. Chest radiography showed patchy infiltrates in the right upper lobe and bilateral lower lobes. A respiratory pathogen panel was positive for Influenza A, and sputum cultures later grew methicillin?sensitive Staphylococcus aureus; tests for other pathogens were negative. Admission laboratory values included a white blood cell count of 9.8 k/uL (92.4% neutrophils, 3.8% lymphocytes), platelet count of 97 k/uL, creatinine 2.34 mg/dL, AST 343 U/L, ALT 144 U/L, lactate 9.8 mmol/L, procalcitonin 26.86 ng/mL, and ferritin 5181 ng/mL. Bronchoscopy revealed copious airway secretions, a thick, sloughi

title: Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease Presenting as Expressive Aphasia and Nonconvulsive Status Epilepticus
case_summary:
A 60-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia presented with a four-week history of progressive speech difficulty--primarily word-finding problems with stuttering--and confusion, including failure to recognize family. Symptoms began with headache, fatigue, and vertigo, then evolved to intermittent confusion, slurred speech, and spasms of the right upper and lower extremities, causing a fall without loss of consciousness. He recently returned from a four-month stay in Mexico working in cattle manure. He had no prior neuropsychiatric history and no family history of dementia or seizures.

Ten days before admission, he had a normal neurologic exam, brain MRI, and carotid Doppler and was diagnosed with transient ischemic attack. On transfer to our emergency department, vital signs were stable; neurologic exam revealed persistent word-finding difficulty only. Noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. Initial CSF showed mildly elevated protein (65 mg/dL) but no clear evidence of infection.

title: Peritoneal Malignant Mesothelioma with Epithelioid Type, Demonstrating High Serum and Ascitic KL-6 Levels: Immunohistochemical Analyses
case_summary:
A 56-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of progressive abdominal distention. She had no abdominal pain, palpable mass, weight loss, respiratory symptoms, or constitutional complaints. Her medical, surgical, travel, family, and social histories were unremarkable; she had worked in an iron factory for 4 years without known asbestos exposure.

On examination, she was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. There was no lymphadenopathy, anemia, or jaundice. The abdomen was markedly distended, soft, and non-tender, with evidence of massive ascites but no palpable masses.

Laboratory studies showed thrombocytosis, hypoalbuminemia, and elevated C-reactive protein. The white-cell count, hemoglobin, renal function, and electrolytes were normal. Liver tests revealed an alkaline phosphatase level of 292 IU/L and lactate dehydrogenase of 159 IU/L; aminotransferases, ?-glutamyltransferase, and amylase were within normal limits. Serum cholesterol and triglycerides were normal. Tumor marker

title: Ectopic uterus? A rare presentation of a giant leiomyoma in the posterior mediastinum
case_summary:
A 17-year-old female student presented in March 2021 with 3 months of gradually progressive left-sided chest pain associated with cough, dyspnea, and chest tightness. The pain worsened when she lay on her left side. She denied hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, weight loss, exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, or prior tuberculosis exposure. Her medical history was unremarkable, and she did not smoke or drink alcohol.
On examination, she was comfortable, with a temperature of 36.4  degreesC, pulse rate 100 beats/min, blood pressure 116/79 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. The trachea was deviated to the right, and air entry was reduced over the left hemithorax. Cardiovascular and other systems were normal.
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, serum creatinine 72 ?mol/L, prothrombin time 11.3 s, INR 0.89, and normal electrolytes. A chest radiograph confirmed tracheal deviation to the right and a mass occupying the left hemithorax.
Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest revealed

title: Unexpected Primaquine?Induced Hemolysis in a G6PD?Normal Patient: A Case Report From Nepal
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man presented with a 7-day history of intermittent fever with evening spikes up to 103  degreesF, chills, rigors, fatigue, three episodes of vomiting over 4 days, and epigastric pain. He had no known medical conditions. On examination, he was alert; blood pressure was 110/60 mmHg, pulse 107 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, axillary temperature 99.8  degreesF, and SpO2 96% on room air. The abdomen was soft, with no palpable hepatosplenomegaly.

A rapid test confirmed Plasmodium vivax malaria. Serologies for typhoid, dengue, scrub typhus, brucella, Leptospira, hepatitis, and HIV were negative. An initial complete blood count showed hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, leukocytes 3370/mm3, and platelets 45,000/mm3. Abdominal ultrasound noted gallbladder calculi. He received intravenous paracetamol and pantoprazole, chloroquine (600 mg daily ? 2 days, then 300 mg on Day 3), and primaquine (15 mg daily for 14 days). Quantitative G6PD activity was 8.4 U/g Hb (normal range).  

On D

title: Pyolaryngocele: Case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 70-year-old man with lymphoma treated with chemotherapy and type 2 diabetes presented with left neck swelling, sore throat, exertional dyspnea, and low-grade fever for several days. He had no history of smoking, alcohol use, respiratory infections, allergies, or medication noncompliance. Examination showed left lateral neck swelling with normal overlying skin. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy revealed swelling over the left arytenoid and false vocal cord with reduced mobility and slight reduction in respiratory airflow; the true vocal cords were normal. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 23.5?10^9/L, hemoglobin of 10.9 g/dL, and C-reactive protein of 112 mg/L; other biochemistry and coagulation profiles were normal. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the neck demonstrated a 17?13?12 mm cystic lesion with fluid collection and an enhancing wall in the supraglottic region at the level of the thyroid cartilage, extending through the thyrohyoid membrane.

title: Primary gastric actinomycosis: report of a case diagnosed in a gastroscopic biopsy
case_summary:
An 87-year-old man with nonischemic cardiomyopathy, frequent premature ventricular contractions, chronic renal impairment, hypertension, and dyslipidemia presented with two days of diffuse abdominal pain, distension, constipation, and vomiting. He denied fever, prior abdominal surgery, or trauma. Home medications included low-dose aspirin, atorvastatin, irbesartan, and hydrochlorothiazide. 

On examination, vital signs were stable. The abdomen was distended with mild epigastric tenderness. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (16.6 ? 10^3/ul, 89% neutrophils), normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 11.5 g/dl), elevated serum lipase (1123 U/L) and amylase (269 U/L), serum creatinine 1.4 mg/dl, and potassium 3.1 mEq/L. 

A plain abdominal radiograph demonstrated marked gastric dilatation. A nasogastric tube yielded a large amount of greenish fluid. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a markedly distended stomach with thickened wall, abnormal configuration, and a single air

title: Trans-synaptic degeneration of the optic radiation from optic nerve atrophy
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman with no significant past medical history other than recurrent migraines presented with a 5-year history of progressive decreased vision in her left eye. She denied fever, altered mental status, or other neurologic symptoms, and she had no history of intravenous drug use or recent infections. She recalled a head injury at age 19 but was uncertain whether it involved orbital trauma. Her metabolic profile was within normal limits.

Optical coherence tomography showed thinning of the peripapillary nerve fiber layer, more pronounced on the left than the right, consistent with optic nerve atrophy. MRI of the orbits demonstrated a decreased caliber of the left optic nerve and increased T2 signal within the left perioptic nerve sheath. MRI of the brain revealed a T2-hyperintense signal along the course of the left optic radiations.

title: Fetal hypoxia secondary to severe maternal anemia as a causative link between blueberry muffin baby and erythroblastosis: a case report
case_summary:
A male neonate was born at 30 weeks' gestation by urgent cesarean delivery because of an abnormal nonstress test, oligohydramnios, and cerebral blood flow redistribution. The 25-year-old mother had developed severe anemia (hemoglobin 5.0 g/dL) from multiple episodes of vaginal bleeding beginning at 24 weeks' gestation; she received eight red?cell transfusions in total. Maternal screening for TORCH infections, parvovirus B19, hepatitis B and C, and syphilis was negative, and the indirect Coombs test was negative. Placental histology showed terminal villous hypotrophy, infarction, and intervillous fibrinoid deposition, consistent with chronic hypoxia.

At birth (weight 1530 g; Apgar scores 1 at 1 minute, improving to 8 by 20 minutes), the neonate required full ventilatory support (FiO? 100%). Umbilical pH was 6.97 with a base excess of -12.2 mmol/L. Physical examination revealed a red-violaceous, nonblanching macular rash on the head, neck, and trunk. During the first 24 hours, he develo

title: Cutaneous Macroglobulinosis Presenting as Serpiginous Purpura, a Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 78-year-old man with a history of type II diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and coronary artery disease was evaluated for two months of malaise. Laboratory studies showed normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL), hyperglobulinemia, hypercalcemia (corrected calcium 12.1 mg/dL), and elevated creatinine (1.88 mg/dL). Serum protein electrophoresis revealed a monoclonal IgM spike; immunofixation confirmed IgM kappa, and the serum IgM concentration exceeded 58.5 g/L. Bone marrow biopsy demonstrated 20-25% infiltration by a small B-cell neoplasm with plasma cell differentiation, consistent with lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma, establishing the diagnosis of Waldenstr?m macroglobulinemia.

Four days before presentation, he developed an asymptomatic, progressive erythematous rash on the abdomen and thighs. He had no symptoms of hyperviscosity and was untreated for his macroglobulinemia when the rash appeared. Examination revealed multiple linear and serpiginous nonblanchable erythematous patch

title: Heyde Syndrome: An Unusual Cause of Gastrointestinal Bleeding
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 73?year?old woman with end?stage renal disease on hemodialysis, chronic anemia, diabetes mellitus, and hypertension presented with a 1?day history of dark, tarry stools and abdominal cramping. She also noted new?onset mild dyspnea on exertion and significant fatigue over the same period. She had multiple prior admissions for similar melena requiring transfusion, most recently 2 months earlier. She denied abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits, NSAID use, fevers, weight loss, or bleeding elsewhere.

On examination, temperature was 98  degreesF, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, heart rate 100/min, and blood pressure 90/60 mm Hg. She appeared pale with cold extremities and weak peripheral pulses. A left upper?extremity arteriovenous fistula was present. Cardiac auscultation revealed a crescendo?decrescendo systolic murmur at the right sternal border radiating to the carotids. The abdomen was soft, nontender, and nondistended.

Laboratory results showed hemogl

title: An unusual cause of pericardial effusion: A case report of a hepatic abscess following foreign body migration and duodenal perforation
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 59-year-old woman with schizoaffective disorder, hepatitis C, prior substance use disorder, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus, residing in a tertiary mental health facility, underwent evaluation for asymptomatic hyponatremia. A CT scan of the chest and head demonstrated a small pericardial effusion. No further investigations were documented at that time.

Seven months later, she developed a fever. Chest radiography revealed enlargement of the pericardial silhouette, and transthoracic echocardiography confirmed a small-to-moderate pericardial effusion without an apparent cause. She denied chest or abdominal pain, dyspnea, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, appetite changes, recent illness, or infectious contacts. Her medications included antihypertensive, antihyperglycemic, and antipsychotic agents. She was a current smoker and had a remote history of cocaine, amphetamine, marijuana, and opioid use.

On examination, she was afebrile, normotensive, and not jaundiced. The abdomen w

title: Ossifying Renal Tumor of Infancy: Laparoscopic Treatment and Literature Review
case_summary: A 6-month-old boy presented with an episode of gross hematuria. The parents denied fever, trauma, or other symptoms. Renal ultrasound showed a right lower-pole, predominantly solid, well-defined lesion measuring 3.2?1.8?2.6 cm with multiple small cystic components and a 2.1 mm linear calcification with shadowing. MRI of the abdomen with and without contrast revealed a right lower-pole lesion measuring 2.8?2.5?1.8 cm with multiple T2 hypointense, nonenhancing cystic areas and mild enhancement of intervening septa. On examination, the abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender, with no palpable masses. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 10 800/uL, hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL, creatinine 0.44 mg/dL, urinalysis positive for blood, and a negative urine culture. Liver-function and electrolyte panels were normal, and the mass had no hormonal function.

title: Epstein-Barr Virus Associated Hemophagocytic Syndrome after Scrub Typhus Infection
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man was transferred for evaluation of persistent fever despite 7 days of doxycycline for scrub typhus, diagnosed by an axillary eschar and an indirect immunofluorescence antibody titer of 1:1,280. He had no prior illnesses or medications. He lived in a rural area and worked as a farmer. On admission, his temperature was 36.9 degreesC, pulse 72/min, respiratory rate 21/min, and blood pressure 97/54 mmHg. Examination revealed hepatosplenomegaly, a maculopapular rash on the abdomen, and a 1-cm eschar in the left axilla.  

Laboratory studies showed WBC 2,610/mm3 (41.7% neutrophils, 55.8% lymphocytes), hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 237,000/mm3, lactate dehydrogenase 1,500 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 675 U/L, AST 245 U/L, ALT 106 U/L, total protein 6.6 g/L, albumin 2.3 g/L, ?-glutamyl transpeptidase 444 U/L, total bilirubin 0.65 mg/dL, total cholesterol 61 mg/dL, ferritin 2,000 ng/mL, fibrinogen 70 mg/dL, triglycerides 138 mg/dL, CRP 85.2 mg/L, BUN 18.4 mg/dL, creatinine 0.67

title: Renal Intravascular Large B-cell Lymphoma: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of low-grade fever (up to 38.5 degreesC), weight loss, anorexia, fatigue, and night sweats. She had no history of travel, dental procedures, tick or animal exposures. On examination, temperature was 38.2 degreesC, blood pressure 100/55 mmHg, pulse 73/min, respiratory rate 13/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. The abdomen was soft and non-tender, with no palpable masses; there was no lymphadenopathy, skin rash, or neurologic deficit.

Laboratory results showed hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, hematocrit 28%, normocytic indices, white-cell count 6430/mm3 (67% neutrophils, 22% lymphocytes), platelets 256,000/mm3, and reticulocytes 56,000/mm3. C-reactive protein was 137 mg/L (normal <5), haptoglobin 4.65 g/L (normal 0.3-2), lactate dehydrogenase 533 U/L (normal 5-248), and ?2-microglobulin 4.56 mg/L (normal 1.2-2.5). Liver enzymes were elevated (AST 73 U/L, ALT 132 U/L). Serum creatinine was 72 umol/L (eGFR 77 mL/min/1.73 m2), and there wa

title: A Young Woman with Autosomal Dominant Hyper Immunoglobulin E (job's) Syndrome Presenting with Acute Abdomen: a Case Report
case_summary: A 25?year?old woman presented to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain. Her medical history was notable for recurrent pulmonary infections and a diagnosis of Job's syndrome since childhood; a parent had the same syndrome. On arrival, her temperature was 38.3  degreesC; other vital signs were normal. Physical examination revealed right?upper?quadrant tenderness, hepatomegaly, and a palpable mass; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 15.32 K/?l (range 4.0-11 K/?l) with 70.6% neutrophils and 11.90% eosinophils; hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelets were within normal limits. Electrolytes, liver enzymes, pregnancy test, and basic metabolic panel were normal. C-reactive protein was elevated at 11.30 mg/dL (normal <0.5 mg/dL). Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a large intrahepatic fluid collection with free intraperitoneal fluid and a smaller collection in the right paracolic gutter.

title: Case Report: Spontaneous Rupture of Inferior Epigastric Artery Masquerading as Inguinal Hernia
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 29-year-old male presents to the emergency department with progressive right groin pain and swelling for 24 hours. He reports symptom onset shortly after sexual intercourse. He notes increased tenderness and a persistent bulge in the right groin. He had a normal bowel movement one day before presentation and passed gas on the day of presentation. He denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fever, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, hematuria, flank pain, penile discharge, testicular pain, or skin lesions.

His medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications. He smokes cigarettes and drinks alcohol occasionally. He has no history of trauma.

Vital signs: temperature 37.1?C, pulse 102 beats/min, blood pressure 125/73 mm Hg, oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Physical examination is notable for a soft, nontender, nondistended abdomen with normoactive bowel sounds. There is a large, fixed, mildly tender mass in the right groin with no overlying skin changes and no fluctuance; t

title: A challenging case of granulomatosis with polyangiitis with cardiac involvement: a rare case report
case_summary:
A 15-year-old girl with a history of allergic rhinitis and mild intermittent asthma was referred after a chest CT showed diffuse bilateral cavitary lung lesions. Six months earlier, she had developed congestion and epistaxis treated as sinusitis. Over 8 weeks, she worsened with cough, dyspnea, intermittent fevers, migratory arthralgias, right otitis media, night sweats, weight loss, and small-volume hemoptysis despite multiple courses of antibiotics. On arrival, she was febrile (38.6  degreesC), tachycardic (122 bpm), tachypneic (26 breaths/min), and required 2 L/min of oxygen to maintain normal saturation. Examination revealed boggy nasal turbinates, dried blood in the nares, cobblestoning of the oropharynx, diffuse coarse breath sounds without egophony, and tenderness of the hips and knees without synovitis. 

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (17.3 k/mm3), thrombocytosis (698 k/mm3), anemia (hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL), elevated ESR (119 mm/hr), CRP (197 mg/L), ferritin (687 ng/mL), D

title: Coinfection with SARS-CoV-2 and Cytomegalovirus in a Patient with Mild COVID-19
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man presented with a fever persisting for approximately 2 weeks. Three weeks earlier, he had been diagnosed with COVID-19 by PCR testing of a nasopharyngeal swab. During the second week, he developed a low-grade fever while at home, which progressed to a high-grade fever above 39.0 degreesC, accompanied by mild headache, nausea, and anorexia. Two days before admission, he underwent chest CT, which was negative for pneumonia, and received acetaminophen. 

His medical history was notable for diabetes mellitus diagnosed 4-5 years earlier, which was untreated. He drank alcohol occasionally, did not smoke, and took no medications. He had received two doses of mRNA SARS-CoV-2 vaccine. He denied recent travel or animal exposures. His wife and daughter had COVID-19 before his illness.

On examination, he was alert and not in acute distress. Blood pressure was 118/83 mmHg, heart rate 84/min, axillary temperature 36.7 degreesC, and SpO2 97% on room air. There was no cervical lympha

title: Retroperitoneal fibrosis--the long and winding path
case_summary:
A 57-year-old Caucasian woman was referred for evaluation of worsening lower back pain over 3 months. She had a history of chronic mechanical back pain from her occupation as a driver and a remote lumbar injury after a motor vehicle accident 15 years earlier. She denied fevers, weight loss, or urinary symptoms.

On examination, she was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. There was no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Initial blood tests showed a mild normocytic anaemia (haemoglobin 114 g/L) and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate elevated to 71 mm/hr (baseline 33 mm/hr). Six months earlier, her serum creatinine was 52 ?mol/L with an eGFR of 90 mL/min/1.73 m2; at referral it had risen to 88 ?mol/L with an eGFR of 57 mL/min/1.73 m2.

Given the acute-on-chronic back pain and laboratory abnormalities, the initial differential included osteoporotic wedge fracture, an inflammatory rheumatological disorder, and haematological malignancy such as multiple myeloma. A bone scan showed no oste

title: Epoetin ? pegol (continuous erythropoietin receptor activator, CERA) is another choice for the treatment of anemia in myelodysplastic syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old Japanese woman with a history of refractory anemia due to low-risk myelodysplastic syndrome (Int-1) diagnosed in 2008 was referred in February 2015 for maintenance hemodialysis, which had been initiated in January 2015 for diabetic renal insufficiency. Before dialysis, she was transfusion-dependent, with hemoglobin maintained at 6.5 g/dL by red-cell transfusion every 7-10 days, and had developed transfusion-associated circulatory overload. On transfer, her serum erythropoietin level was low (127 mIU/mL), and she began epoetin ? 9000 IU/week plus darbepoetin ? (DPO) 40 ?g/week. Despite ten transfusions over the next four months, she remained anemic; in July 2015, the DPO dose was increased to 240 ?g/week. Two weeks later, her hemoglobin rose to >10 g/dL, and transfusions were discontinued.

From March 2016, she noted gradual worsening of her conjunctival pallor without fever, bleeding, or neurologic symptoms. Her average temperature was 36.3  degreesC, blood pressure 144/5

title: Olanzapine-Induced Diabetic Ketoacidosis: A Reversible Etiology Overlooked in Psychiatric Patients
case_summary:
A 73-year-old African-American woman presented with a 1-week history of confusion, polyuria, and polydipsia. Her medical history included type 2 diabetes mellitus without microvascular or macrovascular complications, hyperlipidemia, and severe depression with psychotic features. Three weeks before presentation, she had a nonhypoxic COVID-19 infection treated as an outpatient and a urinary tract infection treated with sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim. Her medications were olanzapine 5 mg daily, duloxetine 90 mg daily, and rosuvastatin 5 mg daily. 

On examination, blood pressure was 156/95 mmHg, heart rate 107 beats/min, respiratory rate 31 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. The mucous membranes were severely dry, and respirations were labored. There were no focal neurologic deficits or abdominal tenderness. 

Urinalysis was positive for ketones (2+) and glucose (4+). Initial laboratory results revealed: serum glucose 748 mg/dL, creatinine 2.14 mg/dL, potassium 5.4 mmol/L,

title: Native kidney BK nephropathy: A case report
case_summary:
A 67-year-old Caucasian man with a history of mantle cell lymphoma in remission after allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant 5 years earlier presented with progressive renal failure. His post-transplant course was complicated by hypogammaglobulinemia managed with IVIG, chronic cutaneous graft-versus-host disease manifesting as diffuse scleroderma (now limited to the lower extremities on ibrutinib), and recurrent respiratory infections. His current medications included mycophenolate mofetil, sirolimus, acyclovir, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and prednisone.

Over the past year, his creatinine rose from a baseline of 1.0 mg/dL to 4.84 mg/dL, accompanied by fatigue and worsening dyspnea. On admission, he appeared chronically ill; examination was notable only for skin thickening and mottling of the lower extremities. Laboratory studies revealed hyperkalemia (5.7 mmol/L), metabolic acidosis, moderate to large hemoglobin on urine dipstick with >50 RBCs per high-power field, and only

title: Pituitary abscess in an HIV-1-infected patient
case_summary: A 40-year-old Japanese man with a 9-year history of HIV-1 infection (CD4 T-cell count >500 cells/uL, plasma viral load ~1000 copies/mL, untreated) was admitted urgently with high fever, severe malaise, headache, and a diffuse tender skin rash. One month earlier he had mild sinusitis treated symptomatically. On examination: temperature 39.5 degreesC, blood pressure 84/- mmHg, pulse 110 beats/min, mild alteration of consciousness, slight neck stiffness, diminished skin turgor, and no focal neurologic deficits or visual disturbance. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (11 600/uL), elevated C-reactive protein (12.1 mg/L), hyperproteinemia (9.6 g/dL), creatinine 1.54 mg/dL, hyponatremia (116 mEq/L), and hyperkalemia (7.3 mEq/L). CT scans revealed moderate cervical lymphadenopathy. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis: protein 66.5 mg/dL, IgG 14.6 mg/dL, 56 leukocytes/uL (98.2% monocytes), and normal glucose. Dermatology diagnosed atypical generalized zoster; high-dose acyclovir led to rapid rash

title: A non-alcohol-related case of Madelung's disease: Challenging patient with progressive jugular vein distension
case_summary: A 48-year-old man presented with progressive neck swelling without fever or respiratory symptoms. He denied alcohol use or metabolic disorders. His medical history was notable for ?-thalassemia intermedia status post splenectomy and autoimmune hemolytic anemia requiring transfusions. On examination, he had bilateral nonpitting jugular?venous distention most evident in the supraclavicular regions and no lymphadenopathy or peripheral edema. Vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory evaluation showed hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, hematocrit 28.8 %, red?cell count 3.65?10^6/?L, RDW 23.2 %, total bilirubin 2.5 mg/dL (direct normal), LDH 397 mU/mL, and otherwise normal thyroid, lipid, renal, and liver panels. Neurologic examination was unremarkable. ECG and echocardiography showed no cardiovascular pathology. Doppler ultrasonography revealed mild compression of the right jugular vein without jugular, axillary, or subclavian thrombosis or lymphadenopathy. Chest radiographs were normal. Contras

title: The spectrum of cutaneous polyarteritis nodosa. A case report of two contrasting cases and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 34-year-old Filipino woman presented with a three-month history of multiple bilateral tender red nodules over the distal lower limbs. She felt well overall and denied fevers, weight loss, chills, or preceding infections. She had no history of tuberculosis exposure, animal contact, smoking, alcohol use, or intravenous drug use. Her past medical history was unremarkable, and she was not taking any medications or supplements.

On examination, there were tender erythematous subcutaneous nodules on the lower legs with angular purpura and bilateral ankle effusions; no other joints were inflamed. The abdomen was soft and non-tender.

Laboratory studies showed normal urinalysis, complete blood count, metabolic panel, and creatine kinase. Inflammatory markers were elevated (ESR 44?mm/hr, CRP 10?mg/dl). Testing for antinuclear antibody, extractable nuclear antigen, cryoglobulin, cryofibrinogen, rheumatoid factor, anticardiolipin antibody, ?-2 glycoprotein antibody, lupus anticoagulant, antineu

title: Case Report: Ziprasidone induced neuroleptic malignant syndrome
case_summary: A 78-year-old man was admitted with a urinary tract infection complicated by toxic metabolic encephalopathy. His medical history included parkinsonism--for which he took levodopa-carbidopa--and three-vessel coronary artery bypass grafting. During the first night of hospitalization, he became agitated and combative and received a total of 30 mg of intramuscular ziprasidone over 12 hours. The following morning, the nurse noted generalized muscular rigidity. On examination, his temperature was 107 degreesF, pulse 120 beats per minute, blood pressure 160/90 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. He had diaphoresis and severe rigidity involving flexor and extensor muscles of all limbs, the abdominal wall, back, neck, and jaw. He was unresponsive (Glasgow Coma Scale score, 3). Cardiovascular examination showed sinus tachycardia; chest auscultation revealed minimal mid-inspiratory crackles at the bases; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Emergent electrocardiography show

title: Incidental peritoneal loose body in a polytrauma patient: The unnoticed scenario: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man presented to the emergency department 12 hours after a road-traffic accident, reporting 4 hours of worsening abdominal pain, distension, nausea, and non-bilious vomiting. He also complained of left leg pain and difficulty walking. He had no prior abdominal surgeries or significant past medical history.

On examination, he was hypotensive (blood pressure 80/40 mmHg) and tachycardic (pulse 120/min). His abdomen was distended, with diffuse guarding, tenderness, and hypoactive bowel sounds. The left proximal leg was tender, but distal pulses were intact. Initial laboratory tests showed a hemoglobin level of 6.0 g/dL, a creatinine level of 2.6 mg/dL, and normal electrolytes.

He was diagnosed with hypovolemic shock due to intra-abdominal bleeding from blunt abdominal trauma and a left proximal tibia fracture. After resuscitation with crystalloids and whole blood, he underwent emergent laparotomy. Intraoperatively, approximately 3 L of hemoperitoneum and a grade IV splenic

title: Up in smoke: An unusual case of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage from marijuana
case_summary:
A 21-year-old man presented with acute onset of hemoptysis. The night before admission, he smoked more than five marijuana joints and then noted blood-streaked sputum that progressed to frank blood. In the emergency department, he was not in distress, vital signs were normal (BP 122/84 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air), and he was alert and oriented without oronasal bleeding. His respiratory examination revealed diffuse rhonchi; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. He reported two days of nasal congestion and headache but denied fever, chills, sweats, myalgia, sore throat, rash, or arthralgias. He smoked 2-5 non-synthetic marijuana joints daily and denied other illicit drug use or occupational exposures. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 16.4 g/dL, leukocytosis of 30.1?10^3/?L (83% neutrophils, which normalized within 24 h), normal platelets, electrolytes, renal and liver function tests, and normal coagulation studies. Urinalysis had 0-2 RBCs/hpf. Uri

title: Metformin-associated Encephalopathy in Hemodialysis
case_summary:
A 63-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a remote cerebrovascular accident with minimal residual weakness has been on twice-weekly maintenance hemodialysis for 2 years. He presented with a 1-month history of decreased speech output and difficulty walking, and over the preceding 2 weeks he developed progressive sensorium impairment, tiredness, drowsiness, and weakness. He had no fever, headache, or new focal neurological deficits. His last dialysis session was 2 days before admission.

On examination, he was drowsy and mute. Vital signs were: pulse 56/min, blood pressure 180/90 mm Hg, SpO? 99% on room air, afebrile, and he appeared anemic. Neurologic evaluation revealed mild right-sided limb weakness, asterixis, rigidity of all limbs, and bradykinesia.

Laboratory studies showed: blood urea nitrogen 19.63 mmol/L; serum creatinine 609 ?mol/L; sodium 138 mmol/L; potassium 4.8 mmol/L; hemoglobin 104 g/L; WBC count 4.9 ? 10?/mm?; platelets 90 ? 10?/mm?; serum ammon

title: Successful Control of Acute Myelofibrosis with Lenalidomide
case_summary:
A 59-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of anemia. Over the prior month, she had experienced progressive fatigue and malaise but denied fever or weight loss. Her medical history included mild mitral and aortic valve insufficiency and atrial fibrillation, managed with digitalis, an ACE inhibitor, and acenocoumarol (target INR, 2.5). 

On examination, she was pale and afebrile, with no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. 

Laboratory studies revealed:  
- Hemoglobin, 7.2 g/dL; mean corpuscular volume, 94.8 fL  
- White blood cell count, 3,400/uL (56% neutrophils, 38% lymphocytes, 5% monocytes, 1% eosinophils)  
- Platelet count, 756,000/uL; peripheral smear showed giant platelets without dysplastic features or leukoerythroblastic changes  
- Serum ferritin, 12? upper limit of normal; lactate dehydrogenase, 1.8? upper limit of normal; other chemistries within normal limits  

Bone marrow aspiration yielded a "dry tap." Core biopsy showed a hypercellular marrow with marked reticulin f

title: 10-year-old girl with life-threatening idiopathic systemic capillary leak syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 10-year-old girl, previously healthy, presented with a 1-day history of diffuse abdominal pain, watery diarrhea, and a temperature of 37.6  degreesC. She reported no rash or pruritus. On initial evaluation at a local clinic, she was hypotensive and tachycardic (heart rate 156 beats/min), with a tendency toward somnolence. Rapid infusion of crystalloid restored her blood pressure to 120/68 mmHg within 15 minutes, but within hours her systolic pressure again dropped to 40 mmHg, prompting transfer to our hospital.

On arrival, she remained somnolent and pale. Vital signs were: blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, heart rate 180 beats/min, temperature 38.1  degreesC. Her weight was 34.3 kg (baseline 34.0 kg). Initial laboratory studies revealed:
- WBC 41.0 ? 10^3/uL, hemoglobin 23.3 g/dL, hematocrit 64.4%
- Albumin 2.3 g/dL, BUN 42 mg/dL, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, CRP 3.24 mg/dL
- Arterial blood gas consistent with metabolic acidosis
- Urinalysis on admission: protein 100 mg/dL, blood 3+, RBC >100/HP

title: Adult-onset Kawasaki disease (mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome) and concurrent Coxsackievirus A4 infection: a case report
case_summary:
A 37-year-old previously healthy man was admitted 12 days after onset of fever and erythema of the hands, arms, and legs, with swollen, painful fingers. He had been treated empirically with cefcapene-pivoxil and loxoprofen without improvement. Seven days before transfer, he was hospitalized elsewhere for fever and erythema involving the neck, abdomen, back, and legs. Initial labs showed total bilirubin 3.58 mg/dL, AST 48 U/L, ALT 61 U/L, ?-GTP 116 U/L, BUN 45 mg/dL, and creatinine 3.45 mg/dL. On hospital day 2, he developed conjunctival congestion; on day 5, desquamation of the hands, fingers, and toes. 

On transfer, his temperature was 37.7 degreesC, blood pressure 136/84 mmHg, pulse 88 beats/min, respiratory rate 28 breaths/min. Physical examination revealed desquamation around the lips, fingers, and feet; edematous erythema on the trunk, extremities, axillae, flank, and hip; brawny edema of the hands and feet; marked conjunctival congestion; reddish, dry, swollen lips; and mildly r

title: Nicolau syndrome after lumbar puncture: A case report in a 22-month-old girl
case_summary: A 22-month-old girl with a history of failure to thrive presented with fever and a single generalized tonic-clonic seizure. She was found to have prerenal acute renal insufficiency, transaminitis, and anemia. A lumbar puncture was performed without difficulty after subcutaneous lidocaine infiltration. One day later, a retiform purpuric patch developed over the lumbosacral area at the puncture site. A workup, including biopsy and tissue culture, was performed to rule out other causes of large plaque retiform purpura, such as calciphylaxis, warfarin necrosis, and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia syndrome. Laboratory studies showed a creatinine level of 2.76 mg/dL, an aspartate aminotransferase level of 293 U/L, an alanine aminotransferase level of 111 U/L, a hemoglobin value of 6.4 g/dL, and a hematocrit value of 19.3%; values for platelet count, coagulation studies, antiphospholipid panel, protein C and S, and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were within normal limits. The tissue c

title: Tubercular Mycobacterial Spindle Cell Pseudotumour: A Case Report
case_summary: A 38-year-old man with AIDS (CD4 count 50 cells/mm^3) presented with a 2-month history of fever and weight loss, initially responsive to HAART but then complicated by recurrence of low-grade fever and development of generalized lymphadenopathy. Examination revealed multiple non-tender, mobile, firm cervical and inguinal lymph nodes up to 3 cm, pallor, and a BMI of 18 kg/m^2. Laboratory studies showed normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL) and mild neutropenia. Chest radiograph demonstrated miliary infiltrates in both lung fields. Excisional lymph-node biopsy showed effacement of nodal architecture by spindle-shaped histiocytes; Ziehl-Neelsen stain revealed numerous acid-fast bacilli. Immunohistochemical staining of the spindle cells was positive for CD68 and S-100 and negative for CD31. Polymerase chain reaction on the biopsy specimen was positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

title: Adult-Onset Still's Disease in an Elderly Patient Presenting as Aseptic Meningitis: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Medicine Insights. Arthritis and Musculoskeletal Disorders
case_summary:
A 78-year-old woman presented with one day of altered mental status characterized by irrelevant speech, increased talkativeness, and inability to recognize family members. She reported a two-week history of daily evening high-grade fevers associated with malaise, myalgia, and anorexia. She denied cough, abdominal pain, jaundice, diarrhea, dysuria, or rash. Her medical history included hypertension, bilateral knee osteoarthritis, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; her medications were losartan, furosemide-spironolactone, and as-needed salbutamol. She was a social drinker, non-smoker, used firewood for cooking, and had no recent travel.

On examination, her temperature was 98 degreesF, blood pressure 140/90?mm?Hg, pulse 108?beats/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and Glasgow Coma Scale score was 15/15. Neurologic examination was normal without meningeal signs. Laboratory tests showed anemia, neutrophilic leukocytosis, and elevated C-reactive protein; electrolytes were normal. A head CT

title: Mucinous Signet-Cell Adenocarcinoma of the Ileum: A Diagnostic Challenge--Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 68-year-old woman with a history of hysterectomy and a maternal history of Crohn's disease was transferred for recurrent severe lower abdominal pain. Four weeks earlier, she had presented with constant lower abdominal pain without diarrhea, intermittent constipation, and a 4-pound weight loss. She denied smoking, alcohol, or drug use. 

Initial abdominal CT showed abnormal wall thickening and fat stranding within the terminal ileum consistent with an infectious or inflammatory process; mesenteric lymph nodes in the right lower quadrant were mildly prominent, and neoplastic disease could not be ruled out. Colonoscopy revealed normal mucosa except for superficial linear erosions in the distal rectum, sigmoid diverticulosis, and an 8 mm ascending colon polyp. The terminal ileum appeared diffusely congested without ulcerations; biopsy specimens from the ileocecal valve showed no pathology. 

Laboratory studies demonstrated a hemoglobin of 12 g/L, hematocrit 37.5%, WBC 10.2 cells/mcL, pla

title: Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis secondary to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presenting with recurrent multi-territory infarcts: A case report
case_summary:
A 56-year-old Vietnamese woman presented with prolonged fever and fatigue. Initial laboratory evaluation showed severe anemia (hemoglobin 60 g/L), microcytosis (MCV 72.9 fL), WBC 9.65 G/L with 55% neutrophils and 20% monocytes, and platelets 193 G/L. HIV antibody, ANA, anti-dsDNA, rheumatoid factor, and Coombs tests were negative. Triglycerides were 565 mg/dL, ferritin 3154 ng/mL, soluble CD25 3317 U/mL, and LDH 592 U/L. EBV PCR was positive. Lumbar puncture yielded 14 WBC, 6 RBC, protein 32.6 mg/dL, and glucose 3.58 mmol/L; MTB PCR was negative. Bone marrow aspirate demonstrated red cell and platelet phagocytosis. Abdominopelvic CT scan revealed hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. Brain MRI showed subacute bilateral pontine infarction. Gastrointestinal endoscopy was unremarkable. The patient was diagnosed with HLH probably secondary to EBV infection and pontine infarction and was treated with dexamethasone and aspirin, resulting in partial improvement.

Three months later, she was admitted

title: Persistent Paradoxical Reaction to Midazolam despite General Anesthesia with Dexmedetomidine
case_summary:
A 63-year-old woman with obesity and a history of sleep apnea, hypothyroidism, hyperlipidemia, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, anxiety, depression, and tardive dyskinesia was admitted after a mechanical fall causing right type VI tibial and fibular fractures. Her medications included levothyroxine, atorvastatin, sertraline, quetiapine, hydroxyzine (Atarax), gabapentin, and benztropine (Cogentin). Preoperatively, she was alert and oriented, with chronic upper-extremity intention tremors.

Before urgent open reduction and internal fixation, she received 2 mg of IV midazolam for anxiolysis. As she entered the operating room, she became agitated, disoriented, and developed diffuse tremulousness with myoclonus. Due to the urgent nature of surgery, general anesthesia was induced with propofol, fentanyl, rocuronium, and a dexmedetomidine infusion. The 2.75-hour surgery proceeded without intraoperative desaturation or hypotension. After reversal with sugammadex, she was extubated.

In the postanesth

title: An incidental intra-operative finding of sclerosing encapsulating peritonitis, "abdominal cocoon" in an emergency presentation of small bowel obstruction, a case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 57-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of persistent nausea and vomiting, anorexia, constipation, and a 30-kg weight loss. Two months earlier, esophagogastroduodenoscopy had shown tiny erosions of the distal esophagus with a sliding hiatal hernia; diluted bile and retained food in the stomach; erythema, edema, and erosions in the gastric corpus and antrum; one small smooth protrusion along the greater curvature; and erythematous changes in the duodenum. His medical history was notable only for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with oral agents; he had no prior surgeries, allergies, transfusions, or other medications.

On examination, his pulse was 103 beats per minute, blood pressure 126/90 mm Hg, temperature 36.8  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, non-tender, and otherwise normal. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 9.2?10^3/mm^3, hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL, an elevated amylase level (140 U/L), elec

title: Surgical approach for appendicitis in neutropenia: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary: A 33-year-old Caucasian man presented with 2 days of fever, chills, extremity pain, sore throat, and right lower quadrant abdominal pain. He had Hodgkin's disease treated with BEACOPP in 2015 (currently in remission) and HIV infection (stage A2) on antiretroviral therapy with undetectable viral load. On examination, he was tachycardic, mildly hypotensive, and febrile (39  degreesC). Abdominal palpation elicited tenderness in the right lower quadrant without peritoneal signs. The oropharynx showed enlarged, stippled tonsils and mildly enlarged right cervical lymph nodes. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 117 g/L [140-180 g/L], leukocyte count 1.0 ? 10^9/L [4-10 ? 10^9/L], absolute neutrophil count 0.0 ? 10^9/L [1.4-8.0 ? 10^9/L], and C-reactive protein 281 mg/L [< 8 mg/L]. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck, thorax, and abdomen demonstrated appendicitis with a phlegmonous inflammatory reaction surrounding the appendix, without abscess or appendicolith. There was no imaging evidence o

title: Graft lymphoma in a kidney transplant recipient: a case report
case_summary:
A 37-year-old woman was admitted in September 2020 with a sudden onset of fever to 39  degreesC and mild discomfort over her transplanted kidney. She had end-stage renal disease from IgA nephropathy and received a deceased-donor kidney transplant at age 29, with induction basiliximab and maintenance tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisolone. Three years posttransplant, recurrent IgA nephropathy was confirmed on biopsy. The graft subsequently failed, and she initiated peritoneal dialysis in April 2020, at which time tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisolone were tapered. Two months later she developed intermittent gross hematuria and worsening anemia (hemoglobin 6.3 g/dL from a baseline of 9.7 g/dL), which transiently improved with increased immunosuppression but recurred with tapering. 

On examination at admission, she was febrile (39  degreesC) and had tenderness over the graft site without lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies showed WBC 4.9?10^3/?L,

title: Unilateral renal mucormycosis in a patient presenting with pyelonephritis and acute kidney failure: A case report
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man presented with fever, chills, renal colic, and urinary obstruction. Five years earlier, he was diagnosed with cystinuria and multiple bilateral kidney stones, including staghorn calculi, which rendered his right kidney atrophic. Despite seven percutaneous nephrolithotomies (PCNLs), two extracorporeal shock-wave lithotripsies, and one transurethral lithotripsy, he progressed to chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine, 3.0 mg/dL) and had multiple hospitalizations for presumed pyelonephritis treated with intravenous antibiotics, antifungals, double-J stents, and nephrostomy tubes without lasting benefit.

Thirty-five days before the current admission, PCNL drained pus from the left renal pelvis; histology showed acute inflammation without evidence of malignancy. An abdominopelvic CT scan demonstrated multiple bilateral renal calculi, an atrophic right kidney (79 mm), and severe hydronephrosis of the left kidney (143 mm).

On presentation (day 0), temperature was 38.4

title: Post-transfusion purpura in an African-American man due to human platelet antigen-5b alloantibody: a case report
case_summary:
A 68-year-old African-American man was admitted for atrial flutter and started on anticoagulation. On hospital day 4, he had hematemesis and was transferred to the ICU. His medical history was notable for a packed red-cell transfusion in 2002. On admission, his hemoglobin was 13.6 g/dL, platelet count 265 ? 10^9/L, prothrombin time 17.6 seconds, and aPTT 30.4 seconds. In the ICU he completed antibiotic courses for pneumonia and C. difficile colitis, and received six units of PRBC and two units of plasma on day 4, six units of PRBC on day 5, and additional plasma on days 13 and 14. He improved and was transferred to the ward on day 21.

On day 25, his platelet count began to fall. By day 29, it was 51 ? 10^9/L and he developed hematuria; he was afebrile and without signs of infection. On day 30, the platelet count was 31 ? 10^9/L, coagulation studies were normal, and a peripheral smear showed large platelets with decreased overall numbers. The differential diagnosis included drug-relate

title: Diclofenac-induced thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura with concomitant complement dysregulation: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 35-year-old Hispanic man presented with 1 week of generalized malaise, abdominal pain, fatigue, subjective fever, sore throat, joint pain, watery diarrhea, oral-intake intolerance, and a petechial skin rash. The rash began as blisters on his palms and soles, progressing to non-confluent erythematous macules with burning and itching that spread to his arms, chest, and trunk. He had 2-3 episodes of diarrhea daily, which had resolved by presentation. His only medication was diclofenac potassium, 100 mg twice daily for back pain over the past week; he denied other medications, tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. Family and psychosocial histories were unremarkable.

On examination, temperature was 39.6  degreesC, pulse 132 beats/min, blood pressure 132/77 mmHg, and respiratory rate 18/min. He appeared well groomed. Skin showed non-confluent, non-blanching erythematous macules. Head, eyes, ears, nose, and throat examination was normal. Lungs were clear, heart sounds were normal without

title: ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction in the Presence of Septic Shock
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman with insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (noncompliant) and recurrent urinary tract infections presented with a 3-day history of right lower-quadrant and right flank dull abdominal pain, fever, and dysuria. She had been lethargic, prompting ED evaluation. She denied medications or allergies, reported a 30-pack-year smoking history, and no alcohol or drug use.  

On arrival, she was minimally responsive and obtunded, requiring intubation. Initial vital signs: blood pressure 89/55 mmHg, temperature 97.8  degreesF, heart rate 107 bpm, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 89% on room air. Physical exam showed an intubated, minimally responsive woman with equal, reactive pupils.  

Laboratory studies revealed WBC 18.48 ?10^3/?L, BUN 73 mg/dL, creatinine 3.36 mg/dL. Blood and urine cultures were later positive for Escherichia coli. A noncontrast CT of the abdomen/pelvis showed mild right hydroureter and hydronephrosis without obstruction, plus perinephric in

title: Imipenem-cilastatin-induced psychosis: a case report
case_summary: A 64-year-old Hispanic man with traumatic paraplegia and a neurogenic bladder presented with fever and a urinary tract infection. He had recurrent UTIs but no psychiatric history. On admission he was febrile (100.7  degreesF), hypotensive (93/56 mm Hg), tachycardic (147 beats/min), and tachypneic (28 breaths/min); examination revealed labored breathing, suprapubic tenderness, and purulent urine. He was intubated for cardiorespiratory deterioration. Initial laboratory studies showed a WBC count of 16,500/uL (75 % neutrophils, 12.5 % lymphocytes, 8.4 % bands), serum creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (eGFR 25 mL/min/1.73 m?), and BUN 52 mg/dL; other biochemistry tests were within normal limits. Two sets of blood cultures and a urine culture grew Klebsiella pneumoniae. A renally adjusted dose of imipenem-cilastatin was started given a history of ESBL Klebsiella UTIs. Sedatives were discontinued and he was successfully extubated; he was fully oriented (place, person, time, situation) and had a negative

title: Crimean?Congo hemorrhagic fever in the COVID?19 pandemic: A case study
case_summary:
A 41-year-old previously healthy Persian man presented with a 7-day history of fever, myalgia, and malaise and a 2-day history of gastrointestinal bleeding manifested as coffee-ground vomitus and melena. Fourteen days before presentation, he had traveled to Karbala, Iraq. He was evaluated twice at other centers for presumed COVID-19 infection because of fever and myalgia but did not improve and had not developed GI bleeding at those times.

On examination at our hospital, he was alert. Blood pressure was 120/75 mmHg, pulse 100/min, respiratory rate 17/min, temperature 38.5? degreesC, and oxygen saturation 93% on room air. The sclera were not icteric. There were no respiratory symptoms. Abdominal examination revealed mild right upper-quadrant tenderness without hepatosplenomegaly. Lung computed tomography and abdominopelvic sonography were normal.

Laboratory studies on admission showed: WBC 4,700/ul, hemoglobin 15.0?g/dl, platelets 45,000/ul, prothrombin time 14.2?sec, partial thrombop

title: Noncaseating suppurative granulomatous lymphadenitis in adult onset Still's disease - a diagnostic dilemma in a tuberculosis-endemic region: a case report
case_summary:
A 67-year-old Sri Lankan woman was referred for evaluation of an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) found during workup of a febrile illness; she was asymptomatic on admission. She had a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, bronchial asthma, and bilateral knee osteoarthritis, managed with rosuvastatin, hydrochlorothiazide, glucosamine, and low-dose aspirin. Two years earlier, she had a biopsy of painful cervical lymphadenopathy showing noncaseating granulomatous inflammation; atypical mycobacteria, fungal infection, and tuberculosis were considered, and she completed 6 months of category 1 anti-tuberculous therapy.

Current evaluation revealed mild normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL), lymphocyte-predominant leukocytes without lymphoproliferative features, mild eosinophilia, and an ESR of 102 mm/hr with normal C-reactive protein. Liver and renal tests were normal. Chest radiography, sputum for acid-fast bacilli, urine AFB, and urine cultures were negative; serum protein

title: Fatal outcome of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) in a lupus nephropathy patient: A case report
case_summary:
A 14-year-old girl with a history of biopsy-proven lupus nephropathy maintained on mycophenolic acid (500 mg/day) and prednisolone (40 mg/day) presented with a 3-day history of headache and her first generalized seizure. She had also received two doses of an mRNA SARS-CoV-2 vaccine. On examination, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 12/15. Her blood pressure was 190/117 mm Hg, heart rate 150 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. She had generalized edema and a urinary tract infection but no meningeal signs. Metabolic causes were eliminated.

Laboratory studies revealed:
  ? Creatinine 18.47 mg/L  
  ? Potassium 5.6 mmol/L  
  ? ESR 116 mm/h  
  ? Hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL  
  ? WBC count 28.12 ? 10^3/mm^3  
  ? CRP 58 mg/L  
  ? Procalcitonin 5.9 ng/mL  
  ? ANA positive  

A noncontrast CT scan of the brain demonstrated subtle bilateral, grossly symmetrical cortical and subcortical hypodensities in the posterior parietal lobes, which remained unchanged after contrast administratio

title: Spontaneous Subdiaphragmatic Hemorrhage From an Aneurysm of Inferior Phrenic Artery
case_summary:
A 50-year-old man presented with a 1-day history of worsening epigastric and left upper quadrant abdominal pain. He noted unintentional weight loss of 20 lb over 6 weeks but denied pancreatitis, trauma, surgery, fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, or bowel or urinary symptoms. His medical history included hypertension, untreated hepatitis C infection, intravenous drug use, and smoking; he denied recent alcohol use. On examination, he was afebrile, heart rate 92/min, blood pressure 150/113 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Breath sounds were diminished at the left lung base. He had tenderness in the epigastrium and left upper quadrant without peritoneal signs. 

Laboratory studies showed: WBC 18,700/mm?, hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL (baseline 14.1 g/dL), platelets 222,000/mm?, PT 14 seconds, INR 1.2; electrolytes and renal function were normal. Total bilirubin was 0.7 mg/dL; ALT 120 U/L; AST 80 U/L; ALP 71 U/L. Troponin I was 0.014 ng/mL; procalcitonin 0.11 ng/

title: Young Woman with Unexplained Neutropenia and Neutrophils with Bilobed Nuclei: Marrow Findings
case_summary:
A 27-year-old woman with chronic sinusitis was referred for evaluation of asymptomatic neutropenia. She denied weight loss, fevers, infections, recent travel, or new medications. She had no family history of hematologic disorders. Medications included fluticasone nasal spray, and she had recently discontinued an oral contraceptive after 7 years.  
Routine labs showed a white blood cell count of 3300/?L (normal 4000-10 500/?L) and an absolute neutrophil count of 1500/?L (normal 1500-6500/?L). Review of the prior four years revealed persistently normal counts. On evaluation, her WBC count was 3600/?L, hemoglobin 15.7 g/dL, platelet count 219 000/?L, and ANC 1100/?L, which later normalized. The liver and spleen were not palpable. Peripheral-blood flow cytometry was normal.  
A peripheral-blood smear demonstrated numerous neutrophils with bilobed "pince-nez" nuclei consistent with Pelger-Huet-type morphology; other cell lines appeared normal.

title: Cutaneous tuberculosis: An infrequent manifestation of a common pathogen in South Africa
journal: Southern African Journal of Infectious Diseases
case_summary:
A 38-year-old woman with advanced HIV disease (antiretroviral treatment experienced with treatment interruption) presented with a 3-month history of subcutaneous swellings over her arms, legs, buttocks and perianal area. These swellings began as hyperpigmented patches, progressed to form subcutaneous collections, and eventually developed spontaneous draining sinuses with purulent discharge. She reported weight loss, anorexia and night sweats. Her history was notable for three prior episodes of drug-sensitive tuberculosis, all treated to completion, and a recent surgical admission for presumed lower-limb cellulitis complicated by soft-tissue collections; incision and drainage had yielded no bacterial pathogen on routine culture, and she received amoxicillin-clavulanate without improvement.

On transfer to medicine, she was febrile and tachycardic, cachectic with pallor and had generalized lymphadenopathy. Cutaneous examination revealed multiple hyperpigmented, fluctuant subcutaneous nod

title: Severe Sepsis with Multiorgan Failure due to Melioidosis: A Lesson to Learn
case_summary:
A 54-year-old previously healthy farmer from rural Sri Lanka presented with 10 days of fever with chills and rigors and painful right leg swelling after a cut injury. Twelve days earlier, he sustained a laceration on his right leg while walking in a paddy field; the wound became painful with purulent discharge and was associated with a tender right inguinal lymph node, loss of appetite, and malaise. He was an occasional alcohol consumer and a nonsmoker.  

Initially, he received oral antibiotics from a general practitioner. He then was hospitalized locally for five days and treated for presumed wound sepsis with intravenous cefotaxime and flucloxacillin, with incision and drainage--without clinical improvement.  

On transfer, he was febrile. Examination showed a superficial purulent wound on the anterior right leg (5 cm proximal to the ankle), surrounding cellulitis, and tender right inguinal lymphadenopathy; cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal exams were otherwise normal.

title: Phlebosclerotic colitis in a non-Asian patient: a case report
case_summary: A 62-year-old Spanish man presented with 3 days of epigastric pain that became diffuse, accompanied by constipation but no fever or vomiting. He had a 32-pack-year smoking history, past alcohol use (completed disulfiram 10 years earlier), residual lower-limb pain after lumbar surgery treated with naproxen, and a known mild factor XII deficiency. On examination, his abdomen was rigid with lividity and peritonism; there were no signs of deep-vein thrombosis. Vital signs: blood pressure 112/88 mmHg, heart rate 122/min, temperature 35.8 ?. Laboratory tests showed glucose 123 mg/dL, urea 238 mg/dL, creatinine 4.29 mg/dL (previously normal), total bilirubin 1.45 mg/dL (conjugated 0.91 mg/dL), amylase 111 mg/dL; electrolytes and blood count were normal. A thoracoabdominal CT scan suggested intestinal perforation in the upper hemiabdomen, but the perforation site was not identified. He received IV fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics and underwent urgent laparotomy, which revealed generalized

title: Case report--An unusual diagnosis of a gastric phytobezoar in a middle?aged woman
case_summary:
A 44-year-old woman was evaluated for a 6-week history of productive cough with white sputum, without fever, night sweats, or hemoptysis. During this period she had weight loss, poor appetite, intermittent vomiting, and persistent epigastric pain that worsened a few days before admission. The pain was aggravated by eating and lasted minutes to hours. Her medical history was notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus of 19 years' duration managed with twice-daily Actraphane insulin, sputum?positive pulmonary tuberculosis 9 years earlier, and chronic gastritis with prior Helicobacter pylori infection treated by triple therapy.

On examination she was cachectic and mildly pale. Temperature was 36.6  degreesC, blood pressure 140/90 mm Hg, pulse 80 bpm, and her capillary blood glucose was 11.7 mmol/L. Lung examination revealed a dull percussion note over the left lower zone. Abdominal examination showed epigastric tenderness without palpable masses.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 11.6 ? 10^9/L, h

title: Nonsecretory Multiple Myeloma Presenting as Recurrent Vertebral Compression Fractures in an Older Male Patient
journal: Annals of geriatric medicine and research
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with dyslipidemia presented with a 5-month history of recurrent vertebral compression fractures and back pain. Five months earlier, he developed acute back pain while changing his clothes and was treated for T11 and L3 compression fractures. Two months later, after slipping down stairs, he sustained L2 and L4 compression fractures. Five days before referral, he experienced back pain while sneezing and had difficulty walking.

Spine bone densitometry at prior visits showed osteopenia. Lumbar MRI revealed multiple compression fractures with inconspicuous retropulsion of the cortex into the canal. T-L spine X-rays showed degenerative changes and compression fractures at T12 and L1-L3; pelvic X-rays confirmed osteopenia. A bone scan demonstrated an L1 compression fracture, post-traumatic rib changes, and degenerative changes in T9-L5 and both hips.

Laboratory tests revealed normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL), a normal serum calcium level (8.9 mg/dL), a

title: Non-healing painful ulcers in a patient with chronic kidney disease and role of sodium thiosulfate: a case report
case_summary: A 60-year-old Caucasian woman with stage 5 chronic kidney disease presented with a 7-day history of heaviness and pain in the medial aspect of her left thigh. She denied fever, chills, or rigors. Her medical history included coronary artery disease, hypertension, well-controlled type II diabetes mellitus (HbA1C 5.6% six months earlier), and chronic kidney disease stage 5 (GFR 12 mL/min/1.73 m?). Medications were pioglitazone, furosemide, atenolol, lovastatin, lisinopril, isosorbide mononitrate, and metolazone. On examination, there was tenderness of the left thigh without edema or erythema; peripheral pulses were palpable bilaterally. A Doppler ultrasound of the lower extremities was negative for deep vein thrombosis. Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 13.1?10?/mm?, hemoglobin 8.6 g/dL, BUN 52 mmol/L, creatinine 4.7 mmol/L (baseline ?5.0 mmol/L), calcium 10.4 mg/dL, albumin 2.2 g/dL, phosphorus 8.0 mg/dL, and PTH 2138 pg/mL (1381 pg/mL six months earlier). On hospital day 5,

title: A rare cause of bowel obstruction in pregnancy
case_summary:
A 30-year-old primigravid woman at 38 weeks and 3 days of gestation presented with 24 hours of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She continued to pass flatus and had a bowel movement the previous day. Her history was notable for polycystic ovarian syndrome and a right donor hepatectomy performed 6 years earlier. She took no regular medications and had no allergies.

On examination she appeared uncomfortable. Her abdomen was tympanitic and generally tender but soft, with scant bowel sounds. Vital signs were normal, and fundal height was appropriate. A chevron scar extended to the xiphoid. Urinalysis was negative for infection, pelvic examination was unremarkable, and cardiotocography showed a reassuring fetal trace. Rectal examination revealed an empty vault without masses and no occult blood. Laboratory studies showed a high-normal white-cell count of 16 ? 10^9/L with mild neutrophilia; hemoglobin, electrolytes, renal and liver function tests, and lactate were within normal limits.

title: TAFRO syndrome with abdominal pain as the first symptom accompanied by liver damage with hyperbilirubinemia: A case report
case_summary:
A 61-year-old Chinese man presented with a 2-week history of poor appetite, lassitude, gastric distention, and dark yellow urine. He had no prior medical or surgical history but reported 20 years of alcohol use (20-40 g/day). In March, he underwent gastroscopy showing chronic superficial gastritis with Helicobacter pylori positivity and colonoscopy with polypectomy; all labs were normal. He received his first COVID-19 vaccine dose on June 11, 2021. On June 15, he developed chills and yellow urine; three days later he experienced intermittent upper abdominal pain radiating to the right back, nausea, fatigue, and anorexia.  
At an outside hospital, laboratory tests revealed a ten-fold elevation of total bilirubin, and he was diagnosed with cholecystitis, biliary tract infection, and suspected drug-induced liver injury. Despite anti-infective and hepatoprotective therapy, his jaundice worsened, bilirubin rose further, coagulopathy developed, renal function declined, and hypoalbuminemia wi

title: Development of Fulminant Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus in a Patient with DRESS Syndrome
case_summary:
A 39-year-old African American woman with chronic focal epilepsy presented with three days of fevers (>38 degreesC) and a mildly pruritic, erythematous maculopapular rash. The rash began two weeks earlier on her left arm and gradually spread to the trunk, face, oropharynx, periorbital area, and legs. Her medications included long-term carbamazepine, a tapering course of lamotrigine started one year ago, and a recent course of amoxicillin initiated for unilateral neck lymphadenopathy and exudative pharyngitis.  

Vital signs were notable for fever; skin examination showed a diffuse maculopapular eruption with periocular edema. Initial laboratory studies revealed a neutrophil count of 10.47?K/uL, mild eosinophilia (6-8%), and mildly elevated transaminases (AST 48 IU/L; ALT 42 IU/L).  

Dermatology consultation raised an initial differential of acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP) secondary to amoxicillin versus drug-induced hypersensitivity syndrome (DIHS) from lamotrigine o

title: Cutaneous eruption associated with Streptococcus dysgalactiae (group C Streptococcus) bacteremia
case_summary:
A 48-year-old man with Down syndrome and gout presented with a 2-day history of a new, pruritic, nontender rash that began on his buttocks and spread to his thighs. He denied new exposures, travel, or contactants; indomethacin had been started 3 weeks earlier. One week before the rash, he had a productive cough; a chest radiograph was normal. 

On examination, there were symmetric, well-demarcated, erythematous plaques on the buttocks and bilateral thighs without pain or rubor. Drug reaction was less likely given the lack of truncal involvement, and contact dermatitis was initially favored; he was treated with emollients, triamcinolone 0.1% ointment, and hydroxyzine. 

Punch biopsy of a thigh lesion showed dermal edema and a mixed inflammatory infiltrate extending to the mid-dermis, composed of lymphocytes, neutrophils, and rare eosinophils, interpreted as most consistent with a hypersensitivity reaction. 

Two days later, he returned to the emergency department with worsening rash, fe

title: Septic shock secondary to acute bacterial prostatitis in an HIV-positive male: a novel presentation
case_summary:
A 44-year-old man with nephrolithiasis and well-controlled HIV presented with 5 days of left flank and lower abdominal pain radiating into the left testicle, achy to sharp in nature. He also reported painful ejaculation, nausea, vomiting, decreased appetite, subjective fever, and chills. He noted that over the prior week he began using a prostate vibrator for the first time.  
On arrival: temperature 101.2  degreesF, blood pressure 92/54 mmHg, respiratory rate 21 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air. He appeared ill, diaphoretic, and in distress. Oral mucosa was dry. Cardiovascular: sinus tachycardia without murmur. Lungs: clear. Abdominal: tenderness at the left costovertebral angle, left upper and lower quadrants with voluntary guarding. Genitourinary: tenderness along the left inguinal canal and left epididymis; no abscess, cellulitis, or crepitus.  
Laboratory studies: white-cell count 18.2?10^3/mm^3 (neutrophil predominant), hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, platelets 220?10^3/mm^3. BUN 27 mg/dL

title: Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis Complicated by Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome and Multiorgan Failure
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 74-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis status-post colectomy and hypertension initially presented with fatigue, myalgia, and confusion. Her temperature was 100 degreesF, blood pressure 108/60 mmHg, heart rate 106 bpm, and respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute. She was disoriented but had normal heart sounds and decreased breath sounds at the lung bases without crackles. Laboratory tests showed hyponatremia (sodium 128 mEq/L), creatinine 1.21 mg/dL, lactate 3.0 mEq/L, and a urinalysis suggestive of urinary tract infection. CBC revealed WBC 8.8 k/cmm, hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL, and platelets 131 k/cmm. CT of the brain and chest radiograph were unremarkable. She received IV fluids and ceftriaxone, improved, and was discharged after 3 days.

Two days later she returned with fever (103 degreesF), heart rate 115 bpm, blood pressure 90/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, and normal mentation. Labs showed WBC 3.31 k/cmm, hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, platelets 60 k/cmm, lactic acid 5.4 m

title: A Primary Hepatic Lymphoma Treated with Liver Resection and Chemotherapy
case_summary:
A 57-year-old woman presented with a 5-month history of upper abdominal discomfort, nausea, fatigue, intermittent low-grade fever, and weight loss. Four months earlier, gastroscopy had revealed Helicobacter pylori infection, for which she received treatment. She had no other significant medical history. 

On examination, there was a large, tender epigastric mass with significant hepatomegaly; no splenomegaly or peripheral lymphadenopathy was noted. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, white-cell count 13.9?10^3/uL, aspartate aminotransferase 47 IU (normal <31), alanine aminotransferase 22 IU (normal <34), alkaline phosphatase 134 IU (normal <120), ?-glutamyl transpeptidase 147 IU (normal <38), and lactate dehydrogenase 555 IU (normal <247). Serologic tests for hepatitis B and C and for HIV were negative. Tumor markers revealed CA 19-9 58.1 IU/mL (normal <27), CA 15-3 31.5 IU/mL (normal <5), and CA 125 88.9 IU/mL (normal <35); ?-fetoprotein and carcinoembryonic antigen were

title: An Elderly Patient Developed Ulcerative Colitis after SARS-CoV-2 mRNA Vaccination: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
An 86-year-old man presented with a 2-week history of diarrhea and bloody stool that began two weeks after his third dose of SARS-CoV-2 mRNA-1273 vaccine. His medical history was notable only for mild hypertension and hyperuricemia treated with febuxostat; there had been no changes in medications for years and no use of as-needed drugs. Nine years earlier, colonoscopy had been normal.

At the first evaluation, colonoscopy showed continuous coarse, erythematous mucosa with loss of vascular markings extending from the ascending colon to the rectum. Given the recent onset, he was managed with probiotics. Ten days later, he was admitted because of worsening symptoms.

On admission, laboratory tests revealed a white blood cell count of 23 390/uL, hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL, total protein 8.2 g/dL, albumin 3.1 g/dL, blood urea nitrogen 40.7 mg/dL, creatinine 1.24 mg/dL, and C-reactive protein 25.1 mg/dL. Stool cultures were negative for pathogens. Sigmoidoscopy demonstrated marked erythema and los

title: How Harmful Can Herbal Remedies Be? A Case of Severe Acute Tubulointerstitial Nephritis
case_summary:
