A 60-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of decreased urine output and pedal edema. He reported that urine output declined over several days, progressing to complete anuria within 2-3 days. He denied fever, rash, dysuria, gross hematuria, or abdominal pain. He has a 3-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, treated with metformin-glimepiride and atenolol-amlodipine, respectively. He denied NSAID or over-the-counter drug use but had taken one tablet daily of a proprietary Ayurvedic formulation ("hyponidd") for 7 days before symptom onset. 

On examination, temperature was normal; pulse 90/min; blood pressure 170/100 mmHg in all four limbs; bilateral pitting pedal edema was present. Systemic and funduscopic examinations were unremarkable. 

Laboratory studies showed serum urea 177 mg/dL and creatinine 9.1 mg/dL. Urinalysis revealed numerous red blood cells and white blood cells, no casts, and 2+ protein on dipstick; urine output was <10 mL/day. Renal ultrasono

title: Successful use of montelukast in eosinophilic gastroenteritis: a case report and a literature review
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, and hypertension presented with several weeks of cramping abdominal pain, vomiting, and frequent watery, nonbloody diarrhea. The pain began as generalized cramping and later localized to the epigastrium without radiation; there were no alleviating or aggravating factors. She denied dysphagia, odynophagia, fever, chills, rashes, joint pains, recent travel, sick contacts, or antibiotic use. She had been admitted twice previously for similar symptoms; a prior CT scan showed fluid?filled bowels and colonic wall thickening, and she received empirical antibiotics without improvement.

On examination, she appeared uncomfortable. Blood pressure was 146/93 mmHg, pulse 88/min, temperature 36.5  degreesC. The abdomen was tender, worst in the epigastrium, without rebound or distention; bowel sounds were normal, and there was no organomegaly. Other systems were unremarkable except for xanthelasmas on the eyelids.

Laboratory studies showed a W

title: Rapidly progressive organizing pneumonia associated with COVID-19
case_summary:
A 70-year-old woman with no prior medical history and no smoking history presented with a one-week history of cough, dyspnea, anorexia, and fever. She was diagnosed with COVID-19 by PCR from a nasopharyngeal swab and admitted for treatment.  
On admission: temperature 37.9  degreesC; blood pressure 136/67 mmHg; heart rate 88 beats/min; respiratory rate 29 breaths/min; SpO? 95% on 5 L/min oxygen via mask.  
Laboratory studies showed elevated C-reactive protein, lactate dehydrogenase, and ferritin; normal procalcitonin, D-dimer, KL-6, and SP-D; and lymphopenia with eosinopenia.  
Chest CT on admission demonstrated bilateral ground-glass opacities with superimposed intralobular reticulations in a crazy-paving pattern. There were no findings of pulmonary embolism, purulent sputum, infiltrative shadows, or signs of bacterial infection, and no clinical evidence of heart failure (no lower leg edema).  
She was treated with favipiravir, ciclesonide, and nafamostat. Her fever, cough, dyspnea, a

title: Hydrocele in a case of atypical Kawasaki disease: case report and review of diagnostic criteria
case_summary:
An 18-month-old boy presented with 4 days of fever (?38  degreesC), vomiting, cough, and nasal congestion. He had a history of a urinary tract infection at 4 months and a febrile seizure at 9 months. On admission, vital signs were age-appropriate and physical examination was unremarkable apart from nasal congestion. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 294.4 mg/L, a white blood cell count of 14.92?10^9/L, and pyuria on urinalysis. He was started on intravenous ceftriaxone (50 mg/kg/day) after catheterized urine culture grew pan-sensitive Escherichia coli. Blood cultures remained sterile.

Despite optimization of ceftriaxone to 100 mg/kg/day on day 7, he continued to have daily fevers reaching 41.3  degreesC. Renal ultrasound on day 6 showed left focal pyelonephritis; repeat ultrasound on day 11 revealed hypoechoic foci in the left kidney consistent with small abscesses (largest 0.8 cm). Repeat urine cultures on days 9 and 16 were sterile. Nasopharyngeal aspirate on d

title: Unraveling Omenn syndrome in a newborn: A case report
case_summary: A term male newborn, first child of nonconsanguineous parents, was born by cesarean delivery at 40 weeks' gestation after an uneventful pregnancy and discharged on exclusive breastfeeding. At 15 days of age, he developed diffuse erythroderma with desquamation; emollient therapy produced no improvement. At 24 days, he presented with fever and was admitted. On examination, he weighed 3850 g, had diffuse erythroderma, desquamation, alopecia, and absence of eyelashes and eyebrows. Abdominal examination disclosed a soft abdomen, a liver palpable 1 cm below the costal margin, and no palpable spleen. Laboratory tests showed a C-reactive protein level of 68 mg/L, leukocytosis of 28 800/mm? with lymphocytosis (20 448/mm?) and eosinophilia (288/mm?), hemoglobin of 12 g/dL, and thrombocytopenia (88 000/mm?). Serum electrolytes were normal. Chest radiography was unremarkable, with a visible thymus. He was treated with a third-generation cephalosporin and an aminoglycoside for suspected postnatal i

title: Severe lupus pneumonitis: A case of life?saving multimodal therapy with rituximab and corticosteroids
case_summary:
A 24-year-old Aboriginal woman with class IV lupus nephritis on hydroxychloroquine, mycophenolate mofetil, and low-dose prednisone presented with 3 days of fever, productive cough, and worsening dyspnea. Her history included rheumatic heart disease, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and triple-positive lupus antiphospholipid antibodies without prior thromboses. On examination, her respiratory rate was 28/min, temperature 38.6  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 88% on room air (improving to 92% with supplemental oxygen). Auscultation revealed bibasilar crackles and a pansystolic murmur.  
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 63 g/L, white cell count 6.8 ? 10^9/L, platelets 91 ? 10^9/L, creatinine 146 ?mol/L, ANA positive with dsDNA > 667 IU/mL, and low C3 (0.32 g/L) and C4 (0.04 g/L).  
Chest radiography and high-resolution CT demonstrated bilateral pulmonary infiltrates with upper-lobe predominance. A CT pulmonary angiogram excluded pulmonary emboli but revealed extensive ground-glass opaciti

title: Acquired pure red cell aplasia after severe acute respiratory syndrome corona virus 2 infection: a case report
case_summary:
A 77-year-old Arab man with hypertension and no prior anemia was admitted in November 2020 for COVID-19 pneumonia, treated supportively, and discharged ten days later. Two months after recovery, he presented with progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, palpitations, and tinnitus. He denied bleeding, fever, or bone pain. On examination, heart rate was 110 bpm, temperature 36.2  degreesC, respiratory rate 17/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. He was pale without jaundice, had no lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly, and cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Chest radiograph was clear. Laboratory studies showed:  
  ? Hemoglobin 50 g/L, MCV 84.8 fl, MCHC 34.2%  
  ? Reticulocyte count 9 300/ul  
  ? White blood cells 3 400/ul, platelets 202 000/ul  
  ? Peripheral smear unremarkable  
  ? Ferritin 836 ng/ml, C-reactive protein 10.65 mg/L  
  ? Total and direct bilirubin normal; lactate dehydrogenase normal  
  ? Renal function normal; vitamin B12 and folate n

title: Bilateral macular hemorrhage in a patient with COVID-19
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary: A 46-year-old North-African man with well-controlled hypertension and hereditary spherocytosis presented after 12 days of fever, cough, and progressive dyspnea when he developed visual loss in his left eye. He was admitted to the ICU for respiratory failure and unilateral visual impairment. Chest radiograph showed bilateral peripheral airspace opacities, and SARS-CoV-2 infection was confirmed by RT-PCR; bacterial co-infection was excluded. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 6.2 g/dL, normal platelet and leukocyte counts, total bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL (mostly indirect), lactate dehydrogenase 690 U/L (normal 230-460), and haptoglobin 244 mg/dL (normal 34-200). Peripheral blood smear demonstrated erythrocyte agglutinates (confirmed with direct antiglobulin test), spherocytes, polychromasia, and circulating erythroblasts; G6PD levels were normal. Ophthalmic evaluation showed visual acuity 20/20 in the right eye and 20/70 in the left. Fundus examination revealed bilateral retinal hemorrhage

title: Giant non-parasitic splenic cyst: a case report
case_summary:
A 14-year-old Iranian girl presented with 7 months of vague, progressive left upper-quadrant abdominal pain. One month before evaluation she also noted left shoulder pain, a sense of fullness, early satiety, and shortness of breath. Initially, she awoke with acute severe left shoulder pain, followed three days later by hypochondrial pain; primary workup then revealed only microcytic anemia (hemoglobin, 10.3 g/dL; mean corpuscular volume, 63.6 fL), and she received analgesics and folic acid. After three more monthly visits with worsening pain and no additional diagnostics, she sought reevaluation.

She denied nausea, vomiting, jaundice, fever, or bowel or urinary symptoms. She lived in the countryside and reported ingestion of unwashed fruit. On examination, her abdomen was asymmetrically distended with a large, nontender mass in the left hypochondrium; vital signs were normal. There was no past medical history. A basic metabolic panel was within normal limits.

Ultrasonography showed a

title: Primary Tuberculosis of Buccal and Labial Mucosa: Literature Review and a Rare Case Report of a Public Health Menace
case_summary:
A 43-year-old immunocompetent man of low socioeconomic status was referred for evaluation of persistent, nonhealing ulcers on the lower lip and buccal mucosa lasting 6 months. Initially painless, the ulcers became tender over the last 3 months. He denied weight loss, fever, hemoptysis, or systemic medication use. He had a remote history of occasional tobacco chewing but quit over a year ago. Previous treatments, including chlorhexidine and metronidazole gels, topical corticosteroid paste, and multiple oral antibiotics, provided no improvement.

On examination, two oval ulcers with slightly raised borders and a yellowish-gray pseudomembrane were noted: one 1.5 ? 1 cm lesion on the left lower labial mucosa and another 1 ? 0.8 cm lesion on the right buccal mucosa near the labial commissure. The bases were granular and mildly indurated, with mild tenderness on palpation. No cervical lymphadenopathy or other mucocutaneous lesions were present.

Considering chronic nonhealing ulcers recalcit

title: Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis syndrome in an adolescent female: a case report
case_summary:
A 14-year-old white girl was referred to nephrology in November 2018 for evaluation of right-eye inflammation and abnormal urinalysis. She had a history of obesity, impaired glucose tolerance, hyperlipidemia, and borderline hypertension managed with lifestyle modifications. Family history was positive for hypertension but negative for metabolic syndrome. She denied any recent drug exposures, allergies, infections, or systemic symptoms.

One week before presentation, she developed redness, pain, tearing, and decreased vision in the right eye. Ophthalmologic examination led to a diagnosis of acute anterior uveitis, treated with topical corticosteroids and cycloplegics, with symptomatic improvement.

On admission, her pulse was 100 beats/min, blood pressure 126/81 mmHg, and temperature 36.5  degreesC. Review of systems was otherwise unremarkable, and right-eye symptoms were improving.

Investigations:
- Renal ultrasound: normal  
- Chest radiograph: normal  
- Laboratory results: ESR 98 m

title: PD-1 Checkpoint Inhibitor Associated Autoimmune Encephalitis
case_summary:
A 78-year-old man with squamous cell carcinoma of the lung received nivolumab (3 mg/kg every 2 weeks) as palliative second-line therapy after disease progression. After 2 months of treatment, tumor regression was confirmed. His medical history included hypertension, chronic renal failure, COPD, and a residual intracranial epidermoid tumor in the left temporal lobe, stable on serial brain MRIs. He had occasional focal seizures with right-hand tonic convulsions but had been seizure-free for 3.5 years on lamotrigine. Other medications were bisoprolol, lisinopril, clorazepate, and baclofen.

Twelve days after the 14th nivolumab infusion (28 weeks after initiation), he presented with 24 hours of progressive apathy and aphasia. On examination, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 7 (eye opening to pain, withdrawal from pain, no verbal response), with preexisting left facial paresis, no meningism, normal strength, a left Babinski sign, and recurrent myoclonus in all extremities. He was afebrile.

title: Recurrent cutaneous eosinophilic vasculitis characterized by annular purpuric lesions: A case report
case_summary:
A 71-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a 1-month history of an intensely pruritic eruption that appeared on her palms, soles, lips, and hard palate one day after a left retinal vein occlusion. She denied fever or constitutional symptoms, though she had unintentionally lost 5 kg over the preceding months. Her medical history included Parkinson's disease, glaucoma, and age-related macular degeneration. Current medications were carbidopa/levodopa, clonazepam, vitamin D, and ocular brimonidine and tafluprost/timolol.

On examination, there were multiple annular purpuric patches and plaques on the palms, soles, lips, and hard palate; the remainder of the systemic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed a peripheral eosinophil count of 2.0 ? 10^3/uL (normal < 0.5), mild megaloblastic anemia (hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL; mean corpuscular volume 96 fL) with folate deficiency, hyperhomocysteinemia (26 umol/L; normal 4-14), and mild hyperglycemia (103 mg/dL; normal 70-99). Autoimm

title: Exudative pharyngitis and Corynebacterium pseudodiphtheriticum: A case report and review of the literature
journal: Southern African Journal of Infectious Diseases
case_summary:
A 14-month-old, previously well boy presented to a district hospital in the Western Cape in December 2016 with respiratory distress related to upper airway obstruction. He was moderately acutely malnourished and newly diagnosed with HIV on admission. The child had a short history of fever, dysphagia, and lethargy. There was a household contact with pulmonary tuberculosis, but no chemoprophylaxis had been given. He had no travel history. His vaccination status was incomplete, with only birth vaccinations (BCG and oral polio vaccine) administered.
On examination, he was in shock and had respiratory distress with drooling, forward posturing, grunting, and pulsus paradoxus. Oropharyngeal examination revealed an inflamed and oedematous uvula and tonsils with a thick, white, friable membrane adherent to the oropharynx that bled on touch. There was no neck swelling, cervical lymphadenopathy, cranial nerve palsies, or muscle weakness. Chest examination was unremarkable.
Initial management incl

title: Intimal sarcoma of the aortic valve and ascending aorta: a case report
case_summary: A 69-year-old woman presented with chest distress and reduced exercise tolerance for one month. She had no significant past medical history. Laboratory tests showed elevated interleukin-6 (7.3 pg/mL; normal 0-6.4), lactate dehydrogenase (342 U/L; 120-250), high-sensitivity troponin I (556 pg/mL; 0-11.6), and brain natriuretic peptide (1,551 pg/mL; 0-100), with mild anemia (hemoglobin 105 g/L; red blood cell count 3.6?10^12/L). C-reactive protein, anti-streptolysin O, and rheumatoid factor were normal. The patient denied fever or upper respiratory symptoms. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed severe aortic regurgitation and a 9.8?6.6 mm irregular, oscillating, soft-textured echogenic mass on the left coronary leaflet of the aortic valve, along with severe mitral regurgitation, mild tricuspid regurgitation, and an ejection fraction of 47%. Contrast-enhanced CT angiography showed a 6 mm low-density area in the aortic root extending into the proximal ascending aorta. Nonbacterial throm

title: Atypical case of hantavirus infection in Sri Lanka mimicking leptospirosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 36-year-old Sri Lankan Sinhalese man working as a paddy farmer presented with a 3-day history of high-spiking fever, chills, severe arthralgia and myalgia, and lower-limb muscle cramps. One day before admission he developed resting dyspnea with a nonproductive cough and was anuric for 12 hours. He had no significant past medical history, occasional alcohol use, and no tobacco use. 

On examination he was febrile (39.5  degreesC), hypotensive (80/40 mm Hg), tachycardic (140/min), tachypneic (32/min), and dehydrated, with warm peripheries and marked orthostatic changes. He had mild icterus, injected conjunctivae, bilateral coarse pulmonary crepitations, and tender hepatomegaly without splenomegaly. He was alert and oriented.

Laboratory studies on admission showed: leukocyte count 24.6 ? 10^9/L (90% neutrophils), platelet count 86 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 14.5 g/dL; peripheral smear with neutrophil leukocytosis, toxic neutrophils, myelocytes, atypical lymphocytes, and thrombocytopenia. Liv

title: Presumed antiphospholipid syndrome and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura: An infrequent association
case_summary:
A 27-year-old African-American woman with a history of systemic lupus erythematosus complicated by lupus nephritis (diagnosed at age 13 via kidney biopsy), asthma, two miscarriages at 10 weeks' gestation, antiphospholipid syndrome, heart failure (October 2017), and ischemic stroke (December 2017) was admitted to the intensive care unit with a 48-hour history of shortness of breath and 2 weeks of progressive lower-extremity swelling. She also noted mild rectal bleeding of unclear duration. Home medications included mycophenolate mofetil, hydroxychloroquine, labetalol, isosorbide, and prednisone.

On examination, her blood pressure was 204/131 mm Hg, respiratory rate 21 breaths/min, pulse 91 beats/min, and body-mass index 39.35 kg/m2. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 5.5 g/dL, hematocrit 16.1%, white-cell count 2.8 ? 10^9/L, platelets 48 000/mm^3, haptoglobin < 30 mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 432 U/L, D-dimer 7884 ug/L, fibrinogen 182 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.43 mg/dL rising t

title: Plasma cell myeloma lytic lesions mimicking vanishing bone syndrome in a young patient
case_summary: A 25-year-old woman with HIV infection since birth, on antiretroviral therapy, presented with bilateral upper and lower extremity pain and progressive lower limb weakness leading to wheelchair dependence for 2 years. She had prior radiographs at multiple centers showing lytic bone lesions but no definitive diagnosis. On examination, she was normotensive, pale, and had digital clubbing. Multiple soft-tissue swellings and deformities were noted at the left elbow, dorsum of the left hand, both knees, and just above the right medial malleolus. Laboratory studies showed normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 5.6 g/dL, MCV 85.5 fL, RBC count 2.3?10^12/L), WBC 11.4?10^9/L, platelets 489?10^9/L; C-reactive protein was normal. Renal function was normal (creatinine 86 ?mol/L). HIV viral load was undetectable and CD4 count was 733 cells/?L. A skeletal survey revealed multifocal lytic expansile lesions with gross bone destruction and associated soft-tissue swelling, resembling vanishing bone syndrome.

title: Lateral pharyngeal wall myeloid sarcoma as a relapse of acute biphenotypic leukemia: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 31-year-old Arabian man, with a history of acute biphenotypic leukemia treated with chemoradiation followed by allogeneic stem cell transplant 4 years earlier, was referred for evaluation of a 1-month history of sore throat that did not respond to two courses of oral antibiotics. He denied fever, chills, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms. His medical history also included controlled essential hypertension, treated hepatitis B infection, and prior mandibular mucormycosis during transplant.

On oropharyngeal examination, there was a 3?2 cm granular mass arising from the right lateral pharyngeal wall, reaching the midline and pushing the uvula contralaterally; the contralateral tonsil appeared normal. There was no cervical lymphadenopathy and the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed normal hemoglobin, platelet, and white-cell counts; normal coagulation profile, liver enzymes, and electrolytes. Contrast-enhanced CT of the head and nec

title: Mild Encephalopathy/Encephalitis with Reversible Splenial Lesions after Amphetamine and Opioid Use
final_diagnosis: Mild encephalitis/encephalopathy with reversible splenial lesions
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman was found collapsed in a plaza; the duration of unconsciousness was unknown. She lives alone, has a long history of amphetamine addiction, and is on multiple psychotropic medications (lithium, alprazolam, brotizolam, eszopiclone, levomepromazine, chlorpromazine). On arrival, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 14 (E4V4M6) and vital signs were normal after initial hypotension corrected with leg elevation. Physical examination was notable only for a caesarean-section scar. She admitted to consuming an excessive amount of her prescribed medications, but a urine drug screen was positive for amphetamines and opioids and negative for other prescribed agents.  
Laboratory studies showed dehydration (hematocrit 47.5%), renal failure (creatinine 4.10 mg/dL), normal vitamin B1, B12, and folate levels, and a low blood lithium concentration of 0.04 mEq/L (therapeutic 0.6-1.2). Chest radiography and whole-body CT scans were unremarkable.  
Over the next days, her GCS declined to E4

title: An Uncommon Side Effect of Bupropion: A Case of Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis
case_summary:
A 30-year-old woman presented with acute eruptions that had appeared 4 days earlier on her face and trunk and then spread to her extremities. She reported taking a single dose of bupropion for smoking cessation 1 day before the onset of the eruptions and noted a fever for the past 4 days. She had no personal or family history of psoriasis or prior drug allergies, and she denied use of any other prescription or over-the-counter medications, supplements, herbal remedies, or new topical products. 

On examination, numerous small pustules were present on erythematous skin over the face, trunk, and legs; the erythematous areas tended to coalesce and were not limited to hair follicles. There were no mucosal lesions, and examination of other organ systems was unremarkable. Her temperature was 38.2 degreesC. 

Laboratory studies revealed a leukocyte count of 12.90 ? 10^9 per L with 88.8% neutrophils, an elevated C-reactive protein level, and no eosinophilia. Liver enzymes, serum protein and al

title: A challenging diagnosis for thiamine transporter deficiency anemia (Rogers syndrome) in two young siblings: A rare case report
case_summary:
A 3-month-old female infant, born at term by elective cesarean delivery to third-degree consanguineous parents, presented with pallor and dehydration following an upper respiratory infection. Psychomotor development was normal. On examination, her weight was 7 kg, length 63 cm, and head circumference 42 cm.

Laboratory studies revealed:
- Plasma glucose: 270 mg/L  
- HbA1c: 8.2%  
- Hemoglobin: 5.3 g/dL  
- White blood cell count with lymphocyte predominance; platelet count within normal limits  
- Abdominal ultrasound and echocardiography: no noteworthy findings  
- Peripheral blood smear: hypochromatic red cells with anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, histiocytes, and target cells  
- Bone marrow aspirate: normal cellularity with enhanced megaloblastic erythroblasts  
- Iron profile: low ferritin  
- Endocrine and metabolic tests: TSH 1.2 mLU/L; C-peptide 1.1 ng/mL; vitamin B9 20 ng/mL; vitamin B12 365 pg/mL (all within normal limits)  
- Auditory brainstem response testing: bilateral sen

title: Pulmonary nodules associated with pulmonary embolism: A rare and misleading presentation of amyloidosis
case_summary: A 74-year-old woman presented with a 2-day history of dyspnea and bilateral chest pain, continuous and worse on exertion; she denied cough, hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Her past medical history included glaucoma, hip arthrosis, and cystocele repair. She was a non-smoker with no relevant exposures. Medications: boric acid and timolol eye drops. Physical examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed WBC 4860/?l, hemoglobin 133 g/l, platelets 205 000/?l, normal PT and aPTT, D-dimer 5360 ng/ml, calcium 2.35 mEq/l, albumin 4.3 U/l, potassium 4.3 mEq/l, CRP 3.4 mg/l, creatinine 0.852 mg/dl, LDH 306 UI/l, and normal liver tests. Chest radiography revealed multiple bilateral nodules. CT pulmonary angiography confirmed bilateral pulmonary emboli and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, some calcified, the largest measuring 35 mm in the right middle lobe. She was anticoagulated with enoxaparin. Flexible bronchoscopy was normal; bronchoalveolar lavage was nondiagnostic, w

title: Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma associated with silicone breast implant, HLA-DRB1*11:01, and HLA-DQB1*03:01 manifesting as macrophage activation syndrome and with severe neurological symptoms: a case report
case_summary:
A 48-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of recurrent fever and splenomegaly. Her medical history was notable for right mastectomy with silicone breast-implant reconstruction 4 years earlier for intraductal carcinoma, followed by implant removal and re-implantation due to local inflammation, and a subsequent left breast reduction and bilateral lipofilling.

Eight and five months before admission she had 3-week febrile episodes treated empirically with levofloxacin. Three months later she developed fever, sore throat, fatigue, night sweats, cough, and a 10-kg weight loss over a year. In a peripheral hospital she was found to have:
? Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, leukocytes 3.6?10^9/L, LDH 638 U/L, ferritin 398 ng/mL  
? Splenomegaly (15-cm span) on ultrasound  
? Chest CT: bilateral apical pleural scarring, subpleural densities  
? Abdominal CT: hepatosplenomegaly, ovarian cysts, pelvic fluid; no lymphadenopathy  
? Bone marrow biopsy: non-diagnostic  
? 18FDG-PET/CT: hypermetabolism of

title: Acute graft-versus-host disease after double lung transplantation
case_summary: A 47-year-old woman with pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis underwent double lung transplantation and received cyclosporine, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone postoperatively. On postoperative day 50, she developed a painful pruritic eruption on the palms and soles that rapidly generalized. She was finishing a course of amoxicillin for an upper respiratory infection at that time. On postoperative day 69, she developed watery diarrhea. On postoperative day 71, examination revealed a generalized morbilliform eruption, vesicles on the palms and soles, and a buccal mucosal ulcer; she was afebrile. Her other medications included trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, furosemide, pantoprazole, nystatin, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, and valganciclovir. Punch biopsies of the back and thigh showed interface change with apoptotic keratinocytes and adjacent lymphocytes; there were no eosinophils, neutrophils, or viral cytopathic effects. Pathology suggested either grade II acute graft-

title: Successful treatment of idiopathic mast cell activation syndrome with low?dose Omalizumab
final_diagnosis: Idiopathic non?clonal mast cell activation syndrome
case_summary:
A 48-year-old man presented after a road traffic accident that followed a syncopal episode 15 minutes after a bee sting. He had a history of large local reactions to bee stings but no prior anaphylaxis. His medical history included avocado oral allergy and a transient ischemic attack for which he was taking aspirin. The episode resolved rapidly after intramuscular adrenaline (500 ?g). Baseline serum mast cell tryptase (MCT) was elevated at 19.2 ?g/L (normal <14.0 ?g/L), and total IgE was 344 kU/L (normal <111 kU/L). Bee venom-specific IgE was very high at 60.5 kU/L (very high >17.5 kU/L). 

He began bee venom subcutaneous immunotherapy. Six months later, on maintenance dosing, he developed rapid-onset visual changes, malaise, light-headedness, and a sensation of impending collapse, which resolved after adrenaline. Two years into therapy, he again experienced hypotension, light-headedness, and prolonged symptoms requiring two doses of adrenaline, IV hydrocortisone, and fluids. One week

title: A Rare Case of Recurrent Renal Infarcts With Unique Etiologies in Different Kidneys Occurring Six Years Apart
case_summary:
A 56-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with 2 days of progressively worsening abdominal and back pain, nausea, vomiting, and fever. She had a history of rheumatic mitral stenosis treated with balloon valvuloplasty one month earlier, after which she had stopped warfarin therapy. Six years prior, at age 50, she had presented with right flank pain, nausea, and vomiting; CT at that time had shown alternating bands of nonenhancement in the right kidney consistent with multiple renal infarcts, and she was found to have a stenotic rheumatic mitral valve and was started on warfarin.

On current presentation, temperature was 37.9 degreesC; other vital signs were within normal limits. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the left lower quadrant and left costovertebral angle tenderness; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Initial laboratory tests showed: creatinine 6.06 mg/dL, estimated GFR 8 mL/min/1.73 m2, potassium 6.1 mmol/L, white blood cell count 17

title: New onset ANCA-associated vasculitis in an adolescent during an acute COVID-19 infection: a case report
case_summary:
A 17-year-old previously healthy male presented with a one-week history of fevers to 102  degreesF, drenching night sweats, nonproductive cough, nasal congestion, and chest tightness. One week before symptom onset, he had exposure to a COVID-19-positive contact, and nasopharyngeal PCR testing at symptom onset returned positive for SARS-CoV-2. Over the next seven days, he continued to have fevers, weight loss of 5 lb, and worsening fatigue. On illness day 7, he developed acute shortness of breath, lightheadedness, two episodes of hemoptysis, and recorded an oxygen saturation of 86% at home. He was taken to a community hospital, where he was febrile to 101.2  degreesF with otherwise normal respiratory rate, heart rate, lung examination, and an oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Repeat SARS-CoV-2 PCR remained positive.

Chest radiography revealed a 5-cm left upper-lobe mass and a 3-cm right paratracheal mass. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, electrolytes, liver

title: Late Onset Agranulocytosis with Clozapine Associated with HLA DR4 Responding to Treatment with Granulocyte Colony-stimulating Factor: A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A 32-year-old university-educated man and smoker with treatment-resistant schizophrenia was started on clozapine 450 mg daily. After 9 months, trifluoperazine 20 mg daily was added because of a partial response. Weekly hematological monitoring was performed for 5 months, then monthly for the next 26 months, during which he remained stable.

Thirty-five months after starting clozapine, he developed sudden high-grade fever without focal symptoms. A complete blood count showed a total leukocyte count of 1100/mm3 with an absolute neutrophil count of zero; the differential count was 98% lymphocytes, 1% monocytes, and 1% eosinophils. Hemoglobin was 13.5 g/dL, and the platelet count was 1.5/?L. He had no history of antibiotics, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or antiepileptic use.

On examination, there was a small ulcer on the prepuce and a tender right axillary swelling. Evaluation for hematological malignancies; autoimmune disorders (systemic lupus erythematosus, Crohn's disease, rheumatoid ar

title: Case report of metastatic melanoma presenting as an unusual cause of gastrointestinal hemorrhage in an elderly gentleman
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented with one week of progressive shortness of breath and a near-syncopal episode, accompanied by blurry vision. He also reported several weeks of melena, two months of fatigue, and mild unintentional weight loss. He took daily aspirin, had a normal colonoscopy one year ago, and denied smoking or significant alcohol or drug use.  
Vital signs: T 98.2  degreesF, HR 91 bpm, RR 20/min, BP 124/55 mmHg, SpO? 98% on room air. Physical exam showed a soft, non-tender abdomen with normoactive bowel sounds; remainder unremarkable.  
Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 5.4 g/dL, MCV 78.6 fL, RDW 17.5%, BUN 33 mg/dL (BUN/Cr ratio 24), glucose 141 mg/dL, and D-dimer 2070 ng/mL. Chest radiograph was normal. CT pulmonary angiography excluded pulmonary embolism. Abdominopelvic CT showed non-obstructing, moderately prominent jejunal wall thickening without significant inflammation or adenopathy.  
He was transfused 3 units of packed red cells

title: Macroscopic hematuria as an initial symptom of testicular cancer, an unusual presentation and initial management. A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 31-year-old man with no significant past medical history or family history of cancer presented to the emergency department with four weeks of clot-forming gross hematuria. Five weeks earlier, he had an episode of asymptomatic gross hematuria that was treated as a urinary tract infection. On this admission he was in hypovolemic shock from ongoing hematuria. Vital signs showed tachycardia and diaphoresis. Lung auscultation revealed bilateral rales; the abdomen was soft with no palpable masses or peritoneal signs. Genital examination showed a normal penis and descended testes, but there was an indurated, painless mass in the left scrotal sac without erythema or warmth.  
Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 7560/?L, hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, platelets 204 000/?L; prothrombin time 16 s, partial thromboplastin time 33.2 s, INR 1.3; serum glucose 116 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL; creatinine 1.3 mg/dL; sodium 134 mmol/L; potassium 3.5 mmol/L; chloride 102 mmol/L. Tumor marke

title: Leukocytoclastic Vasculitis in a Patient with Type 1 Cryoglobulinemia
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man presented with fatigue, bilateral leg swelling, and a painful rash on his lower extremities for 1 month. He denied recent infections, new medications, or other systemic symptoms. Physical examination showed multiple 1-5 mm tender, violaceous, nonblanching macules on both legs. Topical corticosteroids produced no improvement. Laboratory studies revealed:  
? Normal complete blood count, creatinine, liver enzymes, urinalysis, prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, thyroid function tests, and lactate dehydrogenase  
? Negative hepatitis B and C serologies and HIV test  
? Negative antinuclear antibodies (and subtypes), rheumatoid factor, and antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibodies  
? Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (47 mm/hr) and C-reactive protein (1.8 mg/dL)  
? Beta-2 microglobulin 3.1 mg/L (reference 0-3), C3 139 mg/dL (ref 90-180), C4 18.8 mg/dL (ref 20-47)  
? Serum cryoglobulins present at 14%  
? Elevated IgM at 1770 mg/dL with normal serum visco

title: Paralytic squint due to abducens nerve palsy : a rare consequence of dengue fever
case_summary:
A 29-year-old man presented during a dengue epidemic with 4 days of high fever, arthralgia, and myalgia. Initial examination was notable only for a temperature of 39  degreesC. Laboratory studies showed WBC 7000/mm3 (59% lymphocytes), hemoglobin 17.1 g/dL, hematocrit 51%, platelet count 82,000/mm3 (nadir 40,000/mm3), ALT 83 U/L, and AST 82 U/L. Dengue infection was confirmed by positive IgM capture ELISA, and he was managed with intravenous fluids per WHO guidelines. He had no bleeding, shock, or plasma leakage.

On hospital day 3, he developed sudden binocular diplopia that was worse on right gaze. Neurological examination was otherwise unremarkable except for a right convergent squint. Assessment of extraocular movements revealed isolated weakness of the right lateral rectus muscle without fatigability or nystagmus. Visual acuity, funduscopic examination, and color perception were normal.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the brain and MRI with thin orbital slices showed no abnormalities. ESR

title: Liver injury after aluminum potassium sulfate and tannic acid treatment of hemorrhoids
case_summary:
A 41-year-old Japanese man was admitted on postoperative day (POD) 15 for evaluation of persistent liver dysfunction. One year earlier, his ?-glutamyl transpeptidase had been elevated, and he had gradually reduced his alcohol intake over the past year. He had no history of smoking, recreational drugs, or new supplements. He underwent outpatient treatment of internal hemorrhoids with an aluminum potassium sulfate and tannic acid (ALTA) injection plus lidocaine. On POD 1 he developed fever to 38  degreesC, dark urine, and pruritus, for which he intermittently took loxoprofen. By POD 7, symptoms persisted and laboratory tests indicated liver dysfunction; there was no improvement by POD 14. 

On admission (POD 15), his temperature was 36.6  degreesC, blood pressure 103/59 mmHg, heart rate 82 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Examination revealed a diffuse rash, mild edema, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and jaundice. 

Laboratory studies showed:  
  ? White blood cell

title: Successful Treatment of Refractory Autoimmune Enteropathy With Ustekinumab
case_summary:
A 75-year-old man with a history of congestive heart failure, coronary artery disease, and atrial fibrillation presented with 4 months of progressively worsening, watery diarrhea and a 30-pound weight loss. He reported 30 watery bowel movements daily (total stool output 8 L per 24 hours), including nocturnal movements. He had multiple emergency visits for dehydration, hypotension, hypokalemia, and renal insufficiency. On admission, vital signs were normal. Stool electrolytes were K 53 mmol/L and Na 58 mmol/L (stool osmotic gap 68 mOsm/kg). Laboratory tests showed Na 137 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 109 mmol/L, bicarbonate 17 mmol/L, Mg 1.5 mg/dL, lipase 14 U/L, albumin 2.5 g/dL, total protein 5.5 g/dL, C-reactive protein 6.7 mg/L, and white blood cell count 9.3 K/?L with a normal differential.  
Tests that were normal included celiac serology; stool culture with ova and parasite screen; stool Clostridium difficile toxin assay; serum gastrin, vasoactive intestinal peptide, somatostatin, and

title: Acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia: two case reports and literature review
case_summary: A 70-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of cough and fever. His blood pressure was 120/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, temperature 38.2  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 98% on 2 L/min of supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula. Laboratory studies showed: WBC 7.41 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 132 g/L, platelets 387 ? 10^9/L, ESR 72 mm/h, CRP > 150 mg/L, IL-6 88.19 pg/mL, and procalcitonin 0.244 ng/mL. Arterial blood gas analysis, hepatic and renal function, electrolytes, respiratory virus panel, and fungal tests were all within normal limits. Blood cultures showed no growth; sputum cultures grew normal flora; three consecutive acid-fast bacilli smears were negative. There was no other identifiable source of infection. Autoimmune studies (ANA, ANCA, rheumatoid factor) were negative. A chest CT scan showed right upper lobe consolidation with air bronchograms, patchy infiltrates, and ground-glass opacities in the right lower lobe. The patient was treated e

title: Dengue encephalitis
journal: Indian Journal of Critical Care Medicine : Peer-reviewed, Official Publication of Indian Society of Critical Care Medicine
case_summary:
A 17-year-old girl presented with a 3-day history of high-grade fever, rigors, two episodes of vomiting, generalized headache, and brief altered sensorium a few hours before admission. On examination, temperature was 101 degreesF, pulse 132/min, blood pressure 90/40 mmHg (mean arterial pressure 60 mmHg), and extremities were cold; oxygen saturation was 100% on room air. She appeared pale without rash, edema, icterus, or mucosal bleeding. Neurologic examination revealed no focal deficits or neck stiffness; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable, and there was no hepatosplenomegaly.

She was resuscitated with crystalloids. Empirical intravenous third-generation cephalosporin and artesunate were administered. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 14.5 g/dl, packed cell volume 45%, white-cell count 7100/mm3 (64% neutrophils, 34% lymphocytes), platelet count 135,000/mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 48 mm/h. A malaria antigen rapid test was negative. Liver enzymes were eleva

title: Unveiling the enigma of spontaneous cervical swelling syndrome: A case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman with iron deficiency anemia presented with acute left neck and upper chest swelling and a pressure sensation. The swelling began suddenly on the left side of her neck, increased in size over a few hours, and extended into the left upper chest. She denied fever, cough, chills, shortness of breath, discharge, insect bites, trauma, or recent medications; she had no known allergies.

On examination, she was alert and oriented. Vital signs were: blood pressure 126/86 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 94% on room air, and temperature 37  degreesC. There was a left-sided neck swelling extending from the upper border of Zone 1 to below the left clavicle, which had expanded over 2 hours, with a clear line of demarcation. The trachea was deviated to the right, and carotid pulses were intact bilaterally.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 11.9 g/dl, hematocrit 36.4%, mean corpuscular volume 77.3 fl, mean corpuscular hemoglobin

title: Thyroid storm in a pregnant woman with COVID-19 infection: A case report and review of literatures
case_summary: A 42-year-old multiparous woman at 35 weeks and 2 days' gestation presented to the emergency department with altered mentation, generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and a fever of 38.3  degreesC. Two days earlier, she had tested positive by nasopharyngeal PCR for SARS-CoV-2, with throat pain and rapidly progressed to respiratory failure requiring endotracheal intubation. On arrival, her blood pressure was 121/71 mmHg, heart rate 115 beats/min, and she exhibited drooling and continuous upward deviation of the eyes; pupillary reflexes were symmetric and brisk. Laboratory testing showed C-reactive protein 15.7 mg/L (normal 0-5), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 37 mm/h (0-20), and D-dimer 2.7 ug/mL (0.0-0.5). Urinalysis revealed no proteinuria and a normal protein-to-creatinine ratio. Liver function tests, serum electrolytes, glucose, blood urea nitrogen, and creatinine were within normal limits. Thyroid studies revealed TSH < 0.01 mIU/L (third-trimester reference 0.38-4.04), free T4 1.95 ng/

title: A Rare Case of Hairy Cell Leukemia with Unusual Loss of CD123 Associated with COVID-19 at the Time of Presentation
case_summary: A 58-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with 7 days of fever, fatigue, cough, and progressive dyspnea. On arrival, his oxygen saturation was 70% on room air, and he was intubated the same day. Chest radiography showed bilateral diffuse pulmonary infiltrates with interstitial thickening and basal consolidation. A nasopharyngeal swab was positive for SARS-CoV-2. Initial laboratory studies revealed pancytopenia (WBC 2.6 ? 10^3/uL, ANC 1.8 ? 10^3/uL, hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, platelets 85 ? 10^3/uL), acute kidney injury (creatinine 120 umol/L), transaminitis (ALT 170 U/L, AST 330 U/L), and elevated LDH (857 U/L). He received hydroxychloroquine, azithromycin, tocilizumab, methylprednisone, and intravenous immunoglobulin. His course was complicated by septic shock requiring vasopressors, broad-spectrum antimicrobials, and dialysis. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a normal?sized spleen without focal lesions. Despite granulocyte colony?stimulating factor support, by hospital da

title: A Rare Association of Mixed Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia with Gastric Carcinoma
final_diagnosis: Mixed warm and cold autoimmune hemolytic anemia
case_summary: A 70-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive dizziness, pre-syncope, and malaise. On arrival: hemoglobin 62 g/L, hematocrit 0.16 L/L, mean corpuscular volume 105 fL, leukocytes 13.6 ? 10^9/L, neutrophils 9.6 ? 10^9/L, and C-reactive protein 18.95 mg/L; electrolytes, thyroid-stimulating hormone, INR, aPTT, and creatinine were within normal limits. Her history included COPD, hypertension, irritable bowel syndrome, a 45 pack-year smoking history (quit 3 years prior), and daily alcohol use. She received two units of packed RBCs. A direct antiglobulin test was positive for IgG and C3 with a pan-reactive eluate. Chest radiograph was normal. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no lymphadenopathy or mass. Upper endoscopy revealed a 3.0 ? 2.0 cm moderate-grade gastric adenocarcinoma at the gastroesophageal junction; PET scan confirmed no metastases. She began neoadjuvant FLOT chemotherapy, and one week later developed painful acrocyanosis of the feet. Repeat labs:

title: Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome in Neonate
case_summary:
A 21-day-old female newborn was admitted for widespread skin redness and blistering. The illness began with sore throat and conjunctivitis. Within 48 hours she developed fever and tender erythema that progressed to generalized erythematous lesions, predominantly in the axillary and groin regions, associated with large, fragile, superficial blisters that ruptured with minimal pressure and left extensive denuded skin; the Nikolsky sign was positive. 

Maternal history was notable for an otherwise uneventful pregnancy except for hemoglobin AC trait; she received Tanakan, Folifer, and Fansidar at 26 and 32 weeks and used traditional medicines from the third month until delivery. Vaginal delivery at 40 weeks under epidural anesthesia resulted in a birth weight of 3.7 kg, length of 45 cm, head circumference of 33 cm, and Apgar scores of 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes.

On admission, the newborn was afebrile (36.2  degreesC), with a respiratory rate of 45 breaths/min, heart rate of 120 beats/min,

title: Heat stress presenting with encephalopathy and MRI findings of diffuse cerebral injury and hemorrhage
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man with hypertension, hypothyroidism, hyperhomocysteinemia, and major depressive disorder was transferred for altered mental status. Four days earlier he had been cattle ranching in ambient temperatures above 100  degreesF. On days 1-2 he developed malaise, hand cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea; his wife noted dehydration. On day 3 his mental status declined, and he was intubated at an outside hospital. Initial laboratories there showed WBC 11.4 K/uL, hemoglobin 15.6 g/dL, sodium 143 mEq/L, potassium 5.9 mEq/L, phosphorus 6.4 mg/dL, anion gap 22 mEq/L, creatinine 5.5 mg/dL, CRP 22.7 mg/L, ESR 50 mm/hr, lactate 2 mmol/L, troponin 0.229 ng/mL, CK-MB 8.1 ng/mL, and lipase 1091 U/L, consistent with multiorgan failure. 

Five days after initial presentation, on arrival to our hospital he was afebrile (97.8  degreesF), heart rate 86 bpm, blood pressure 162/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min. Neurologic examination revealed lethargy, dysarthria, impaired vertical gaze, diffuse limb

title: Toxic shock syndrome responsive to steroids
case_summary: A 28-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with a 2-day history of diarrhea, vomiting, high fevers, sore throat, and skin flushing. She had no recent travel, toxin or drug exposure, or gynecologic complaints, and she had not used tampons, as she was on intramuscular Depo-Provera contraception and not menstruating. Six years earlier, she had a toxic shock-like illness requiring intensive care. On examination, her temperature was 39.6 degreesC, pulse 120/min, and blood pressure 110/70 mmHg; she appeared distressed and flushed with widespread erythema of the skin. Cardiac and other system examinations were normal; pelvic examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 9.4?10^9/L with a left shift, C-reactive protein 250 ng/mL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hr, and arterial blood gases with a respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.57, PaO2 13.8 kPa, PaCO2 2.2 kPa). A chest radiograph was normal. She received aggressive intravenous fluids and, after blood

title: Prolonged Thrombocytopenia in a Case of MIS-C in a Vaccinated Child
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 9-year-old girl of eastern Indian origin with no past medical history presented with a 3-day history of fever, abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She had completed a 2-dose Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccination series 31 days earlier and had an asymptomatic positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR test 16 days before presentation; a subsequent home antigen test was negative. After travel to the US Virgin Islands without illness, she returned and developed persistent fever, emesis, and abdominal pain. A respiratory viral panel was negative, and she received a dose of oseltamivir. 

On day 3 of illness, in the emergency department, her temperature was as high as 40.0  degreesC, heart rate 124-133 bpm, respiratory rate 20-30 breaths/min, blood pressure 94-102/44-54 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 96-100% on room air. Examination showed mild conjunctival injection and epigastric tenderness; no rash was noted.

Initial laboratory studies revealed mild hyponatremia, leukopenia (2,580 cells/uL), thrombocytop

title: Idiopathic spontaneous compartment syndrome of the right lower limb: a case report
case_summary:
A 54-year-old Caucasian man with no prior comorbidity developed sudden-onset severe right calf pain while walking. Over the next few hours, the pain worsened and swelling extended to the ankle. He denied trauma, insect bites, prolonged immobility, and was taking no medications. He self-medicated with codeine-paracetamol and ibuprofen without relief, and noted numbness below the knee.

On examination, he was afebrile and in severe pain. Vital signs: pulse 85/min, BP 133/76 mmHg, SpO? 97% on room air. NEWS2 score was 0. Systemic exam was unremarkable. The right calf measured 45 cm versus 44 cm on the left; the overlying skin was tense and stretched without erythema or blisters. The right leg and calf were tender. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were present; capillary refill was <2 seconds. The anterior and peroneal compartments appeared tense, especially with toe movement, and light-touch sensation was reduced below the knee.

Venous blood gas: pH 7.4; electrolytes normal; gl

title: Successful Treatment of Calciphylaxis in a Young Female With End-Stage Renal Disease on Peritoneal Dialysis With Parathyroidectomy, Intensification of Dialysis, and Sodium Thiosulphate--A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 34-year-old Hispanic woman was admitted with a 2-week history of a painful, nonhealing infra-umbilical wound. Two weeks earlier she noted scabs in the infra-umbilical area that became pruritic and irritated; she applied plain gauze and petrolatum, but the scabs evolved into tender ulcers.  

Her medical history included end-stage renal disease secondary to amphotericin-B-induced nephrotoxicity, managed with continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis for 5 years; secondary hyperparathyroidism (intact PTH as high as 2400 pg/mL and outpatient phosphorus 10 mg/dL six months ago); gastroesophageal reflux disease; obesity; anemia of chronic kidney disease; and prior disseminated coccidioidomycosis. She reported nonadherence to phosphate binders due to financial constraints. Home medications included sevelamer carbonate. She denied tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use.

Her peritoneal dialysis regimen was 4 daily exchanges of 2 L 2.5% dextrose solution, with a weekly Kt/V of 2.1 and no resi

title: Rituximab induced cytokine release syndrome in an MS patient: A case report
case_summary:
A 44-year-old man with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis was referred for evaluation of bilateral lower limb edema that appeared two days after his most recent rituximab infusion. He also reported headache, generalized arthralgia, and increasing somnolence. He was not febrile, and vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination showed bilateral symmetric pitting edema of the lower limbs without skin lesions. Laboratory tests revealed: serum creatinine 1.4 mg/dL; alanine aminotransferase 78 U/L (normal 6-45); aspartate aminotransferase 64 U/L (8-40); C-reactive protein 57 mg/dL (normal <10); hemoglobin 13.6 mg/dL; hematocrit 44%; white-cell count 5,600?10^9/L; platelets 123,000/?L.

His neurologic history began in 2001 with right optic neuritis, followed a year later by ataxia and diplopia when MRI showed hyperintense cervical spinal cord lesions and interferon-beta 1a was initiated. Five years later he developed bilateral lower limb paresis, and three years after that

title: Two Fatty Liver Conditions Masquerading as Autoimmune Hepatitis
case_summary: A 41-year-old gravida 4 para 2 woman at 15 weeks 5 days of gestation presented with decreased appetite and severe vomiting. Her BMI was 45.1 kg/m2; she had no other medical history. Initial laboratory tests revealed WBC 14 k/uL, Hgb 13.7 g/dL, PLT 354 k/uL, AST 354 IU/L, ALT 574 IU/L, ALP 125 IU/L, and total bilirubin 4.8 mg/dL. Abdominal ultrasound and MRCP showed hepatic steatosis, pericholecystic fluid, and gallbladder wall thickening. Serologic testing was notable for a positive ANA at 1:640; infectious, metabolic, and other serologic studies were negative. She was treated with methylprednisolone and antibiotics, and by hospital day 5 her AST had decreased to 91 IU/L, ALT 185 IU/L, ALP 127 IU/L, and total bilirubin 7.3 mg/dL; she was discharged on a steroid taper and antiemetics. Three days later, she returned with vomiting, pruritus, and jaundice. Labs showed WBC 18.4 k/uL, Hgb 12.1 g/dL, PLT 166 k/uL, AST 51 IU/L, ALT 88 IU/L, ALP 191 IU/L, total bilirubin 15 mg/dL, total protein

title: Challenges in treatment of renal echinococcosis with gross hydatiduria and unsalvageable kidney: a case report
case_summary: A 49-year-old Syrian man, a lifelong shepherd, presented with acute right flank pain, dark urine, and passage of stones and cream-colored, grape-skin-like structures in the urine. He had a history of a hepatic hydatid cyst treated by PAIR and albendazole years earlier but lost follow-up. He denied fever, dysuria, or other urinary symptoms. On examination he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable; abdominal examination revealed right flank tenderness without masses or peritoneal signs. Laboratory tests showed normal leukocyte count without eosinophilia, serum creatinine 1.03 mg/dL, hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, normal electrolytes and liver function tests, and an estimated GFR of 81 mL/min/1.73 m2. Urinalysis revealed leukocytes and 6-7 RBCs per high-power field, with sterile cultures. Passed stones were  calcium oxalate and urate. Echinococcus indirect hemagglutination assay was positive at a titer of 1:2048. A KUB x-ray was unremarkable. Renal ultrasound demonstrated multiple unilocular hypo

title: Mendelian Susceptibility to Mycobacterial Diseases (MSMD) in a 13-Year-Old Ethiopian Girl with Autosomal Dominant Interferon Gamma Receptor 1(IFN-? R1) Defect: A Clinical Diagnostic and Treatment Challenge
case_summary:
A 12-year-old girl was referred for evaluation of a 3-week history of cough, high-grade fever, and chest pain and for progressive ulceration of scalp lesions. Since early infancy, she had recurrent mycobacterial?type infections: at 45 days she developed an exaggerated ulcer at her BCG vaccination site; at 14 months she had bilateral cervical lymphadenitis with caseous necrosis treated as tuberculosis; at ages 3 and 4 years she had pulmonary infiltrates, generalized lymphadenopathy, and presumed multidrug-resistant TB treated with second-line therapy; at 7 years she developed multifocal osteomyelitis and lupus vulgaris-like skin plaques treated again with anti-TB agents; and at 11 years she had sacroiliac arthritis with adjacent abscess and positive TB PCR treated for a fifth time. There was no family history of similar illnesses.

On examination she appeared chronically ill and tachypneic (oxygen saturation 56% on room air). She was severely underweight (BMI <5th percentile) with front

title: Association of multifocal Hodgkin's lymphoma and tuberculosis infection: A challenging entity
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 63-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with a 3-month history of chronic cough, chest pain, and purulent sputum, accompanied by fatigue, intermittent fever, and night sweats. On examination, he had enlarged bilateral cervical lymph nodes. Chest CT showed multifocal patchy consolidations with multiple nodules in both lungs and necrotic lymphadenopathy in the cervical, paratracheal, and hilar regions. A cervical lymph-node biopsy demonstrated epithelioid cell granulomas with giant cells and caseating necrosis. Tuberculin skin testing was positive (11 mm). He was started on rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol.

After 5 months of directly observed therapy, he returned with persistent and enlarging cervical nodes, erythematous papular skin lesions over the nodes, diffuse abdominal pain, and further weight loss. Examination revealed multiple painless, voluminous cervical lymphadenopathies causing drooping of the right corner of the mouth, confluent erythema

title: Procalcitonin -Vital Tool to Differentiate Septic Progression of Spondylodiscitis from Drug Hypersensitivity: A Case Report and Brief Review of Literature
case_summary: A 35-year-old previously healthy man underwent uncomplicated L5-S1 interbody fusion for symptomatic spondylolisthesis. Three weeks postoperatively, he returned with increasing nocturnal back pain and arthralgia. Magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine showed only nonspecific postoperative changes. His C-reactive protein was 22 mg/L, and a CT scan showed no bone resorption or hardware loosening. He was observed. Two weeks later, the CRP had risen to 38 mg/L, and repeat CT demonstrated bone resorption around one interbody cage, leading to a presumptive diagnosis of postoperative discitis. With no suitable target for biopsy, empiric intravenous teicoplanin was started via a PICC line, and oral rifampicin was added on day 4. The patient's symptoms improved dramatically within 48 hours. On day 7 of antibiotic therapy, he developed fevers above 39.0  degreesC, and the CRP rose to over 200 mg/L. A repeat CT scan showed progression of the previous bony resorption. A differential diagnos

title: Benign cystic mesothelioma of the appendix presenting in a woman: a case report
case_summary: A 19-year-old Irish Caucasian woman presented with a 3-day history of gradually worsening lower abdominal pain and fever, associated with nausea and one episode of vomiting. She reported no urinary symptoms, and her last menstrual period had ended the previous day. Her medical history was notable only for viral meningitis two years earlier; she had no history of sexually transmitted disease, recent urinary tract infection, prior surgery, or regular medications, and no allergies. On examination, her temperature was 37.4  degreesC and other vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination revealed a tender mass in the right iliac fossa. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 10,500/mm3, hemoglobin 13.3 g/dL, platelets 212,000/mm3, normal electrolytes and renal function, a urine test negative for leukocytes, and a negative urinary ?-hCG. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a 10.4 ? 4.5 ? 3.8 cm loculated cystic mass in the right pelvis that appeared to conta

title: X-Linked Lymphoproliferative Disease Presenting as Pancytopenia in a 10-Month-Old Boy
case_summary:
A previously healthy 10-month-old boy was admitted with one week of fever, rash, and malaise. He appeared lethargic and was febrile to 102 degreesF with a heart rate of 180 beats per minute. Examination revealed a purpuric petechial rash over the trunk and extremities, an erythematous bulging right tympanic membrane, and hepatomegaly with the liver palpable 4?cm below the costal margin; the spleen was not palpable.
Initial laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 3.9?10^9/L with 68% lymphocytes and 13% atypical lymphocytes, hemoglobin 8.1?g/dL, and platelets 36?10^9/L. Serum aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, and lactate dehydrogenase were elevated at 152?U/L, 146?U/L, and 2041?U/L, respectively. Epstein-Barr virus serologies indicated acute infection, and quantitative viral load demonstrated persistent viremia. Serum immunoglobulin levels were within normal limits.
He was treated for otitis media with ceftriaxone. Over the next several days, his lethargy impr

title: Brodie's Abscess in a Patient Presenting with Sickle Cell Vasoocclusive Crisis
case_summary:
A 20-year-old woman with hemoglobin-SS sickle cell disease, right?hip osteonecrosis with chronic hip pain, and asthma presented with 5 days of bilateral thigh pain that began after ice?skating; she denied direct trauma. On arrival she was febrile (38.5  degreesC) and tachycardic; leukocyte count was 25.3 ? 10^9/L and hemoglobin was 7.0 g/dL. Examination showed no tenderness of the hips or joints, but an ill?defined chronic swelling of the right anterior thigh. An initial diagnosis of sickle cell vasoocclusive painful crisis with systemic inflammatory response syndrome was made, empiric analgesics, intravenous fluids, and a simple transfusion were administered, and sepsis work-up was initiated.

On hospital day 2, fever and leukocytosis persisted, and intravenous levofloxacin was started. The left thigh pain worsened, with new swelling and warmth but no tenderness. Plain radiographs of the left hip and thigh revealed a heterogeneous appearance of the proximal left femoral shaft, which w

title: Favism?induced methemoglobinemia in a G6PD deficient male with a subsequent hemolytic cascade, a therapeutic challenge: Case report and review of literature
case_summary:
A 56-year-old Qatari man with type 2 diabetes and hypertension presented with a 5-day history of progressive dyspnea and dizziness. He also reported 3 days of mild hematuria and one episode of nonbloody vomiting. He denied recent infection or exposure to new medications. He smokes five cigarettes per day and does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs.

On examination, he was afebrile, blood pressure was 136/76 mm Hg, heart rate 93/min, and pulse oximetry showed an SpO2 of 70% on room air. He had pallor and jaundice; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Supplemental oxygen did not improve his SpO2. An arterial blood gas showed an SpO2 of 101% and a methemoglobin level of 5.6%.

A complete blood count revealed a hemoglobin concentration of 9.9 g/dL. Laboratory studies showed an elevated indirect bilirubin, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, low haptoglobin, and a reticulocyte index of 2.47, consistent with hemolysis. Chest radiography and electrocardiography were unr

title: Vancomycin- and piperacillin-induced acute interstitial nephritis in a patient with lupus: A case report showcasing rapid decline in renal function
case_summary:
A 41-year-old woman with a history of discoid lupus presented with 2 days of progressive left knee pain, swelling, subjective fevers, chills, and myalgia. She denied trauma, sick contacts, or prior similar episodes. On arrival, her temperature was 38.6  degreesC and heart rate 136 bpm. Examination revealed a swollen, warm left knee with restricted range of motion. Initial laboratory studies showed: hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, platelets 209 K/?L, WBC 5.79 K/?L (neutrophils 4.1 K/?L), a normal basic metabolic panel, AST 29 U/L, ALT 51 U/L, ALP 91 U/L, normal procalcitonin, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 65 mm/h, and C-reactive protein 16.9 mg/dL. Urinalysis showed no evidence of infection.

In the emergency department, she received a single dose of vancomycin and piperacillin?tazobactam. Left knee radiography revealed a moderate suprapatellar effusion; synovial fluid was bloody with 1,400 cells/mm3 (57% neutrophils) and no crystals. Blood and synovial fluid cultures remained negative. Antibiot

title: Pure White Cell Aplasia and Necrotizing Myositis
case_summary:
A 74-year-old woman presented with two weeks of progressive, painless, bilateral proximal muscle weakness involving her legs, shoulders, and arms. Two weeks earlier, she had received a five-day course of azithromycin for a sinus infection. Her medical history included hypertension, hypothyroidism, hyperlipidemia (on atorvastatin 40 mg daily), and remote transient ischemic attacks. Medications were otherwise stable. A sibling had limited scleroderma.

On examination, blood pressure was 155/61 mm Hg and temperature 97.6  degreesF. She was unable to lift her proximal legs against gravity or to ambulate. Admission laboratory values were: hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 4.34?10?/?L (50.3% neutrophils), platelets 165?10?/?L, blood urea nitrogen 44 mg/dL, creatinine 1.34 mg/dL. Liver tests showed ALT 545 IU/L, AST 975 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 621 IU/L, total bilirubin 5.0 mg/dL (direct 3.5 mg/dL). Creatine phosphokinase was 2597 IU/L and aldolase 20.8 IU/L. Thyroid function was

title: Hyperinflammatory syndrome in a paediatric patient with a recent diagnosis of HIV/AIDS infection: hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis or immune reconstitution syndrome?
case_summary:
A 4-year-old girl was admitted with fever and acute respiratory failure. She had no known SARS-CoV-2 infection. Chest imaging revealed bilateral ground-glass opacities, patchy consolidations in the lower lobes, pneumomediastinum, and bilateral cervical subcutaneous emphysema. Bronchoalveolar lavage grew Pneumocystis jiroveci and was galactomannan positive; SARS-CoV-2 PCR was negative. She was intubated and ventilated.

Her history included recurrent respiratory infections since age 3, an ocular HSV infection, and oral thrush. Immunologic evaluation showed severe CD4 lymphopenia (6 cells/uL), and HIV infection was confirmed with a viral load of 83 429 copies/mL (CDC stage 3). She started ART (lamivudine, zidovudine, lopinavir/ritonavir) with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, caspofungin, and liposomal amphotericin B. Testing also revealed CMV (31 446 copies/mL), EBV (8542 copies/mL), and oral HSV lesions; acyclovir and ganciclovir were added.

On day 51 of ART, she developed persistent fev

title: Stevens-Johnson syndrome and COVID-19: a case report with suspected multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C)
case_summary: A 4-year-old girl was referred on January 20, 2022, for evaluation of generalized erythematous macular lesions. She had a history of a febrile seizure but was not receiving phenobarbital. Initial assessment led to a presumed drug reaction; she was treated with diazepam and methylprednisolone, improved over 3 days, and was discharged without new medications. One week later, she was readmitted with recurrence of skin findings--erythematous papules on the arms, legs, and abdomen--and involvement of the eyes and oral mucosa. On examination, her heart and lung sounds were normal, and there were no neurological or abdominal abnormalities. Laboratory studies were obtained: a white blood cell count of 3,500/?l, hemoglobin of 10.1 g/dl, platelets 327,000/?l, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 89 mm/hr, C?reactive protein negative, and normal renal function and electrolytes. Intravenous fluids were administered. Given the concern for Stevens-Johnson syndrome and the possibility of multisystem

title: Superinfected COVID?19 in a young patient with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome: A case report
case_summary: A 15-year-old female presented with a 2-day history of bilateral blurred vision and headache. She initially declined hospitalization but returned with recurrent generalized seizures, decreased level of consciousness, worsening headache, and persistent blurred vision. She had no past medical or medication history. Two weeks earlier, a rapid COVID-19 test was positive and lung HRCT demonstrated bilateral multilobar ground-glass and consolidation opacities in the peripheral lung fields. On examination, her vital signs were normal, with no fever and no hypertension. Glasgow Coma Scale was 10. Neurologic examination revealed mid-sized reactive pupils, grade I-II bilateral papilledema, and decreased visual acuity; other findings were unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies showed normal electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen, and creatinine; C-reactive protein was 50 mg/dL; white blood cell count was 12 000/?L; and oxygen saturation was approximately 92%. She was admitted to the ICU for three da

title: Starvation Ketoacidosis: A Cause of Severe Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis in Pregnancy
case_summary: A 41-year-old woman, gravida 6 at 32 weeks' gestation, was admitted to the ICU with a 4-day history of recurrent vomiting that worsened over the prior 48 hours; the last two episodes were bloody. She denied fever, chills, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or urinary symptoms and had no past medical or surgical history. She reported occasional alcohol use that continued during pregnancy. A urine toxicology screen was positive for cannabinoids. On examination, she was afebrile, appeared dehydrated, was tachycardic and tachypneic, and was normotensive. Initial laboratory studies showed: WBC 16,000/mm^3; arterial pH 7.162, PCO2 13 mm Hg, bicarbonate 5 mmol/L, anion gap 31; creatinine 1.4 mg/dL; lactate 1.4 mmol/L; urine positive for protein and acetone; serum acetone moderate; serum alcohol, salicylate, and acetaminophen levels undetectable; glucose 83 mg/dL; ammonia 49 mcg/dL; mild transaminitis; and normal CK. She developed worsening respiratory distress with Kussmaul breathing and mild hemateme

title: Symmetrical peripheral gangrene following testicular torsion surgery: A case report
case_summary:
A 14-year-old boy was referred from a mission hospital for postoperative wound sepsis after right scrotal exploration and orchidectomy for testicular torsion. He had 24 h of scrotal pain and swelling before surgery. On arrival he was tachypneic, tachycardic (124 bpm), febrile (38.7  degreesC), and diaphoretic, with pallor of mucous membranes. Blood pressure was 109/68 mmHg, neck veins were flat, and heart sounds were normal. The tips of his fingers and toes appeared cyanosed bilaterally with peripheral oxygen saturations fluctuating between 20% and 30% on room air, despite no severe dyspnea. Ecchymoses were present over the extremities, and he bled easily from puncture sites. The right groin wound appeared necrotic and septic. He had no chronic illnesses or medications and denied smoking.  

Initial laboratory studies showed white blood cells 16 ? 10^3/uL with neutrophils 10.8 ? 10^3/uL, hemoglobin 4.8 g/dL, and platelets 26 ? 10^3/uL; liver and renal function tests, electrolytes, mala

title: Cardiac Aspergilloma: A Rare Case of a Cardiac Mass Involving the Native Tricuspid Valve, Right Atrium, and Right Ventricle in an Immunocompromised Patient
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man was evaluated for a one-month history of productive cough, progressive dyspnea, intermittent fevers, and a syncopal episode one week before presentation. His medical history was notable for hypertension, prostatic adenocarcinoma with bone metastases (diagnosed 11 months earlier), atrial fibrillation, and bilateral pulmonary embolism treated 8 months earlier. Medications included abiraterone (Zytiga) with prednisone, leuprolide, and apixaban. He had no history of intravenous drug use.

On admission for anemia (hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL) and presumed pneumonia with sepsis, he received four units of packed red blood cells. Laboratory studies revealed thrombocytopenia (platelets 48,000/mcL). Chest radiography showed a 7.4 cm right lower-lobe opacity and multiple right pulmonary nodules. Chest CT confirmed these findings and extensive bony metastases. Initial management included vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam.

Infectious workup revealed positive Aspergillus fumigatus Ig

title: Secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis due to nivolumab/ipilimumab in a renal cell cancer patient--A case report
case_summary: A 68-year-old man with hypertension, coronary artery disease, and impaired glucose tolerance was diagnosed with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma and received four cycles of ipilimumab (1 mg/kg) plus nivolumab (3 mg/kg) followed by maintenance nivolumab (420 mg every 4 weeks). Three months after starting immunotherapy, he presented with decreased appetite, dizziness, generalized weakness, intermittent fevers, and tachycardia. Initial studies revealed hyponatremia (Na 122 mmol/L) and pancytopenia (WBC 1.1 ? 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.7 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 7.7 g/dL, platelets 46 ? 10^9/L) with a ferritin level of 57 931 ng/mL. The differential diagnosis included sepsis, undiagnosed hemochromatosis, disease progression with bone marrow involvement, and irHLH. He underwent a comprehensive infectious workup, supportive transfusions, antibiotic therapy, and restaging imaging, which revealed no infection or tumor progression. Further evaluation showed hypofibrinogenemia (148 mg/dL), hypert

title: Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis Presenting With ARDS in a Young Adult: A Case Report
case_summary: A 20-year-old Caucasian man with no prior medical history presented with five days of intractable vomiting. In the emergency department he had multiple seizures requiring intubation. On examination he was tachycardic with dry mucous membranes. Laboratory evaluation showed metabolic alkalosis, acute kidney injury, and a positive urine drug screen for cannabis; he was treated for presumed cannabis?induced cyclic emesis. On hospital day five he developed high-grade fevers to 39.5  degreesC and new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. He was started on vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam. Bronchial aspirate cultures grew methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, both sensitive to the prescribed antibiotics. Despite ten days of antibiotic therapy--including escalation to meropenem and addition of micafungin--his fevers persisted. His respiratory status worsened to acute respiratory distress syndrome requiring venovenous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation. Review of i

title: Fournier's gangrene mimicking an acute epididymitis
case_summary:
A 61-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, and hypertension presented with 48 hours of left scrotal pain and swelling. The pain was constant, throbbing, worsened by movement, and localized to the posterior left hemi-scrotum with radiation to the right. He also reported a 3-day history of subjective fevers, chills, dysuria, urgency, frequency, and urinary retention. He denied trauma and any history of sexually transmitted infections.

On arrival: temperature 98.7 degreesF, blood pressure 156/60 mm Hg, heart rate 69 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He appeared comfortable. Cardiac, respiratory, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Urogenital examination showed swelling and erythema of the left hemi-scrotum, marked tenderness over the left epididymis, and a swollen, erythematous penis. There was no crepitus, bullae, blisters, or skin discoloration. Prehn sign was negative, cremasteric reflexes were

title: A case of pulmonary melioidosis in Germany: a rare differential diagnosis in returning travelers from South-East Asia
case_summary:
A 46-year-old woman was admitted for evaluation of a pulmonary mass of unknown origin. Four weeks earlier, she developed a dry cough, chest pain, and mild sore throat unresponsive to inhaled corticosteroids and a ?2-agonist. She denied fever, weight loss, night sweats, smoking, alcohol, or illicit drug use. She was born in Thailand, had lived in Germany for 27 years, and three weeks before symptom onset had returned from a 6-month visit to rural northeastern Thailand.

On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory tests showed mild thrombocytosis and a C-reactive protein level of 12.9 mg/l; complete blood count, liver and renal function, and lactate dehydrogenase were normal. Chest CT revealed a 5.3 ? 4.6-cm right central soft-tissue mass with patchy-confluent opacities in the right upper lobe and a 1.2-cm circumscribed cavitation, along with coalescing adenopathy at the right hilum and infracarinal mediastinum.

Bronchoscopy demonstrated an endobronchial tumor in t

title: Manifestation of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency in the wake of new-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus: a case report
case_summary:
A 10-year-old boy of Iraqi descent presented with a 2-week history of lethargy, weight loss, polydipsia, and polyuria. He was born in the UK to healthy parents of Iraqi origin, had a history of nocturnal enuresis and adenotonsillectomy, and took no regular medications. On examination his weight was 56.6 kg; vital signs were normal. Initial laboratory studies showed:  
? Blood glucose, 42.9 mmol/L  
? Venous pH, 7.38; bicarbonate, 21.3 mmol/L; base deficit, 3.3 mmol/L  
? Urine ketones, negative  
? Hemoglobin, 130 g/L; WBC count, 8.1 ? 10^9/L; platelets, 274 ? 10^9/L  
? Serum sodium, 127 mmol/L; renal and liver function, and thyroid function, normal  
? Positive islet cell antibodies; GAD antibodies > 2000 U/mL  
? HbA1C, 116 mmol/mol  

He was diagnosed with new-onset type 1 diabetes without ketoacidosis, started on subcutaneous insulin, and discharged on day 5 on a basal-bolus regimen. Eleven days later, a home visit noted normal blood glucose but new jaundice and extreme lethargy.

title: Morbilliform exanthem associated with COVID-19
case_summary:
A 58-year-old Hispanic man presented on March 23, 2020, with a widespread, pruritic dermatitis that arose on March 21 and had been expanding in severity. Two days earlier, he had telephoned his primary care physician to report a new cough and pain in his legs and hands; he was prescribed azithromycin (500 mg on day 1, 250 mg for 4 days) and benzonatate (100 mg every 6 hours as needed), which he started on March 20. He reported never having experienced a similar cutaneous eruption before. On arrival, his cough had resolved but hand and lower-extremity pain persisted; he had no fever. 

On examination, he was well-appearing with Fitzpatrick skin type IV.
