There were erythematous macules with islands of spared skin, arranged in a morbilliform pattern, on the legs, thighs, forearms, arms, shoulders, back, chest, and abdomen. These lesions coalesced into confluent erythematous patches larger than 10 cm on the back, abdomen, and chest. The face, hands, and feet were spared; there were no pern

title: Miescher's Cheilitis as a Diagnostic and Therapeutic Challenge--A Case Report
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man presented with severe swelling of both the upper and lower lips. Six months earlier, he had a one-week episode of lip swelling that resolved spontaneously; three weeks later, swelling recurred and progressively increased over six months. He denied prior surgical, dental, or cosmetic procedures, and reported no dyspnea, throat obstruction, or urticaria. He had no fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, fatigue, weight loss, or known allergies, and was not taking antihistamines or steroids.

Medical history included uncontrolled diabetes with retinopathy, hypertension, obesity, hyperlipidemia, and ischemic heart disease. Medications were rosuvastatin, bisoprolol, aspirin, fenofibrate, amlodipine/valsartan/hydrochlorothiazide, and insulin.

Examination showed indurated, firm, non-tender, diffuse swelling of the lower lip (more pronounced than the upper lip) and left buccal area, with scaling and dryness. The tongue was normal, facial nerve function intact, and no lymphadenopat

title: Familial Mediterranean fever phenotype progression into anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody-positive rheumatoid arthritis: a case report
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman presented with 6 months of swelling of the dorsum of her left foot and intermittent arthralgia in both hands without fever. She reported no urinary tract infection or diarrhea before onset. The joint swelling and pain occurred only during menstruation. Her history included a malignant sweat gland tumor treated at age 30 and iron deficiency anemia managed with iron supplements for a uterine myoma. There was no family history of autoinflammatory or autoimmune diseases. 

On examination, cardiovascular and respiratory systems were unremarkable; there was no hepatosplenomegaly. During menses, she had swelling and tenderness of the left foot and redness and tenderness of the left thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, with the swelling involving the entire digit rather than fusiform synovitis. She was afebrile. 

Laboratory tests showed normal hemoglobin, leukocyte, and platelet counts; C-reactive protein was 0.41 mg/dL; rheumatoid factor was 64 IU/mL. Anti-cyclic citrullinate

title: Lemierre's Syndrome: A Neglected Disease with Classical Features
case_summary: A previously healthy 23-year-old man presented with general malaise and a one-week history of progressively worsening sore throat. He reported low-grade fever, dyspnea, and inspiratory chest pain but denied gastrointestinal symptoms, headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, rash, or hemoptysis and had no history of smoking, alcohol use, illicit drugs, or recent travel. On examination, he was tachycardic (150 beats/min), hypotensive (97/50 mmHg), tachypneic (28 breaths/min), febrile (39.0  degreesC), and required 4 L/min of oxygen to maintain a saturation of 95%. He had pharyngeal erythema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and tenderness over the anterior neck without restriction of movement. Lung auscultation revealed reduced air entry and inspiratory crackles at both bases; the cardiovascular, abdominal, and neurologic examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory findings included leukocytosis (26.4 ? 10^9 cells/L) with neutrophilia, thrombocytopenia (28 ? 10^9 cells/L), C-reactive protein 250 m

title: Nontraumatic adrenal hemorrhage: the adrenal stress
case_summary:
A 76-year-old woman underwent a pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) for a malignant ampulloma. On the fifth postoperative day, she developed acute epigastric pain with nausea, vomiting, and fever (maximum temperature, 38.6 degreesC). A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen showed nonspecific inflammatory stranding surrounding mildly enlarged adrenal glands, which had appeared normal on a preoperative CT scan. No anastomotic leak or other surgical complications were identified. The leukocyte count was within the normal range, but systemic infection was suspected and empiric antibiotic therapy was initiated. 

Despite antibiotics, the patient's epigastric pain persisted, and on the 13th postoperative day she presented with a drop in hemoglobin (120 g/dL), hypotension, signs of hypovolemia, and tachycardia (130 bpm). Laboratory studies revealed sodium 126 mmol/L, potassium 6.9 mmol/L, a low serum cortisol level (5 mcg/dL), and an elevated plasma ACTH level (>300 ng/L), findings

title: Primary diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the kidney: A rare case report and review of literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 73-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of gradual right flank pain, lower back pain, loss of appetite, malaise, and a significant unintentional weight loss. He denied hematuria or fever. He had a 24 pack-year smoking history and social alcohol use. On examination, he appeared cachectic and pale without lymphadenopathy. Abdominal examination revealed a firm, bimanually palpable, minimally mobile mass in the right renal angle. Laboratory tests showed: leukocytes 9.87 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL, platelets 200 ? 10^9/L, creatinine 171 ?mol/L, potassium 5.83 mmol/L, sodium 124 mmol/L, calcium 3.19 mmol/L, lactate dehydrogenase 478 U/L, and negative HIV serology. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a large hypo?echoic mass in the right kidney measuring 15.6 ? 14.1 cm; the left kidney was normal. A working diagnosis of right renal cell carcinoma was made. Contrast CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed a hypo?enhancing soft?tissue mass on the medial aspect of the right ki

title: Vulvar ulcers as a rare manifestation of hematologic malignancy
case_summary:
A 57-year-old woman presented with fever and painful vulvar lesions of 1 week's duration that had not improved after 6 days of oral doxycycline (200 mg daily). She also reported mild migratory polyarthralgia for 1 month but denied other systemic symptoms. Her past medical history was notable only for dyslipidemia managed with atorvastatin.

On examination, she was febrile (38.0  degreesC) but hemodynamically stable. Multiple ulcerative lesions with violaceous borders and a friable vegetating center were noted on the labia majora and inner thighs, against a background of indurated vulvar edema. Lesions measured 0.5-3 cm, some with infiltrated borders and superficial skin detachment. Multiple millimetric shallow oral ulcers on an erythematous base were present on the hard palate and gingivae.

Laboratory studies showed C-reactive protein 36.50 mg/dL (normal < 0.50), procalcitonin 1.10 ng/mL (normal < 0.50), D-dimer 71 148 ng/mL (normal < 500), leukocytosis 40.8 ? 10^9/L (normal 3.9-10.2)

title: Spontaneous nosocomial Proteus mirabilis meningitis in a Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV)-infected adult patient: a case report
case_summary:
A 23-year-old Congolese woman with HIV diagnosed in November 2019 and on tenofovir-lamivudine-dolutegravir presented with a history of TB meningitis treated since February 2021. She was hospitalized in November 2021 for severe abdominal pain and wasting; CD4 count was 68 cells/mm3 and HIV viral load 137 copies/ml. Serum cryptococcal antigen was positive but CSF antigen was negative, and she had no neurologic symptoms. Oral therapies were held for nausea and vomiting; she received nutritional support and niacin for pellagra. 

On day 18 of hospitalization she developed fever (39  degreesC) and altered consciousness. Examination showed Glasgow Coma Scale 9/15, no focal neurologic deficits or nuchal rigidity, and evidence of polyserositis without accessible effusions or catheter-site infection. 

Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 8.6 g/dl, white-cell count 15.7?10^9/L (88% neutrophils), mild transaminase elevation with improving cholestasis, acute renal failure (creatinine 52 umol/L),

title: Clinical Improvement of Disseminated Acanthamoeba Infection in a Patient with Advanced HIV Using a Non-Miltefosine-Based Treatment Regimen in a Low-Resource Setting
case_summary:
A 42-year-old man with a 14-year history of HIV infection, on antiretroviral therapy with two prior episodes of defaulting treatment, presented with a 4-month history of headaches, right-eye visual impairment, and malaise, and a 2-month history of tender, nonpruritic skin lesions. The lesions began on the lateral aspect of his foot and subsequently involved his legs, arms, chest, and abdomen, sparing palms, soles, and mucous membranes. He also reported arthralgias of the wrists, ankles, and knees, with reduced mobility. He denied fever, cough, or diarrhea. 

He appeared chronically ill and wasted, with generalized shotty lymphadenopathy. Numerous subcutaneous nodules were present on the anterior chest, arms, and lower limbs; lesions began as papules, progressed to nodules, and then ulcerated with scab formation. There was synovitis of small joints in his right hand and dactylitis. Neurologic, cardiovascular, respiratory, and gastrointestinal examinations were unremarkable. Ophthalmic e

title: Multifocal Osteomyelitis in an Adolescent Patient With Cat Scratch Disease
case_summary:
A 15-year-old girl with no prior medical history presented with right axillary swelling. She had high fevers and evening low back pain for 4 days and axillary pain and swelling for 1 week. On examination, she had a 20?10 mm right axillary lymph node; otherwise the physical exam was normal.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, white blood cell count 8800/mm3, platelet count 188 000/mm3, C-reactive protein 88.5 mg/L (normal < 5), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 64 mm/h (normal 0-20), procalcitonin 0.21 ng/mL (normal < 0.05), aspartate aminotransferase 65 U/L, and creatine kinase 1299 U/L. Tests for Brucella (Rose Bengal and tube agglutination), Widal, CMV IgM, Brucella abortus IgM, and Quantiferon were negative; peripheral smear showed no blasts or atypical cells; other biochemical and electrolyte analyses were unremarkable.

She reported a recent cat scratch and was started on oral azithromycin. Two days later she was readmitted with severe back pain. She had painful lumbar f

title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome due to Mycoplasma pneumoniae Misinterpreted as SARS-CoV-2 Infection
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man presented on March 14, 2020, with a 3-day history of fever, dry cough, headache, and limb aches. He was previously healthy and had completed a half marathon one week before presentation. On arrival at a nearby hospital, he reported progressive shortness of breath and had a reduced general condition. Physical examination revealed crackles in the right upper lung field. His medical, family, and psychosocial history was unremarkable.

Because of concern for SARS-CoV-2 infection, he was admitted directly to the ICU. Initial therapy included piperacillin/tazobactam (4.5 g three times daily i.v.) and clarithromycin (500 mg twice daily i.v.). Polymerase chain-reaction tests for SARS-CoV-2, influenza virus, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and Legionella species were all negative. Two days later, because of persistent fever, elevated inflammatory markers, and further respiratory deterioration, broadening antimicrobial coverage to meropenem (1 g three times daily i.v.), linezolid (

title: Acute Psychosis Associated with Phenibut Ingestion
case_summary: A 40-year-old man with a history of anxiety, depression, hypertension, and substance abuse was brought by emergency medical services for agitation, auditory and visual hallucinations, and one seizure-like episode. His mother reported that over the preceding 3-5 days he had experienced hallucinations after taking a supplement purchased online identified as phenibut. His regular medications included hydroxyzine, gabapentin, and trazodone. On arrival, a urine drug screen and blood alcohol level were negative. In the emergency department, he became increasingly agitated and belligerent, requiring intravenous lorazepam and physical restraints; he had incoherent speech, profuse sweating, and a heart rate above 140 beats per minute. A decreased Glasgow Coma Scale score prompted endotracheal intubation for airway protection and sedation. Initial laboratory tests showed a lactate level of 9.1 mmol/L, creatine phosphokinase of 5422 U/L, serum creatinine of 2.03 mg/dL, a white blood cell count of

title: Phencyclidine Induced Oculogyric Crisis Responding Well to Conventional Treatment
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man presented to the emergency department with involuntary, sustained upward deviation of both eyes for one day. He reported weekly marijuana use for five years and that, one day earlier, he had smoked a small amount of "angel dust" (phencyclidine) mixed with tobacco and snorted a little. He felt dizzy and slept through the night. On awakening, his eyes were involuntarily deviated upward, and subsequent attempts to bring them down by forceful effort lasted only seconds before they deviated upward again.

He denied fever, headache, light-headedness, slurred speech, weakness, diplopia, auditory or visual hallucinations. His medical history was notable only for marijuana use; he denied any prescription medication use or accidental use of antiemetics or antipsychotics. He had no family history of seizures, stroke, or cancer.

On presentation, he was hemodynamically stable and fully oriented. Pupils were equal and reactive to light. Sustained conjugate upward eye deviation was

title: Unusual primary HIV infection with colonic ulcer complicated by hemorrhagic shock: a case report
case_summary: A 42-year-old Caucasian man with a history of past hepatitis B infection presented with a one-week history of sore throat, fever, and fatigue. He was treated with amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed pharyngitis. Ten days later, he was admitted with swelling of the tongue and lips and a widespread pruritic maculopapular rash; an allergic reaction to amoxicillin-clavulanate was diagnosed and treated with intravenous clemastin, with rapid resolution of swelling and rash. Despite this, he remained febrile (38.5  degreesC) and had exudative pharyngitis and painful bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy (up to 3 cm). Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 136 g/L, white blood cells 7.0 g/L (7% band neutrophils, 15% lymphocytes), C-reactive protein 168 mg/L, aspartate aminotransferase 500 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 400 U/L, ?-glutamyltransferase 1200 U/L, and total bilirubin 36 ?mol/L. A pharyngeal swab grew only normal oral flora. He was admitted for evaluation of a nonresolving mononucleosis

title: High-Anion-Gap Metabolic Acidosis During a Prolonged Hospitalization Following Perforated Diverticulitis: An Educational Case Report
journal: Canadian Journal of Kidney Health and Disease
case_summary:
A 56-year-old woman with a history of schizophrenia was admitted 2 months earlier for perforated diverticulitis requiring sigmoid resection. Her hospitalization was complicated by an anastomotic leak, defunctioning ileostomy for high-output losses, feculent peritonitis, wound dehiscence, multiple laparotomies, percutaneous drainage, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and wound debridements. She remained malnourished despite a high-protein/calorie diet. For pain, she received acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours from admission (total dose 78 g over the first several weeks), which was increased over 3 weeks to 650 mg every 4 hours (3.9 g/d).

On transfer to the intensive care unit for hypovolemic shock attributed to gastrointestinal losses and poor oral intake, she was found to have new abdominal collections, acute kidney injury, and a severe high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Laboratory testing on ICU day 0 showed:  
  ? Serum sodium 143 mmol/L, chloride 110 mmol/L, total CO? 5 mmol/L, anion gap

title: Cefoperazone and sulbactam-related eosinophilic peritonitis: a case report and literature review
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
case_summary:
A 59-year-old Chinese woman on continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis for 5 years presented with diarrhea, abdominal pain, and turbid dialysate after eating contaminated food. She had chronic renal disease from primary glomerulonephritis and a prior episode of Staphylococcus epidermidis peritonitis in 2016. She had no known drug allergies and was afebrile, with slight abdominal tenderness and no exit-site infection. Peripheral leukocyte count was 12,460/mm3 (89.1% neutrophils); hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL; platelets 22,000/mm3; hsCRP 5.13 mg/L; BUN 8.09 mg/dL; creatinine 8.61 mg/dL. Initial peritoneal fluid showed 152 leukocytes/mm3 (75% neutrophils), rising to 420/mm3 (80% neutrophils) on day 2. Abdominal ultrasound was unremarkable except for a 0.4-cm gallbladder polyp. Initial dialysate cultures were negative, and she was diagnosed with peritonitis and started on intraperitoneal cefazolin plus cefoperazone-sulbactam. 

On day 3, she developed persistent symmetrical pruritus of the palms

title: Acute Liver Injury and Bilateral Pulmonary Artery Thrombosis Due to Hypereosinophilic Syndrome
case_summary:
A 46-year-old man was referred with a 5-day history of nausea, anorexia, slight fever, and bilateral thigh pain. His temperature was 37.0 degreesC; blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation were normal. He was muscular with a BMI of 26.8 kg/m2. He had undergone cholecystectomy 4 years earlier, at which time preoperative labs showed a white blood cell count of 13,700/?L with 22.4% eosinophils; testing for the FIP1L1-PDGFR? fusion gene was negative and he received no treatment. He had drug allergies to bepotastine and mequitazine but no history of asthma, dermatitis, or recent new medications, and no family history of liver or blood disorders.

On admission, the white blood cell count was 31,700/?L with 72.3% eosinophils; platelet count was 6.7?10^4/?L; prothrombin time was 53%; activated partial thromboplastin time was 47.4 seconds; fibrinogen was 90 mg/dL; fibrin degradation products were 25.3 ?g/mL; D-dimer was 8.6 ?g/mL. Liver enzymes were elevated (AST 146

title: Collagenous Gastritis Masquerading as Eosinophilic Gastritis
case_summary: An 11-year-old boy presented with 3 months of worsening fatigue, intermittent crampy abdominal pain, occasional nausea, and pallor. Laboratory studies showed microcytic iron?deficiency anemia (hemoglobin 4.1 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 62 fL, absolute reticulocyte count 74.8 ? 10^3/?L, ferritin 1.9 ng/mL). The initial differential included hematologic malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, celiac disease, Helicobacter pylori infection, and excessive milk intake. Initial evaluation revealed normal fecal calprotectin, thyroid-stimulating hormone, urinalysis, fecal occult blood test, C-reactive protein, chest radiograph, and metabolic panel. Initial celiac testing showed borderline antiendomysial antibodies and normal tissue transglutaminase antibody, with repeat celiac testing normal. He received packed red blood cells and 3 months of oral iron and was advised to reduce milk intake. After stopping iron supplementation, his anemia recurred, and an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) was v

title: Bullous systemic lupus erythematosus - a case report
case_summary:
An 18-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of slightly painful, pruritic vesicles and bullae involving the face, oral mucosa, abdomen, thighs, and back. Over the preceding 2 months, she had experienced weight loss, migratory polyarthralgias with morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes, myalgias, fatigue, fever, alopecia, and a malar rash with photosensitivity. Her only medication was medroxyprogesterone acetate, administered every 3 months. She had been pregnant at age 17; venereal disease testing had shown a low?titer reactive VDRL and a negative treponemal assay.

On examination, she appeared pale and fatigued but had normal vital signs. Skin examination revealed a malar rash and multiple tense vesicles and bullae (1 mm to 6 cm) on erythematous macules and plaques involving the eyelids, arms, abdomen, back, thighs, and vermilion border of the lips, with extension to the oral mucosa. There was no lymphadenopathy, no synovitis, and otherwise unremarkable findings.

Laboratory s

title: 49-years-old women with unusual presentation of severe hypokalemia mimicking Guillain-Barr? Syndrome: A rare case report
case_summary: A 49-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department with 48 hours of rapidly progressing, ascending weakness of all four extremities and increasing difficulty breathing. She had no prior similar episodes, no recent trauma, vaccinations, infections, or toxin exposures, and denied drug use. On examination, she was alert but in acute respiratory distress, using accessory muscles and receiving oxygen via face mask. Vital signs were: blood pressure 140/90 mmHg, heart rate 98 beats/min, respiratory rate 40 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92% on supplemental oxygen. Chest auscultation was clear. Neurologically, she had hypotonia in all limbs (lower > upper), muscle strength of 1/5 in the lower limbs and 3/5 in the upper limbs, absent deep tendon reflexes, and equivocal plantar responses. Sensation and cranial nerves were normal. Her abdomen was distended with absent bowel sounds without tenderness. A provisional diagnosis of Guillain-Barr? syndrome was made, and she was intubated f

title: Cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis associated with verapamil and atorvastatin: A case report
case_summary:
An 82-year-old woman presented with a 2-day history of a non-pruritic, mildly tender purpuric rash that began on her distal calves and ascended to her thighs. She denied fever, myalgia, arthralgia, oral or genital ulcers, respiratory or neurological symptoms, and had no history of a similar rash. Ten days before onset, she was started on verapamil 40 mg twice daily and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly for palpitations and dyslipidemia; she had taken both daily until presentation.

Her medical history was notable for Sj?gren's syndrome, diagnosed 3 years earlier with a high-titre centromere ANA and stable sicca symptoms on hydroxychloroquine, and a submandibular MALT lymphoma excised 1 year ago. Other long-term medications included aspirin; she also took magnesium, fish oil, and vitamin C. She had no known drug allergies.

On examination, she was afebrile with normal vital signs. She had confluent purpura on both lower limbs, which over 2 days began to coalesce and spread to the trunk and upp

title: Intravascular lymphoma masquerading as septic shock
case_summary: A 64-year-old man presented with a 2-week history of dyspnea on exertion, severe fatigue, anorexia, and a 5-kg weight loss. One week prior, endoscopic evaluation revealed advanced rectal adenocarcinoma. He denied fevers, night sweats, or chills. He smoked 44 pack-years and drank minimally. On arrival, he was distressed: temperature 35.8 degreesC, blood pressure 80/57?mm?Hg, heart rate 96?beats/min, respiratory rate 30/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on 2?L/min nasal oxygen. Examination showed conjunctival pallor, jaundice, and 4+ pitting edema of the lower extremities; there was no jugular venous distension, murmur, or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies revealed white blood cell count 21?000/mm3, hemoglobin 9.5?g/dL, platelets 65?000/mm3, INR 1.90, fibrinogen 343?mg/dL, sodium 131?mmol/L, potassium 6.5?mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 101?mg/dL, creatinine 2.05?mg/dL, uric acid 13.8?mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 478?U/L, total bilirubin 3.0?mg/dL, albumin 1.6?g/dL, lactate 19.6?mmol/L, C-rea

title: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy in a Patient with Severe Leptospirosis Complicated with Pulmonary Haemorrhage, Myocarditis, and Acute Kidney Injury
case_summary: A 29-year-old manual labourer presented with a 4-day history of fever, myalgia, and oliguria. He had no chronic illnesses but was addicted to heroin. On admission, he was febrile (102  degreesF), icteric, and complained of severe myalgia. Vital signs: HR 110 b/min, BP 100/60 mmHg, SpO? 96% on room air. Laboratory studies showed WBC 13.1 ? 10?/L with neutrophilia, platelets 31 ? 10?/L, CRP 234 mg/L, and creatinine 189 umol/L. ECG revealed anterior T-wave inversions and a troponin I of 3 ng/mL. He was treated as severe leptospirosis with IV cefotaxime. On day 2, he developed hypotension, hemoptysis, and worsening renal failure (creatinine 245 umol/L) requiring ICU transfer. In the ICU, HR 115 b/min, BP 90/40 mmHg, mild LV dysfunction on echo, RR 32 breaths/min, bilateral alveolar shadows on chest X-ray, and normal coagulation. He received fluids, noradrenaline, plasma exchange, methylprednisolone, and dialysis. Leptospira microagglutination was positive. He improved over 5 days and was w

title: Impending splenic rupture as indicator of atypical chronic lymphocytic leukemia: A case report from Saudi Arabia
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 55-year-old man presented with a 4-month history of progressive fatigue, anorexia, and an unintentional 20-kg weight loss. He also reported intermittent left upper abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and increasing abdominal girth. He denied fevers, night sweats, bleeding, recent travel, illicit-drug use, or alcohol use. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. He appeared pale. No lymphadenopathy was noted. Abdominal examination revealed a firm, non-tender spleen palpated 22 cm below the left costal margin; the liver was not enlarged. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 81.6 fL, platelet count 108 ? 10^3/?L, white-cell count 6.8 ? 10^3/?L with 4.2 ? 10^3/?L lymphocytes, and normal renal and liver biochemistry. Lactate dehydrogenase was 250 U/L. Peripheral blood smear showed anisocytosis and poikilocytosis without circulating blast or smear cells. Tests for hepatitis B and C, HIV, EBV, and CMV were negative. Contrast-enhanced CT of t

title: Chronic Eosinophilic Leukemia--Not Otherwise Specified (NOS) in the Background of a Large Cell Lymphoma
case_summary: A 51-year-old woman presented with a two-month history of progressive dyspnea, fatigue, unintentional weight loss, and fevers. On examination, breath sounds were diminished over the right lung, and there was bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly. A right-sided pleural effusion was drained; fluid analysis showed an exudate containing CD30+ lymphoma cells. Excisional biopsy of a cervical lymph node revealed CD30+, CD20-, ALK- large cell lymphoma, with coexpression of CD79a, PAX-5, and MUM-1. Initial laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 178?10^9/L, hemoglobin 9.3 g/dL, and platelets 56?10^9/L. Differential counts demonstrated 4% neutrophils, 90% eosinophils (absolute count 160?10^9/L), 1% monocytes, 1% basophils, and 4% lymphocytes. Despite several weeks of hydroxyurea at 3000 mg/day, the eosinophil count remained elevated. Lactate dehydrogenase was 2450 U/L. PET/CT imaging showed extensive FDG uptake in the spleen, liver, and cervical, axillary, and mesente

title: Pembrolizumab-induced cytokine release syndrome in a patient with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma: a case report
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man with a history of hyperlipidemia and metastatic lung adenocarcinoma (PD-L1 > 50 %, EGFR exon 20 insertion) presented 19 days after his third cycle of pembrolizumab (given alone after prior chemoimmunotherapy and a dexamethasone taper). He had initially responded, with regression of lung, brain, and bone metastases. He now reported fever and chills.  
On arrival, temperature 102.0  degreesF, blood pressure 64/44 mm Hg, heart rate 87 beats/min, respiratory rate 35 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92 % on room air. He was lethargic with increased work of breathing.  
Laboratory studies revealed:  
? Ferritin > 40 000 ng/mL (normal 28-365)  
? LDH 3465 U/L (normal 87-241)  
? ESR 85 mm/h (normal 0-16)  
? CRP 281 mg/L (normal 0-3)  
? Acute kidney injury (creatinine 1.9 mg/dL)  
? Elevated AST 536 U/L and ALT 384 U/L  
? WBC 2200/uL with ANC 658/uL and ALC 579/uL, normal hemoglobin and platelets  
Chest radiograph showed the known right apical mass without new consolida

title: Catastrophic right-sided Candida empyema from spontaneous esophageal perforation
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man with hypertension, arthritis, seizure disorder, Parkinson's disease, hiatal hernia, and chronic anemia presented with three months of non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting, decreased oral intake, one month of progressive weakness, and new dyspnea that left him bed-bound by the day before presentation. He took excess NSAIDs for joint pain and had no history of alcohol, smoking, or illicit drugs. 

In the ED, he was hypotensive (65/30 mmHg), heart rate 84 bpm, respirations 20-22 /min, O? saturation 96% on 4 L, and afebrile. He appeared ill, with labored breathing and decreased right-sided breath sounds. Labs showed WBC 25.5 k/uL, hemoglobin 2.3 g/dL, mild ALT/AST elevations, and metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (venous pH 7.23, HCO? 13 mmol/L, albumin-corrected anion gap 21.3 mEq/L). 

Chest X-ray revealed a right-sided pneumothorax with >1 cm air gap and a moderate right-sided pleural effusion. CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed diverticulosis without retro

title: Gastric involvement of sarcoidosis in a patient with multiple lung nodules
journal: Journal of Research in Medical Sciences : The Official Journal of Isfahan University of Medical Sciences
case_summary:
A 56-year-old Caucasian woman presented with chest tightness, discomfort, and inability to take a deep breath. Two weeks earlier she had developed fever, vomiting, cough with scant purulent sputum, and dyspnea. She was treated empirically with oral levofloxacin for presumed pulmonary infection.  
   
Chest radiography and thoracic CT showed bilateral multiple small pulmonary nodules, predominantly in the left lower zone. Her medical history was notable for a 10-pack-year smoking history and remote cholecystectomy; family history included breast cancer. On examination, vital signs were normal, oxygen saturation was 98% on room air, and crackles were heard at the left lung base. An abdominal incision scar was noted.  
   
Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 11.3 g/dL with microcytic hypochromic anemia, white blood cell count 14.0?10^3/mm^3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 102 mm/h, C-reactive protein 81 mg/L, albumin 3.3 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 212 U/L, ?-glutamyl transpeptidase 80

title: Oxalate nephropathy induced by octreotide treatment for acromegaly: a case report
case_summary:
A 78-year-old Caucasian man was referred for evaluation of acute kidney injury. Twenty-five years earlier he had been diagnosed with acromegaly, initially treated with radiotherapy and for the past 10 years maintained on octreotide (10 mg every six weeks) with good control of IGF-1 levels. Over the preceding four months he had lost 15 kg without fever, dysuria, diarrhea, or other symptoms. Several months before admission, he received sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim and ciprofloxacin for a urinary tract infection; his serum creatinine rose and then partially improved, and was attributed to immuno-allergic nephritis, with an estimated GFR of 50 ml/min/1.73 m2. In the weeks before admission, multiple courses of penicillin and two cephalosporins over five weeks for a multiresistant Enterobacteria were followed by a further rise in creatinine.

On admission he was afebrile, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, pulse 72 beats/min, and examination was unremarkable without rash or lymphadenopathy. Labora

title: Atypical skin manifestation in severe acute chikungunya infection in a pregnant woman: a case report
case_summary:
An 18-year-old Thai woman, G1P0 at 31 weeks' gestation, presented with 6 hours of fever and severely painful swelling of her wrists, hands, and feet. She was unable to make a fist and had difficulty walking. She also reported a headache but denied nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or myalgia. A pruritic, erythematous rash was noted on her abdomen. She lived in a community where neighbors had experienced fever and arthralgia in the past week. She was not on any medications, had no recent hospitalizations, and no family members were ill.

On examination, temperature was 38.1  degreesC, blood pressure 100/40 mmHg, pulse 126 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Maculopapular rashes were present on the forehead, cheeks, nose, and abdominal wall. There was arthritis of both wrists, ankles, and all metatarsophalangeal joints. Arthrocentesis of the left wrist yielded clear yellow synovial fluid with high viscosity; Gram stain showed no organisms.

Labora

title: Macrophage Activation Syndrome/Secondary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis in Adult?Onset Still's Disease: An Uncommon Initial Presentation in a Young Nepalese Female: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 36?year?old Nepalese woman presented with one month of insidious, daily fevers to 104.5  degreesF with chills and rigors, generalized myalgias, polyarthritis involving more than five joints with swelling and stiffness during febrile episodes, and an evanescent pruritic pink rash over the limbs, trunk, and face sparing palms and soles that flared with fever. She reported 6 kg weight loss, anorexia, constipation, fatigue, sore throat, oral ulcers, hair loss, and dry eyes, but denied respiratory, gastrointestinal, or neurological symptoms. She had no significant medications or family history. On examination, she appeared ill, was febrile (104  degreesF), tachycardic, hypotensive, tachypneic, and hypoxemic (SpO2 90% on room air). Notable findings included bilateral eyelid swelling and erythematous macular rash on the face, trunk, and extremities.

Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 24,400/mm3 with 75% neutrophils, platelet count 209,000/mm3, ES

title: Frosted Branch Angiitis Secondary to Familial Mediterranean Fever Resembling Central Retinal Vein Occlusion
case_summary:
A 32-year-old woman presented with 2 days of progressive, painless vision loss in her left eye. Two months earlier, she had been diagnosed with familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) based on clinical features, family history, and laboratory findings and was started on oral colchicine. She had no other systemic or ocular disease.

On examination, the best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and light perception in the left. Intraocular pressures were normal bilaterally. Slit-lamp examination of the left eye revealed +1 cells in the anterior chamber and +1 cells in the anterior vitreous. Fundus examination of the left eye showed severe, continuous, white perivascular sheathing from the optic disc to the periphery, dilated and tortuous veins, intraretinal hemorrhages in all quadrants, papillary edema, and a subhyaloid hemorrhage over the macula. The right eye examination was normal.

Fluorescein angiography demonstrated dilated and tortuous veins, blockage of fluorescein by re

title: Tongue Necrosis as an Initial Manifestation of Giant Cell Arteritis: Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 68-year-old woman was evaluated first by her primary care physician for a moderate headache and swelling of the neck for four weeks; she was treated with antibiotics and nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs without improvement. Shortly thereafter, she presented to the emergency department with acute onset of severe pain and progressive swelling of the tongue that compromised her speech and swallowing. She denied fever, malaise, weight loss, vision changes, jaw claudication, and temporal tenderness. Her past medical history was negative for vasoconstrictor use, radiation therapy, cardiac arrest, or embolization. 

On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. The anterior half of the tongue was edematous and discolored, with limited movement; there was no temporal-artery tenderness. Hematologic studies showed mild leukocytosis (WBC 13.8???10^3/?L), anemia (Hgb 12.4?g/dL), thrombocytosis (platelets 414???10^3/?L), and neutrophilia. The comprehensive metabolic panel was unremark

title: Macrophage activation syndrome in a newborn: report of a case associated with neonatal lupus erythematosus and a summary of the literature
case_summary:
A female infant was born at 37 2/7 weeks' gestation to a 29-year-old mother with cutaneous lupus erythematosus and positive ANA, anti-ENA, anti-SSA, and anti-SSB antibodies. Pregnancy was complicated by fetal bradycardia at 20 weeks from complete AV block; dexamethasone was given without improvement. At birth, the infant weighed 2330 g (5th percentile) with APGAR scores 9 and 10; ECG confirmed third-degree AV block with junctional escape at 50 bpm. On day 6, an epicardial VVI pacemaker was placed due to circulatory insufficiency.

On day 8, the patient developed fever and erythema around the pacemaker wound. Blood and wound cultures were repeatedly sterile, but C-reactive protein rose to 80 mg/L (ref < 5). She received flucloxacillin and gentamicin, then ceftazidime after surgical exploration showed purulent infiltrates. Despite improving wound appearance, fever persisted and CRP normalized.

On day 22, a generalized non-pruritic eruption of round-to-oval erythematous papules and plaqu

title: Fatal Mucormycosis Post COVID-19 Infection in Uncontrolled Diabetes with Misuse of Glucocorticoids and Antibiotics
case_summary:
A 34-year-old woman with a history of gestational diabetes and COVID-19 infection 30 days earlier presented with 3 weeks of worsening left facial pain and recent loss of vision and movement on the left side. Her COVID-19 course lasted 10 days, during which she received multiple outpatient steroid injections. One week before presentation, she was treated with ceftriaxone 2 g daily and dexamethasone 4 mg three times daily for presumed facial nerve palsy without improvement. 

On admission, she was alert and hemodynamically stable. Examination showed periorbital edema, complete left-eye proptosis, a dilated nonreactive pupil, left-eye ophthalmoplegia, vision loss, hypoesthesia in the ophthalmic, maxillary, and mandibular distributions of the trigeminal nerve on the left, decreased left hearing, and a vesicular rash on the left cheek. 

Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, white blood cell count 14.5 K/uL (90% neutrophils), platelets 269 K/uL, creatinine 0.54 mg/dL, potassium

title: Dapsone?Induced Heinz Body Hemolytic Anemia: A Case Report
case_summary:
An 85-year-old man presented with one day of acute shortness of breath. His medical history included basal cell carcinoma, hypertension, and myasthenia gravis. His medications were amlodipine, losartan, pantoprazole, prednisone, and dapsone. One month earlier, he had a mechanical fall but did not seek care; since then he was less mobile due to back pain. He denied fever, cough, leg swelling, overt bleeding, or other symptoms.  
On examination: temperature 37 degreesC; blood pressure 126/76 mmHg; pulse 78/min; respiratory rate 18/min; SpO2 95% on room air; cardiac and pulmonary exams were unremarkable.  
Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL (baseline 13.0) with MCV 108 fL; platelets and leukocytes were normal; basic metabolic panel was unremarkable. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm; chest radiograph was normal.  
CT angiography of the chest revealed new right upper and middle lobe segmental pulmonary emboli, and he was started on a heparin infusion. Over the next 2 days his hemoglo

title: Abdominal Lymphonodular Cryptococcosis in an Immunocompetent Child
case_summary:
A 5-year-old girl was brought for evaluation of a 10-week history of fever, central abdominal pain, and anorexia. Ten weeks before presentation, she had developed high-grade continuous fever. After two weeks of persistent fever and nontender abdominal lymphadenopathy, she was started on empiric three-drug antituberculous therapy. By week 8 of therapy there was no clinical improvement, and she had lost 2.5 kg.

Her medical history was unremarkable; immunizations, including BCG, were up to date. There was no known exposure to tuberculosis or travel outside her hometown. On examination, she was febrile, icteric, and pale, with hepatomegaly; there was no peripheral lymphadenopathy.

Laboratory studies showed normochromic, normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 7.4 g/dL), progressive leukocytosis (from 21 to 32 ?10^9/L over 12 weeks), elevated ESR (135 mm/hr), CRP 29 mg/L, and direct hyperbilirubinemia (total bilirubin 4.6 mg/dL, direct 3.7 mg/dL). An episode of Escherichia coli urinary tract infect

title: Acute edematous pancreatitis caused by Fasciola hepatica infection: A rare case report
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman presented 17 hours after the onset of acute upper abdominal pain that was persistent, gnawing, and stabbing in character. She also had nausea and vomiting. She denied alcohol consumption, drug use, gallstone disease, or other significant medical history. On examination, she was afebrile with normal vital signs. Her abdomen was mildly distended with tenderness in the epigastric region but no guarding. Laboratory studies showed:  
? Lipase 6110 U/L (0-67 U/L), amylase 3060 U/L (0-90 U/L)  
? Normal liver enzymes, electrolytes, and lipid panel  
? White blood cell count 6.12 G/L with 26.1% eosinophils  
? Hemoglobin 111 g/L (mild normocytic anemia)  
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated multiple small subcapsular, confluent, ill-defined hypoechoic nodules in the liver. Contrast CT of the abdomen revealed slight dilation of the common and intrahepatic bile ducts, enlargement of the pancreatic head, and multiple round, clustered, hypodense lesions in the subcapsular liver.

title: Visceral Leishmania as Unusual Cause of Splenic Peliosis in the United States
case_summary: A 3-year-old Caucasian girl was referred for evaluation of splenomegaly. Six months earlier she had a febrile illness with cough, mouth sores, and cervical lymphadenopathy; empiric antibiotics resolved the fever and malaise, but her spleen remained enlarged. Over time she developed abdominal distention, pain, anemia, and thrombocytopenia. Initial evaluations for HIV, CMV, and EBV were negative, and gastrointestinal and metabolic causes were excluded. Abdominal ultrasonography and MRI showed a mildly prominent liver with diffuse multiple hypoechoic lesions and a markedly enlarged spleen with non-enhancing structures at the splenic hilum. On presentation she was playful and afebrile. Examination revealed a distended abdomen, splenomegaly extending to the superior iliac crest, and an umbilical hernia. Outside evaluation excluded hemolytic anemia (normal hemolytic anemia panel, hemoglobin electrophoresis, red blood cell enzymes, and osmotic fragility tests). Malignant infiltration was excl

title: Heatstroke?like symptoms in a patient with coronavirus disease pneumonia
case_summary: A 47-year-old man with a 5-year history of untreated type 2 diabetes mellitus (hemoglobin A1c 13% on admission) presented with a 4-day history of fever and dyspnea. Two days before admission, he tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 by RT-PCR. He denied known contact with COVID-19 cases. On admission, temperature was 38.3 degreesC, heart rate 90/min, blood pressure 132/83 mm?Hg, respiratory rate 24/min, and SpO2 93% on room air. Chest CT showed bilateral ground-glass opacities and patchy shadows consistent with COVID-19 pneumonia. On hospital day 2, his respiratory status worsened, and he was intubated and transferred to the ICU. From hospital days 6 to 8, he had remittent fevers of 37.5 degreesC to 40.4 degreesC. On day 9, the patient developed sustained fever of 40 degreesC to 41 degreesC unresponsive to acetaminophen, accompanied by marked disturbance of consciousness and hypotension with systolic pressures <70 mm?Hg, consistent with warm shock. His PaO2/FiO2 ratio declined from 202 to 140

title: A case of bovine ketoacidosis in a lactating woman
case_summary:
A 36-year-old woman, 5 weeks postpartum, presented with 48 hours of nausea and vomiting, plus 2 days of malaise and worsening dyspnea. She denied chest pain, palpitations, diarrhea, headaches, alcohol use, or illicit substances. To lose weight postpartum, she had been eating only small, high-protein, carbohydrate-free meals and had lost 30 lb since delivery. Initial vital signs were temperature 37  degreesC, blood pressure 140/63 mmHg, heart rate 84 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min; weight was 59.8 kg. She appeared unkempt and lethargic. Examination was otherwise benign except for blunted mentation. Her only medication was percocet for post-cesarean pain; her past history was notable for a cesarean section complicated by bladder injury, now resolved. 

Laboratory results showed: sodium 144 mEq/L, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, bicarbonate <5 mEq/L, BUN 3 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, glucose 133 mg/dL, lactate 2.1 mmol/L, phosphate 1.0 mg/dL, magnesium 1.6 mg/dL. Art

title: Opana-induced thrombotic microangiopathy masquerading as thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
case_summary: A 47-year-old Caucasian man with a history of substance abuse presented with four days of nausea, vomiting, watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, anuria, fatigue, and paresthesia of the extremities. His only outpatient medications were trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and clindamycin for an arm rash. On examination, he was hypotensive (67/48 mm Hg), tachycardic (115 beats/min), and had altered mental status. Petechiae were present on all extremities, and he had diffuse abdominal tenderness. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (35.2?10^3/?L), anemia (hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL), acute kidney injury (BUN 64 mg/dL; creatinine 5.3 mg/dL), and thrombocytopenia (21?10^3/?L). Stool tests and blood and sputum cultures were negative. Further evaluation revealed microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with an elevated lactate dehydrogenase (4,473 U/L), decreased haptoglobin (<10 mg/dL), indirect hyperbilirubinemia (2.2 mg/dL), and schistocytes on peripheral smear. A diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpu

title: When a Seemingly Harmless Prescription Turns into Toxicity
case_summary:
A 71-year-old African American man with a history of liver cirrhosis (post-orthotopic liver transplant), end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, peripheral vascular disease (right above-knee amputation), and seizure disorder was brought from his nursing home with altered mental status. Three days earlier, he had developed a shingles rash and was started on valacyclovir. He missed his scheduled hemodialysis session on the day of presentation. There was no fever, headache, convulsions, illicit drug use, or new medications other than valacyclovir.

On examination, he was afebrile, blood pressure 176/85?mmHg, alert but disoriented, with no meningeal signs or focal neurologic deficits. Skin exam showed crusted vesicles on an erythematous base over the lower back in an S1 dermatomal distribution. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal exams were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies: hemoglobin 10.0?g/dL, white blood cell count 2,770/uL, platelets 201,000/uL; liver-function tests normal; bl

title: Lupus Disguised as Chorea: Uncommon Presentation of a Common Disease
case_summary:
A preadolescent girl presented with a 2-week history of random, dance-like involuntary movements affecting first her left limbs and then all four limbs. The movements were not associated with changes in consciousness and were absent during sleep. There was no history of altered sensorium, seizures, joint pain, rash, ulcers, weight loss, fever, vomiting, headache, toxin exposure, or other neurologic symptoms. Four months earlier she had a self-limited sore throat and fever. 

On examination, she was well-oriented and well-nourished. Neurologic findings included generalized choreiform movements, a darting tongue, milkmaid's grip, pronator drift, and the spooning sign; the rest of the examination was normal.

Initial laboratory tests showed: 
? Total leukocyte count 3590/mm3 with lymphocytes 70%  
? Hemoglobin 11 g/dL, hematocrit 33%  
? Platelet count 166,000/mm3  
? Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 87 mm/h  
? Antistreptolysin O negative  
? Urea, creatinine, electrolytes within normal li

title: A distinct case of an 8-year-old female with cyclic neutropenia presenting with C. septicum abdominal sepsis and myonecrosis requiring a bowel resection and leg fasciotomy
case_summary:
An 8-year-old girl presented with a 3-day history of fever, diffuse abdominal pain, and coffee-ground emesis.  
On arrival she was afebrile, tachycardic, hypotensive, and had a rigid abdomen with features of septic shock.  
Laboratory studies showed venous pH 7.08, PCO? 47 mm Hg, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L, lactate 3.9 mmol/L; WBC 3.7?10?/L (ANC 0.96?10?/L); CRP 389 mg/L; INR 1.7; PTT 36 s; fibrinogen 5 g/L; creatinine 100 umol/L; urea 10 mmol/L.  
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a starry-sky appearance of the liver and moderate right-upper-quadrant free fluid.  
She was not fluid-responsive, so was started on an epinephrine infusion, ceftriaxone, and vancomycin, and transferred to a tertiary center.  
During transport, she developed right calf erythema with severe pain on palpation and dorsiflexion out of proportion to clinical findings; her CK was 10 000 U/L.  
CT of the right leg revealed extensive soft-tissue gas and myonecrosis.  
Abdominal CT showed loculated fluid, free air in the

title: A rare case of transient portal venous gas
case_summary: A 68-year-old woman with a history of stage IIIA ER+/PR+ breast cancer (post paclitaxel, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide), ischemic stroke with left-sided weakness, and atrial fibrillation presented with a 1-week history of decreased oral intake, nausea, vomiting, near-syncope, and altered mentation. She denied fever, abdominal pain, or diarrhea. On admission, she was afebrile, hypotensive, and tachycardic. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed: leukocytosis (24.5 k/uL), mild microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL; MCV 79.5 fL), thrombocytopenia (119 k/uL), BUN 26 mg/dL, creatinine 1.03 mg/dL (baseline 0.46 mg/dL), lactic acid 3.4 mmol/L, AST 53 U/L, ALT 67 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 133 U/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 3.3 mmol/L, albumin 2.2 g/dL, total protein 6.1 g/dL, normal total bilirubin (0.6 mg/dL), and bicarbonate 27 mmol/L. A noncontrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated extensive peripheral branching radiolucencies

title: Unmasking latent extrapulmonary tuberculosis with newly diagnosed HIV-1 infection in a COVID-19 patient with prolonged fever
case_summary:
A 33-year-old woman was admitted for evaluation of persistent fever.   
Twelve days earlier, she had a positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR on nasopharyngeal swab after presenting only with fever of 39 degreesC.  Over the subsequent 10 days, her fever continued, prompting admission on Day 0.  
She had immigrated from Nepal 1 year earlier, lived with her husband, and occasionally served restaurant customers; she denied known exposures to tuberculosis.  
On admission, her temperature was 37.6 degreesC, blood pressure 104/75 mmHg, heart rate 107 bpm, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air.  Physical examination, including lung auscultation, was unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.3 g/dL, C-reactive protein 3.33 mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 275 IU/L, D-dimer 6.1 ?g/mL, with otherwise normal counts and chemistries.  SARS-CoV-2 PCR remained positive.  
A chest radiograph was normal, but computed tomography revealed multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes.  
Deep venous thrombosis of

title: Gastroduodenal intussusception caused by gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary: An 84-year-old woman presented with one month of postprandial fullness, nausea, and occasional vomiting. She had a two- to three-year history of intermittent hematemesis, melena, and orthostatic hypotension. Her medical history included hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and hepatitis B infection. She denied tobacco, alcohol, or betel-nut use. On examination, she was alert, afebrile, with normal vital signs. The abdomen was flat, with normoactive bowel sounds, no tenderness, guarding, or rebound. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, WBC 9.37 ?10^9/L, platelets 302 ?10^3/uL, creatinine 1.53 mg/dL, BUN 25.9 mg/dL, CRP 2.04 mg/L, electrolytes within normal limits, and CEA 3.58 ng/mL. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 4.5 cm ? 4.6 cm mass with an alternating concentric echogenic "doughnut" sign. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy demonstrated tortuous distortion of gastric folds from the lesser curvature into the duodenal bulb, gastric outlet stenosis, and a friable ulcerated mass barel

title: Diagnostic challenges and management of kikuchi-fujimoto disease: a rare case report
case_summary:
A 42-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes mellitus presented with a 1?-year history of intermittent high-grade fevers, each episode lasting 7-10 days with 2-3-month symptom-free intervals, and a 15- to 20-kg unintentional weight loss. He described daily fevers up to 102 degreesF with rigors, chills, and night sweats unresponsive to oral antipyretics. He denied rashes, arthralgia, myalgia, or other systemic complaints, reporting only intermittent constipation, a mild cough, left shoulder pain, and nonspecific headaches. He had multiple prior hospitalizations; a prior EEG and neurologic evaluation were unremarkable. Medications included metformin PRN, glargine insulin, clonazepam at bedtime, and a recent 14-day course of meropenem. He had a ceftriaxone allergy with prior rash. He used naswar and smoked 2-3 cigarettes daily. 

On examination the temperature was 102 degreesF. A single 1?1-cm firm, nontender, fixed lymph node was palpable in the occipital region. The remainder of th

title: Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis in an eight year old male child: A case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: An 8-year-old African boy presented with a 3-month history of progressive left flank pain, malaise, and dysuria. He denied fever, anorexia, vomiting, or bowel symptoms. His history was notable for nephrolithiasis and multiple treated urinary tract infections. On examination, there was left-sided lower abdominal distension with percussion tenderness. Vital signs were: blood pressure 106/69 mmHg, pulse 98/min, temperature 37.8  degreesC, SpO? 96%. He had albinism, no pallor or lymphadenopathy, and the remainder of the exam was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL, ESR 28 mm/h, white-cell count 6.13 ? 10?/L, creatinine 94 ?mol/L, AST 40.6 U/L, ALT 11 U/L. Urinalysis revealed 10-15 WBCs/HPF but sterile culture. Chest radiograph was normal. Renal ultrasound showed an enlarged left kidney (13 ? 9 cm) with a tumorous appearance. Contrast CT of the abdomen revealed a complex left renal mass with a hypodense fluid component and peripheral hyperdense calcifications, entire

title: Rapidly Fatal Metastatic Cholangiocarcinoma Associated With Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease and Metabolic Syndrome: A Case Report Highlighting Emerging Risk Factors
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman presented with a 3-day history of intermittent, pressure-like epigastric and right upper quadrant abdominal pain that was relieved by eating. She denied nausea, vomiting, jaundice, melena, hematochezia, or changes in bowel habits. She had no known medical or surgical history, no regular medications or supplements, and no alcohol, tobacco, or drug use. She emigrated from Mexico at age 16, had no primary care, and reported no environmental or animal exposures. Her family history was negative for cancer and inflammatory bowel disease.

On examination, her body mass index was 36 kg/m2 and vital signs were within normal limits. Abdominal examination revealed hepatomegaly and moderate tenderness in the epigastric and right upper quadrant regions without rebound tenderness or guarding. An electrocardiogram was normal.

Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, renal chemistry, coagulation studies, thyroid function, troponin, lipase, urinalysis, and urine dru

title: Metastatic methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection with infective endocarditis, pulmonary septic emboli, axillary abscess with a subacute presentation in a patient with chronic end-stage kidney disease on maintenance haemodialysis
case_summary:
A man in his 40s with end-stage diabetic nephropathy on thrice-weekly maintenance haemodialysis via a cuffed, tunnelled right internal jugular catheter presented with 3 weeks of intermittent, low-to-moderate-grade fever accompanied by generalized weakness, night sweats, exertional dyspnoea, mild cough, and anorexia. He also reported 2 days of left axillary pain but denied chills, rigours, catheter-site symptoms, respiratory haemorrhage, gastrointestinal or urinary symptoms. His comorbidities included poorly controlled diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.5%), hypertension, anaemia, and coronary artery disease; a left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula had been created 1 month earlier. 

On examination, he was tachycardic (120 bpm), normotensive (110/76 mm?Hg), afebrile, with normal oxygen saturation. Lung auscultation revealed reduced breath sounds on the left; cardiac and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. The catheter exit site was clean without signs of infection. There was focal te

title: Acute HIV infection presenting as hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis: case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man with well-controlled hypertension presented with a one-week history of four to six non-bloody diarrheal stools per day associated with subjective fevers, sore throat, and dizziness. He denied recent travel, intravenous drug use, or alcohol use and was monogamous with his wife. On examination, his temperature was 38.0  degreesC, blood pressure 163/100 mm Hg, heart rate 76 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 93% on room air. He had erythematous tonsils without exudate, clinical oral candidiasis, mild gum bleeding, and a fluctuant, purulent draining mass on his left upper thigh. No rash, lymphadenopathy, or oral ulcers were noted; the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 159 g/L, white blood cell count 3.0 ? 10^9/L, platelets 50 ? 10^9/L, creatinine 160 umol/L, creatine kinase 25 000 U/L, lactate dehydrogenase 1739 U/L, ferritin 69 717 ug/L, D-dimer >10 mg/L, fibrinogen 2.0 g/L, and triglycerides 4.36

title: Postpartum Polymyositis Following Intrauterine Fetal Death
journal: Indian Journal of Critical Care Medicine : Peer-reviewed, Official Publication of Indian Society of Critical Care Medicine
case_summary: A 20-year-old primigravida was transferred 4 days after delivering a nonviable fetus at 24 weeks' gestation because of progressive respiratory distress. On arrival she was intubated. There was no past medical history and no pregnancy complications such as preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, or infection. The temperature was 101 degreesF, pulse 122/min, blood pressure 118/78 mm Hg, and Glasgow Coma Scale score E4M6Vt. No rash was observed. Bilateral lung crepitations were present. Chest radiograph showed bilateral infiltrates. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed poor left ventricular systolic function without right?side dilatation. She was treated for pneumonia, sepsis, acute kidney injury, and peripartum cardiomyopathy; her hemodynamic, pulmonary, and renal parameters improved but she continued to have high-grade fevers. On hospital day 5, she was noted to have gross symmetric weakness of the proximal muscles of all four limbs and dark-colored urine. Laboratory studies showed posit

title: Seizures provoked by over-the-counter cough and cold medications in an elderly patient: a case report
case_summary:
An 83-year-old Arabic man was brought to the emergency department after a transient episode of altered consciousness and urinary incontinence. Earlier that day he had symptoms of an upper respiratory tract infection (runny nose and cough) and had taken one tablet of loratadine/pseudoephedrine 10 mg/240 mg and two tablets of paracetamol/caffeine 500 mg/65 mg. His family found him snoring loudly in bed with wet sheets; on arousal he was confused, dizzy, and fell from the bed without trauma. While awaiting evaluation, he had a witnessed tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. He had no fever, recent travel, or sick contacts. His medical history was remarkable only for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension for 20 years; his medications included linagliptin, metformin, rosuvastatin, and lisinopril. He did not smoke or drink alcohol. 

On examination, vital signs were normal; he was pale and disoriented to person, place, and time, with no focal neurologic deficits or meningeal signs. Ini

title: A good excuse for skipping the test: electrical storm in a teenager
case_summary:
A 16-year-old male presented to the emergency department after losing consciousness during a school test. He described a brief prodrome of dizziness, and witnesses noted tonic-clonic movements during the event. His history included febrile seizures and parieto-occipital epilepsy in childhood, but he had been seizure-free and off medication for over a year. He also had mild cognitive impairment and cognitive visual dysfunction; family history was unremarkable.

While under evaluation, he experienced a second syncopal episode with spontaneous recovery. On examination, a cardiologist noted an irregular pulse and obtained a 12-lead ECG, which showed sinus rhythm, widespread J waves, a normal QT interval, and frequent monomorphic premature ventricular complexes with right bundle-branch-block morphology. Continuous telemetry captured frequent PVCs that degenerated into polymorphic ventricular tachycardia and ventricular fibrillation, requiring six defibrillation shocks over one hour. After i

title: SARS-CoV-2 and dengue virus coinfection in an adult with beta-thalassemia (trait): A case report from Bangladesh with literature review
case_summary: A 30-year-old man with no comorbidities and a body-mass index of 31.2 kg/m2, living in Dhaka, presented on hospital day 4 of a confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection with an 11-day history of fever (maximum 39.4  degreesC), 2 days of headache and generalized body ache, and one episode of vomiting fresh blood at home. He had been treated as an outpatient with paracetamol, ivermectin, doxycycline, famotidine, zinc, and vitamins C and D. On admission his temperature was 38.6  degreesC, pulse 76/min, blood pressure 110/79 mm Hg, respiratory rate 24/min, and SpO2 99% on room air. Examination was unremarkable without signs of dehydration. Chest X-ray showed ill-defined ground-glass opacities in the lower lung zones bilaterally, with an overall radiographic score of 2. Complete blood count revealed a leukocyte count of 5560/uL, platelet count of 170,000/uL, hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL with a mean corpuscular volume of 60 fL and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 30.3%, consistent with microcytic h

title: From an incidental lipoma to ileo-ileal intussusception in an adult: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with 4 hours of severe cramping abdominal pain in the periumbilical and right iliac regions, associated with dry heaving, nausea, and three episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. He reported a three-month history of milder, intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort attributed to indigestion. He denied fever, changes in bowel habits, hematochezia, flatus difficulty, weight loss, or urinary symptoms.

On examination, he was hemodynamically stable and afebrile. Abdominal examination revealed a soft, non-distended abdomen with tenderness on deep palpation in the periumbilical and right iliac regions; no masses, guarding, organomegaly, or ascites were noted.

Initial management included intravenous fluids, ondansetron, a proton-pump inhibitor, and analgesia, but his pain persisted. Differential diagnoses considered included acute gastritis, appendicitis, incarcerated or strangulated inguinal hernia, kidney stones, me

title: Toxic epidermal necrolysis-like toxic erythema of chemotherapy: 2 illustrative cases
case_summary: A 66-year-old woman with T-cell lymphoma complicated by cytomegalovirus and human herpesvirus 6 viremia presented with a bullous flexural eruption 3 weeks after initiation of cyclophosphamide and brentuximab. On examination, magenta papules coalesced into plaques with central dusky necrosis in flexural and dependent areas. The vulvar and perianal regions developed extensive large flaccid bullae with a positive Nikolsky sign over the buttocks. She subsequently developed fever, severe hypotension requiring vasopressors, acute respiratory distress syndrome, and diffuse anasarca. Laboratory testing revealed pancytopenia without eosinophilia, an elevated C-reactive protein level of 279.5 mg/L, ferritin >40 000 ug/L, elevated creatinine, mild transaminitis, and hypoalbuminemia. Biopsies from multiple sites showed a cell-poor vacuolar interface dermatitis with many single necrotic keratinocytes and numerous mitotic figures among keratinocytes, without epidermal thickening or crowding of kerat

title: Catastrophic presentation of peripartum cardiomyopathy: a case report of a challenging diagnosis
case_summary:
A 36-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of smoking and pectus excavatum, and no family history of sudden cardiac death or cardiovascular disease, suffered a cardiac arrest at home 3 months after an otherwise uneventful second pregnancy. She had experienced progressive post-partum fatigue but no infection or other stressors. Bystander CPR was initiated, and advanced life support lasted 70 minutes until return of spontaneous circulation. On arrival, she was intubated, mechanically ventilated, and receiving noradrenaline support.

On examination, she was afebrile, normotensive on vasopressors, with a normal body-mass index of 22.6 kg/m2, no cardiac murmurs, no pulmonary crepitations, no peripheral edema, and a prolonged capillary refill time.

A 12-lead ECG showed sinus rhythm, poor anteroseptal R-wave progression, diffuse ST-segment depression, and a normal QTc interval. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a dilated left ventricle with global hypokinesis, severe left-ventricular

title: Cryptogenic cirrhosis: Decoding diagnostic challenges through radiological insights
case_summary:
A 42-year-old previously healthy man presented with a 1-week history of progressive jaundice, abdominal distention, and fluctuating fatigue. He denied abdominal pain, pruritus, hematemesis, fever, pale stools, use of homeopathic or allopathic medications or supplements, IV drug use, needle-stick injuries, and any family history of liver disease. On examination, he had deep jaundice, grade 2 pedal edema, and tense ascites without abdominal tenderness; vital signs were within normal limits.  

Laboratory studies showed: AST 35 U/L, ALT 58.3 U/L, ALP 268 U/L, total bilirubin 31.6 mg/dL (direct 22.9 mg/dL), albumin 2.69 g/dL, prothrombin time 16 s, creatinine 2.17 mg/dL. Serum iron and ferritin were normal. Alpha-fetoprotein, ceruloplasmin, and urine copper were within normal ranges. Viral serologies (HBsAg, anti-HCV, HIV) were negative. Autoantibodies (ANA, ASMA, AMA, anti-LKM) were negative.  

Abdominal ultrasound showed a normal-sized liver with mild coarse echotexture, pleural effusio

title: Rapunzel Syndrome: A Rare Postpartum Case
case_summary:
A 22-year-old gravida 5 para 4 woman, four weeks postpartum, presented with a two-week history of progressive, constant, moderately severe epigastric pain radiating to her chest. The pain worsened with food intake and was accompanied by nausea, nonbloody vomiting, and diarrhea. She denied fever, chills, weight loss, abnormal vaginal discharge, chest pain, or shortness of breath. She also denied tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. Family history was notable only for hiatal hernia and diverticulosis.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness localized to the left upper quadrant without rebound or guarding; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies on admission showed a white-blood-cell count of 8500/?L, hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL, hematocrit 30.7%, and a platelet count of 217,000/?L. Electrolytes were within normal limits. Iron studies revealed iron 67 ng/mL, ferritin 17 ng/mL, total iron-binding capacity 256 ?g/

title: A case of toxic epidermal necrosis-like cutaneous eruption as the first manifestation and clue to the diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus: A case report
case_summary:
A 61-year-old woman presented with a 3-week history of a progressive, painful skin eruption that began on her torso and spread to her extremities. She reported anorexia, fatigue, and one episode of fever the day before presentation, but denied night sweats, chills, weight loss, recent infections, Raynaud's phenomenon, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, or serositis. Her medications included amiloride for hypertension, used for 10 years, with no recent new drugs.

On examination, she was afebrile, non-toxic-appearing, and hemodynamically stable. Skin examination showed widespread erythematous papules coalescing into dusky, annular plaques with central erosions, distributed over the trunk and extremities; the face, palms, and soles were spared. There was no mucosal involvement, joint findings, or lymphadenopathy.

On hospital day 2, despite remaining afebrile and stable, she developed peripheral sheet-like detachment and desquamation over 30% of her body surface area, with a positive Nikolsk

title: Case Report on Renal Failure Reversal in Lambda Chain Multiple Myeloma with Bortezomib and Dexamethasone
case_summary:
A 67-year-old Caucasian woman was brought to the hospital for acute changes in mental status characterized by confusion, transient aphasia, and a brief period of unresponsiveness. Over several days she had experienced generalized malaise, body aches, and fatigue. Her past medical history was notable for treated hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage III (baseline SCr 1.1 mg/dL), anemia of chronic disease, chronic back pain, and polymyalgia rheumatica treated with low-dose prednisone. Ten days earlier she had been hospitalized for viral gastroenteritis and had received intravenous contrast for an abdominal CT. She took ibuprofen intermittently for pain.

On examination she was lethargic but arousable, oriented only to person, with dry mucous membranes and diffuse abdominal and extremity tenderness. She was afebrile, blood pressure 84/53 mm Hg, pulse 104 bpm, respirations 16/min, and pulse oximetry 96% on room air.

Initial laboratory studies showed: WBC 18,900/uL without bands, hemo

title: Unilateral mydriasis in a child with a ventriculoperitoneal shunt for obstructive hydrocephalus: a diagnostic dilemma
case_summary:
A 3-year-old boy with extreme prematurity, quadriplegic cerebral palsy, global developmental delay, and a left-sided ventriculoperitoneal shunt for obstructive hydrocephalus was admitted with acute respiratory distress. He had a history of chronic lung disease and multiple prior admissions for lower respiratory tract infections associated with wheeze. On this admission, he tested positive for respiratory syncytial virus. Blood tests showed neutrophilic leukocytosis and a peak C-reactive protein of 78 mg/L; electrolytes, glucose, and blood gases (lactate peak 2.6 mmol/L) were stable. Chest radiography demonstrated minimal left mid-zone opacity with hyperinflation. 

He was treated with high-flow oxygen, then CPAP, regular salbutamol and ipratropium bromide nebulisations, and subsequently intravenous salbutamol and aminophylline infusions along with IV hydrocortisone. On day 4, he was noted to have unequal pupils: the right pupil was 3 mm with a sluggish light reaction, and the left pupi

title: Addressing the Challenges to Diagnose Neonatal Hemolytic Anemia's Aetiologies in Low Ressources Countries: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 3-month-old male infant was referred for evaluation of anemia. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy. At 5 days of age he developed neonatal jaundice, and at 1 and 2 months he had two episodes of acute anemia following febrile upper respiratory symptoms, each requiring red-cell transfusion. During the second episode he received paracetamol and a 5-day course of amoxicillin; a malaria smear was negative, hemoglobin electrophoresis showed no hemoglobin S, and the hemoglobin F fraction was 12% (normal for age). 

At 3 months of age, he was in good general condition but pale, with a palpable spleen; there was no jaundice. Vital signs were normal. Laboratory studies showed anemia with evidence of hemolysis. A thin blood smear demonstrated parasite inclusions in 11% of red cells identified as Plasmodium falciparum, and a rapid test was positive for P. falciparum. The mother's smear was also positive. Intravenous artesunate was given to both mother and infant for 3 days, then

title: Concurrent reactive arthritis, Graves' disease, and warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia: a case report
case_summary: Patient is a 40-year-old man with a history of reactive arthritis, Graves' disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, mitral valve prolapse, and Gilbert's disease who presented with one month of progressive jaundice, fatigue, lightheadedness, and exertional dyspnea. He described dark urine and dark brown to black stools. He denied fevers, chills, or recent transfusions and had no history of hepatitis, alcohol or intravenous drug use. His medications included etanercept, methimazole, niacin, and aspirin; he had discontinued metformin and pioglitazone more than one month before symptom onset. Family history was notable for autoimmune hemolytic anemia in his mother and anemia of unknown cause in his maternal grandmother. On examination, he was jaundiced with scleral icterus; vital signs showed heart rate 102 beats per minute and normal blood pressure, respiratory rate, and temperature. There was no lymphadenopathy. The thyroid gland was non-tender without enlargement or exophthalmos. Cardiopulmon

title:
Hyperleucocytosis as an unusual presentation of hypereosinophilic pneumonitis
with acute respiratory distress syndrome
case_summary:
A previously healthy 5-year-old boy presented with a 2-week history of fever, cough, and progressive shortness of breath. On examination, he was tachypneic and hypoxic, with bilateral diffuse wheezes and crepitations. His past history included occasional ER visits for wheezing responsive to albuterol; there was no personal or family history of asthma or atopy, and he was on no regular medications.

Initial laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 194,000 cells/mm3 with 50% eosinophils, rising to 224,000 cells/mm3 over 24 hours; there was concomitant anemia and thrombocytopenia. A peripheral smear revealed marked eosinophilia with reactive lymphocytes and no blast cells. Lactate dehydrogenase was elevated at 785?U/L. Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral diffuse alveolar opacities progressing rapidly to a "white-out" consistent with early acute respiratory distress syndrome; echocardiography showed normal cardiac function. Urgent leukopheresis was performed.

Despite this inte

title: An autopsy case of acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia with periorbital MRSA cellulitis and rheumatoid arthritis
case_summary: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis presented with 1 week of bilateral periorbital edema and 3 days of fever and dyspnea. His medications for rheumatoid arthritis included methotrexate, iguratimod, and prednisone. On admission, his temperature was 40.3  degreesC, and he required 3 L/min of nasal oxygen. Examination revealed marked bilateral eyelid swelling with blood-tinged conjunctival discharge; lung auscultation was clear. Contrast-enhanced head CT showed conjunctival thickening with enhancement, consistent with periorbital cellulitis. Chest radiography demonstrated diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities, and non-contrast chest CT confirmed extensive bilateral ground-glass opacities with a focal consolidation in the left lower lobe. Urinalysis revealed occult blood and proteinuria. Laboratory tests showed a WBC count of 12,100/?L with 72.5% neutrophils, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 161,000/?L, AST 44 IU/L, ALT 23 IU/L, LDH 391 IU/L, CRP 12.2 mg/dL, K

title: Postinfectious Glomerulonephritis with Crescents in an Elderly Diabetic Patient after Acute Gastroenteritis: Case Report
journal: Case Reports in Nephrology and Dialysis
case_summary:
A 75-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of loose stools, nausea, vomiting, and decreased urine output, and one episode of high-grade fever. He denied sore throat, skin infection, joint pain, respiratory or urinary tract symptoms, smoking, alcohol use, and NSAID use. His medical history included type 2 diabetes mellitus for 10 years and hypertension for 2 years. Funduscopic examination showed evidence of diabetic retinopathy.

Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, leukocyte count 12,700/mm3, platelet count 280,000/mm3; urinalysis showed 3+ protein, 30-40 white blood cells/high-power field, 50-60 red blood cells/high-power field, and sterile urine culture. Stool culture grew Escherichia coli. Serum albumin was 3.1 g/dL; sodium 134.4 mEq/L; potassium 4.1 mEq/L; calcium 8.8 mg/dL; phosphorus 5.2 mg/dL; random blood sugar 182 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 103 mg/dL; creatinine 7.3 mg/dL. Serologic tests showed anti-streptolysin O titer <110 IU/mL, complement C3 21.0 mg/dL

title: AIDS-Associated BK Virus Nephropathy in Native Kidneys: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 28-year-old Caucasian man with HIV diagnosed 10 years earlier presented with a 1-month history of fever and diarrhea. He was cachectic and febrile, with a temperature of 103 degreesF, a heart rate of 150 beats per minute, a blood pressure of 145/95 mm Hg, and a body-mass index of 18 kg/m2; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests revealed a serum creatinine level of 3.0 mg/dL (baseline, 0.9 mg/dL), a protein-to-creatinine ratio of 2.4, and a urinalysis with a specific gravity of 1.008 and no red cells or white cells. The absolute CD4+ T-lymphocyte count was 1 cell/?L (0.001?10^9/L). Additional studies showed a white-cell count of 3800?10^6/L, a hemoglobin level of 7.4 g/dL, a platelet count of 107?10^9/L, a serum sodium concentration of 146 mmol/L, a serum potassium concentration of 4.8 mmol/L, and a blood urea nitrogen level of 27 mg/dL. Renal ultrasonography demonstrated bilaterally echogenic kidneys without hydronephrosis. Hemodynamically mediat

title: Unexpected severe malaria in a postoperative patient, New York, USA
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia on aspirin suffered a mechanical fall with head trauma and was found to have a left subdural hematoma.
