He underwent craniotomy and hematoma evacuation on September 18 and was transferred for postoperative care on September 21. His postoperative course was uneventful: he was afebrile, CBC and liver enzymes remained normal, and he did not require blood transfusion. Metformin was started for new diabetes and levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis; he continued losartan, metoprolol, and atorvastatin. He was discharged home on September 24.

He returned to the ED on October 1 with a 4-day history of fever, malaise, and dark urine. On presentation, temperature was 100.8  degreesF, pulse 92/min, blood pressure 98/62 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. He was alert and oriented, with an unremarkable exam; the craniotomy incision was clean and dry and there were no rashes or lesions. Laboratory studies showed elevated lactate and

title: An unrecognised case of metabolic acidosis following neobladder augmentation cystoplasty
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man presented to the emergency department with dyspnea at rest and right loin pain eight weeks after augmentation of an ileal neobladder. His past medical history included polycystic kidney disease, and he was receiving oral antibiotics for a presumed urinary tract infection. He had undergone radical cystoprostatectomy and Studer pouch reconstruction one year earlier and neobladder augmentation cystoplasty three months before presentation.

On examination, he appeared cachectic with dry mucous membranes. His respiratory rate was 26 breaths per minute, heart rate 108 beats per minute, and blood pressure 92/64 mmHg. Cardiorespiratory and abdominal examinations were otherwise unremarkable, and his surgical incision was well healed.

Initial blood tests showed a leukocyte count of 14.1 ? 10^3/?L and a C-reactive protein of 23.3 mg/dL. Biochemistry revealed a potassium of 3.3 mmol/L, creatinine of 304 ?mol/L (baseline 98 ?mol/L), and urea of 33.1 mmol/L; all other electrolytes

title: Hemoadsorption Treatment with CytoSorb? in Probable Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis: A Role as Adjunctive Therapy?
case_summary:
A 76-year-old man was admitted to the ICU with abdominal septic shock due to a perforated colon. An obstructive colonic adenocarcinoma with metastatic lymph node spread was diagnosed during an emergency hemicolectomy. Postoperatively, he received meropenem, vancomycin, and supportive care, and shock initially resolved. On day 3, he developed persistent high fever (?40 degreesC), altered level of consciousness (GCS 7/15), severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL), and thrombocytopenia (platelets 20 G/L) despite multiple transfusions and hemodynamic support. Blood cultures remained negative. He subsequently experienced hemorrhagic shock from a massive right hemothorax, requiring fluid resuscitation and high-dose norepinephrine (?0.34 ?g/kg/min).  
Differential diagnoses were evaluated:  
? Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia  
? Disseminated intravascular coagulation  
? Thrombotic microangiopathy  
? Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome  
Laboratory studies revealed ferritin 14,299 ng/mL, trig

title: Congenital chloride diarrhoea in a Chinese infant with a compound heterozygous SLC26A3 mutation
case_summary:
A female infant, the second child of healthy nonconsanguineous parents, was noted antenatally to have polyhydramnios. She was born at 37 weeks' gestation, weighing 2500 g (Apgar scores 10 at 1, 5, and 10 min). Beginning on the day of birth, she passed 3-6 liquid stools per day and had abdominal distension despite routine antidiarrhoeal therapy and lactose-free formula. Over the first 6 months, feeding of breast milk and cow's milk was associated with very slow weight gain; at 5 months she could raise her head, but by 7 months she could not sit unaided and weighed only 6 kg. There was no family history of protracted infantile diarrhea.

At 7 months of age, she was admitted with more than 10 watery stools per day and severe dehydration. Blood pressure was 84/45 mmHg, heart rate 162 beats/min, and respiratory rate was increased. She appeared dehydrated and malnourished, with a distended abdomen and active bowel sounds; lung fields were clear. Laboratory studies showed haemoglobin 11 g/dL,

title: Fever, pulmonary interstitial fibrosis, and hepatomegaly in a 15-year-old boy with Gaucher disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 15-year-old Han Chinese boy presented with a 2-week history of fever, weakness, pallor, edema, dyspnea, and progressively increasing abdominal girth. He was the product of a full-term, non-consanguineous pregnancy with markedly slow growth and development, but good school performance. At age 9, he underwent splenectomy for an unexplained splenomegaly. Since then he had multiple hospital admissions for fever of unknown origin without a clear diagnosis.

On examination, his height was 135 cm and weight 30 kg, with no secondary sexual characteristics. His temperature fluctuated between 37.2  degreesC and 39.7  degreesC. He appeared pale and distressed. There was no jaundice or rash. Superficial inguinal lymph nodes were palpable. He had periorbital edema, large bilateral tonsils without pharyngeal congestion, harsh breath sounds with bilateral crackles, and a heart rate of 114 beats/min without murmur. His abdomen was distended; the liver was firm, nontender, and palpable 10 cm below th

title: Cronkhite-Canada Syndrome: Sustained Clinical Response with Anti-TNF Therapy
case_summary:
A 76-year-old man was referred for evaluation of an 18-month history of chronic nonbloody watery diarrhea and unintentional weight loss of approximately 57 kg. He noted progressive onycholysis of the hands and feet and hyperpigmentation of the palms, soles, legs, and abdomen over the same interval. He also reported hypogeusia, early satiety, chronic heartburn, and intermittent nonspecific abdominal discomfort. He denied fever, night sweats, or overt gastrointestinal bleeding. His medical history included prostate cancer treated in 2012, gout, a remote transient ischemic attack, osteoarthritis, and cataracts. An outpatient workup was negative for recurrent malignancy, autoimmune disease, celiac sprue, and pancreatic insufficiency. 

On examination, he appeared cachectic (weight 50.9 kg; BMI 16.5) with marked sarcopenia. He had nonscarring scalp alopecia; dystrophic nails on hands and feet; diffuse hyperpigmentation of the palms, dorsal fingers, face, and limbs; and loss of abdominal, ax

title: Benign metastasizing leiomyoma, a rare imposter of metastatic cervical cancer
case_summary:
A 45-year-old G7P4 Micronesian woman presented with one week of heavy intermenstrual vaginal bleeding and symptomatic anemia. Her history included a 32-pack-year smoking history and no prior cervical cancer screening; she had two Cesarean deliveries in 2015-2016. On exam, moderate blood was noted in the vaginal vault, with a foul-smelling discharge and a necrotic upper vaginal mass. 

CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a diffusely enlarged cervix and a distended uterus containing enhancing nodular endometrial masses and fluid, bladder distension with mild bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, and incidental numerous nodules at the lung bases. Chest CT revealed more than 30 round, discrete pulmonary nodules scattered bilaterally, the largest measuring 1.2 cm in the right upper lobe. 

Because of ongoing bleeding and a hemoglobin of 7 g/dL, she received two units of packed red blood cells and was referred to gynecologic oncology. Over the following days, she developed urinary retention requi

title: Successful treatment of suspect Babesia?induced ARDS in a dog using lung?protective positive?pressure ventilation and neuromuscular blockade
case_summary:
An 11?week?old intact male mixed?breed dog was referred for severe anemia and suspected babesiosis. Three weeks earlier, he had been transported from Texas. One week after transport, he developed acute lethargy and fever (40.4 degreesC). In?house CBC showed severe microcytic, normochromic, non?regenerative anemia (HCT 0.147?L/L; RI 0.373-0.617?L/L) and severe thrombocytopenia (23???10^9/L; RI 148-484???10^9/L). He received 15?ml/kg pRBC over 4?h and was discharged on omeprazole, sucralfate, and amoxicillin/clavulanate. The next day, PCV was 23%. Eight days later, he was lethargic with PCV 15%; a blood smear was suspicious for Babesia, and clindamycin was started. When he became too weak to stand, he was referred.

On presentation, the dog was nonambulatory, pale, mildly icteric, and splenomegaly was noted. Weight was 5.75?kg; temperature 38.2 degreesC; heart rate 150?beats/min; respiratory rate 60?breaths/min. Point-of-care testing showed PCV 14%, total protein 5.4?g/dL, and compensate

title: Bloom syndrome with myelodysplastic syndrome that was converted into acute myeloid leukaemia, with new ophthalmologic manifestations: the first report from Syria
case_summary:
A 22?year?old Syrian man presented with a 2?week history of recurrent fever, intermittent vomiting, aphthous ulcers, fatigue, polyuria, and polydipsia. He had no significant weight loss or anorexia. His medical history was notable for low birth weight, sparse subcutaneous tissue, short stature, high?pitched voice, orchiopexy for undescended testes at age 10, recurrent chest and middle?ear infections, and gastroesophageal reflux disease. His parents are first cousins; a sister has proportionate short stature and similar facial features.  

On examination, he weighed 33 kg and was 146 cm tall (BMI 15.5 kg/m2). Vital signs were normal except for a respiratory rate of 26 breaths/min and temperature 37.5  degreesC. He had dolichocephaly, a prominent nose, and pale conjunctivae. Ophthalmologic findings included right?sided lateral strabismus, amblyopia, and bilateral lower?eyelid ectropion; pupils, lenses, media, and fundi were unremarkable. A sun?sensitive malar rash with telangiectasias, c

title: Spinal Cord Compression as the First Presentation of Primary Hyperoxaluria in a Patient With Kidney Failure: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman was admitted with an 18-month history of intermittent back pain radiating to her lower limbs, which over the past 5 months became severe and constant. Two weeks before admission she became unable to walk and had numbness up to the xiphoid process without sphincter incontinence.

Her history was notable for cholecystectomy at age 40; two episodes of nephrolithiasis (one treated with lithotripsy at age 42); hypothyroidism, hypertension, and end-stage kidney disease diagnosed at age 46 treated with twice-weekly hemodialysis; and longstanding anemia resistant to iron and erythropoietin requiring transfusions. Medications included levothyroxine, methyldopa, folic acid, erythropoietin, and iron supplements.

On examination she had conjunctival pallor, grade-2 pretibial edema, hepatomegaly, and splenomegaly. Neurologic examination revealed normal cranial nerves and upper limbs; lower limbs had strength 3+/5, tone normal to flaccid, absent reflexes, bilateral flexor plantar

title: A case of hypoglossal nerve palsy with evolving cranial nerve involvement in renal cell carcinoma: a case report
case_summary:
A 14-year-old previously healthy female of Indian descent presented with 3 weeks of mild left posterior auricular, "nummular" headache, gradually progressive left-sided neck pain, and dysphagia without dysarthria. She denied fever, weight loss, vision or hearing changes, weakness, sensory changes, neck stiffness, abdominal pain, or hematuria.  
On examination, she had isolated leftward tongue deviation. Two days into admission, she also developed left-sided uvular deviation. A dysphagia study showed oropharyngeal impairment.  
Laboratory studies revealed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 43 mm/hour and mild normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL); other blood counts, metabolic panel, C-reactive protein, and urinalysis were normal.  
Brain MRI, cervical spine MRI, and CT angiogram of the head and neck (all with and without contrast) were unremarkable. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed normal opening pressure, cell count, glucose, and protein, with no bacterial growth on culture.

title: Severe distributive shock, neutrophilic dermatosis, and ST-elevation myocardial infarction in the setting of azathioprine hypersensitivity syndrome
journal: Allergy, Asthma, and Clinical Immunology : Official Journal of the Canadian Society of Allergy and Clinical Immunology
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man with longstanding fibrostenotic Crohn's disease presented with a one-day history of fever and abdominal pain. One week earlier, he had a surveillance colonoscopy showing progression of his disease, and azathioprine 125 mg daily was started after TPMT levels were found to be intermediate. His history included iron deficiency and benign prostatic hyperplasia; he had no known allergies. Current medications were azathioprine 50 mg daily, budesonide, tamsulosin, and ferrous gluconate.

On admission, he was febrile, tachycardic, and hypotensive; intravenous fluids were given. A working diagnosis of sepsis led to empiric ceftriaxone. Chest radiograph, blood and urine cultures, and CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed no source of infection. Azathioprine was held. Over the next three days, he remained febrile with persistent tachycardia, progressed to distributive shock and acute renal failure, and required piperacillin-tazobactam, vancomycin, stress-dose methylprednisolone,

title: Mesenteric Panniculitis Presenting as Fever of Unknown Etiology in a Patient with History of Abdominal Surgery
case_summary:
A 46-year-old woman was evaluated for a 2-year history of intermittent fevers (up to 38.9  degreesC), fatigue, and frequent urination. Episodes were accompanied by muscle weakness, joint stiffness, and severe headache lasting about 30 hours. She denied chest or abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits, or weight loss.  
Her medical history included thalassemia, dermatographism, and three early miscarriages. Surgical history was notable for a gastric sleeve and a hysterectomy complicated by a pelvic abscess treated with drainage 4 months earlier. Her family history was positive for autoimmune diseases (gout, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus).  
On examination, temperature was 36.6  degreesC, and the abdomen was soft with no tenderness. Laboratory studies showed a microcytic anemia with occasional schistocytes; results of a comprehensive metabolic panel, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, HIV assay, Quantiferon tuberculosis test, blood cultures, lactate

title: Concomitant Polycythemia Vera and Primary Splenic Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma Complicated by Gastrosplenic Fistula: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 72-year-old man with JAK2 V617F-positive polycythemia vera and possible early post-polycythemia myelofibrosis reported 3 months of left upper quadrant abdominal pain, a 30 lb weight loss, early satiety, poor appetite, and fatigue. Three months earlier, CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a predominantly cystic/solid splenic mass measuring 10.7 ? 9.7 ? 13.6 cm; hematoma, malignancy, or an abscess was considered, with abscess thought less likely. Repeat CT demonstrated interval growth to 14 ? 11.5 ? 15.4 cm and perisplenic stranding. PET-CT 1 week later revealed a hypermetabolic, necrotic splenic lesion, extensive upper abdominal lymphadenopathy, and a sternal lesion concerning for malignancy; possible gastrosplenic fistula was uncertain. He denied fever, chills, night sweats, bleeding, or trauma. Vital signs were normal. Examination showed fatigue, significant splenomegaly, and left upper quadrant tenderness. Laboratory studies revealed microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL), thrombo

title: Acute necrotizing encephalopathy in adult patients with influenza: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary: A 44-year-old Taiwanese woman with a history of thyroid goiter presented in August with a 3-day history of headache, fever, sore throat, cough, and rhinorrhea. On day 3, a rapid throat swab confirmed influenza A infection, and inhaled zanamivir was initiated. The next day, she developed sudden alteration of consciousness and recurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures and was brought to the emergency department. On examination, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 6 (E1V1M4); temperature, 37.2  degreesC; blood pressure, 112/52 mm Hg; heart rate, 95 beats/min. Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 7100/?L (neutrophils 87.7%), C-reactive protein 7.89 mg/L, aspartate aminotransferase 41 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L, ammonia 72 ?g/dL, and creatinine 0.61 mg/dL. Noncontrast head CT revealed multiple subcortical hypodense lesions in the bilateral cerebral hemispheres. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 1 monocyte/?L, 1080 erythrocytes/?L, protein 77 mg/dL, glucose 84 mg/dL, and

title: Relapsed angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma presenting as psoriasiform dermatitis
case_summary: A 72-year-old man with a history of systemic angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma, recently completing six cycles of brentuximab-CHP and two cycles of ICE therapy one week before presentation, was admitted with worsening dyspnea and a new pruritic rash of two weeks' duration. He denied pain, oral lesions, fevers, chills, or weight loss. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 18.2 ? 10^3/uL with 49% lymphocytes and a hemoglobin level of 8.8 g/dL; the metabolic panel and respiratory viral panel were unremarkable, and blood cultures showed no growth. On examination, red-pink macules and thin papules coalesced into thin plaques and patches on the chest, arms, abdomen, and back, some with trailing scale and islands of sparing; a diffuse red patch with scale involved the face and frontal scalp. Given the exam findings of plaques with intermittent trailing scale and recent chemotherapy initiation, diagnoses of morbiliform drug eruption, pityriasis rubra pilaris, psoriasis, and pityrias

title: Metformin-associated lactic acidosis presenting as an ischemic gut in a patient who then survived a cardiac arrest: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old Caucasian woman with a 15-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with a 3-week history of generalized malaise, poor oral intake, and intermittent diarrhea. Her medical history included hypertension, asthma, and depression. Medications were metformin 3 g daily, modified-release gliclazide 60 mg daily, aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, ramipril 10 mg daily, and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily.

Prehospital vital signs: Glasgow Coma Scale score 15, blood glucose 2.8 mmol/L, blood pressure 90/40 mmHg, pulse 54 beats/min, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on 8 L oxygen via mask. On arrival, she was confused (GCS 14) with similar hemodynamics.

Laboratory studies: Na 140 mmol/L, K 7.3 mmol/L, Cl 91 mmol/L, HCO3 1 mmol/L, anion gap 55 mmol/L, glucose 2.3 mmol/L, urea 30.8 mmol/L, creatinine 768 umol/L; normal liver function tests; troponin 50 ng/L. Venous blood gas: pH 6.58, PCO2 38.6 mmHg, HCO3 3.6 mmol/L, glucose 2.0 mmol/L,

title: Ameboma: an unusual cause of gastrointestinal bleeding during severe leptospirosis
case_summary: A 60-year-old male smoker from rural Reunion Island, who worked barefoot in sugar cane fields, was admitted after one week of fever, intense asthenia, diffuse pain, myalgia, headache, oliguria, and jaundice. On examination, he was icteric and acutely confused without focal neurologic signs or meningeal features; no hemorrhagic signs were noted. Laboratory tests showed acute renal failure (creatinine 1022 umol/L), hyponatremia (126 mmol/L), thrombocytopenia (43 000/mm3), leukocytosis (19 600/mm3), mild cytolysis (ALT 213 IU/L), severe hyperbilirubinemia (> 500 umol/L), and rhabdomyolysis (CPK 4475 IU/L). Prothrombin ratio was 70% (normal), APTT was prolonged at 59 s, fibrinogen was elevated (8.9 g/L), LDH and haptoglobin were normal, and no schistocytes were seen. CSF analysis showed WBC 20/mm3 and protein 2.5 g/L. Chest radiograph and abdominal ultrasonography were unremarkable. Blood and urine PCR confirmed leptospirosis, and serology was positive for Leptospira interrogans IgM (titer

title: Neonatal lupus erythematosus--a rare syndrome of transient autoimmunity
case_summary:
A 3-week-old female infant was referred for evaluation of an annular rash on her face and trunk that was initially thought to be tinea corporis. The lesions first appeared at 2 weeks of age and increased in size over the following week. She was feeding well and gaining weight appropriately. The baby was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy to a 26-year-old healthy mother (gravida 2, para 1) with a history of one previous spontaneous abortion. Prenatal laboratory studies and fetal ultrasonography were normal. Delivery was spontaneous vaginal; birth weight was 3060 g, length 50 cm, and Apgar scores were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. In the nursery, she received antibiotics for a presumed nonspecific neonatal infection and was discharged on day 5 with mild thrombocytopenia deemed transient.

On examination at 3 weeks, the infant appeared well but was pale. Multiple discrete and confluent annular lesions measuring 1-3 cm with central hypopigmentation were present on the face, anteri

title: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms Syndrome in a Child with Cystic Fibrosis
case_summary:
A 2.5-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis complicated by pancreatic insufficiency, bilateral bronchiectasis, and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization was admitted with a 2-day history of fever and cough. He was started on intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam (100 mg/kg/day in three divided doses) and amikacin (30 mg/kg/day once daily) based on prior sputum cultures; there was no prior history of drug reactions. Home airway clearance, pancreatic enzyme replacement, and nutritional support were continued.  

Over the first 17 days, his respiratory symptoms gradually improved. On hospital day 18, he developed a high-grade fever to 39  degreesC. On day 19, he developed a diffuse, pruritic, erythematous maculopapular rash that began on the face and trunk and rapidly spread over his entire body; facial edema was noted. Physical examination revealed no lymphadenopathy, no mucous membrane involvement, and no organomegaly.  

Laboratory studies showed:  
? WBC 4.1 ? 10^9/L with ANC 0.9 ? 10

title: Mesenteric lymphadenitis as a presenting feature of Whipple's disease
case_summary:
A 39-year-old African American man presented with a 1-week history of gnawing, constant epigastric pain that began after dining out. He denied fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. His medical history was notable for a 7-year history of chronic arthritis affecting the hands, feet, and back, for which he took naproxen as needed. He reported poor appetite and a 30-lb weight loss over the past 3 months. He emigrated from Cape Verde 20 years earlier, did not smoke or use illicit drugs, rarely drank alcohol, and had no relevant family history. 

On examination, temperature was normal, blood pressure 112/70 mm Hg, pulse 111 bpm, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Abdominal examination revealed a soft, flat abdomen with normoactive bowel sounds and tenderness localized to the epigastrium without rebound or guarding. There was no hepatosplenomegaly or palpable masses.

Laboratory studies showed microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, mean corpuscular vol

title: METASTATIC ANGIOSARCOMA PRESENTING AS DIFFUSE ALVEOLAR HEMORRHAGE
journal: Lung India : Official Organ of Indian Chest Society
case_summary:
A 26-year-old man was admitted with two months of recurrent hemoptysis and progressive dyspnea. He had no fever or weight loss. Three weeks earlier he had been treated empirically for disseminated tuberculosis (pulmonary and pericardial) with anti-tubercular therapy and oral steroids after presenting elsewhere with hypotension, diffuse bilateral opacities on chest radiograph, and pericardial effusion. 

On examination he was afebrile; blood pressure 120/80 mm Hg; pulse 80 /min; respiratory rate 18 /min. He had grade II digital clubbing and scattered crackles over both infrascapular areas; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 13 g/dL; leukocyte count 7600/mm3 (64% neutrophils); platelets 210 000/mm3. Blood glucose, renal and liver function tests, electrolytes, urinalysis, coagulation studies (bleeding time, clotting time, prothrombin time) were normal. HIV serology was negative. Peripheral smear revealed a microcytic hypochromic pictu

title: Thrombocytopenia?associated multiorgan failure occurring in an infant at the onset of type 1 diabetes successfully treated with fresh frozen plasma
case_summary:
A 13-month-old girl with no prior health issues was found unresponsive at home following 2 days of vomiting and 1 day of lethargy. Paramedics recorded a Glasgow Coma Score of 3, and she was intubated and transferred to a regional hospital. A noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. On admission she was hypothermic (core temperature 33.2  degreesC). Initial laboratory studies revealed:  
? Arterial pH 6.6, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L  
? Urine ketones positive; blood glucose 33 mmol/L  
? Platelet count 99 ? 10^9/L; hemoglobin 106 g/L; creatinine 82 ?mol/L  
? Fibrinogen, prothrombin time, and activated partial thromboplastin time within normal limits  

She was managed according to DKA protocols and transferred to a tertiary pediatric ICU. Over the next 48 hours, serum ketones normalized and blood glucose ranged 6-12 mmol/L, but metabolic acidosis persisted and she remained obtunded without fever. By day 7 she developed oliguria with urea 27.5 mmol/L and creatinine 175 ?mol/L; renal ultrasound s

title: Sprue-Like Enteropathy and Liver Injury: A Rare Emerging Association with Olmesartan
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 79-year-old African American man was referred from his gastroenterologist to the emergency department for dehydration and orthostatic hypotension. During the previous 4 weeks, he had developed chronic nonbloody diarrhea--approximately four loose, greenish, foul-smelling bowel movements per day--sometimes postprandial, accompanied by nonradiating, intermittent epigastric pain, nausea, occasional nonbloody vomiting, generalized weakness, anorexia, and a 30-lb weight loss. Over that period, he noted gradual onset of jaundice but denied fever, chills, dysphagia, rash, or joint symptoms. There was no recent travel, sick contacts, or dietary change.

His medical history included hypertension, nonischemic cardiomyopathy, benign prostatic hypertrophy, prostate cancer treated with radiation 6 years earlier, vitiligo, depression, COPD, and insomnia. Medications were olmesartan, metoprolol, escitalopram, trazodone, finasteride, aspirin, isosorbide mononitrate, furosemide, and doxazosin. He dran

title: Rare case of intracranial hemorrhage associated with seoul virus infection diagnosed by metagenomic next?generation sequencing
case_summary:
A 57-year-old previously healthy man was transferred with sudden loss of consciousness and vomiting. Four days earlier, he developed fever up to 39 degreesC of unknown origin and was diagnosed locally with dengue fever based on a weakly positive IgM. He had no history of trauma, antiplatelet or anticoagulant use, or other significant medical problems. He reported no recent travel.  

On arrival, blood pressure was 161/82 mmHg, heart rate 97/min, respiratory rate 25/min, and temperature 37.8 degreesC. He was comatose with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 6; pupils measured 3.0 mm (left) and 4.0 mm (right) with slow light reflexes. Chest, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were normal, and there were no rashes, petechiae, or ecchymoses.  

Laboratory tests from the day before transfer showed WBC 8.32?10^9/L, hemoglobin 171 g/L, hematocrit 52.5%, platelets 31?10^9/L, AST 272 IU/L, ALT 225 IU/L, BUN 6.47 mmol/L, and creatinine 83.4 umol/L.  

Head CT revealed a left subdural hemorrhage with

title: Vein of Galen Aneurysmal Malformation: A Case Report
case_summary:
A term male infant was born at home to a 29-year-old primigravida after an uncomplicated pregnancy. Apgar scores were 4, 5, and 8 at 1, 5, and 10 minutes; intermittent positive-pressure ventilation was required for 3 minutes. Birth weight was 3760 g (57th percentile), length 54 cm (88th percentile), head circumference 38.2 cm (98th percentile). 

At 18 hours of age, the infant was mottled with subcostal and intercostal recession. Pre-ductal oxygen saturation was 88% on room air; respiratory rate was 70 breaths/min. He was afebrile and normotensive, with a normal cardiovascular examination and clear breath sounds. The abdomen was soft, tone was normal, and he was not encephalopathic. 

Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, electrolytes, and inflammatory markers. Capillary blood gas revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.24, pCO? 40 mm Hg, HCO?? 16.1 mmol/L, base excess ?10.3, lactate 3.4 mmol/L). A chest radiograph showed cardiomegaly without collapse or consolidation. Ech

title: Development of a reticular rash in a febrile woman:?An unusual cutaneous presentation of angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma
case_summary: A 59-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of progressive, pruritic, pink, lacy patches on her trunk and extremities. She reported associated fevers, abdominal pain, dyspnea, and episodes of hypotension. On examination, there were generalized erythematous papules, macules, and patches arranged in a netlike, reticular pattern over the chest, abdomen, back, and upper and lower extremities. The lesions blanched on pressure without scaling, and mucosal surfaces were spared. Laboratory studies showed an elevated lactate dehydrogenase level, leukocytosis, anemia, hyperbilirubinemia, and hypofibrinogenemia. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed prominent lymphadenopathy in the cervical, axillary, mediastinal, hilar, retroperitoneal, and inguinal regions. Her recent medical history was notable for recurrent pericardial effusions and pneumonia, but she had not received any antibiotics or new medications in the preceding 2 weeks.

title: Challenging Diagnosis of Addison's Disease Presenting with Adrenal Crisis
case_summary:
A 27-year-old woman was referred with shock, initially attributed to hypovolemia or septic shock, as well as acute gastroenteritis with dehydration, suspected autoimmune disease, and pneumonia.  
History: 7 days of fever and productive cough with yellow phlegm, no dyspnea; preceding nausea, vomiting, and watery diarrhea (?3/day); 10?kg weight loss over 2 months; irregular menses since age 17 with amenorrhea for 18 months; no chronic illnesses or family history.  
On arrival: somnolent; blood pressure 90/70?mmHg on norepinephrine 0.1?ug/kg/min; heart rate 108/min; respiratory rate 22/min; temperature 37 degreesC.  
Examination: whitish tongue plaque; gingival mucosal hyperpigmentation; bilateral hyperpigmentation of interphalangeal joints and knees; cold, moist extremities (capillary refill 2-3?s); clear lungs; normal abdomen.  
Laboratory results: prior leukocytosis 20.4???10^3/uL with lymphocytosis; creatinine 1.2?mg/dL; sodium 134?mmol/L; potassium and glucose low-normal. Repeat CBC,

title: Immunoglobulin M (IgM) multiple myeloma versus Waldenstr?m macroglobulinemia: diagnostic challenges and therapeutic options: two case reports
case_summary: A 70-year-old man presented with fatigue, headache, and arthralgia of recent onset. He had no fever, weight loss, night sweats, adenopathy, splenomegaly, or bone pain. Vital signs were normal. Neurologic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed total serum protein 10 700 mg/L, IgM 7665 mg/L, and an M-spike of 4600 mg/L (43.3%) on protein electrophoresis. Serum creatinine and calcium were normal. Hemoglobin was 9.5 g/dL (macrocytic anemia); platelet and leukocyte counts were normal. ?2-Microglobulin was 3.15 mg/dL. Bone marrow biopsy revealed 65-70% clonal plasma cells that were CD138+, CD79a+, monoclonal ?-light chain-restricted, IgM+, cyclin D1+, CD20?, CD45?/+, CD56?. Testing for MYD88 L265P mutation was negative, and fluorescence in situ hybridization showed translocation t(11;14). Low-dose CT of the skeleton and chest/abdomen showed no lytic bone lesions, lymphadenopathy, or organomegaly.

title: Unilateral adrenal infarction in pregnancy with associated acute hypoadrenalism and subsequent spontaneous biochemical and radiological resolution
case_summary:
A 25-year-old woman, gravida 1 at 32 weeks' gestation, presented with acute onset of left flank pain initially attributed to musculoskeletal strain and discharged on analgesia. Later the same day she developed sharp central lower chest pain, back pain, and vomiting; her pain was 9/10. A few hours later she reported new right-sided abdominal pain. She denied fever, diarrhea, trauma, or preceding illness, and her pregnancy had been uncomplicated to date.

On examination, her blood pressure was 112/58 mm Hg (later 100/64), heart rate 101-108 beats/min, respiratory rate 24-26 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. She had mild, generalized abdominal tenderness. A venous blood gas showed pH 7.28, bicarbonate 13.8 mmol/L (23-28), base excess -13.3 mmol/L, lactate 9.0 mmol/L (0.5-1.0), and glucose 10 mmol/L. Initial laboratory tests revealed sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, urea 2.5 mmol/L, creatinine 48 umol/L, C-reactive protein 38 mg/L, prothrombin time 11.4 s, activa

title: Clinical course of Capnocytophaga canimorsus bacteremia from acute onset to life crisis
case_summary: A 67-year-old man presented with fever and a constant occipital headache. The fever began 5 hours before presentation and the headache began 9 hours before. He rated the headache 10/10, described it as pulsating, and denied nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. His medical history included well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.1%) and hyperlipidemia. He drank 2-3 glasses of shochu daily but had normal prior liver tests. On examination, he was alert and oriented; temperature was 39.1 degreesC, pulse 110/min, blood pressure 123/78 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min; there were no meningeal signs and the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed WBC 8.3?10^9/L, hemoglobin 144 g/L, platelets 169?10^9/L, creatinine 0.65 mg/dL, blood glucose 117 mg/dL, and CRP 2.5 mg/dL (normal ? 0.3). Urinalysis and chest radiograph were normal. Head CT showed no acute findings. Lumbar puncture revealed clear fluid, no xanthochromia, 1 monocyte/uL, and glucose 80 mg/dL. Blood

title: Cefazolin-induced hemolytic anemia: a case report and systematic review of literature
case_summary:
An 80-year-old woman with a history of minor penicillin allergy, hypertension, hypothyroidism, gout, diastolic dysfunction, hyperlipidemia, transient ischemic attack, and NSTEMI presented with 4 days of back pain and shortness of breath. Initial evaluation showed an elevated troponin level and urinalysis with leukocytes and bacteria. CT angiography of the chest demonstrated bibasilar atelectasis and consolidation, and she was diagnosed with severe sepsis due to pneumonia and urinary tract infection. She was started on piperacillin-tazobactam, vancomycin, and azithromycin. 

During treatment, her hemoglobin fell and esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed a bleeding gastric ulcer with visible vessels that were treated; her hemoglobin then stabilized. After transfer, blood cultures grew methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus and antibiotics were narrowed to cefazolin. MRI of the spine showed discitis and osteomyelitis at T1-T2, and echocardiography revealed mitral valve endocarditis.

title: A rare giant gastric trichobezoar in a young female patient: Case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 15-year-old Caucasian female was admitted with a 6-month history of progressive postprandial epigastric discomfort, nausea, vomiting (relieved by emesis), and weight loss. She appeared pale, dehydrated, and at the lower limit of her growth curve (weight 46 kg, height 150 cm). Vital signs were temperature 36.9  degreesC, heart rate 83 bpm, respiratory rate 21 breaths/min, and blood pressure 98/59 mm Hg. She had no prior surgeries, no medications, and no known medical or intellectual disabilities.

On examination, she had halitosis and a mobile palpable mass measuring approximately 25 ? 9 cm between the xiphoid and umbilicus. Bowel sounds were normal, with no guarding or peritonitis. Digital rectal examination was normal. Cardiopulmonary and urogenital exams were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed anemia (hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL, hematocrit 25.1%); all other values including white cells, platelets, electrolytes, renal and liver tests, and C-reactive protein were within normal limit

title: Duodenal stenosis in adult malrotation: When Ladd procedure is not enough: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman presented with 2 days of sudden onset, constant, central abdominal pain rated 9/10, aggravated by walking or lying flat and relieved by bending forward. The pain began with fever, chills, headache, nausea, and non-bilious vomiting, which later became bilious. She had a seven-year history of chronic recurrent abdominal pain with vomiting, treated empirically as peptic ulcer disease or gastroesophageal reflux disease without confirmatory tests. She had no prior surgeries and no tobacco, alcohol, or drug use.

On examination she was bent forward and moaning; on lying supine she assumed a knee-chest position. Temperature was 37.4  degreesC, pulse 105 bpm, blood pressure 110/68 mm Hg, respiratory rate 30 cpm, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. The abdomen was flat, moved with respiration, and had generalized tenderness most marked in the epigastrium and periumbilical area with guarding and rebound; there were no scars or organomegaly. Rectal examination was normal. L

title: Extreme hypernatremic dehydration due to potential sodium intoxication: consequences and management for an infant with diarrhea at an urban intensive care unit in Bangladesh: a case report
case_summary:
A 6-month-old Bangladeshi girl was brought to the hospital with a 2-day history of moderate-volume watery stools (12-15 per day), fever, and 12 hours of lethargy. She had no vomiting, rash, convulsions, or blood/mucus in the stool. The mother reported that the child became excessively thirsty, and, instead of her usual diet, received five packets of rice-based ORS (each mixed in 500 mL water instead of 1100 mL) and five packets of properly diluted glucose-based ORS. The child passed urine a few hours prior to admission. 

On examination: axillary temperature 39.4  degreesC; pulse 134/min, normal volume; blood pressure 100/50 mmHg; respiratory rate 54/min; capillary refill 3 s. Weight 5.9 kg; length 63 cm; Z-scores for weight-for-age ?1.29, weight-for-length ?0.40, length-for-age ?1.21. She was lethargic and irritable to touch. There were no signs of dehydration, edema, pallor, or cyanosis. Chest and heart sounds were normal; abdomen was soft, non-tender, with active bowel sounds. Bedsi

title: Heterotopic pancreatic tissue presenting as an unusual cause of gastric outlet obstruction in infancy: a case report
case_summary: A 2-month-old Caucasian girl, weight 2.7 kg, presented with 3 weeks of continuous, projectile, nonbilious vomiting that increased in frequency over the last week despite antiemetic therapy. She had significant weight loss and was lethargic and severely dehydrated. On abdominal examination, there was epigastric fullness with visible peristaltic waves. Laboratory studies showed anemia and hypokalemic, hyponatremic metabolic alkalosis; blood urea nitrogen and creatinine levels were normal. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed abnormal thickening of the pyloric canal with gastric dilatation, but pyloric stenosis could not be excluded. An upper gastrointestinal contrast study demonstrated a markedly dilated double?looped stomach with significant prepyloric constriction and delayed gastric emptying. A diagnosis of gastric outlet obstruction was confirmed, and the patient was scheduled for elective laparotomy after nasogastric decompression, intravenous fluids, antibiotics, and correction of el

title: Development of Multiple Myeloma of the IgA Type in a Patient with Cold Agglutinin Disease: Transformation or Coincidence?
case_summary:
A woman in her late 60s was referred in July 2012 for anemia of at least one and a half years' duration. Her history included type 2 diabetes, hypertension, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and recurrent urinary tract infections, one complicated by urosepsis. She noted fatigue beginning in 2010-2011; hemoglobin (Hb) had fallen from 13.4 g/dL in 2008 to 8.9 g/dL in February 2011. She had received one transfusion without reaction and experienced exacerbations of anemia during febrile infections. There was no history of acrocyanosis or Raynaud phenomena.

On admission, she appeared well; physical examination was unremarkable, without lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, or acrocyanosis. Chest radiography and abdominal ultrasonography were normal. Laboratory data included: Hb 8.2 g/dL, leukocytes 7.8 ? 10^9/L, platelets 263 ? 10^9/L, MCV 99 fL, reticulocytes 88 ? 10^9/L, CRP 11 mg/L. Iron studies, cobalamin, and folate were normal; ferritin was elevated at 1257 ug/L. LDH was 544 U/L, bilirubin 43

title: Surprising visit: the rare plasma cell variant
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman presented with persistent fever, myalgia, and bilateral lower-limb pain. Initial evaluation showed anemia, leukocytosis, and thrombocytopenia; she did not respond to empirical treatment for presumed hematologic malignancy and was referred for further management.

On arrival, she reported severe back pain and exhibited paraparesis. Clinical features were consistent with acute kidney injury. Magnetic resonance imaging of the spine showed a posterior C5-C6 disc bulge effacing the anterior thecal sac with spinal cord compression and diffuse altered marrow signal in the cervical, dorsal, lumbar, and sacral vertebrae, suspicious for leukemic infiltration; multiple lytic lesions were noted throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton. A CT scan of the abdomen demonstrated bulky, edematous kidneys.

Laboratory studies revealed a hemoglobin level of 5.8?g/dL and a platelet count of 34,000/cumm. Peripheral-blood smear showed marked rouleaux formation and 54% atypical cells

title: CT and 18FDG-PET/CT findings in progressive mediastinal idiopathic fibrosis as a benign mimicker of esophageal carcinoma: a case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
case_summary:
A man in his late 60s presented with progressive dysphagia. He had no fever. Laboratory studies showed mild leukocytosis (WBC 13.9?10^9/L) with neutrophilia, normal eosinophil and platelet counts, mildly elevated C-reactive protein (18.3 mg/L) and D-dimer (3.62 mg/L), and a normocytic anemia consistent with anemia of chronic disease. Other blood tests, including renal, liver, electrolyte panels, and immunological markers (SSA, SSB, MPO-ANCA, PR3-ANCA, dsDNA, RNP, Sm, cryoglobulins, TPO, Tg), were unremarkable.  
   
Upper endoscopy revealed narrowing of the mediodistal thoracic esophagus with dense fibrin deposition that barely allowed passage of the scope. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest showed an extensive, irregular, partly necrotic, infiltrative mass involving the distal esophagus, paraesophageal fat, mediastinal pleura, pericardium, and adjacent aortic wall. No other mediastinal or abdominal pathology was identified. These imaging features were interpreted as consistent with a l

title: Congenital chikungunya in a neonate with early?onset sepsis and petechiae: An unusual case report
case_summary:
A female neonate was born at 38 + 6 weeks' gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery to a 23-year-old primigravida. Her birth weight was 2250 g (<3rd percentile), Apgar scores 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. The mother had regular prenatal care; first-trimester serologies including hepatitis B, HIV, and syphilis were negative. Two days before labor she experienced a flu-like syndrome with fever (38.5  degreesC); on admission she was afebrile, COVID-19 rapid test negative, and dengue serology was negative. She was treated with ampicillin for presumed intrapartum sepsis. Maternal blood cultures were negative and CRP was elevated at 130 mg/L.  

At admission to the special care nursery, the infant was active, pink, and feeding well. Initial labs showed a white-cell count of 11.4 ? 10^9/L, neutrophils 7.2 ? 10^9/L, I/T ratio 0.01, hemoglobin 173 g/L, platelets 169 ? 10^9/L, CRP 34.6 mg/L, and procalcitonin 1.48 ?g/L. Blood and gastric-aspirate cultures were drawn, and empiric ampicillin plus

title: Acute kidney injury due to glomerular haematuria and obstructive erythrocyte casts associated with thrombocytopaenia and thin basement membrane disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 56-year-old man was admitted with acute gout of the right knee on a background of 6 months of lethargy, easy bruising, epistaxis, and recent episodes of visible hematuria. He denied fever, night sweats, or weight loss. His medical history included gout, daily alcohol consumption (10 drinks per day), gastroesophageal reflux disease, and prior gastric fundoplication; he took no regular medications. He smoked 20 pack-years and denied illicit drug use. 

On examination, his blood pressure was 140/80 mmHg, with a normal mental status, no focal neurologic deficits, mild hepatomegaly, subcutaneous bruises, spider naevi, and frank hematuria on voiding. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 73 g/L, white cells 5.8?10^9/L, platelets 35?10^9/L, creatinine 260 ?mol/L (baseline ~113-100 ?mol/L over the preceding months), urea 17.6 mmol/L, and normal coagulation studies (INR 1.2, aPTT 27 s). Blood film confirmed thrombocytopenia without schistocytes. Retrospective review showed thrombocytopenia (

title: Gastric duplication cyst in an adult with autoimmune hemolytic anemia: a case report and review of the?literature
case_summary: A 28-year-old man with an 8-month history of fatigue and breathlessness presented for evaluation before a planned splenectomy for relapsing autoimmune hemolytic anemia. He reported no abdominal pain or gastrointestinal symptoms. His medications included prednisone, bisphosphonates, vitamin D, and calcium. He was a non-smoker and did not use alcohol. On admission, blood pressure was 90/70 mm Hg, heart rate 70 beats/min, and temperature 36  degreesC. Examination revealed marked pallor and icteric sclerae; the abdomen was soft with no palpable masses or tenderness. Laboratory studies showed WBC 11.5 ? 10^9/L, RBC 2.2 ? 10^12/L, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, hematocrit 24.3%, MCV 110 fL, platelets 400 ? 10^9/L, reticulocyte count 18%, total protein 80 g/L, CRP 4 mg/L, urea 0.3 g/L, and creatinine 7 mg/L. Abdominal CT revealed a large, well-circumscribed cystic mass between the stomach and spleen with no evident communication to the stomach or pancreas. Endoscopic ultrasound confirmed a cystic lesio

title: Human Infection with Fusobacterium necrophorum without Jugular Venous Thrombosis: A Varied Presentation of Lemierre's Syndrome
case_summary: A 16-year-old male presented with a 2-week history of sore throat, progressive dyspnea, and spiking fevers. On arrival, his temperature was 103  degreesF and SpO? was 89% on room air; other physical findings were unremarkable. Initial laboratory tests showed a normal white cell count, normal liver function and coagulation tests, but mild anemia, mildly elevated bilirubin, and increased LDH. Notable abnormalities included a creatinine of 277 ?mol/L, urea of 94 mg/dL, and D-dimers of 13 970 ug/L. A chest radiograph was initially normal. He was admitted with a working differential of sepsis-induced acute kidney injury, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, and pulmonary-renal syndrome. Over the next 48 hours, he deteriorated with worsening dyspnea, hypoxia (SpO? 70% despite supplemental oxygen), and a leukocytosis to 24 ? 10?/L. Repeat chest radiography demonstrated extensive bilateral consolidation, and a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the chest revealed right-sided extensive consolidation

title: A Case of the Serotonin Syndrome Secondary to Phenelzine Monotherapy at Therapeutic Dosing
case_summary:
A 27-year-old woman was admitted with a one-day history of unsteadiness and feeling generally unwell. Five minutes of dysarthria had spontaneously resolved; hours later she was "drunk" on her feet. The next day she saw her family doctor and was referred to hospital. She denied fever, substance use, headache other than mild, gastrointestinal symptoms, chest pain, or dyspnea.  

Medical history: depression, suspected epilepsy (normal workup), and episodic migraine. Medications: phenelzine 15?mg t.i.d. (started two weeks ago), propranolol 40?mg b.i.d., and folic acid; mirtazapine and sertraline had been stopped prior to phenelzine initiation. No illicit drug or alcohol use, no tyramine ingestion.  

On arrival: T 36.9  degreesC, pulse 80 bpm, BP 100/54 mmHg, RR 18 breaths/min, SpO? 95% on room air; GCS 15; pupils dilated at 5 mm but reactive; remainder of exam unremarkable. Twenty minutes later, GCS fell to 9, temperature rose to 40.4  degreesC, pulse 164 bpm, she became diaphoretic, with

title: Atypical presentations of primary acquired hypothyroidism - a case series
case_summary:
A 10-year-6-month-old boy was referred from the rheumatology clinic for evaluation of generalized myalgia and arthralgia involving the back, hips, knees, and ankles for the past 3 to 4 months. The pain occurred daily, was worse in the morning, and interfered with activities of daily living, causing an antalgic gait. He reported a poorer appetite but denied prolonged fever or weight loss. He had a recent intercurrent illness with cough and diarrhoea. 

On examination, he was short for age and pale, with hepatomegaly. Cardiopulmonary and neurologic examinations were normal. Musculoskeletal examination was notable only for a left antalgic gait. There was no rash and no goitre. 

Growth-chart plotting showed height 119.5 cm (SDS ?3.29), weight 26.2 kg (SDS ?1.61), and BMI 18.3 kg/m2 (SDS 0.58). Laboratory studies revealed normochromic normocytic anaemia (hemoglobin 9.3 g/dL), elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (56 mm/hr), alanine aminotransferase 226 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 27

title: A pain in the neck: Salmonella spp. as an unusual cause of a thyroid abscess. A case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman presented with acute onset of right-sided neck pain over 12-24 hours. The pain was continuous, dull, 10/10 in severity, located in the right anterior neck, nonradiating, and worsened by neck rotation. She reported fever to 39.9  degreesC, diaphoresis, and chills, but denied voice changes, dyspnea, or dysphagia. Her medical history was notable for multiple colloid cysts in the right thyroid lobe under ultrasound surveillance, type II diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and dyslipidemia. She had a brief nonspecific diarrheal illness 17 days before presentation. Medications included levothyroxine, sitagliptin, canagliflozin, and others; she had allergies to penicillin, sulfa drugs, and macrolides.

On examination, she appeared uncomfortable but well. There were no stigmata of endocarditis or lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasal endoscopy was normal with intact vocal cord mobility. The neck exam revealed an asymmetric right-sided

title: Life-threatening necrotizing myometritis, due to Group A streptococcus - still a life-threatening condition
case_summary:
A 34-year-old gravida 4, para 3 woman at 39 weeks' gestation was admitted for induction of labor because of suspected fetal macrosomia. Her medical history was notable only for obesity (BMI 34) and mild depression. Amniotomy yielded clear fluid, and oxytocin augmentation was used for 4 hours. She delivered a 4230 g male infant vaginally with Apgar scores of 7 and 10. The mother and infant were well, and she was discharged 4 hours postpartum.

Approximately 24 hours after delivery, she returned with chills and lower abdominal pain. On examination, temperature was elevated, blood pressure was 96/72 mmHg, pulse 112 beats/min, and urine output was decreased. Abdominal palpation revealed guarding in both lower quadrants, peritoneal signs, and an enlarged uterus palpable to the umbilicus. Transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound showed an empty uterus without free fluid. After blood and urine cultures were obtained, intravenous metronidazole and cefuroxime were started. Laboratory tests re

title: Pigment nephropathy associated with percutaneous hemodynamic support during ventricular tachycardia ablation
case_summary:
A 33-year-old man with known arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy presented with palpitations, tachycardia, and multiple shocks from his dual-chamber implantable cardioverter-defibrillator for ventricular tachycardia (VT) with left bundle branch-block morphology and inferior axis (cycle length, 375 ms). Despite amiodarone and lidocaine infusions, VT recurred with hypotension and signs of volume overload. Transthoracic echocardiography showed biventricular dysfunction: left ventricular ejection fraction of 14% and a severely enlarged right ventricle with reduced systolic function.

He underwent VT ablation under general anesthesia (propofol and fentanyl) with phenylephrine and epinephrine infusions to maintain blood pressure. Epicardial and endocardial mapping identified targets in the right ventricular outflow tract and inferolateral tricuspid valve annulus; ablation terminated VT. Because of hemodynamic instability during induced VT, an Impella CP device was placed prophyla

title: Recurrent Clostridium difficile infection treated with home fecal transplantation: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman presented in December 2012 with a 10-day history of explosive, watery diarrhea accompanied initially by fever and chills for two days, crampy abdominal pain, nausea, and anorexia; there was no rectal bleeding. She had lost 8 kg over the preceding four months. She had no recent travel or known infectious contacts but made weekly visits to a nursing home. Four months earlier she had received amoxicillin for a dental infection. She had two prior one-week episodes of diarrhea, 14 months and nine months before. Her medical and family histories were unremarkable. Medications included atorvastatin and diclofenac/misoprostol. 

On examination she appeared thin; vital signs were normal and she was afebrile. Abdominal examination revealed generalized tenderness, worse in the left lower quadrant; the remainder of the physical examination was normal. 

Laboratory studies showed normal results for complete blood count, electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, estimated glo

title: Management of atypical chronic lymphocytic leukemia presenting with extreme leukocytosis
case_summary:
A 65-year-old Caucasian man was referred for evaluation of leukocytosis. Two months of progressive exertional dyspnea prompted a CBC showing WBC >100 K/?L (predominantly lymphocytes) and hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL; a WBC >100 K/?L had been documented two years earlier without further work-up. He reported diminished appetite and fatigue but denied fevers, night sweats, weight loss, headaches, dizziness, blurred vision, or chest pain. His history was notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension; medications included amlodipine, valsartan, metformin, and glimepiride. He had no recent infections or new medications.

On examination, blood pressure was 129/76 mm Hg and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. He appeared well, with bilateral supraclavicular lymph nodes ~2 cm and palpable splenomegaly. There were no signs of infection.

Laboratory studies revealed: WBC 593 K/?L (3.9-11.0 K/?L), hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL (12.4-18.0 g/dL), MCV 103 fL (80-99 fL), platelets 85 ?10^3/?L (160-392 ?10^3/?L).

title: Acute pulmonary tumour embolism and right systolic dysfunction in a hidden intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma: case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman presented with acute-onset dyspnea and palpitations. Her oxygen saturation was 66% on room air, heart rate 145 beats/min, and respiratory rate 40 breaths/min. Four weeks earlier she had developed unilateral left lower-leg edema, erythema, and pain. Over the preceding week her functional class had declined from New York Heart Association grade I to III. With supplemental oxygen her saturation improved to 88%, and pulmonary embolism was suspected. CT pulmonary angiography showed acute pulmonary emboli in lobar branches, deep-vein thrombosis of the left leg, and a 10 cm multicystic, septated pelvic mass in the left adnexal region.  
On examination she had jugular venous distention and a positive hepatojugular reflux. NT-proBNP was >25 000 pg/mL, troponin I 186.8 pg/mL, and lactate 4.3 mmol/L. She underwent thrombolysis with alteplase and was intubated for respiratory failure. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a markedly dilated right ventricle with systolic dysfunc

title: Metastatic Merkel Cell Carcinoma Masquerading as Multiple Immune-Related Adverse Events
case_summary:
A 77-year-old Caucasian woman with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and degenerative joint disease presented with a 4- to 5-month history of a left cheek nodule that had grown rapidly over the preceding weeks. Two months earlier, shave biopsy of the 14 mm firm nodule had shown Merkel cell carcinoma extending to peripheral and deep margins with perinuclear dot-like CK20 positivity, synaptophysin positivity, weak AE1/AE3 reactivity, and negative TTF-1. PET/CT revealed intense FDG uptake in the primary left cheek mass and multiple intraparotid nodes without distant metastases. Core biopsy of a left submandibular lymph node confirmed lymphatic invasion. She was staged as IIIB (T2pN1bM0) and started on pembrolizumab (200 mg IV every 3 weeks) plus radiotherapy to the left cheek and neck.

One week after the first pembrolizumab dose, she developed acute abdominal pain and nausea; labs including CMP, CBC, troponin, and lipase were normal. Gastroenterology judged her symptoms likely pembrolizumab-

title: Myeloperoxidase-antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody-associated diffuse alveolar hemorrhage caused by denosumab
case_summary:
A 67-year-old woman with well-controlled asthma, hypertension, and severe osteoporosis (on denosumab every 6 months for 3 years) was admitted for 3 days of nonmassive hemoptysis and progressive dyspnea. She denied fever, chest pain, orthopnea, edema, or new environmental exposures; she was on no other new medications and had no history of vasculitis or renal disease. On examination, she was in respiratory distress: T 37  degreesC, BP 167/84 mmHg, HR 98 beats/min, RR 48 breaths/min, SpO2 84% on room air; she had pale conjunctivae, bilateral bronchial breath sounds, and crackles in both lower lung fields. She rapidly progressed to require intubation for hypoxemic respiratory failure. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 5.7 g/dL (baseline ~11.6 g/dL), WBC 17,490/uL with 91% neutrophils, platelets 635,000/uL, normal renal and liver function, ESR 119 mm/hr, CRP 141 mg/L, and normal coagulation studies. Urinalysis revealed no hematuria or proteinuria. Arterial blood gas on FiO2 0.6 showed

title: Young Man with Hepatomegaly: A Case of Glycogenic Hepatopathy
case_summary:
A 15-year-old boy with a 6-year history of poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus was brought to the emergency department for evaluation of right upper-quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting of one day's duration. He denied changes in urine or stool, melena, anorexia, weight loss, pruritus, jaundice, fever, or bleeding. His diabetes had been complicated by several prior episodes of diabetic ketoacidosis, and he admitted to intermittent insulin noncompliance. He took no new medications and denied alcohol or over-the-counter drug use.

On examination, he was afebrile, heart rate 109 beats/min, blood pressure 109/60 mm Hg, BMI 15.8 kg/m2. The abdomen was mildly distended with tenderness in the right upper quadrant; the liver edge was palpable 6 cm below the costal margin without rebound or guarding, and there was no splenomegaly or ascites.

Laboratory evaluation on admission showed: blood glucose 480 mg/dL; arterial pH 7.25, PCO2 30 mm Hg; anion gap 21; BUN 29 mg

title: Chronic Recurrent Multifocal Osteomyelitis - A Rare Clinical Presentation and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A 12-year-old girl presented with a 6-month history of insidious onset of pain and swelling over both clavicles, which began bilaterally on the same day. The discomfort temporarily subsided with analgesics but recurred days after stopping medication. She had no fever, no history of trauma, no other swellings, no polyarthralgias or morning stiffness, and no weight loss, throat infections, or skin lesions. Her activities of daily living were unaffected, and there was no relevant family history.

On examination, both medial halves of the clavicles were enlarged. The swellings were hard, smooth, and ill-defined, without tenderness or warmth. The sternoclavicular and acromioclavicular joints were non-tender, shoulder range of motion was normal, and there were no distal neurovascular deficits. Systemic examination was unremarkable.

Plain radiographs of the clavicles showed expansile, sclerotic lesions involving the medial halves bilaterally. Laboratory studies revealed an erythrocyte sedime

title: 36-Year-Old Female with Catastrophic Antiphospholipid Syndrome Treated with Eculizumab: A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A 36-year-old woman with no significant past medical history was found in her bathtub with decreased responsiveness and profound weakness. One month before admission she developed frequent headaches and blurry vision, and her family noted that she had become socially withdrawn. 

On presentation she appeared confused and dehydrated, with multiple bruises on her extremities. Vital signs were notable for a blood pressure of 207/148 mmHg and a heart rate of 110 beats per minute. 

Laboratory studies on admission revealed:  
? White blood cell count 20.5 ? 10^9/L  
? Hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL  
? Platelet count 44 000/uL  
? Creatinine 7.0 mg/dL  
? Anion-gap metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis  
? Prothrombin time 11.6 sec, activated partial thromboplastin time 21.5 sec, fibrinogen 345 mg/dL  
? Elevated troponin levels without ST-segment changes on ECG

Ophthalmology evaluation attributed visual loss and color blindness to intraretinal hemorrhages. Noncontrast CT of the head showed n

title: Atypically distributed cutaneous lesions of Norwegian scabies in an HIV-positive man in South India: a case report
case_summary:
A 16-year-old boy with known HIV infection presented with a one-month history of skin lesions. He first noticed thickened, hyperkeratotic plaques on the scalp and forehead, which rapidly spread over his entire body without associated pruritus. On admission, there were extensive, generalized, thick, hyperkeratotic, crusting, yellowish papular lesions covering the face (including ear lobes), shoulder blades, trunk, limbs, and back.

He was afebrile (98.8 degreesF) with a pulse rate of 80 beats per minute. He had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated with anti-tuberculosis therapy for three years, with poor adherence. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed:  
? Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (normal 12-17)  
? ESR 20 mm/h (normal 0-14)  
? Platelets 144 ? 10^9/L (normal 137-367)  
? Leukocytes 18.2 ? 10^9/L (normal 3.9-9.4)  
? Lymphocytes 3.7 ? 10^9/L (normal 1.2-3.4)  
? CD4 T-cell count 342 cells/?L (normal 350-1411), CD4% 8%

title: Bilateral peripheric facial nerve palsy following SARS?CoV?2 infection: A case report and review of literature
case_summary:
A 35-year-old man presented with fever and loss of consciousness. Five days earlier, he had acute watery diarrhea. Overnight he developed a high fever (105.8  degreesF) and altered consciousness. He reported recent household exposure to COVID-19; his SARS-CoV-2 PCR was positive on admission. He had no underlying disease, drug allergies, or trauma.

On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mmHg, pulse 90/min, respirations 18/min, temperature 98.6  degreesF, and O2 saturation 95%. He opened his eyes to pain and withdrew his limbs (GCS 10/15). Pupils were equal and reactive; gag reflex was intact. Bronchial breath sounds were heard bilaterally; abdominal and other systemic examinations were unremarkable.

Initial labs showed sodium 117 mEq/L, potassium 3.1 mEq/L, BUN 11 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, WBC 6900/?L, platelets 132?000/?L, INR 1.3. His consciousness normalized as sodium corrected to 137 mEq/L (GCS 13/15).

On hospital day 2, neurologic exam revealed bilate

title: Idiopathic systemic capillary leak syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old man presented with general weakness and acute lower back and abdominal pain that began earlier that day. He had no past medical history and took no medications. He was alert; mucous membranes and axillae were dry. Initial vital signs were stable, but 3 hours after arrival his blood pressure fell to 74/60 mmHg.

Initial laboratory studies showed:
? WBC 29 180/uL, hemoglobin 22.6 g/dL, hematocrit 66.1%  
? BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.95 mg/dL, fractional excretion of sodium 0.13%  
? Albumin 2.6 g/dL, CRP 7.7 mg/L, procalcitonin 0.8 ng/mL  
? Arterial blood gas: normal anion-gap metabolic acidosis (pH 7.352; PaCO? 24.5 mmHg; HCO?? 13.3 mmHg; lactate 3.0 mmol/L)

Noncontrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed normal-sized kidneys and borderline splenomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal global left ventricular systolic function, concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, and minimal pericardial effusion. Bone marrow biopsy was unremarkable, and JAK2 V617F mut

title: Lupus catatonia in a young girl who presented with fever and altered sensorium
case_summary:
A 20-year-old woman was admitted with a 1-week history of high fever, altered sensorium, psychosocial withdrawal, minimal speech, immobility, and urinary and fecal retention. She had a 1-year history of polyarthralgias, oral ulcers, and alopecia but had not sought medical care. Seven months earlier she was diagnosed with hypertension and treated with losartan. She had two remote episodes of pulmonary tuberculosis, treated successfully. 

On examination, temperature was 100 degreesF, blood pressure 150/110 mmHg, pulse 130/min. She was conscious but mute, unresponsive to commands, with waxy flexibility and variable increased tone. She made no spontaneous movement except brief eye tracking. Neck rigidity was present without meningeal signs. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal exams were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 11.7 g/dL, WBC 12 ?10^3/?L, platelets 235 ?10^3/?L, ESR 76 mm/hr, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, and 24-hr urinary protein 720 mg. Serum electrolytes, live

title: Severe hepatic impairment after sevoflurane anesthesia in a 10-month-old child: Case report
case_summary:
A 10-month-old boy, born at term by cesarean delivery after an uneventful pregnancy and with normal psychomotor development, was admitted for surgical treatment of an acute intussusception. He had no prior medical, family, or drug?exposure history. Preoperative laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 8600/mm3, hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL, hematocrit 36.8%, C-reactive protein 5.5 mg/L, normal liver and kidney function, and normal electrolytes.

Anesthesia was induced with incremental sevoflurane (up to 7%) and propofol (3.5 mg/kg) via a peripheral IV line and maintained with sevoflurane throughout an uneventful 45-minute procedure. He awoke normothermic, normocardic, and normotensive and was transferred to pediatric surgery.

On the third postoperative day, he became lethargic and drowsy and was transferred to the pediatric intensive-care unit with new-onset jaundice. At admission he was afebrile (37.2 degreesC) and hemodynamically stable, with no clinical or laboratory evidence of sep

title: Gastroduodenal Sarcoidosis With Concomitant Cytomegalovirus Gastritis
case_summary:
A 67-year-old Middle Eastern woman with a history of Child-Pugh Class A cirrhosis (presumed NASH), mild pancytopenia, GERD, prior H. pylori infection, remote cholecystectomy, and hyperlipidemia presented with acute on chronic diffuse abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She denied fevers, night sweats, weight loss, extraintestinal symptoms, diet changes, or recent travel. Medications included metformin, domperidone, and pantoprazole. Physical examination showed mild epigastric tenderness without organomegaly, rash, or joint findings.

Laboratory studies revealed WBC 3.2?10^9/L, hemoglobin 105 g/L, platelets 88?10^9/L, AST 45 U/L, ALT 54 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 150 U/L, GGT 26 U/L, total bilirubin 12 ?mol/L, albumin 24 g/L, INR 1.2. Serologies were negative for hepatitis C, HAV IgM, HIV, ANA, ANCA, anti-smooth muscle, antienterocyte, antigoblet cell, antitransglutaminase, antiparietal antibodies; antimitochondrial antibody was intermittently low-titer positive. Serum pr

title: Acute Warfarin Toxicity as Initial Manifestation of Metastatic Liver Disease
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on warfarin, COPD, active smoking, hypertension, and a history of laryngeal cancer treated 4 years earlier presented with 10 days of generalized weakness and epigastric discomfort. She had a fall 1 week before presentation and noted dark stools and red urine. Medications--including warfarin--had been unchanged for 1.5 years, with a stable INR over that period. 

On arrival, she was alert but lethargic, pulse 160 beats/min, afebrile, normotensive. Examination showed a right subconjunctival hemorrhage; mucous membranes were dry. Cardiopulmonary exam was unremarkable except for tachycardia. The abdomen was soft with mild epigastric tenderness. Rectal exam revealed stool mixed with dark blood. Neurologic examination was nonfocal.

Laboratory results: hemoglobin 7.2?g/dL (baseline ~12?g/dL), WBC 15???10^3/uL, platelets 232???10^3/uL, PT 169?s, PTT 92?s, INR unrecordable. Chemistry showed BUN 54?mg/dL, creatinine 1.3?mg/dL, bicarbonate 10?mEq/L. L

title: Autoimmune epilepsy due to N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor antibodies in a child: a case report
case_summary:
A previously healthy 5-year-old Sri Lankan boy presented with a 2-week history of recurrent seizures manifesting as staring episodes with behavioral arrest, some associated with retching, vomiting, and tachycardia. Between seizures, his behavior fluctuated between normal, irritable, and somnolent. He was afebrile, had no meningeal signs, and neurologic examination--including cranial nerves, motor, sensory, and cerebellar testing--was normal. Glasgow Coma Scale was 15/15.

Initial laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 11.3?10^3/uL with a normal differential, C-reactive protein <5 mg/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 10 mm/hour, calcium 9.2 mg/dL, magnesium 1.9 mg/dL, sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, normal renal and liver function (SGPT 14 U/L; SGOT 33 U/L; urea 14 U/L; creatinine 36 umol/L), and normal thyroid hormones (T3 3.44 ng/dL; T4 1.2 ng/dL). 

Electroencephalography revealed intermittent theta and delta slowing over the right temporal and occipital regions; vide

title: Anaphylactic Reactions Caused by Nafamostat Mesylate during Hemodialysis before Surgery for Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man with end-stage renal disease from diabetic nephropathy, on thrice-weekly hemodialysis for 20 years, was evaluated for right carpal tunnel syndrome due to nocturnal numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. Physical examination revealed positive Tinel's sign and Phalen's test. Nerve conduction studies showed a median distal motor latency of 10.86 ms and absent sensory conduction in the right hand. Conservative measures failed, and surgical release was scheduled immediately after dialysis.

Before admission, hemodialysis sessions (4 hours, three times weekly) used an Asahi Kasei ABH-21F hemodiafilter with heparin anticoagulation. On the first scheduled surgery day, he developed fever (~38 degreesC) during dialysis; surgery was postponed. One week later, during a second preoperative dialysis session, he again developed fever (~38 degreesC), chills, shivering, hypotension (80-90 mmHg systolic), and tachycardia (108/min). Laboratory tests met DIC criteria (systemic

title: HHV8-Associated Multicentric Castleman Disease: A Case Report on a Rare Complication of HIV in a Low-Income Setting
journal: Research and Reports in Tropical Medicine
case_summary: A 45-year-old man with HIV-1 infection (diagnosed 2010) on tenofovir/lamivudine/dolutegravir presented after 1 week of fever, productive cough, and dyspnea. He was 3 months into treatment for extrapulmonary tuberculosis. On examination, temperature 38.1 degreesC, pulse 114/min, blood pressure 88/56 mmHg. He had bilateral posterior cervical lymph nodes (3?2 cm and 1?1 cm), which were mobile, non-tender, and rubbery; splenomegaly 8 cm below the costal margin; and mild epigastric tenderness. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 5.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 69.5 fL, WBC 3.8?10^9/L, creatinine 712 umol/L, urea 43 mmol/L, eGFR 8.8 mL/min/1.73m^2, direct bilirubin 19.1 umol/L, total protein 10.7 g/dL, AST 11.2 U/L, ALT 4.2 U/L, LDH 243.4 U/L. Viral load was undetectable; CD4 count 219 cells/uL. Chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and obscured right cardiac border. Ultrasound revealed hepatomegaly, mild splenomegaly, small pericardial and left pleural effusions, an

title: Tubulointerstitial Nephritis as the Initial Presentation of Crohn's Disease and Successful Treatment with Infliximab
case_summary:
A 14-year-old boy presented with 4 weeks of intermittent low-grade fever, fatigue, headache, congestion, generalized abdominal pain, tenesmus, bloody diarrhea, and bilateral flank pain. He was initially treated with doxycycline and metronidazole for presumed infectious gastroenteritis and urinary tract infection, without improvement. He had no history of NSAID use or other medications before symptom onset. Family history was notable for a maternal uncle with Crohn disease.

On examination, his blood pressure was 143/97 mm Hg, heart rate 80 bpm; he appeared tired, had mild cervical lymphadenopathy, and no uveitis. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (14.1 ? 10^3/?L), hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 70 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein 7.7 mg/L. Urinalysis revealed 62 leukocytes and 3 red cells per high-power field, without protein or glucose. A hemoccult stool test was positive, but stool cultures were negative. Renal ultrasound demonstrated mild

title: Lead Poisoning Can Be Easily Misdiagnosed as Acute Porphyria and Nonspecific Abdominal Pain
case_summary:
A 48-year-old man was admitted to the emergency department for recurring crampy, generalized abdominal pain of one month's duration, refractory to evaluation at another hospital. He was a farmer by occupation. On presentation, he was afebrile, blood pressure was 144/80 mmHg, and pulse was 88 beats per minute. He reported associated poor appetite, nausea, abdominal distention, cold sweating, and general weakness, but denied fever, diarrhea, or other symptoms. 

On examination, there was hepatosplenomegaly and diffuse tenderness to deep palpation of the entire abdomen without rebound tenderness or guarding. Results of other systemic examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hypochromic microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, hematocrit 27.9%) with basophilic stippling of erythrocytes and mildly abnormal liver function tests. The white blood cell count and levels of creatinine, glucose, total bilirubin, lipase, and electrolytes were within normal limits. Abdominal radiogr

title: Two Atypical Cases of Hantavirus Infections from Sri Lanka
case_summary:
A 46-year-old previously healthy man from central Sri Lanka presented on day 3 of illness with fever, headache, arthralgia, myalgia, eight episodes of watery diarrhea, and two episodes of vomiting. He works barefoot in rice paddies. On examination: temperature 38.9  degreesC, pulse 100/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, and conjunctival suffusion; cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal examinations were otherwise unremarkable.  

Initial labs showed leukocytes 10.57 ? 10^9/L (89.4% neutrophils), platelets 99 ? 10^9/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, sodium 135 mmol/L, potassium 3.4 mmol/L, and ALT 56 U/L. NS1 antigen test for dengue was negative. A provisional diagnosis of leptospirosis was made, and IV ceftriaxone, IV furosemide, and IV omeprazole were started.  

On day 4, he remained oliguric despite IV fluids, developed progressive dyspnea with bilateral lung crepitations, and transient hypotension (nadir 80/50 mmHg) requiring IV dobutamine. Creatinine rose to 3.54 mg/dL; abdominal ultraso

title: Cytomegalovirus ventriculoencephalitis presenting with hydrocephalus in a patient with advanced HIV infection
case_summary:
A 38-year-old woman with no prior comorbidities presented with a 3-week history of holocranial headache, weight loss, and anorexia, followed over 1 week by fluctuating alertness and a single episode of nonbilious, non-blood-stained projectile vomiting. She denied fever, photophobia, blurred vision, or seizures. One week before transfer she was diagnosed with HIV-1 infection at another hospital and started on tenofovir, lamivudine, and efavirenz. 

On examination, her pulse was 136 beats/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, and respiratory rate 24/min. She was drowsy but arousable and afebrile. Neurologic findings included bilateral papilledema, restriction of upward gaze, bilateral gaze-evoked nystagmus, and neck stiffness. The remainder of her physical examination was unremarkable.

Initial laboratory studies showed anemia (hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL), leukopenia (total WBC count 2 ? 10^9/L), thrombocytopenia (platelets 95 ? 10^9/L), and a CD4+ T-cell count of 46 cells/?L. 

Noncontrast CT of th

title: Postpartum hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis: A case report
case_summary:
A 40-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, mild headache, and abdominal pain. She was 3 weeks post-cesarean section and had been residing in a postpartum care center. Eleven days before presentation, she had neck swelling and underwent fine-needle aspiration at a private facility, which revealed no abnormalities of the thyroid gland. She had no other medical history and no family history of malignancy.

On examination, temperature was 40.6  degreesC, blood pressure 100/48 mmHg, heart rate 100 bpm, and respiratory rate 18 breaths/min. There was no thyroid tenderness or swelling, no neck stiffness, no tonsillar enlargement, and the surgical sites (cesarean section and needle aspiration) showed no signs of infection.
