Abdominal examination was unremarkable, and colposcopy performed to exclude endometritis showed no abnormalities.

Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, platelets 133 000/?L, absolute neutrophil count 2512/?L, total bi

title: Ondansetron?induced pseudoallergy with non?ischemic myocardial injury: A rare case report of Kounis syndrome
case_summary:
A 45?year?old man presented to the emergency department with a 3?day history of periumbilical, nonradiating abdominal pain and four episodes of nonbilious, nonprojectile vomiting. He had a history of mild duodenitis treated with esomeprazole for 1 month; no other significant medical, surgical, family, or allergy history. He drank alcohol occasionally, did not smoke, and denied drug use.

On admission, vital signs were: temperature 36.4 degreesC; blood pressure 120/80?mmHg; respiratory rate 16/min; heart rate 72/min; oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Physical examination showed dry mucous membranes and mild epigastric tenderness; cardiovascular, pulmonary, and abdominal exams were otherwise unremarkable.

Laboratory tests (reference range in parentheses) revealed: WBC 7040/mm3 (4000-10,000), hemoglobin 15.8?g/dL (14-17), platelets 196,000/mm3 (150,000-350,000), sodium 140?mmol/L (136-145), potassium 4.2?mmol/L (3.5-5.0), calcium 10.2?mg/dL (9-10.5), AST 28?U/L (0-35), ALT 65?U/L (0-35)

title: A 60-year-old man with chronic renal failure and a costal mass: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 60-year-old man was transferred from the hemodialysis unit to the emergency department with 15 days of malaise, subjective fevers, shortness of breath, dry cough, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and mild non-radiating anterior chest pain. His history was notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus (with retinopathy), chronic kidney failure on hemodialysis, hypertension, peripheral and carotid arterial disease, first-degree AV block, and a 20-pack-year smoking history. Medications included sevelamer, erythropoietin, folic acid, lovastatin, gemfibrozil, NPH insulin, amlodipine, and aspirin; he was not taking calcium or vitamin D supplements.

On examination he was afebrile, alert, and oriented; heart rate 92/min, respiratory rate 14/min, blood pressure 130/70 mmHg, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. He had bilateral blindness, mild left nasal epistaxis, and tenderness over several costochondral junctions without clear localization. Lung sounds were diminished without crackles or wheezes; peripheral

title: Case report of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome with retinal arterial and venous occlusion treated with eculizumab
case_summary:
A 26-year-old African American woman presented with 3 days of progressive abdominal and pelvic pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Two months earlier, she had been hospitalized for possible rheumatic fever with polyserositis and cardiac tamponade requiring pericardial drainage. Medications included colchicine, lisinopril, proton-pump inhibitors, ranitidine, tramadol, penicillin G, and albuterol; she had recently quit smoking. Family history was significant for hypertension.

On examination, she was tachycardic (112 beats/min) with a 2/6 systolic murmur. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (41?10^9/L), hemoglobin 107 g/L, hypoalbuminemia (28 g/L), and serum creatinine 53 umol/L. Urinalysis revealed 1+ protein. Abdominal CT demonstrated colitis and mild ascites. A presumptive diagnosis of sepsis related to Clostridium difficile colitis was made, and she received IV metronidazole, oral vancomycin, and IV fluids.

Four days later, serum creatinine rose to 159 umol/L, leukocyte count to

title: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy complicated by coagulopathy: A case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old primigravida at 39 weeks and 3 days of gestation was referred for evaluation of jaundice. She reported 5 days of yellow discoloration of her urine and sclera and 2 days of polyuria and polydipsia. Her antenatal course had been uneventful; she denied medication use, gestational hypertension, flu-like symptoms, or travel to malaria-endemic regions. HIV and hepatitis panels were negative.

On examination, she was jaundiced with moderate pallor. Temperature was 98.4 degreesF, pulse 100/min, and blood pressure 120/80 mm Hg. Abdominal examination revealed a term-sized uterus, breech-presenting fetus with a heart rate of 140/min. Cervix was posterior, soft, uneffaced, and closed.

Laboratory studies showed:
? Random blood sugar 2.6 mmol/L (3.9-5.6)  
? Hemoglobin 8.6 g/dL; peripheral smear with microcytic hypochromic cells, target cells, spherocytes, bite cells  
? WBC 14,000/uL; platelets 163,000/uL  
? Total bilirubin 14.8 mg/dL (0.1-1.2), direct bilirubin 9.2 mg/dL (<0.3)  
?

title: Aggressive Disease and Rare Sequelae in a Unique Case of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome Secondary to Adult Onset Still's Disease
case_summary: A 69-year-old Caucasian woman with hypertension, hypothyroidism, and a history of stage IA breast cancer in remission presented with a 3-month history of daily high fevers up to 39.5  degreesC, severe fatigue, arthralgias of the hips and knees, a diffuse erythematous rash on the chest and upper extremities, episodic pharyngitis, and impaired vision. A chest radiograph showed diffuse airspace disease. Initial laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, platelet count 329 ? 10^3/mcL, white blood cell count 11.1 ? 10^3/mcL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 102 mm/h, C-reactive protein 15 mg/dL, ferritin 1608 ng/mL, and negative antinuclear and rheumatoid factor titers. Extensive infectious and autoimmune serologies--including anti-double-stranded DNA, anti-Smith, anti-Ro, anti-La, anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide, ANCA panel, Lyme ELISA/Western blot, creatine kinase, anti-Jo-1, and anti-scl-70--were unremarkable. Her symptoms initially improved with a short course of glucocorticoids a

title: Concomitant manifestations of systemic lupus erythematosus flare?up and nodal marginal zone B?cell lymphoma in a 41?year?old male patient: A challenging case report
case_summary:
A 41-year-old man with a 10-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus (meeting ACR criteria) and secondary antiphospholipid syndrome (two prior DVTs, positive anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant) presented with 2 months of fatigue, fever, arthralgias, oral lesions, and elevated ESR. His regular medications included prednisolone 7.5 mg/day, azathioprine 100 mg/day, hydroxychloroquine 400 mg/day, and warfarin.

After increasing prednisolone to 15 mg/day and azathioprine to 150 mg/day, he developed dyspnea, persistent symptoms, and new pancytopenia. Rituximab was added and azathioprine discontinued. Two weeks later, on admission he was febrile (38  degreesC), tachypneic (RR 30/min), mildly tachycardic (HR 100/min), with O2 saturation 89% on room air. Exam showed splenomegaly and 1-2 cm cervical lymphadenopathy. He had painless oral ulcers and bilateral wrist swelling without motion limitation.

Laboratory studies showed pancytopenia (WBC 1.9 ?10^3/?L, Hb 8.7 g/dL, platelets

title: Euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis caused by empagliflozin complicated by failure to thrive in a geriatric patient
case_summary:
A 74-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, ischemic stroke, peripheral arterial disease, bilateral nonobstructive renal artery stenosis, chronic right internal carotid occlusion, seizures, and vascular dementia was transferred from a nursing home because of decreased oral intake and dehydration for at least one week. En route, his blood pressure was 94/49 mmHg, and he received 1 L of intravenous normal saline. On arrival, he was somnolent and oriented only to self. His vital signs were: temperature 37.1  degreesC, blood pressure 101/59 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute, respiratory rate 17 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Examination revealed a sarcopenic-appearing man with dry mucous membranes and no acute distress.

Initial laboratory results were notable for acute renal failure and severe metabolic acidosis: sodium 145 mEq/L, potassium 6.6 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 207 mg/dL, creatinine 10.2 mg/dL (baseline 1.1 mg/dL), glucose 185

title: Unusual Manifestation of Intravascular Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Severe Hypercalcemia with Parathyroid Hormone-Related Protein
journal: Cancer Research and Treatment : Official Journal of Korean Cancer Association
case_summary: A 68-year-old man was referred for rapidly deteriorating mental status and persistent fever. Over the preceding two months he had experienced low-grade fevers, fatigue, lethargy, and a 7-kg weight loss. On examination he was febrile but had no focal neurologic deficits, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no skin rash. Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 2560/uL (absolute neutrophils 1430/uL), hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, platelets 108,000/uL, and a markedly elevated C-reactive protein (4.48 mg/dL) with a normal procalcitonin level (0.26 ng/mL). Blood cultures were negative. Serum calcium was 20.6 mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 2668 IU/L, blood urea nitrogen 43.3 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.39 mg/dL. A noncontrast brain CT scan showed no intracranial hemorrhage or mass. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest and abdomen showed mild splenomegaly but no lymphadenopathy, no masses, and no osteolytic lesions in the chest wall, pel

title: Severe cholestatic jaundice associated with Graves' disease
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with a one-week history of generalized abdominal pain, jaundice, dark urine, and lower-extremity edema. He denied alcohol use for 20 years, intravenous drug use, or medication use other than a multivitamin. On examination he was afebrile with a pulse of 95/min, respiratory rate 16/min, and SpO2 95% on room air. He was cachectic and markedly jaundiced with scleral icterus and proptosis. His thyroid was symmetrically enlarged with an audible bruit. His cardiac rhythm was irregularly irregular; skin was warm with a fine tremor and intermittent asterixis. Abdominal examination revealed diffuse mild tenderness without ascites or organomegaly; bilateral lower-extremity edema was present.

Laboratory evaluation showed hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, platelets 115?10^3/uL, leukocytes 11.8?10^3/uL, INR 2.4, sodium 128 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 1.77 mg/dL, BUN 57 mg/dL, and glucose 159 mg/dL. Liver tests revealed total bilirubin 19.8

title:
Leukemoid reaction with severe thrombocytopenia in a dying patient: a case
report and literature review
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
case_summary:
A 28-year-old previously healthy Chinese woman at 4 months' gestation underwent water-sac induced abortion. Two days later she developed high fever, chills, and continuous vaginal bleeding. She was treated with intravenous ceftazidime but progressed to shock (BP 70/30 mmHg) and required emergency evacuation of retained fetal tissue. During surgery she sustained a vaginal injury, became comatose, and was transferred to the surgical ICU.

On arrival:  
- Temperature: hypothermia (on ventilator-assisted ventilation)  
- Blood pressure: not detectable off vasopressors  
- Neurologic: coma  
- Skin: cyanosis of the shins  
- Genitourinary: ongoing vaginal bleeding, anuria

Initial laboratory studies:  
- Hemoglobin: 7.0 g/dl  
- Platelet count: 6???10^9/l  
- White blood cell count: 22?360/ul (87.8% segmented neutrophils)  
- INR 1.99, APTT 51 s  
- Procalcitonin: 50 ng/ml  
- Lactate: 8.4 mmol/l, HCO3- 12.8 mmol/l  
- Creatinine: 200 umol/l

Treatments included fluid resuscitation; transfu

title: Dysfunctional labor and hemoperitoneum secondary to an incidentally discovered dysgerminoma: a case report
case_summary:
A 32?year?old G2P1 Caucasian woman at 40 weeks' gestation was admitted for elective induction of labor. Her pregnancy had been uncomplicated apart from mild second?trimester anemia, chronic constipation worsening near 36 weeks, and 17.3?kg weight gain. A 9?week ultrasound showed normal?appearing ovaries; a 19?week anatomy scan noted bilateral choroid plexus cysts and incomplete adnexal visualization. Fundal heights remained appropriate.

On admission she was well appearing; vital signs were normal and fetal heart tracing reactive. Labs showed hemoglobin 8.6?g/dL and platelets 131???10^9/L. Cervical exam was challenging: cervix anterior and shifted left, fetal station -2. Induction with misoprostol and Foley balloon yielded no change in station despite contractions and an epidural for pain control. Oxytocin was started 15?h after admission.

Shortly after, she developed fever (38.0? degreesC), right upper quadrant pain, and intermittent Category II fetal heart tracing with late decelera

title: Macrophage activation syndrome triggered by systemic lupus erythematosus flare: successful treatment with a combination of dexamethasone sodium phosphate, intravenous immunoglobulin, and cyclosporine: a case report
case_summary: A 33-year-old Egyptian woman with a 5-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus (arthritis, oral ulcers, leukopenia, ANA and anti-dsDNA positivity) and antiphospholipid syndrome (recurrent venous thrombosis, miscarriages, antiphospholipid antibodies) presented with a 4-week history of fever, abdominal pain, malaise, and progressive fatigue. She was taking hydroxychloroquine 400 mg/day, prednisolone 7.5 mg/day, and warfarin 5 mg/day. On admission, temperature was 38.6  degreesC, pulse 107 beats/min, and other vital signs were normal. Examination showed hepatomegaly (3 cm below costal margin) and splenomegaly (4 cm below costal margin); otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies revealed pancytopenia (WBC 0.72 ? 10^9/L, hemoglobin 77 g/L, platelets 22 ? 10^9/L), reticulocytosis, hyponatremia, elevated ALT and AST, proteinuria (0.54 g/24 h), markedly elevated D-dimer (4766 ?g/L), ferritin (9560 ng/mL), LDH (1260 U/L), triglycerides (4.13 mmol/L), prolonged aPTT (123.3 s), low C3 (0.62

title: Persistent eyelid ulceration in an immunocompromised host: A cutaneous sign with the potential for early diagnosis and intervention in disseminated cryptococcosis
case_summary:
A 67-year-old man with a history of renal transplantation on tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone presented with 5 days of intermittent fevers, generalized malaise, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. His past infections included Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia and multidrug-resistant urinary tract infections. On admission, he was diagnosed with enteroaggressive Escherichia coli colitis, carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae pneumonia, and Enterococcus faecalis urinary tract infection. Because of new-onset altered mental status, a lumbar puncture was performed, and cerebrospinal fluid polymerase chain reaction testing was positive for cryptococcal antigen. 

During hospitalization, he noted an ulcer on his left eyelid, which he attributed to a pimple he had repeatedly picked over the preceding 2 months. Dermatologic examination revealed a 3-cm tender, deep, ovoid ulcer on the lateral aspect of the left upper eyelid with heaped-up borders, overlying purulence, and honey-co

title: Recurrent Falls Due to Hypoglycemia: Case of an IGF-2-producing Fibrous Tumor of the Pleura
case_summary: A 77-year-old man was evaluated for recurrent falls and episodes of dizziness over several weeks. He sustained a proximal humerus fracture after a fall and was discharged for rehabilitation. During a follow-up outpatient visit 3 weeks later, severe nocturnal hypoglycemia was noted, requiring him to awaken nightly to consume a snack. His weight was stable, with no other symptoms reported. His medical history included hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, a transient ischemic attack, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. He was not taking insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents but was receiving amlodipine, lisinopril, simvastatin, tamsulosin, and clopidogrel. On examination, he had benign-appearing skin verrucae; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed mild normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 8.3 mmol/L), normal renal function and electrolytes except for mild hyperchloremia, normal liver markers except for mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase and lactate dehydrogena

title: A case of a drug reaction to sulfasalazine in a patient infected with HIV
journal: Southern African Journal of HIV Medicine
final_diagnosis: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
case_summary:
A 46-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis and HIV infection presented with a 1-week history of fever (>38  degreesC) and a generalized skin rash. Two weeks earlier, sulfasalazine had been added to her regimen of hydroxychloroquine, risperidone, and antiretroviral therapy (tenofovir, lopinavir/ritonavir, lamivudine). She had no known drug allergies. Her CD4+ count was 411 cells/uL with an undetectable viral load.  

On examination, she was febrile and had bilateral cervical and left submental lymphadenopathy (>1.5 cm) with severe periorbital edema. Dermatologic findings included indurated palms and soles, superficial mucosal erosions of the lips, and widespread urticarial papules and target lesions on the face, trunk, and extremities.  

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 41.1?10^9/L (neutrophils 17.6?10^9/L, lymphocytes 6.3?10^9/L, eosinophils 3.1?10^9/L), ALT 66 U/L, AST 41 U/L, ALP 116 U/L, GGT 145 U/L, CRP 30 mg/L, ESR 45 mm/h. Blood cultures and hepatitis B and C s

title: A severe case of visceral leishmaniasis and liposomal amphotericin B treatment failure in an immunosuppressed patient 15?years after exposure
case_summary:
An 80-year-old man presented in March 2015 with recurrent fever, fatigue and bloody diarrhoea. He appeared septic, with hypotension (86/64 mmHg), tachycardia (115 /min), and fever of 38.6  degreesC. Examination revealed bibasal crepitations and hepatosplenomegaly; weight was 76 kg. Laboratory tests showed leukopenia (3.1 G/l), normal hemoglobin and platelets, elevated liver enzymes (ASAT 60 U/l; ALAT 71 U/l), and impaired renal function (creatinine 183 umol/l; GFR 29 ml/min). 

Since 2009, he had non-specific granulomatous inflammation on liver biopsy, treated empirically with corticosteroids, azathioprine and mycophenolate mofetil without improvement. A kidney biopsy in 2013 showed similar granulomas; immunosuppression was continued. His history included hypertension, type II diabetes, abdominal aneurysm repair, and treated Q-fever in 1984. Born in Holland, he lived in Switzerland and, as an engineer, had prolonged stays until 1995 in Sudan, Bangladesh, Suriname and Indonesia, and sho

title: Tabetic arthropathy of the knee: A case report
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man presented with a 10-year history of electric-shock sensations in the right lower extremity accompanied by progressive, significant swelling of the right knee. He denied pain, fever, or constitutional symptoms. He had no history of diabetes or known neurologic disease. 

On examination, he was afebrile with stable vital signs. The right knee was markedly swollen, firm, and non-tender, with full range of motion. Osteotendinous reflexes were absent in the lower extremities; the left knee was normal. 

Laboratory studies showed a normal C-reactive protein level, a normal complete blood count, normal fasting glucose, and normal glycated hemoglobin. An immune profile and infectious workup were unremarkable.

A CT scan of the right knee demonstrated: destruction of the lateral femoral condyle and ipsilateral tibial plateau with subchondral osteosclerosis; thickening and irregularity of the femoral and tibial cortices; disorganized periosteal reaction with cortical erosions;

title: A case report of non-dominant right coronary artery occlusion: not always benign!
case_summary: A 50-year-old man with no history of cardiovascular disease and no medications experienced a witnessed out-of-hospital collapse. Bystander CPR was started immediately, and EMS found him pulseless in ventricular fibrillation. After 2 minutes of CPR and one defibrillation, he achieved return of spontaneous circulation (downtime ~10 min) and was intubated. In the emergency department he developed recurrent VF requiring five additional shocks and amiodarone boluses without ROSC, prompting initiation of veno-arterial ECMO; ROSC was then achieved (total downtime ~30 min). Post-ROSC ECG showed normal sinus rhythm with ST elevations in the inferior leads. Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35%. Head CT was negative for anoxic injury. Initial laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 22 200/uL, creatinine 1.41 mg/dL, troponin-I 0.04 ng/mL, lactic acid 11.5 mmol/L, and an anion gap of 19 mmol/L.

title: A Rare and Unusual Case of Burkitt's Lymphoma Presenting with a Prostate Mass in a 12-Year-Old Boy
case_summary:
A 12-year-old Caucasian boy presented with a four-week history of jaw pain that made eating difficult. He developed gingivitis and bleeding gums. One week before admission, he noted tailbone pain and had difficulty initiating urination, with retention symptoms and intermittent dysuria. His family reported a 15-lb weight loss over the prior month. He denied fever but had mild night sweats and low energy, missing a week of school. His past medical and family histories were noncontributory; immunizations were up to date.

On examination, there was symmetric swelling around the lower incisors with upward displacement of the teeth by almost 1.0 cm, and both teeth were loose; there was no purulent discharge. Facial and lower jaw swelling was present without palpable lymphadenopathy. Hepatosplenomegaly was noted, and bruises were seen on the knees, shins, forearms, and elbows, which were felt to be from baseball. The remainder of the examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed platelet

title: Hyper-IgE syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 37-year-old man from an under-resourced area presents with a lifelong history of recurrent skin and pulmonary infections. Since age 2, he has experienced multiple "cold" abscesses on the back, groin, and buttocks caused by Staphylococcus aureus, three of which required incision and drainage. He also has a history of atopic dermatitis with early-onset rash, weight loss, and hyperpigmented skin lesions. He has no history of parental consanguinity, and prenatal exposures were denied.

Medications include ascorbic acid, ferrous sulfate, megestrol acetate for appetite stimulation, and acetaminophen. He experienced abscess recurrences in 2012, 2013, 2014, and 2016, and beginning in 2017 developed bowel and bladder incontinence. In 2018 and 2019 he had fevers, chills, rhinitis, and a dry cough treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics and antifungals. In 2021, hydrocortisone was gradually tapered to 5 mg daily. In 2022, he presented with a large 5?7 cm axillary abscess and stage II pressure u

title: Seronegative Intestinal Villous Atrophy: A Diagnostic Challenge
case_summary:
A 53-year-old woman was admitted with a 6-month history of 6-10 daily episodes of bulky, watery, nonbloody diarrhea, abdominal bloating, and progressive asthenia, accompanied by a 20% involuntary weight loss. She denied abdominal pain, fever, recent travel, sick contacts, dietary changes, or recent medication changes. Her only medication was olmesartan, 40 mg daily, for hypertension, which she had taken for six years. Previous outpatient treatments--including probiotics, an opioid agonist, a gluten- and lactose-free diet, and a trial of oral antibiotics--had failed to improve her symptoms. 

On examination, she appeared pale with muscle wasting. Vital signs were stable. Laboratory studies showed mild microcytic anemia (hemoglobin 11 g/dL), leukopenia (3.8 ? 10^9/L), thrombocytopenia (130 ? 10^9/L), and hypokalemia (2.8 mmol/L). Tests for inflammatory markers, liver and kidney function, total protein, albumin, immunoglobulins, vitamin B12, folate, thyroid-stimulating hormone, anti-tissu

title: Severe Systemic Chromobacterium violaceum Infection: A Case Study of a German Long-Term Resident in French Guyana
case_summary: A 59-year-old man presented with a 5-day history of fever up to 40  degreesC, diarrhea, nausea, and general weakness. Nineteen days earlier, he had returned from French Guiana, where he had intermittent fevers treated with an unknown oral ?-lactam antibiotic. He noted a furuncle-like nodule on his abdomen for 4 weeks, which he had disinfected. Two months prior, he had swum in a lake in Brazil; he also reported regular contact with cattle and sheep and past consumption of unpasteurized milk. He had no chronic illnesses or medications. On examination, temperature was 40.3  degreesC, blood pressure 146/84 mmHg, pulse 112 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Cardiac and pulmonary exams were unremarkable. Abdominal exam showed a crusty erythema 5 cm in diameter below the umbilicus; no other skin lesions were noted. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytes 12.7?10^9 cells/L, hemoglobin 8.1 mmol/L, C-reactive protein 1552 nmol/L, procalcitonin 40.4

title: Is It Stevens-Johnson Syndrome or MIS-C with Mucocutaneous Involvement?
case_summary: A 25-month-old boy was admitted with three days of fever and a one-day history of maculopapular rash. The rash began with mild itching on the feet, then spread to the thighs and genital area despite antihistamines. On presentation he had fever, malaise, poor feeding, conjunctival injection, dry red lips, and maculopapular rashes that progressed to ulcer and bulla formation. Vital signs were: temperature 39.5  degreesC, blood pressure 82/10 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, and pulse 86 beats/min. His only recent medication was ranitidine; his mother had an upper respiratory infection three weeks earlier. He was empirically treated with supportive care, systemic steroids, and intravenous immunoglobulin for presumed SJS/TEN. Initial laboratory studies showed: WBC 3200/uL (58% neutrophils, 41% lymphocytes), hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, platelets 29 000/uL, ESR 36 mm/hr, CRP 58 mg/L, normal liver and renal function, elevated ferritin (517 ug/L), and fibrinogen (615 mg/dL). A COVID-19 PCR tes

title: Refractory Intestinal Beh?et-Like Disease Associated with Trisomy 8 Myelodysplastic Syndrome Resolved by Parenteral Nutrition
case_summary: An 80-year-old man with a history of hypertension presented with anemia. Eight months after initial evaluation, he developed intermittent fever exceeding 38  degreesC and an elevated C-reactive protein level of 13.31 mg/dL. Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 4470/uL (46.5% neutrophils, 23.5% lymphocytes, 29.5% monocytes, 0.5% eosinophils), a red blood cell count of 2.39 ? 10^6/uL, hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, hematocrit 27.2%, platelet count 96 ? 10^3/uL, and a reticulocyte percentage of 2.2%. Bone marrow biopsy revealed a slightly hypercellular marrow with mild dysmyelopoiesis and dyserythropoiesis, < 5% myeloblasts, and no megaloid changes. Cytogenetic analysis showed 47,XY,+8 in 1 of 20 cells, and fluorescence in situ hybridization confirmed trisomy 8 in 47 of 1000 cells, leading to a diagnosis of refractory anemia in myelodysplastic syndrome with trisomy 8. Despite investigations to exclude infectious disease, autoimmune disease, and malignancy as the cause of fever, no

title: Sport-Related Portal Vein Thrombosis: An Unusual Complication
case_summary:
A 32-year-old previously healthy man presented with 3 weeks of gradually worsening periumbilical abdominal pain that began approximately 2 weeks after starting Brazilian jiu-jitsu. He attributed the pain to contusion from the opponent's "knee on stomach" technique. The pain was constant (5-7/10), relieved by lying still, and exacerbated by movement. He reported nausea without vomiting, no fevers or chills, and reduced bowel movement frequency without blood or mucus. He denied medications, abdominal surgery, family history of liver disease or thrombosis, and risk factors such as alcohol abuse or illicit drug use.

On examination, he was afebrile with normal vital signs. Abdominal palpation elicited periumbilical and right upper quadrant tenderness without rebound; there was no hepatosplenomegaly or ascites. Laboratory studies showed AST 77?U/L and ALT 44?U/L, with normal bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, amylase, lipase, complete blood count, coagulation studies, electrolytes, and renal

title: Neutrophilic Dermatosis of the Dorsal Hands: Report of a Case and Review of the Literature
final_diagnosis: Neutrophilic dermatosis of the dorsal hands
case_summary:
A 45-year-old obese woman presented with a 3-week history of painful ulcers on the dorsal hands and fingers. She first observed small red macules that over 2 weeks progressed to painful swelling and ulceration. She denied trauma or new medications. Her history included laparoscopic gastric sleeve surgery and untreated mild diabetes. 
Initially diagnosed as a bacterial infection, she underwent wound cultures and was treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and topical mupirocin. She subsequently developed fever and malaise and was admitted for presumed sepsis, receiving intravenous vancomycin. 
A few days later, on re-evaluation, the dorsal aspects of the right index finger, left ring finger, and right third proximal metacarpophalangeal joint showed erythematous ulcerated nodules and plaques with violaceous borders and marked edema. Nonulcerated areas had fusiform swelling. Tender indurated erythematous plaques were also present on the right wrist dorsum. The remainder of the ski

title: Generalized lymphatic anomalies and review of the current management landscape: a case report and review of the?literature
case_summary: A 35-year-old Caucasian woman was referred for evaluation of suspected malignancy and metabolic bone disease after incidental imaging findings. She had a 1-year history of nonspecific epigastric pain and a remote 10-lb weight loss that she had regained; she denied fever, night sweats, bone pain, or fractures. Medications included pantoprazole, an oral contraceptive, calcium, and vitamin D3. Examination revealed a single soft, immobile left axillary lymph node; the remainder of her exam was unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 137 g/L, leukocytes 5.6 ? 10^9/L, platelets 192 ? 10^9/L, normal electrolytes, AST 12 U/L, ALT 14 U/L, ALP 94 U/L, LDH 123 U/L, and mildly elevated amylase and lipase. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen excluded pancreatitis but demonstrated innumerable low-density splenic lesions, a horseshoe kidney, and multiple osteolytic lesions throughout the axial skeleton. Further testing showed absence of monoclonal antibodies on serum and urine elec

title: Acute Adrenal Insufficiency Associated with Tuberculous Vertebral Osteomyelitis and Lymphadenopathy: Case Report
case_summary:
A 51?year?old man from Turkey was admitted with several months of anorexia, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and weight loss. His family noted darkening of his skin and he reported lumbar back pain. He had undergone knee surgery 5 months earlier; otherwise, his medical history was unremarkable. On examination, his blood pressure was 90/50?mm?Hg with severe orthostatic hypotension, and there was right inguinal lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies showed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50?mm/h, C?reactive protein 14.5?mg/L, sodium 121?mmol/L, potassium 7.5?mmol/L, creatinine 2.2?mg/dL, and serum cortisol 5??g/dL. He was diagnosed with acute adrenal crisis and treated with intravenous fluids and methylprednisolone; diarrhea resolved and electrolytes normalized. Fludrocortisone was added. Under treatment, the adrenocorticotropic hormone level was low. 

Magnetic resonance imaging of the upper abdomen showed asymmetrically enlarged adrenal glands (right, 9?mm; left, 11?mm), consis

title: First diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura after SARS-CoV-2 vaccine - case report
case_summary: A 25-year-old man presented 13 days after his first dose of an mRNA SARS-CoV-2 vaccine with progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, headache, nausea, and a diffuse petechial rash. He had no significant past medical history, denied other medications, illicit drugs, or family history of hematologic or autoimmune disease. On examination, he was afebrile, normotensive, and had petechiae on both legs. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 7.4 g/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 999 U/L, haptoglobin <0.1 g/L, schistocytes 2.1%, platelet count 29/nL, and creatinine 1.5 mg/dL (eGFR 64 mL/min/1.73 m2). A PLASMIC score was 6, indicating high risk for severe ADAMTS-13 deficiency. Neurological examination and head CT were normal. He underwent cranial MRI/MRA, which showed no cerebral thrombosis. Routine tests for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia were negative, and assays for PF4-dependent platelet activation were pending.

title: Atypical Manifestation of DRESS Syndrome
case_summary:
A 52-year-old African American man presented with persistent fever one week after discharge for Clostridium difficile colitis treated with oral vancomycin. During that hospitalization he was started on allopurinol for acute gout and developed a generalized purpuric, morbilliform rash on his chest, which spread to his extremities; he was treated with a prednisone taper, which he completed prior to return.

His medical history included hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 5, osteoarthritis, GERD, and gout. On examination, temperature was 38.3 degreesC; other vital signs were normal. Skin showed excoriations but no active rash or lymphadenopathy; cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed WBC count normal, hemoglobin 7.9 g/dL, platelet count 71 K/uL (new), creatinine 6.4 mg/dL (baseline 5-6), BUN 60 mg/dL; AST 878 U/L, ALT 1,117 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 169 U/L, GGT 209 U/L; lactate normal; urinalysis negative; chest X-ray unremarkabl

title: Scalp mass: an atypical presentation of multiple myeloma
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of a gradually enlarging scalp mass over the left parietal bone. He noted recent paresthesia in the area but denied pain, drainage, redness, weight loss, fever, chills, weakness, fatigue, vision changes, infections, or head trauma. His medical history was notable only for anxiety and hypercholesterolemia; he took no medications. He was a non?smoker, minimal alcohol use, and no illicit drugs.

On examination, vital signs were normal. There was a 4-cm fluctuant, mobile mass over the left parietal scalp; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. In the office, aspiration of the mass yielded no fluid, raising suspicion for a solid lesion. Surgical excision was scheduled. Under general anesthesia, dissection revealed soft hemorrhagic tissue and a defect extending through the left parietal bone into the cranial cavity; the wound was packed and the patient transferred for imaging.

Noncontrast CT of the head showed a 3.3-cm lytic les

title: A case of paradoxical reaction after treatment of generalized tuberculous lymphadenopathy in a peritoneal dialysis patient
case_summary: A 55-year-old man receiving automated peritoneal dialysis for end-stage kidney disease presented 3 days after starting anti-tuberculosis therapy with acute-onset dizziness, nausea, low-grade fever, and cloudy dialysate. He had been diagnosed with generalized tuberculous lymphadenopathy based on CT findings of para-aortic, mesenteric, and mediastinal lymphadenopathy and a lymph node biopsy showing necrotizing granulomatous inflammation. His regimen included isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, moxifloxacin, and pyridoxine. On examination, temperature was 38 degreesC, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, pulse 110/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air; his abdomen was distended with clear dialysis fluid, non-tender, with active bowel sounds. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 12.9?g/dL, WBC 9.7?10^9/L, platelets 322?10^9/L, sodium 125?mmol/L, potassium 3.7?mmol/L, bicarbonate 19?mmol/L, BUN 13?mmol/L, creatinine 796?umol/L, CRP 77?mg/L. Peritoneal fluid analysis revealed WBC 2752 cells/mm^3 with

title: Acute kidney injury with partial Fanconi syndrome in a patient with leptospirosis: a case report
case_summary: A 34-year-old man presented with 4 days of severe leg pain, diffuse arthralgia, low-grade fevers, profuse sweating, vomiting, diarrhea without abdominal pain, odynophagia, dry cough, headaches, and fatigue. He had a history of heavy alcohol and multiple recreational drugs use but reported no recent animal contact, travel, or unpasteurized food intake. On exam he was afebrile, hypotensive (94/50 mmHg), with dry mucosa, erythematous non-exudative tonsils, normal cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams, and no rash; neurologic exam was unremarkable. Initial labs showed: hemoglobin 99 g/L, platelets 24?10^9/L, elevated CRP (213 mg/L), transaminases ~3?ULN with cholestasis, conjugated bilirubin 29 umol/L. Serum creatinine rose from a baseline 70 umol/L to 247 umol/L (KDIGO stage 3 AKI) with preserved urine output; urea 12 mmol/L. Urinalysis: proteinuria (1.076 g/g), albuminuria (2+), 97 leukocytes/uL, 25 erythrocytes/uL, renal tubular cells (1+), fractional excretion of sodium 3.56%. Electrolyt

title: Role of point of care ultrasound in the absence of radial pulse and unrecordable blood pressure during the assessment of shock: Case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 60-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of left flank pain, urinary frequency, urgency, dribbling, incomplete bladder emptying, and two to three episodes of vomiting daily. His history included hypertension (on amlodipine) and a 40-pack-year smoking history. On arrival, radial pulses and blood pressure were undetectable; respiratory rate was 24-32/min; temperature 36.8  degreesC; SpO? 96% on room air. Femoral pulses were palpable; pedal pulses were undetectable. Chest was clear; abdomen non-tender; no murmurs; neurological exam unremarkable; no cyanosis, paresis, or sensory/motor deficits; he was alert and oriented. Initial laboratories showed leukocytosis (24,000/uL), elevated creatinine, and ALT/AST >2? ULN; urinalysis was negative for infection. He received 30 mL/kg Ringer's lactate, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and vasopressors. On reassessment, blood pressure remained unrecordable despite adequate urine output and a heart rate of 120-150 bpm. The patient became agitated,

title: SLE strikes the heart! A rare presentation of SLE myocarditis presenting as cardiogenic shock
case_summary:
A 20-year-old Filipino man with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed 6 months earlier and maintained on hydroxychloroquine presented with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, fatigue, dry cough, fevers, and a chronic progressive rash. He denied recent travel, sick contacts, high-risk sexual activity, intravenous drug use, smoking, or alcohol use. On examination, his temperature was 100.8  degreesF, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 118 bpm, and respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable. Skin assessment revealed hyperpigmented to violaceous, scaly plaques with excoriated papules on the extremities, back, and chest.

Laboratory studies showed leukopenia, anemia, non-nephrotic proteinuria without active sediment, elevated troponin and N-terminal pro-BNP, high inflammatory markers, hypocomplementemia, and positive ANA with anti-Smith, RNP, chromatin, SS-A, double-stranded DNA, pANCA, and MPO antibodies. Initial chest radiograph was normal.

title: Spontaneous widespread muscle hematoma complicated by pyomyositis in a case of dengue hemorrhagic fever: a case report from Nepal
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man presented during a dengue outbreak with a 2-day history of fever. He lived in Kathmandu and had no comorbidities. On examination, his temperature was 101.8 degreesF, blood pressure 124/80 mmHg, and pulse 86 bpm. A dengue NS1 antigen test was positive; IgG and IgM antibodies were negative. Initial laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 16.7 g/dL, white blood cell count 6 ? 10^3/mm^3, and platelets 131 ? 10^3/mm^3; other blood and urine studies were unremarkable. He was sent home with antipyretics.

The next day, he returned with vomiting, severe headache, myalgia, and a rash. His temperature was 100.5 degreesF, blood pressure 130/84 mmHg, and pulse 92 bpm. Petechiae were noted over the extremities, torso, abdomen, and face. Repeat tests showed declining white-cell and platelet counts, mild hyponatremia (sodium 130 mEq/L), and mild transaminase elevation. He was admitted for supportive care.

By day 7 of illness, his white-cell count fell to 1.9 ? 10^3/mm^3 and platelet cou

title: Successful treatment of streptococcal toxic shock syndrome complicated by primary peritonitis and bilateral empyema in a healthy young woman: Identification of uncommon clone emm103 and novel sequence type 1363
case_summary:
A previously healthy 22-year-old woman presented with a 1-day history of worsening left lower abdominal pain. She had eaten raw chicken 7 days earlier and had no significant past medical history. On examination, she was alert, febrile (39.6  degreesC), tachycardic (125 beats/min), blood pressure was 108/63 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. She had lower abdominal and rebound tenderness without pharyngitis, skin lesions, or rash. Initial laboratory tests showed a white blood cell count of 12 ? 10^3/mm^3 and a C-reactive protein level of 18 mg/dL; blood cultures were negative. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed localized thickening of the peritoneum in the left lower quadrant. She was observed without antimicrobial therapy. 

On hospital day 5, she developed chest pain and persistent fever. Repeat CT demonstrated generalized peritoneal thickening, increased ascites, and bilateral pleural effusions. Overnight, she developed respirator

title: Diamond-Blackfan anemia RPL35A: a case report
case_summary:
A 6-week-old girl was admitted with 1 day of lethargy, poor feeding, shortness of breath, and irritability on a background of progressive pallor. There was no relevant family history. Antenatal screens had shown low pregnancy-associated plasma protein A and growth restriction, but fetal karyotype and anomaly scans were normal. She was delivered by cesarean section at 37 weeks, birth weight 2465 g, and discharged healthy on day 3.

On admission at 6 weeks, she was tachycardic and lethargic without icterus or dysmorphic features. Weight was 3200 g. Laboratory values (normal range for age in brackets) included: hemoglobin 3.1 g/dL (10-18), hematocrit 9% (31-55%), MCV 106 fL (85-123), MCHC 34 g/dL (32-37), reticulocyte production index 0.0, absolute reticulocyte count 5.1 ? 10^9/L (20-60 ? 10^9/L). White cells 4.0 ? 10^9/L (5-19.5), neutrophils 0.32 ? 10^9/L (1-9), platelets 655 ? 10^9/L (140-420). C-reactive protein was 7.7 mg/L (< 5). Urine culture grew Escherichia coli; other virology t

title: Leukemia cutis revealing relapse of a chronic myeloid leukemia: A case report
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man with a 14-year history of chronic myeloid leukemia treated sequentially with imatinib and dasatinib presented with several weeks of multiple painful, firm subcutaneous nodules on the forearms, left buttock, and knee. The nodules developed circumferential purplish discoloration and surrounding hematoma. He also reported worsening dyspnea, low-grade fever, and arthralgias. He admitted to recent irregular adherence to dasatinib. There was no history of trauma, travel, infection, or new medications.

On examination, he was afebrile. Chest and joint examinations were unremarkable. Skin examination revealed multiple tender, firm, subcutaneous nodules with peripheral hematoma.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL; white-cell count 199.3?10^9/L with 5% blasts; neutrophils 93.05?10^9/L; lymphocytes 7.18?10^9/L; monocytes 24.32?10^9/L; platelets 389?10^9/L; C-reactive protein 18.7 mg/L; haptoglobin <10 mg/dL; creatinine 1.32 mg/dL; lactate dehydrogenase 7268 U/L; uric

title: Acute interstitial nephritis after COVID-19 vaccination
case_summary:
A woman in her 70s presented 8 days after receiving the first dose of the AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine with a 1-week history of feeling generally unwell. She reported a transient right-sided weakness and a generalized headache that improved with glucose ingestion. She denied fever, urinary symptoms, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, joint pains, mouth ulcers, or back pain. She took no over-the-counter or herbal medications and had no NSAID use. 

Her medical history included type II diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 2004) with background diabetic retinopathy and hypertension, treated with alogliptin, glimepiride, metformin, olmesartan, and simvastatin. She was independent in activities of daily living, a non-smoker, and drank no alcohol.

On ambulance arrival, her blood glucose was 1.6?mmol/L. A CT scan of the head showed no acute intracranial pathology. Laboratory studies revealed stage III acute kidney injury with metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia (serum potassium, 6.1?mmol/L). Her serum creati

title: Acute localized exanthematous pustulosis induced by fosfomycin
case_summary:
A 67-year-old woman with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and stage IV chronic kidney disease presented with a 1-week history of dysuria and a 4-day history of a mildly pruritic skin rash. Four days before the rash began, she had completed a 3-day course of fosfomycin for an extended-spectrum ?-lactamase-producing urinary tract infection. She denied fever, recent new medications or herbal remedies other than fosfomycin, and a personal or family history of psoriasis or dermatological conditions.

On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. Her skin (Fitzpatrick type III) showed multiple tiny, annular, nonfollicular pustules with fine central desquamation on an erythematous base, predominantly localized to the bilateral inguinal regions, right side of the abdomen, right inner arm, and left forearm. The face, oral mucosa, and genital areas were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies demonstrated normal hematologic, renal, and liver function tests; an erythrocyte sedimentation

title: Mutism and rigidity due to antipsychotic?induced catatonia improved by hemodialysis: A case report
journal: PCN Reports: Psychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences
case_summary:
A 79-year-old Japanese man with a 1-year history of diabetic nephropathy and a 10-year history of an asymptomatic left chronic subdural hematoma (CSDH) was admitted for renal failure with metabolic acidosis and hyponatremia. He had no psychiatric history and was independent in daily activities. On admission, he was mildly drowsy but without focal motor deficits. Laboratory findings included sodium 113 mEq/L, creatinine 7.91 mg/dL, pH 7.17, PCO2 18.1 Torr, and HCO3- 6.3 mEq/L. He received sodium correction and began hemodialysis on hospital day 4, with improvement in consciousness by day 5 and normalization of electrolytes and renal function by day 9 (sodium 142 mEq/L, creatinine 1.84 mg/dL). 

On day 10, he became agitated at night and was given 2.5 mg intramuscular haloperidol. The following morning he was mute and motionless, his eyes open. Four hours later, he developed severe rigidity of all extremities and firmly resisted mouth opening. Just before his fourth dialysis session he w

title: Chronic recurrent multifocal osteomyelitis in a 3.5-year-old boy
journal: Journal of Research in Medical Sciences : The Official Journal of Isfahan University of Medical Sciences
case_summary: A previously healthy 3?-year-old boy presented with left forearm pain and fever. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.0 g/dL, hematocrit 32.3%, white-cell count 8100 per mm3 with 51.7% lymphocytes, platelet count 462,000 per mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 72 mm/hr. Plain radiographs of the left forearm demonstrated increased medullary density in both ulna and radius, permeative lytic lesions in the radius, and cortical irregularity without soft-tissue mass. MRI of the left forearm confirmed these findings. A biopsy of the left radius revealed extensive necrosis without definitive diagnostic features. Flow cytometry and bone-marrow aspiration were unrevealing for malignancy or infection. The child was observed without specific therapy. Five months later, he returned with right forearm pain and new pain in the left humerus. Repeat radiographs showed resolution of the prior lesion in the left forearm and a new lytic lesion with cortical irregularity in the midshaft of the left

title: Unusual presentation of primary myelofibrosis with spontaneous bleeding after laparoscopic adrenalectomy: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man underwent laparoscopic right adrenalectomy for a rapidly enlarging 3.5 ? 2.5 cm right adrenal mass discovered incidentally on CT performed for dyspnea. Over the preceding two years, MRI and PET scans had shown an increasing lesion with imaging features suggestive of adenoma, pheochromocytoma, or tuberculosis, along with splenomegaly. Laboratory tests excluded a secreting lesion and tuberculosis infection. His medical history included hypertension (on beta-blockers), benign prostatic hyperplasia (on silodosin), COPD (on inhaled steroids), and hypothyroidism; he had prior appendectomy and surgery for head trauma.

The adrenalectomy was performed laparoscopically using an open technique for trocar placement. The adrenal vein was coagulated, and the gland was removed; haemostasis was achieved and two drains were placed. Postoperatively, he was monitored in the ICU for COPD. That night he developed hypotension refractory to fluids, tachycardia, and anemia unresponsive to t

title: Bilateral idiopathic corneal opacity: A report of Ascher ring and a review of the literature
journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and prostate cancer was referred for cataract evaluation of the right eye. He reported no photophobia, eye pain, discharge, contact-lens use, ocular trauma, or prior ocular procedures, and no personal or family history of metabolic disorders or corneal dystrophies. Medications included atorvastatin, chlorthalidone, clopidogrel, metformin, primidone, topiramate, tamsulosin, and propranolol. 

Best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 in the right eye and 20/30 in the left. Pupillary reactions and intraocular pressures were normal bilaterally. Corneal pachymetry measured 549 um centrally and 681 um peripherally in the right eye, and 547 um centrally and 673 um peripherally in the left. 

Slit-lamp examination revealed symmetric bilateral circular mid-peripheral full-thickness stromal white rings (7-8 mm in diameter). Corneal sensation was intact; there was no vascularization, fibrosis, thinning, or fluorescein staining. Gonioscopy show

title: Localized retroperitoneal Rosai-Dorfman-Destombes disease as a cause of fever of unknown origin in adults. Case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary: A 53-year-old woman was admitted with a one-month history of night sweats, fevers up to 39.5  degreesC, 10 kg weight loss, fatigue, weakness, abdominal discomfort, and headache. She had no relevant family, drug, surgical, or psychosocial history. On examination, she was febrile but had no peripheral lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, C-reactive protein 100.6 mg/L, and fibrinogen 6.57 g/L. Blood, throat, and urine cultures and serologic tests for Brucella spp., Chlamydia spp., Cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, Francisella tularensis, HIV, Mycoplasma spp., Toxocara spp., Treponema pallidum, and Yersinia spp. were all negative. Serology for Toxoplasma gondii showed a false-positive IgM with negative IgG and IgA, and follow-up IgM levels trended toward normalization. Antistreptolysin-O titer and rheumatoid factor were negative. Abdominal ultrasonography, transthoracic echocardiography, chest radiography, and abdominal CT scan were unre

title: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia accompanied with COVID?19: a case report
case_summary: A 77-year-old man with a history of bronchial asthma presented with left-sided chest pain and shortness of breath. He was referred after chest radiography showed bilateral pneumonia and reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction testing was positive for SARS-CoV-2. On examination, temperature was 37.7 degreesC, heart rate 105 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 8.4?10^3/?L with a neutrophil fraction of 72.3% and an eosinophil fraction of 12.1%; hemoglobin 15.2 g/dL; platelets 300?10^3/?L; sodium 138 mEq/L; potassium 4.4 mEq/L; chloride 105 mEq/L; C-reactive protein 5.93 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 23 mg/dL; creatinine 0.80 mg/dL; aspartate aminotransferase 15 IU/L; alanine aminotransferase 11 IU/L; and lactate dehydrogenase 149 U/L. Chest computed tomography showed bilateral ground-glass opacities and consolidation in the upper and middle lung zones at subpleural and peribronchial regions. The patient was treated with

title: Chronic Granulomatous Disease Presenting as Aseptic Ascites in a 2-Year-Old Child
case_summary:
A 2-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of fever and abdominal distension. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy. His only prior significant infection was at 6 months of age, when he developed a methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus lymphadenitis near the left ear that recurred once but resolved after two drainages and antibiotic therapy.

At 28 months of age, he had daily fevers up to 39.4 degreesC for nearly 1 month, with intermittent dysuria and mildly loose stools. There was no travel history or family history of immunologic, hematologic, or oncologic disease. His weight percentile declined from the 36th to the 7th over the preceding year. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed:
- Albumin 2.8 g/dL (3.8-5.4)  
- Alkaline phosphatase 509 IU/L (<290)  
- C-reactive protein 10.69 mg/dL (0.08-1.2)  
- Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 131 mm/hr (0-20)  
- Ferritin 178.1 ng/mL (10-60)  
- Hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL (11.5-13.5)  
- Hema

title: Recurrent undifferentiated shock: Idiopathic Systemic Capillary Leak Syndrome
case_summary:
A 51-year-old man presented with a one-day history of increasing fatigue and malaise, preceded by nausea, vomiting, myalgia, and arthralgia. This was his sixth similar presentation over six months. His past medical history included mild obstructive sleep apnea, prior intracranial hemorrhage, and two hospital admissions for undefined "septic shock." He took aspirin and vitamin B12, had no allergies, was a nonsmoker, and reported a long history of heavy alcohol use. Family history was notable for a brother with lymphoma.  

On examination, he was tachycardic at 103 bpm and had an oxygen saturation of 92% on room air; other vital signs were normal. Laboratory studies showed: hemoglobin 224 g/L, red-cell count 6.66?10^12/L, hematocrit 64%, albumin 34 g/L, white-cell count 19.7?10^9/L, and evidence of acute kidney injury (creatinine 121 umol/L, eGFR 59 mL/min/1.73 m^2). A 130-mL venesection was performed, and the patient was admitted.  

Despite aggressive fluid resuscitation, his blood pre

title: Multiple ring-enhancing cerebral lesions in systemic lupus erythematosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 39-year-old Sri Lankan woman with a 5-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus treated with mycophenolate mofetil (1 g/day) and intermittent self-prescribed steroids presented with a two-week history of nausea, vomiting, severe headache, progressive drowsiness, high-grade intermittent fever, and confusion. She had no recent travel, animal exposures, or known tuberculosis contacts.  

On examination, she was febrile and drowsy (Glasgow Coma Scale 12), with no neck stiffness or focal cranial nerve abnormalities. Limb reflexes were exaggerated with bilateral extensor plantar responses. She had a firm, non-tender hepatomegaly 3 cm below the costal margin; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.  

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, leukocyte count 21.5 ? 10^9/L with 88% neutrophils, platelet count 324 ? 10^9/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 90 mm/hour, C-reactive protein 46 mg/L, and normal complement levels. Blood and urine cultures were sterile; chest radiog

title: Fatal fulminant herpes simplex hepatitis following surgery in an adult
case_summary:
A 72-year-old Japanese man with no chronic illness or immunosuppression underwent open cholecystectomy and choledocholithotomy for gallbladder and biliary stones after initial biliary stenting and drainage. Preoperative labs showed mild anemia (hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL), albumin 3.4 g/dL, and normal leukocyte, lymphocyte, and platelet counts. He received a two-day course of postoperative antibiotics; no new medications were added thereafter. No mucocutaneous lesions were noted.

On postoperative day 5, he developed fever and diarrhea. Laboratory tests revealed a leukocyte count of 2600/mm? and elevations in AST and ALT. Hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody were negative. Blood and urine cultures were unrevealing. Contrast-enhanced CT showed no vascular compromise, biliary obstruction, or abscess.

On postoperative day 8, he had sudden marked transaminase elevations (AST 2946 IU/L, ALT 1190 IU/L), relatively normal total bilirubin (1.0 mg/dL), new coagulopathy (INR 2.0, D-di

title: A full-term pregnant woman with secondary Evans syndrome caused by severe coronavirus disease 2019: a case report
case_summary:
A 29-year-old Indonesian woman, gravida 2 para 1 at 39 weeks' gestation, presented with a 5-day history of COVID-19 confirmed by PCR, accompanied by gross hematuria, dry cough, fever, dyspnea, nausea, anosmia, and fatigue. She had no prior medical history and no family history of hematologic disorders. On examination, she was fully conscious, blood pressure 109/77 mmHg, heart rate 89/min, respiratory rate 28/min, temperature 37.8  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 100% on 2 L/min nasal cannula. Fetal heart rate was 142/min, and estimated fetal weight was 4 kg without signs of distress.  
Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral bronchopneumonia. Laboratory studies on admission showed hemoglobin 10 g/dL, white blood cell count 5.8?10^9/L (neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio 11), lymphocytes 696/uL, platelet count 2?10^9/L, PaO2/FiO2 ratio 200, C-reactive protein 7.77 mg/L, procalcitonin 0.35 ng/mL, prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time 54.9 s, d-dimer 1400 ng/mL, fibrinogen 333 mg/dL. U

title: Initial diagnosis of Wegener's granulomatosis mimicking severe ulcerative colitis: a case report
case_summary: A 20-year-old Caucasian woman was transferred to the ICU in septic shock with multiorgan dysfunction. Her history included allergic asthma and Basedow's disease managed by partial thyroidectomy. Four weeks prior, she was treated with cefuroxime for a retroareolar inflammation; persistent fever and diarrhea prompted sequential changes to metronidazole and then vancomycin. Stool studies, blood, and urine cultures repeatedly showed no pathogens and C. difficile toxin was negative. A colonoscopy demonstrated multiple small ulcerations throughout the colon, most pronounced in the ileum, cecum, and sigmoid colon. A necrotic lesion of the right breast grew Staphylococcus aureus, Actinomyces turicensis, and Peptostreptococcus species; surgical exploration excluded an abscess. Despite broad-spectrum antibiotics (imipenem-cilastatin, moxifloxacin, fluconazole) and vasopressor support, she remained anuric (creatinine 5.0 mg/dL), with elevated bilirubin (2.9 mg/dL), AST 2572 U/L, ALT 608 U/L, WBC

title: Fever with Rash is One of the First Presentations of COVID-19 in Children: A Case Report
case_summary:
A previously healthy 12-month-old boy was brought with a 4-day history of fever (maximum 39 degreesC, axillary) and rash. On day 2 of illness, parents noted erythematous maculopapular, erythema multiforme-like targetoid lesions beginning on the soles, ascending symmetrically to the trunk and face; the rash was nonpruritic and slightly edematous. There was no history of drug or food allergy; vaccinations were up to date. The child received a single dose of azithromycin at home. Both parents, who are nurses, had symptomatic COVID-19 20 days earlier and had close contact.

On arrival: temperature 38.2 degreesC, blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg, heart rate 98 bpm, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Skin exam: targetoid erythematous maculopapular lesions on trunk and extremities, erythematous facial patches, mild edema; palms and soles involved, mucous membranes spared. Oropharynx and tonsils were erythematous; right conjunctival mucopurulent discharge. No lympha

title: Diabetes Myonecrosis: A Debilitating Complication in an Indigenous Young Woman With Long Standing Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
case_summary:
A 24-year-old Indigenous Australian woman from a remote community presented with a 3-month history of left thigh swelling and pain associated with limitation of movement. She had type 1 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 14 years with chronically poor glycaemic control (HbA1c, 12.4% on admission; historically as high as 20.9%) and multiple microvascular complications, including proliferative retinopathy, nephrotic-range proteinuria, peripheral neuropathy, and autonomic neuropathy. Other history was notable for acute rheumatic fever, syphilis, recurrent urinary tract infections, active smoking (20 cigarettes per day), and prior right femoral neck fracture with internal fixation. Her only medication was premixed insulin (degludec and aspart 14 units twice daily).

On examination she was pale, cachectic (weight 32.5 kg; BMI 12.9 kg/m2), and sinus tachycardic at 120 bpm. She had significant tender swelling of the left thigh without erythema, warmth, or skin changes; peripheral pulses were

title: Deeply jaundiced: Not so surgical after all
journal: Southern African Journal of Infectious Diseases
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man presented with a 3-day history of nausea, vomiting, headache, photophobia, fever, anorexia, abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. He is a gardener living in a rodent-infested informal settlement. He has a history of weekend binge ethanol use. There was no recent travel. On examination, he was tachycardic (128 bpm), hypotensive (69/35 mmHg), febrile (38.1  degreesC) and profoundly jaundiced with prominent conjunctival suffusion. Lung auscultation revealed left lower-zone crackles; the abdomen was generally tender with guarding. He received fluid resuscitation, vasopressors and empiric ceftriaxone. 
Laboratory studies showed CRP 349 mg/L, leukocytes 20.21?10^9/L, blood urea nitrogen 20.8 mmol/L, creatinine 331 ?mol/L, total bilirubin 126 ?mol/L (conjugated 109 ?mol/L), ALT 35 U/L, AST 98 U/L, sodium 135 mmol/L, albumin 28 g/L, platelets 38?10^9/L; INR 1.23, fibrinogen 9.0 g/L, D-dimer 1.05 mg/L. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis: protein 0.41 g/L, glucose 3.7 mmol/L, 2 lymphoc

title: A case of basidiobolomycosis mimicking rhabdomyosarcoma: A diagnostic challenge
case_summary: An 8-year-old boy from southern Saudi Arabia presented with a 3-month history of perianal pain, constipation, and gluteal swelling associated with intermittent low-grade fever. He underwent incision of a presumed gluteal abscess and received antibiotics without improvement. On referral, he reported episodic fever, decreased oral intake, dysuria with urinary frequency and scant output, and back pain partially relieved by acetaminophen. On examination, he was febrile and underweight, with a firm erythematous gluteal mass extending to the perianal region and bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, hematocrit 0.353 L/L, MCV 59 fL, WBC 16.74 ? 10^9/L with 2.3 ? 10^9/L eosinophils, ESR 88 mm/h, CRP 205 mg/dL, and creatinine 44 mg/dL; blood and urine cultures were negative. Ultrasound, CT, MRI, and PET/CT demonstrated a hypermetabolic mass arising from the bladder base/prostate with intravesical extension, involvement of the left pelvic sidewall a

title: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome secondary to acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis in a child: a case report from the Tibetan plateau
case_summary:
A 14-year-old Tibetan girl presented with multiple tonic-clonic seizures for 14 hours and progressive headache for 2 weeks accompanied by dizziness and vomiting. She had a 1-month history of haematuria, fever, and pharyngalgia. There was no prior history of hypertension, diabetes, head injury, or drug allergies. On examination, her blood pressure was 150/90 mmHg; she was somnolent, had facial swelling, and muscle strength was grade 4/5 in all four limbs.  
Urinalysis showed 3+ haematuria, 3+ proteinuria, and 1+ leukocyturia. Blood tests revealed urea nitrogen 7.33 mmol/L, serum creatinine 168 ?mol/L, WBC 10 770 cells/mm3, C-reactive protein 40.44 mg/L, hypersensitive CRP 10 mg/L, haemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, anti-streptolysin O antibody 890.4 IU/mL, and antinuclear antibody titre 1:1280.  
Brain T2-FLAIR MRI demonstrated bilateral hyperintense signals in the frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes. DWI showed slight hyperintensity in the bilateral frontal cortex and hypointensity in the right

title: Bilateral Sesamoiditis as First Manifestation of Gout
case_summary:
A 38-year-old white man presented with daily, high-intensity, mechanical pain on the medial plantar surface at the base of his left great toe that began insidiously in October. He reported self-limited episodes of mild local swelling without overt erythema or warmth. He was an otherwise healthy athlete, playing soccer and tennis daily, and reported a family history of gout in his father. He did not take regular medications, denied smoking, and consumed alcohol in small to moderate amounts on weekends. 

On examination, vital signs were normal. There was limited dorsiflexion of the left hallux and mild swelling over the medial sesamoid region, with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 14.4 g/dL, leukocytes 5570/mm3, platelets 208,000/mm3, uric acid 5.6 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 11 mm/h, C-reactive protein negative, antinuclear factor negative, rheumatoid factor 5.3 U/mL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and normal glucose and lipid profile. A 24-hour urinary ur

title: Non?acute promyelocytic leukemia variant, acute myeloid leukemia with translocation (11;17)
case_summary:
A 21-year-old woman presented with a one-month history of fatigue, high-grade fevers, and a generalized skin rash. Prior outpatient evaluation revealed pancytopenia without circulating atypical cells, and empirical antibiotics and topical steroids yielded no improvement. On admission, vital signs were notable for persistent fever. Laboratory studies showed:  
? WBC 0.49?10^9/L, RBC 2.98?10^12/L, hemoglobin 87 g/L, hematocrit 25.8%, MCV 86.6 fL, platelets 13?10^9/L  
? Calcium 2.09 mmol/L, urea 3 mmol/L, creatinine 106 ?mol/L  
? D-dimer 14.91 mg/L, fibrinogen 3.91 g/L  
? PT 17.9 sec (INR 1.4), PTT 39.5 sec (ratio 1.2)  
Peripheral-blood smear confirmed marked pancytopenia and showed occasional blasts and rare abnormal promyelocytes. Given the finding of abnormal promyelocytes in the context of coagulopathy, all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) was initiated pending urgent fluorescence in situ hybridization for PML-RARA translocation.

title: Graft-versus-host disease after liver transplantation
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man with hepatitis C cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma underwent orthotopic liver transplantation from an ABO-identical cadaveric donor. His initial immunosuppression included tacrolimus and a tapering course of methylprednisolone. On postoperative day 9, a liver biopsy showed acute cellular rejection, treated with steroid pulses, and a repeat biopsy 16 days later showed regression. He was discharged on day 25.

He was readmitted on day 31 with fever, hypotension, and a generalized maculopapular eruption most pronounced on the face, trunk, and palms. He also had watery diarrhea, severe leukopenia (0.45 ?10^6/mm^3), anemia, and low hemoglobin. 

Skin biopsies demonstrated vacuolar degeneration of the basal epithelial layer, epidermal lymphocytic infiltration, apoptotic keratinocytes including satellite-cell necrosis, spongiosis, basal cell hydropic change, and subepidermal cleft formation consistent with grade III acute GVHD. A subsequent myelogram revealed hypoplasti

title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura and Ulcerative Colitis - A Potential Link Revisited
case_summary:
A 31-year-old man with a 2-year history of ulcerative colitis was admitted to the intensive care unit with generalized tonic-clonic seizures. 

Two years prior, he had been diagnosed with ulcerative colitis (pseudopolyps throughout the colon and proctosigmoiditis on endoscopy; rectal biopsy showing ulcerated mucosa, cryptitis, and crypt abscesses) and was treated with oral prednisolone and mesalazine. He discontinued therapy after 6 months and remained asymptomatic for 18 months. 

One month before admission, he experienced a flare of ulcerative colitis with loose stools, rectal bleeding, and fatigue, and was restarted on mesalazine and prednisolone (0.5 mg/kg/day). Ten days later, he developed a right common femoral vein thrombosis and was treated with unfractionated heparin followed by rivaroxaban. He then developed persistent hematochezia despite withdrawal of anticoagulants. Two weeks after restarting UC therapy, he had new-onset generalized seizures, requiring intubation and sedat

title: The erroneous perception of a clinical sign: Cholestasis preceding sepsis mimicking hepatitis in an adolescent patient
case_summary:
A previously healthy 14-year-old boy first presented with a 5-day history of stabbing left ankle pain and jaundice without fever. Two days earlier, left ankle radiographs and CT were normal. He denied trauma, animal contact, drug use, or sexual activity; he had recently returned from camping in Italy. On exam, he was in good general condition, afebrile, and hemodynamically stable but markedly jaundiced, with localized ankle pain without swelling, warmth, or erythema. Laboratory studies showed total bilirubin 103 umol/L, platelet count 84 G/L, ALT 178 U/L, AST 129 U/L, C-reactive protein 195 mg/L; full blood count and coagulation tests were otherwise normal. An abdominal ultrasound showed hepatosplenomegaly without biliary obstruction. He was discharged with a preliminary diagnosis of viral hepatitis.

Five days later, he returned in poor general condition with tachycardia (220 bpm), normotension, and persistent jaundice. He had increased tenderness, swelling, and erythema of the left a

title: A rare and important case of Staphylococcus haemolyticus?associated ventricular atrial shunt nephritis
case_summary:
A 43-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department after a witnessed seizure. She had a 3-month history of gross hematuria. Initial vital signs were notable only for hypertension. Laboratory studies showed:  
? Hemoglobin 8.6 million/?L (normocytic anemia)  
? BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 3.9 mg/dL (eGFR 12 mL/min)  
? WBC count 17.9 thousand/?L without a left shift  
? Urinalysis: protein 300 mg/dL, 1960 RBC/HPF, 88 WBC/HPF  

Her history was significant for hydrocephalus due to neurocysticercosis treated with a ventriculoperitoneal shunt 2 years earlier. She subsequently underwent shunt revision and conversion to a ventriculoatrial shunt after infection.  

Workup of hematuria:  
? Cystoscopy: no lesions or masses in bladder or lower GU tract  
? CT abdomen and pelvis: no renal or ureteral stones, masses  
? Renal ultrasound: increased echogenicity suggestive of parenchymal disease  
? Urine culture: no growth  
? Patient denied flank pain; was afebrile; WBC elevation trended to

title: Spontaneous iliopsoas hematoma in a trasfusion dependent ?-thalassemia patient with hypersplenism: a case report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man with transfusion-dependent ?-thalassemia presented with acute worsening of low back pain. While traveling abroad, he had been hospitalized for severe anemia and managed with subcutaneous morphine and two units of packed red cells. After discharge, he experienced recurrence of back pain but denied similar prior episodes, personal or family bleeding disorders, falls or trauma, fever, abdominal pain, and motor or sensory deficits; bowel and bladder function remained intact. Two days after symptom onset, examination revealed a positive straight-leg-raising test on the right and hepatosplenomegaly. He was referred for an MRI of the spine as an outpatient but returned a few hours later with severe pain radiating to the right groin and thigh. 

On admission, he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable but lay in bed with the right leg flexed. There was tender fullness over the right iliac crest region and right upper gluteal area; passive motion of the hip aggravated the pain

title: Autoimmune Enteropathy in an Ulcerative Colitis Patient
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman with a history of endocarditis status post bioprosthetic aortic-valve replacement, adrenal insufficiency, and refractory ulcerative colitis presented 1 month after total colectomy with end ileostomy for evaluation of green, nonbloody, high ostomy output of 4 L/day. She had no prior small-bowel disease. Physical examination revealed epigastric and suprapubic pain. Initial laboratory studies showed an acute kidney injury (creatinine, 1.1 mg/dL; baseline, 0.3-0.8 mg/dL) and anemia (hemoglobin, 9.1 g/dL; baseline, 10.5 g/dL). 

Her ulcerative colitis had been diagnosed in 2009 and treated with steroids and multiple biologics until recurrent bacteremia led to cessation of immunosuppression. Because of persistent diarrhea and colitis on colonoscopy, she underwent total colectomy. Biopsies of the small intestine and foregut before surgery showed normal histology.

Postoperatively, ostomy output ranged from 4 to 10 L/day despite trials of loperamide, atropine/diphenoxylate,

title: First confirmed case of multisystem inflammatory syndrome in adult in Egypt
case_summary:
A 22-year-old man presented in March 2020 with a 1-day history of fever (38.5 degreesC), dry cough, and sore throat. Eight weeks earlier he had a mild, PCR-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. He had received two doses of the Sputnik-V vaccine, at 16 and 12 weeks before admission. On presentation, he was hemodynamically stable. Basic laboratory tests (CBC, creatinine, CRP, and liver enzymes) were normal; chest CT showed no abnormalities; and SARS-CoV-2 PCR was negative. He was treated with ceftriaxone and antipyretics, and over the next 2 days he remained intermittently febrile (?39.5 degreesC) but stable.

In the early morning of hospital day 3, he became drowsy and oliguric and developed hypotension and hypoxemia (GCS 13/15; HR 110 bpm; BP 70/30 mmHg; RR 45 breaths/min; SpO2 85% on room air; T 39.5 degreesC). Chest examination revealed bilateral coarse crepitations. He was transferred to the ICU, started on IV fluids, norepinephrine, and oxygen support. ECG showed sinus tachycardia; echoc

title: Disseminated histoplasmosis in a kidney liver transplant patient from a non-endemic area: A diagnostic challenge
case_summary:
A 63-year-old woman with a history of combined liver and kidney transplantation in 2016 for autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease was referred for iron-deficiency anemia and a persistent dry cough. One year earlier, a colonoscopy performed because of a positive fecal occult blood test had shown diverticulosis and internal hemorrhoids. Six months later, worsening anemia prompted plans for repeat colonoscopy and gastroscopy. In the weeks before presentation, she developed a nonproductive cough and a low-grade fever. 

Chest radiography revealed multiple bilateral patchy infiltrates confirmed on CT scan. On admission, she reported dry cough, fatigue, intermittent fever, night sweats, and a 3-kg weight loss over 3 months. Her temperature was 37.9? degreesC, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and other vital signs were normal. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.1?g/dL, C-reactive protein 32?mg/L, a normal total leukocyte count with neutrophi

title: IgA Vasculitis in a Lung Cancer Patient During Chemoradiotherapy
case_summary:
A 72-year-old man was evaluated for a 1-month history of productive cough. His medical history included hypertension and childhood asthma. Medications were olmesartan, levofloxacin, carbocysteine, and brotizolam. He had a 44-pack-year smoking history, quitting 8 years earlier. Chest CT showed a 25?18 mm nodule in the left upper lobe and a bulky subcarinal lymph node invading the right main bronchus. Bronchoscopy confirmed endobronchial tumor stenosis. He was diagnosed with stage IIIA squamous cell lung carcinoma (T1bN2M0), moderately differentiated, p40-positive, with no EGFR mutation or ALK rearrangement. He received four cycles of cisplatin and docetaxel with stable disease and no severe myelosuppression. 

When definitive radiotherapy was planned, he was hospitalized with high fever. Chest CT showed right lower lobe obstructive pneumonia from tumor progression. He was started on antibiotics and radiotherapy. Ten days after radiotherapy initiation, he developed palpable purpura on bo

title: Diffuse xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis with complex spontaneous pyeloduodenal fistula: A case report
case_summary:
A 57-year-old man presented with a 3-week history of a cutaneous discharge in the right lower quadrant, accompanied by fever, malaise, weight loss, and chronic right flank pain. His history was notable for extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy of the right kidney and recurrent urinary tract infections. On examination, there was a 1 cm cutaneous opening with yellowish discharge in the right lower quadrant. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (14.26 ? 10^3/mL; normal 4-10 ? 10^3/mL) and elevated C-reactive protein (43.02 mg/L; normal 0-5 mg/L). Serum creatinine was 95 umol/L (normal 62-106 umol/L). Urinalysis was unremarkable, urine culture was negative, and PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis in urine and pus was negative. Chest radiograph was normal. A plain abdominal radiograph demonstrated a small, deformed right kidney shadow with multiple compacted stones. 

Noncontrast CT urography showed an atrophic and reniform-shaped right kidney with multiple nephrolithiases, parenchymal cal

title: Severe Thrombocytopenic Purpura in a Child with Brucellosis: Case Presentation and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 5.5-year-old girl from a stockbreeding family presented with a 6-day history of fever up to 38.5  degreesC with afternoon spikes, accompanied by pain in the right wrist and right elbow. She had consumed homemade dairy products. Three days before transfer, initial laboratory tests showed:  
? White blood cells 7.2 ? 10^9/L  
? Platelets 180 ? 10^9/L  
? Hemoglobin 131 g/L  
? ESR 19 mm/h, CRP 10 mg/L  
? AST 62 U/L  
? Monotest negative, Rose Bengal negative  
? Wright agglutination reaction positive at 1/1280  
On that basis, she was started on oral trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (10 mg/kg/day) and rifampicin (15 mg/kg/day).  
On the third day of treatment, she developed generalized purpuric lesions and was found to have severe thrombocytopenia (platelets 3 ? 10^9/L). On arrival, her temperature was 38.8  degreesC. Examination revealed diffuse petechiae, purpura, bruising, wet purpura on the palate, gingival bleeding, and hepatosplenomegaly (liver and spleen palpable 2 cm below the co

title: Peripheral T-cell lymphoma presenting as an ischemic stroke in a 23-year-old woman: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary: A previously healthy 23-year-old Caucasian woman presented with left-sided hemiplegia and a fever of 39 degreesC. On arrival, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 7/15, and she was intubated and admitted to the ICU. A brain CT scan showed a massive right sylvian infarction with severe cerebral edema, and she underwent decompressive craniectomy. Physical examination was notable for multiple palpable left inguinal lymph nodes. Laboratory tests revealed leukocytosis (WBC 17,900 cells/mm?, 91% neutrophils), hemoglobin 10 g/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 356 IU/L, and C-reactive protein at 197 and 8 mg/L. An abdominal CT scan demonstrated multiple enlarged lymph nodes and a retroperitoneal mass adjacent to the left psoas muscle. Transesophageal echocardiography was normal. Blood cultures were negative for bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial pathogens. Serologic tests for cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex virus, Epstein-Barr virus, HIV, tularaemia, Yersinia pestis, brucellosis, leptospirosis, Lyme dis

title: Atypical Presentation of Kimura's Disease in a Male Patient: A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A 28-year-old man (BMI 24.8; heavy smoker) with asthma since age 19 and a history of hepatitis A 3 months earlier presented with one week of colicky periumbilical pain radiating to the lower abdomen and thighs, progressive abdominal distension, fatigue, fever (39.1  degreesC), anorexia, and 10 kg unintentional weight loss over one month. He also noted worsening dyspnea, lower-limb swelling, and severe pain with numbness in his upper back and both hands. He denied trauma, gastrointestinal bleeding, or diarrhoea.  

Past surgical history included adenoidectomy and tonsillectomy at age 2 and grade 4 nasal polyposis. He is allergic to NSAIDs and penicillin. At age 19 he was admitted to the ICU for exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, and edema, diagnosed as pneumonia with atelectasis.  

On examination: barrel-shaped chest with diminished expansion and faint wheezes; hepatomegaly (liver span 20 cm); firm, rounded, nontender cervical and inguinal lymph nodes (1-2 cm); audible bowel sounds; marked

title: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome in a patient with stable dose of olanzapine
journal: Journal of Family Medicine and Primary Care
case_summary:
A 30-year-old man with bipolar affective disorder had been stable on olanzapine 5 mg daily and sodium valproate 400 mg daily for 7 years. Three weeks before transfer, he had fever with rigors for 3-4 days that resolved with over-the-counter medications; he continued his usual medications. Two weeks later, he developed recurrent fever with rigors and was admitted to a local hospital for 4 days. While hospitalized, he experienced increased urinary frequency, loose stools, insomnia, and progressive confusion with inability to recognize relatives; he stopped oral intake by the fourth day. His antipsychotics were withheld.

On admission to our hospital, his temperature was 103  degreesF, pulse 122 bpm, and he was mute with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 8 (E3 M4 V1). Neurologic examination showed lead-pipe rigidity in all four limbs and neck, tremors in the left hand and jaw, brisk deep-tendon reflexes, and equivocal plantar responses.
